topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Electrochemical Engineering: Fundamentals of Electrical Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Introduction to Electrochemical Engineering: Fundamentals of Electrical Engineering

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Electrochemical Engineering?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrochemical Engineering** is the branch of chemical/electrical engineering that deals with the interconversion of chemical energy and electrical energy, and the design, analysis, and operation of industrial processes (such as electrolysis, electrorefining, electroplating, batteries, fuel cells, and corrosion protection) that occur at the interface of an **electrode** and an **electrolyte**.

To engineer these systems, one must first master the **fundamentals of electrical engineering**, because every electrochemical cell is, at its core, an electrical circuit element. The flow of ions, the movement of electrons, and the potentials developed at electrodes are all governed by the same physical laws (Ohm's Law, Kirchhoff's Laws, Faraday's Laws) that govern any conventional circuit.

### 1.2 Why Electrical Engineering Basics in Electrochemistry?

A working electrochemical cell can be represented as a **DC source** (driving redox reactions) connected to an external load or power supply. Therefore, understanding:

- The relationship between **Voltage (V)**, **Current (I)**, and **Resistance (R)**
- **Power (P)** and **Energy (E)** consumption
- **Series and parallel** combinations of cells/resistors
- **Direct Current (DC)** vs. **Alternating Current (AC)** behavior

…is the essential foundation upon which all advanced topics (polarization, overpotential, cell design) are built.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Water-Pipe Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Electricity is like water flowing through a pipe.**
> - **Current (I)** ≈ Water flow rate (litres/second)
> - **Voltage (V)** ≈ Water pressure difference (driving force)
> - **Resistance (R)** ≈ Pipe narrowness or friction
> - **Charge (Q)** ≈ Total quantity of water delivered
> - **Battery/Cell** ≈ Water pump that maintains pressure

Just as a narrow pipe restricts water flow, a high-resistance electrolyte restricts ion flow. A pump (battery) is needed to maintain pressure (voltage) — this is the essence of an electrochemical cell!

### 1.4 Standard SI Units & Fundamental Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Electrical Quantities (SI Units):**
> - **Electric Current (I)** — Ampere (**A**)
> - **Voltage / Potential Difference (V)** — Volt (**V**)
> - **Resistance (R)** — Ohm (**Ω**)
> - **Conductance (G)** — Siemens (**S**)
> - **Charge (Q)** — Coulomb (**C**)
> - **Power (P)** — Watt (**W**)
> - **Energy (E)** — Joule (**J**) or **Watt-hour (Wh)**
> - **Capacitance (C)** — Farad (**F**)
> - **Frequency (f)** — Hertz (**Hz**)

**Key Physical Constant:**
- Elementary charge: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- 1 Faraday ($F$) = $96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$ (charge per mole of electrons)
- Avogadro's number: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ohm's Law — The Cornerstone

**Ohm's Law** states that the current flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the applied voltage, provided the temperature remains constant.

- **Statement:** At constant temperature, $V \propto I$
- **Mathematical form:** $V = I R$
- **Reciprocal form:** $I = G V$, where Conductance $G = 1/R$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in Electrochemistry:** Electrolytes and electrochemical interfaces do NOT always obey Ohm's Law. When overpotentials and concentration gradients exist, the V–I relationship becomes non-linear. Recognizing **where Ohm's Law applies (linear region)** vs. **where it fails (mass-transport limited region)** is critical.

### 2.2 Kirchhoff's Laws

**KCL — Kirchhoff's Current Law (Junction Rule):**
- *"The algebraic sum of currents entering a node equals the algebraic sum of currents leaving the node."*
- Mathematical form: $\sum I_{\text{in}} = \sum I_{\text{out}}$

**KVL — Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (Loop Rule):**
- *"The algebraic sum of all EMFs and voltage drops around any closed loop in a circuit is zero."*
- Mathematical form: $\sum V = 0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** KVL is used to derive the **cell voltage equation** $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ and to analyze multi-cell battery stacks, electrolyzers, and corrosion circuits.

### 2.3 Power and Energy

- **Electrical Power:** Rate at which electrical energy is delivered or consumed.
- **Power in a resistor:** $P = V I = I^2 R = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$
- **Energy:** $E = P \times t = V I t$
- **Heat dissipated (Joule's Law):** $H = I^2 R t$

> [!NOTE]
> In an electrolytic cell, a portion of electrical energy is converted into **chemical energy** (Faradaic work) and the remainder is dissipated as **Joule heat** (ohmic losses in the electrolyte and electrodes).

### 2.4 Series and Parallel Resistances

**Resistors in Series:**
- Same current flows through each; voltages add up.
- $R_{\text{eq}} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots + R_n$

**Resistors in Parallel:**
- Same voltage across each; currents add up.
- $\dfrac{1}{R_{\text{eq}}} = \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dfrac{1}{R_3} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{R_n}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrochemical Application:** Electrolyzers and batteries are often connected in **series** to boost voltage, or in **parallel** to boost current capacity. Equivalent resistance analysis determines the **terminal voltage** under load.

### 2.5 DC vs. AC — What's Relevant?

- **DC (Direct Current):** Used in electrolysis, electroplating, battery charging, electrorefining, and corrosion protection (cathodic protection uses DC).
- **AC (Alternating Current):** Used in AC impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to characterize electrochemical interfaces; not for sustained electrolysis.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU Examinations**

| # | Quantity / Law | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohm's Law | $V = I R$ | V, A, Ω | Linear resistors & ohmic electrolytes |
| 2 | Conductance | $G = 1/R$ | S (Siemens) | Inverse of resistance |
| 3 | Power (general) | $P = V I$ | W | Energy consumption in cells |
| 4 | Power (resistor) | $P = I^2 R = V^2/R$ | W | Joule heating |
| 5 | Energy | $E = P t = V I t$ | J or Wh | Electrolysis energy balance |
| 6 | Heat (Joule's Law) | $H = I^2 R t$ | J | Ohmic losses |
| 7 | Series resistance | $R_s = \sum R_i$ | Ω | Multi-cell stacks |
| 8 | Parallel resistance | $1/R_p = \sum (1/R_i)$ | Ω | Bus-bar arrangements |
| 9 | Charge | $Q = I t$ | C | Faraday's 1st Law |
| 10 | Capacitance | $C = Q/V$ | F | Double-layer charging |
| 11 | Charge of 1 mole e⁻ | $F = N_A e = 96{,}485$ | C mol⁻¹ | Faraday constant |
| 12 | Capacitive energy | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | Energy storage |
| 13 | RMS (AC) | $V_{\text{rms}} = V_p / \sqrt{2}$ | V | AC impedance |
| 14 | Reactance (cap.) | $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$ | Ω | EIS analysis |
| 15 | Reactance (ind.) | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ | Ω | EIS analysis |
| 16 | Impedance (Z) | $Z^2 = R^2 + X^2$ | Ω | Total AC opposition |

> **Reminder:** In exam answers, always pair a numerical answer with its correct **SI unit** — KTU examiners award ½ mark for units alone.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Resistance for Series and Parallel Combinations

#### 3.1.1 Series Combination

**Starting setup:** Consider $n$ resistors $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$ connected end-to-end, all carrying the same current $I$. Let the equivalent resistance be $R_{\text{eq}}$.

By Ohm's Law applied to each resistor, the individual voltage drops are:

$$V_1 = I R_1, \quad V_2 = I R_2, \quad \ldots, \quad V_n = I R_n$$

By **KVL**, the total voltage across the combination equals the sum of individual drops:

$$V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n$$

$$V_{\text{total}} = I R_1 + I R_2 + \cdots + I R_n$$

$$V_{\text{total}} = I (R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_n)$$

But by Ohm's Law applied to the equivalent resistor, $V_{\text{total}} = I R_{\text{eq}}$.

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R_{\text{eq, series}} = R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_n}$$

**Logic takeaway:** Resistances in series always **add up** because each resistor must "use up" a portion of the driving voltage.

#### 3.1.2 Parallel Combination

Consider $n$ resistors $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$ connected across the same two nodes, all having the same voltage $V$ across them.

By Ohm's Law, the current in each branch is:

$$I_1 = \dfrac{V}{R_1}, \quad I_2 = \dfrac{V}{R_2}, \quad \ldots, \quad I_n = \dfrac{V}{R_n}$$

By **KCL**, the total current entering the parallel network is the sum of branch currents:

$$I_{\text{total}} = I_1 + I_2 + \cdots + I_n$$

$$I_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{V}{R_1} + \dfrac{V}{R_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{V}{R_n}$$

$$I_{\text{total}} = V \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{R_n} \right)$$

By Ohm's Law for the equivalent resistor, $I_{\text{total}} = V / R_{\text{eq}}$.

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{R_{\text{eq, parallel}}} = \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{R_n}}$$

**Logic takeaway:** Conductances in parallel **add up** because each branch provides an additional pathway for current.

### 3.2 Derivation: Power Dissipated in a Resistor

Starting from the definition of power as the rate of doing work:

$$P = \dfrac{dW}{dt}$$

For a charge $dQ$ moving through a potential difference $V$, the work done is $dW = V \, dQ$.

Therefore:

$$P = V \, \dfrac{dQ}{dt} = V I \quad \text{(General power expression)}$$

Substituting Ohm's Law $V = IR$:

$$P = (I R) \cdot I = I^2 R$$

Alternatively, substituting $I = V/R$:

$$P = V \cdot \dfrac{V}{R} = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$$

**Final boxed results:**

$$\boxed{P = V I = I^2 R = \dfrac{V^2}{R}}$$

**Electrochemical significance:** In an electrolytic cell, the **Joule heat** generated in the electrolyte is given by $H = I^2 R_{\text{cell}} t$. This is the wasted portion of input energy that does NOT contribute to the desired chemical reaction.

### 3.3 Derivation: Capacitive Energy Storage (Double-Layer Concept)

The energy stored in a capacitor when charged from $0$ to voltage $V$ is:

$$E = \int_0^V V \, dQ$$

Since $Q = C V$, we have $dQ = C \, dV$:

$$E = \int_0^V V \cdot C \, dV = C \int_0^V V \, dV = C \left[ \dfrac{V^2}{2} \right]_0^V$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2}$$

**Electrochemical significance:** The **electrical double layer** at an electrode–electrolyte interface behaves like a capacitor (Helmholtz model: $C_{dl} \propto A / d$, where $d$ is the effective double-layer thickness). This is the foundation of **supercapacitor** technology and **EIS** (Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Key Relationships

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Matrix for KTU Numerical Problems**

| Given | Find | Equation to Use |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ and $R$ | $I$ | $I = V/R$ |
| $I$ and $R$ | $V$ | $V = I R$ |
| $V$ and $I$ | $P$ | $P = V I$ |
| $I$ and $R$ | $P$ | $P = I^2 R$ |
| $V$ and $R$ | $P$ | $P = V^2/R$ |
| $Q$ and $t$ | $I$ | $I = Q/t$ |
| $n$ (moles of e⁻) | $Q$ | $Q = n F$ |
| $m$ (mass deposited) | $Q$ | $Q = m F / M_{\text{eq}}$ (Faraday) |
| Series $R_i$ | $R_{eq}$ | Sum directly |
| Parallel $R_i$ | $R_{eq}$ | Sum reciprocals, then invert |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State and explain Ohm's Law. Mention its limitations in the context of electrochemical systems.** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Ohm's Law states that the current flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the applied voltage, provided physical conditions (especially temperature) remain constant.

$$V = I R$$

where $V$ is the potential difference (V), $I$ is the current (A), and $R$ is the resistance (Ω).

**Limitations in electrochemical systems:**

1. **Non-linear V–I behavior** in the activation-controlled region (low current densities) due to charge-transfer overpotential — the Butler–Volmer equation governs this regime, not Ohm's Law.
2. **Concentration polarization** at high current densities causes current to plateau (mass-transport limited), breaking the V ∝ I proportionality.
3. Electrolyte resistance often **changes with temperature** during operation (Joule heating), violating the constant-temperature condition.

> **Marking note:** Definition 1 mark; Equation ½ mark; 2 limitations — 1½ marks.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the terms (i) Electric Current, (ii) Potential Difference, and (iii) Electrical Resistance. State their SI units.** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Term | Definition | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Electric Current | The rate of flow of electric charge through a cross-section of a conductor | **Ampere (A)** |
| (ii) | Potential Difference | The work done per unit charge in moving a positive test charge between two points in an electric field | **Volt (V)** |
| (iii) | Electrical Resistance | The opposition offered by a material to the flow of electric current; equal to the ratio of voltage to current | **Ohm (Ω)** |

Mathematical expressions:

$$I = \dfrac{dQ}{dt} \qquad V = \dfrac{W}{Q} \qquad R = \dfrac{V}{I}$$

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** `(14 Marks)`

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]`**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for equivalent resistance when:
- **(i)** Three resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ are connected in **series**.
- **(ii)** Three resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ are connected in **parallel**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An electrolytic cell has an EMF of **3.5 V** and an internal resistance of **0.5 Ω**. It is connected to an external load resistance of **6.0 Ω**. Calculate:
- **(i)** The current drawn from the cell.
- **(ii)** The terminal voltage across the load.
- **(iii)** The power dissipated in the load resistance.
- **(iv)** The power wasted as heat in the cell's internal resistance.
- **(v)** The total electrical energy consumed in **2 hours** of operation. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Case (i): Series Combination**

Let three resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ be connected in series across a battery of EMF $V$. The same current $I$ flows through all three.

By Ohm's Law:
$$V_1 = I R_1, \quad V_2 = I R_2, \quad V_3 = I R_3$$

By KVL, total voltage:
$$V = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 = I R_1 + I R_2 + I R_3 = I(R_1 + R_2 + R_3)$$

If the equivalent resistance is $R_s$, then $V = I R_s$. Comparing:

$$\boxed{R_s = R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$$

**Case (ii): Parallel Combination**

Let three resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ be connected in parallel, all having the same voltage $V$ across them.

By Ohm's Law (branch currents):
$$I_1 = \dfrac{V}{R_1}, \quad I_2 = \dfrac{V}{R_2}, \quad I_3 = \dfrac{V}{R_3}$$

By KCL:
$$I = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 = \dfrac{V}{R_1} + \dfrac{V}{R_2} + \dfrac{V}{R_3} = V \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dfrac{1}{R_3} \right)$$

If the equivalent resistance is $R_p$, then $I = V/R_p$. Comparing:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{R_p} = \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dfrac{1}{R_3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_p = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dfrac{1}{R_3}}}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:** EMF $E = 3.5 \text{ V}$; internal resistance $r = 0.5 \text{ Ω}$; load resistance $R = 6.0 \text{ Ω}$.

**Step (i) — Current drawn:**
Total circuit resistance: $R_{\text{total}} = r + R = 0.5 + 6.0 = 6.5 \text{ Ω}$

By Ohm's Law:

$$I = \dfrac{E}{R_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{3.5}{6.5} = 0.5385 \text{ A} \approx 0.538 \text{ A}$$

**Step (ii) — Terminal voltage across load:**

$$V_{\text{load}} = I R = 0.5385 \times 6.0 = 3.231 \text{ V}$$

Alternatively: $V_{\text{load}} = E - I r = 3.5 - (0.5385 \times 0.5) = 3.5 - 0.269 = 3.231 \text{ V}$ ✓

**Step (iii) — Power dissipated in load:**

$$P_{\text{load}} = I^2 R = (0.5385)^2 \times 6.0 = 0.2900 \times 6.0 = 1.740 \text{ W}$$

**Step (iv) — Power wasted as heat in internal resistance:**

$$P_{\text{loss}} = I^2 r = (0.5385)^2 \times 0.5 = 0.2900 \times 0.5 = 0.145 \text{ W}$$

**Step (v) — Total electrical energy in 2 hours:**

Time: $t = 2 \text{ hours} = 7200 \text{ seconds}$

Total power supplied by cell: $P_{\text{total}} = E I = 3.5 \times 0.5385 = 1.885 \text{ W}$

$$E_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{total}} \times t = 1.885 \times 7200 = 13{,}571 \text{ J} \approx 13.57 \text{ kJ}$$

**Verification by energy balance:**
$P_{\text{load}} + P_{\text{loss}} = 1.740 + 0.145 = 1.885 \text{ W} = P_{\text{total}}$ ✓

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `(14 Marks)`

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **electrical equivalent circuit of an electrochemical cell** during electrolysis. Identify each component and write the equation for the cell terminal voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A copper electrorefining plant operates at a total current of **15,000 A** with a cell voltage of **0.30 V** per cell. There are **40 cells connected in series**.
- **(i)** Calculate the total voltage across the entire series bank.
- **(ii)** Calculate the total power consumed.
- **(iii)** If 92% of this power is actually used for copper deposition (Faradaic work) and the rest is lost as Joule heat, calculate the rate of heat generation in kW.
- **(iv)** If the plant operates **24 hours/day** for **30 days**, calculate the total energy consumption in **kWh** and the cost of electricity at **₹6.50 per kWh**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Equivalent Circuit of an Electrolytic Cell

**Equivalent Circuit Diagram (textual description):**

A DC source (rectifier) of EMF $V_0$ drives current through:
1. **$R_{\text{leads}}$** — resistance of connecting cables/bus-bars
2. **$R_{\text{anode}}$** — resistance of the anode material
3. **$R_{\text{electrolyte}}$** — ionic resistance of the bath
4. **$R_{\text{cathode}}$** — resistance of the cathode material
5. **$E_{\text{cell}}$** — back-EMF (the reversible thermodynamic cell voltage)

**Terminal voltage equation:**

$$V_{\text{terminal}} = E_{\text{cell}} + \eta_a + \eta_c + I(R_{\text{anode}} + R_{\text{electrolyte}} + R_{\text{cathode}} + R_{\text{leads}})$$

where $\eta_a$ and $\eta_c$ are anodic and cathodic overpotentials respectively. The term $I \times R_{\text{total}}$ is the **ohmic drop**; $\eta_a + \eta_c$ is the **polarization loss**; $E_{\text{cell}}$ is the **thermodynamic minimum**.

> **Key point for valuation:** Students often forget to include the **back-EMF** $E_{\text{cell}}$ term. A cell does NOT behave like a pure resistor; it opposes current flow thermodynamically.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:** $I = 15{,}000 \text{ A}$; $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.30 \text{ V}$; $n = 40$ cells in series.

**Step (i) — Total voltage:**

$$V_{\text{total}} = n \times V_{\text{cell}} = 40 \times 0.30 = 12.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step (ii) — Total power consumed:**

$$P_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{total}} \times I = 12.0 \times 15{,}000 = 180{,}000 \text{ W} = 180 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (iii) — Rate of heat generation (Joule loss):**

Power used for Cu deposition: $P_{\text{Faradaic}} = 0.92 \times 180 = 165.6 \text{ kW}$

Power lost as heat: $P_{\text{heat}} = P_{\text{total}} - P_{\text{Faradaic}} = 180 - 165.6 = 14.4 \text{ kW}$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{heat}} = 14.4 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step (iv) — Total energy & cost:**

Total time: $t = 24 \times 30 = 720 \text{ hours}$

$$E_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{total}} \times t = 180 \text{ kW} \times 720 \text{ h} = 129{,}600 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\text{Cost} = 129{,}600 \times 6.50 = \text{₹}8{,}42{,}400$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total energy} = 1.296 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh}; \quad \text{Total cost} = \text{₹}8.42 \text{ lakhs (approximately)}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — KTU deducts ½ to 1 mark for missing units in numerical answers. Always write V, A, Ω, W, kWh alongside every numerical result.
> 2. **Confusing EMF with terminal voltage** — EMF is the open-circuit voltage; terminal voltage is the voltage under load: $V_t = E - Ir$.
> 3. **Wrong direction in KVL** — When writing KVL for an electrolytic cell, the back-EMF must be summed with the **same sign convention** as ohmic drops, not subtracted arbitrarily.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation** — In 7-mark derivation questions (e.g., series/parallel resistance), KTU expects at least **4–5 explicit steps** with statements of KCL/KVL. A single-line "final formula" gets only 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing AC and DC concepts** — When asked for DC power, do **not** insert RMS conversions unless the problem specifies AC. For an electrolyzer, it's always DC.
> 6. **Sign errors in energy calculations** — Energy is always **positive**; if your computed $E$ is negative, recheck the EMF direction and load.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Electrical Engineering Fundamentals**

- ✅ **Ohm's Law** $V = IR$ applies ONLY in the linear (ohmic) regime; failed at high current densities in electrochemical cells.
- ✅ **1 Ampere** = 1 Coulomb per second; **1 Volt** = 1 Joule per Coulomb; **1 Ohm** = 1 Volt per Ampere.
- ✅ **Conductance** $G = 1/R$, measured in **Siemens (S)**.
- ✅ **Series resistance ADDS**; **Parallel conductance ADDS** (i.e., reciprocals add).
- ✅ **Power** $P = VI = I^2R = V^2/R$; **Energy** $E = P \times t$.
- ✅ **Joule's heating law** $H = I^2Rt$ governs all ohmic losses in electrolyte, electrodes, and bus-bars.
- ✅ **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** = conservation of charge; **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** = conservation of energy.
- ✅ **Faraday's constant** $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$ — the bridge between electrical (Coulombs) and chemical (moles of electrons) worlds.
- ✅ **Capacitance** $C = Q/V$; **Capacitive energy** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$; relevant to the **electrical double layer** at electrode–electrolyte interfaces.
- ✅ For an **electrolytic cell**: $V_{\text{supply}} = E_{\text{back-EMF}} + \eta_{\text{anode}} + \eta_{\text{cathode}} + I R_{\text{ohmic}}$
- ✅ For a **galvanic cell (battery)**: $V_{\text{terminal}} = E_{\text{cell}} - I r_{\text{internal}}$
- ✅ **AC concepts to remember for EIS:** $X_C = 1/(2\pi fC)$, $X_L = 2\pi fL$, $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$.
- ✅ **Electroplating / electrorefining / chlor-alkali / batteries / fuel cells / cathodic protection** — all require DC electrical input/output.
- ✅ Always carry **SI units** through every calculation; the KTU evaluator is strict about dimensional consistency.
- ✅ In **multiple-cell banks**: series → voltage multiplies, current same; parallel → current multiplies, voltage same.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrostatics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electrostatics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Electrostatics

**Electrostatics** is the branch of physics and physical chemistry that deals with the phenomena and properties of stationary or slow-moving electric charges. In the context of **Electrochemical Engineering**, electrostatics is the foundational framework that describes the behavior of charges at rest, the fields they produce, and the potentials that drive ionic and electronic motion. It provides the mathematical scaffolding — primarily **Coulomb's Law**, **Gauss's Law**, and the **Poisson-Boltzmann equation** — required to model phenomena such as the **electrical double layer (EDL)**, **electrode-solution interfaces**, and **ionic transport in electrolytes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For PEPOT637, Module 1 builds the electromagnetic and electrostatic groundwork. Students must master *electric fields in vacuum and dielectrics, electric potential, capacitance, and Poisson/Laplace equations*, since these directly lead to Module 2 (Electrolytic conductance) and Module 3 (Electrode kinetics and double layer).

### 1.2 Key Fundamental Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electric Charge | $Q$ or $q$ | Coulomb (C) | Fundamental property of matter; quantized as $q = n \cdot e$, where $e = \mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ C}$ |
| Electric Field | $\vec{E}$ | V·m$^{-1}$ or N·C$^{-1}$ | Force experienced by a **unit positive test charge** |
| Electric Potential | $V$ or $\phi$ | Volt (V) | Work done per unit charge to bring a test charge from infinity |
| Permittivity | $\varepsilon$ | F·m$^{-1}$ | Ability of a medium to permit electric field lines |
| Electric Flux | $\Phi_E$ | V·m | Total number of field lines crossing a surface |
| Capacitance | $C$ | Farad (F) | Charge stored per unit potential difference |

> [!NOTE]
> **Permittivity of free space (vacuum):**
> $$\varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ F/m} \approx \mathbf{8.85 \times 10^{-12}\ C^2/(N \cdot m^2)}$$
> Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of a medium: $\varepsilon_r = \varepsilon / \varepsilon_0$. For water at 25 °C, $\varepsilon_r \approx 78.5$, which is critical in electrochemical cells.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tank on a hill**. The water in the tank represents **electric charge**, and the height of the hill represents **electric potential**. Just as water flows downhill from high elevation to low elevation (driven by the gravitational potential difference), electric charges flow from a region of high electric potential to low electric potential (driven by the **potential difference**, i.e., *voltage*). The slope of the hill — how steep it is — is analogous to the **electric field**: a steep slope = strong field, a flat region = weak field. The pipes and tanks represent **conductors and capacitors**, which store and channel the "fluid of charge."

> [!TIP]
> **First-Read Insight:** Electrostatics is essentially the *geometry of force around charges*. Everything else in electrochemistry — current, resistance, cell potential, double-layer capacitance — is a *dynamic* consequence of these static foundations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Coulomb's Law — The Foundation

**Coulomb's Law (1785)** states that the electrostatic force between two point charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ separated by a distance $r$ in a medium of permittivity $\varepsilon$ is:

$$\vec{F} = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \hat{r}$$

- The force is **repulsive** if the charges have the same sign and **attractive** if they have opposite signs.
- The force is a **central force**, directed along the line joining the two charges.
- It obeys the **superposition principle**: the net force on a charge is the vector sum of forces from all other charges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In electrochemical systems, Coulomb's law governs the **interionic attraction** described by the Debye-Hückel theory. The activity coefficients of ions in solution are essentially corrections to ideal behavior caused by these interionic Coulombic forces.

### 2.2 Electric Field — The Force per Unit Charge

The **electric field** at a point in space is defined as the force experienced by a small positive test charge $q_0$ placed at that point, divided by the magnitude of the test charge:

$$\vec{E} = \lim_{q_0 \to 0} \frac{\vec{F}}{q_0}$$

For a single point charge $Q$ at the origin, the field at a distance $r$ is:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon} \cdot \frac{Q}{r^2} \hat{r}$$

The electric field is a **vector field**; it has both magnitude and direction at every point in space.

### 2.3 Electric Potential and Potential Difference

The **electric potential** $V$ at a point $P$ is the work done per unit charge in bringing a positive test charge from infinity to that point, against the electric field:

$$V_P = \frac{W_{\infty \to P}}{q_0} = -\int_{\infty}^{P} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

For a point charge:

$$V(r) = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon} \cdot \frac{Q}{r}$$

The **potential difference** between two points A and B is the work done per unit charge in moving from A to B:

$$V_{AB} = V_A - V_B = \int_{A}^{B} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

### 2.4 Relation Between E and V

The electric field is the **negative gradient** of the electric potential:

$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{k} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **KTU-favorite relation**. The $\nabla$ (del/nabla) operator connects field and potential, and forms the basis of the Poisson equation in electrochemistry.

### 2.5 Gauss's Law

**Gauss's Law** (one of Maxwell's four equations) states that the total electric flux through a closed surface is equal to the net charge enclosed, divided by the permittivity:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon}$$

This law is extremely useful for calculating $\vec{E}$ in systems with **high symmetry** (spherical, cylindrical, or planar).

### 2.6 Capacitance and Dielectrics

A **capacitor** stores electric energy in the form of an electric field between two conductors separated by an insulator (dielectric). The capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{Q}{V}$$

For a parallel-plate capacitor with plate area $A$, separation $d$, and dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon A}{d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

**Energy stored in a capacitor:**

$$U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{Q^2}{2C} = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon E^2 \cdot (\text{Volume})$$

> [!TIP]
> **Electrochemical Connection:** The **electrical double layer (EDL)** at an electrode-electrolyte interface behaves like a parallel-plate capacitor on a nanometer scale, with capacitance values of 10–40 $\mu$F/cm$^2$. This is central to **supercapacitor** design.

### 2.7 Electric Dipole

A system of two equal and opposite charges $+q$ and $-q$ separated by a small distance $2a$ forms an **electric dipole**. The **dipole moment** $\vec{p}$ is:

$$\vec{p} = q \cdot 2\vec{a}$$

The potential due to a dipole at a far-field point $(r, \theta)$:

$$V_{dipole}(r, \theta) = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon} \cdot \frac{p \cos\theta}{r^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Water is a dipole**! The permanent dipole moment of H$_2$O ($p \approx 1.85$ D) is why water has such a high dielectric constant and is the universal electrochemical solvent.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Conditions / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{F} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon}\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}\hat{r}$ | Coulomb's Law | Point charges, isotropic medium |
| 2 | $\vec{E} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon}\frac{Q}{r^2}\hat{r}$ | Field of point charge | Outside a spherical charge distribution |
| 3 | $V(r) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon}\frac{Q}{r}$ | Potential of point charge | Reference at infinity |
| 4 | $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ | Field from potential | General vector relation |
| 5 | $\oint_S \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon}$ | Gauss's Law (integral) | Closed surface |
| 6 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}$ | Gauss's Law (differential) | $\rho$ is volume charge density |
| 7 | $\nabla^2 V = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon}$ | **Poisson's Equation** | Inhomogeneous charge region |
| 8 | $\nabla^2 V = 0$ | **Laplace's Equation** | Charge-free region |
| 9 | $C = \frac{\varepsilon A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitance | Uniform field, no fringing |
| 10 | $C = 4\pi\varepsilon a$ | Spherical capacitor (isolated) | Outer sphere at infinity |
| 11 | $C = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon L}{\ln(b/a)}$ | Coaxial / cylindrical capacitor | Length $L$, inner radius $a$, outer $b$ |
| 12 | $\vec{p} = q \cdot 2\vec{a}$ | Electric dipole moment | Vector from $-q$ to $+q$ |
| 13 | $U = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ | Energy stored in capacitor | Steady state, no leakage |
| 14 | $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ | Absolute permittivity | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m |
| 15 | $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$ | Electric displacement | In linear dielectric |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse $\vec{E}$ (field in vacuum/material) with $\vec{D}$ (displacement). Always remember: $\vec{E}$ is the *true* field experienced by charges, while $\vec{D}$ is the *free-charge* field, useful when bound charges in dielectrics are present.

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of Electrostatics |
|---|---|
| **Batteries & Fuel Cells** | Potential distribution across cell, Nernst potential derivation |
| **Supercapacitors** | EDL capacitance, energy density $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ |
| **Electroplating** | Field uniformity, Coulombic deposition ($m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$) |
| **Corrosion Engineering** | Local cell potentials, galvanic series |
| **Microfluidics / Lab-on-chip** | Field-driven (dielectrophoresis) particle manipulation |
| **Semiconductor Industry** | Poisson equation for p-n junction band bending |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Electric Field from Coulomb's Law

We define the electric field at point $P$ as the force experienced per unit positive test charge placed at $P$.

**Step 1 — Place a test charge.**
Let a positive test charge $q_0$ be placed at point $P$, located at a distance $r$ from a source charge $Q$ placed at the origin.

**Step 2 — Apply Coulomb's Law.**
The Coulombic force on $q_0$ is:

$$F = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q \, q_0}{r^2}$$

**Step 3 — Define the electric field.**
By definition, $\vec{E} = \vec{F} / q_0$. Dividing the above by $q_0$:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q}{r^2} \hat{r}$$

This is the field of a point charge in vacuum. For a dielectric medium, simply replace $\varepsilon_0$ with $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Electric Potential from Electric Field

**Step 1 — Work-energy definition.**
The work done by an external agent in moving a charge $q_0$ from point A to B against the field $\vec{E}$ is:

$$W_{A \to B} = -q_0 \int_{A}^{B} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

The negative sign is because the external force opposes the field force.

**Step 2 — Define potential difference.**
The work done per unit charge is the potential difference:

$$V_B - V_A = \frac{W_{A \to B}}{q_0} = -\int_{A}^{B} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Step 3 — Take the reference at infinity.**
If we let $A \to \infty$ where $V_\infty = 0$, then the potential at $B$ is:

$$V_B = -\int_{\infty}^{B} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the field of a point charge.**
For $\vec{E} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} \hat{r}$ and a radial path $d\vec{l} = dr\,\hat{r}$:

$$V(r) = -\int_{\infty}^{r} \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r'^2} dr' = -\frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \left[ -\frac{1}{r'} \right]_{\infty}^{r}$$

$$V(r) = -\frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \left( -\frac{1}{r} + 0 \right) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q}{r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The potential of a point charge is a **scalar quantity** that decreases as $1/r$, while the field decreases as $1/r^2$. This means potential has a *longer range* than the field — a critical concept in electrochemical work functions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Poisson's Equation (KEY KTU TOPIC)

**Step 1 — Start with Gauss's Law in differential form.**
Starting from $\oint_S \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0}$, applying the divergence theorem $\oint_S \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) dV$:

$$\int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) dV = \int_V \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0} dV$$

Equating integrands (true for arbitrary $V$):

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$.**
Since $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ (from $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ definition):

$$\nabla \cdot (-\nabla V) = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}$$

$$-\nabla^2 V = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 3 — Final form of Poisson's equation.**

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 V = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a region with **no free charge** ($\rho = 0$), Poisson's equation reduces to **Laplace's equation**:
> $$\nabla^2 V = 0$$
> These two equations are the *mathematical backbone* of electrochemistry — used to find the potential distribution near charged electrodes, across membranes, and in semiconductor junctions.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Parallel-Plate Capacitor with Dielectric

**Problem:** A parallel-plate capacitor has plates of area $A = 100\ \text{cm}^2$ separated by a dielectric of thickness $d = 0.5\ \text{mm}$ and relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r = 4$. Find (a) the capacitance, (b) the charge stored when $V = 100$ V is applied, and (c) the energy stored.

**Solution:**

**(a) Capacitance:**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

Convert units: $A = 100 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^2$, $d = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$.

$$C = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(4)(10^{-2})}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$C = \frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-13}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.083 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F} \approx \mathbf{708\ pF}$$

**(b) Charge stored:**

$$Q = C \cdot V = 7.083 \times 10^{-10} \times 100 = 7.083 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{C} \approx \mathbf{70.8\ nC}$$

**(c) Energy stored:**

$$U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2}(7.083 \times 10^{-10})(100)^2 = 3.54 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{J} \approx \mathbf{3.54\ \mu J}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Spherical Capacitance via Gauss's Law

**Problem:** Find the capacitance of an isolated conducting sphere of radius $a$ in vacuum.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Field outside the sphere.**
By symmetry, $\vec{E}$ is radial. Apply Gauss's law to a sphere of radius $r > a$:

$$\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = E(r) \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}$$

$$E(r) = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}$$

**Step 2 — Potential of the sphere (relative to infinity).**

$$V(a) = -\int_{\infty}^{a} E(r)\, dr = \int_{a}^{\infty} \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} dr = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 a}$$

**Step 3 — Capacitance.**

$$C = \frac{Q}{V(a)} = \frac{Q}{\frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 a}} = 4\pi\varepsilon_0 a$$

> [!TIP]
> For $a = 1\ \text{cm} = 0.01\ \text{m}$:
> $$C = 4\pi(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(0.01) \approx 1.11\ \text{pF}$$
> This is the **self-capacitance** of a small sphere — useful in electrospray and aerosol electrostatics.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Concept Dependency Map

| Concept | Requires | Leads To |
|---|---|---|
| Charge & Coulomb's Law | — | Electric Field |
| Electric Field | Coulomb's Law, Superposition | Gauss's Law |
| Gauss's Law | Electric Field | Field calculation, Poisson Eq. |
| Electric Potential | Electric Field | Poisson's & Laplace's Eq. |
| Capacitance | Potential difference, geometry | Energy storage, EDL |
| Poisson's Equation | Gauss's Law, $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ | Potential distribution in electrolytes |
| Dipole | Two opposite charges | Molecular polarity, solvent effects |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define electric potential. Derive the relation between electric field and electric potential.

**Model Answer:**

**Electric Potential:** The electric potential at a point in an electric field is defined as the amount of work done in bringing a unit positive test charge from infinity to that point, against the electrostatic force, without any change in kinetic energy. It is a **scalar quantity** measured in **volts (V)**.

**Mathematical form:**

$$V(r) = \frac{W}{q_0} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q}{r}$$

**Relation between $\vec{E}$ and $V$:** The electric field is the **negative gradient** of the potential. In differential form:

$$\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\frac{dV}{dr}\hat{r}\ \text{(in 1D)}$$

In Cartesian coordinates:

$$E_x = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial x},\quad E_y = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial y},\quad E_z = -\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}$$

The negative sign indicates that $\vec{E}$ points in the direction of **decreasing** potential. If $V$ is constant, $\vec{E} = 0$ (electrostatic equilibrium inside a conductor).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
State and explain Gauss's law in electrostatics. Mention any two of its applications.

**Model Answer:**

**Gauss's Law:** The total electric flux through any closed surface (called a Gaussian surface) is equal to $\tfrac{1}{\varepsilon_0}$ times the total (net) charge enclosed within that surface.

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0}$$

In differential form: $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho/\varepsilon_0$.

**Two Applications:**

1. **Field due to an infinite line of charge:** Using a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $r$ and length $L$, one obtains $E = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$, where $\lambda$ is linear charge density.

2. **Field outside a uniformly charged sphere:** Using a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r > R$ (sphere's radius), $E = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2}$, same as a point charge.

> [!NOTE]
> Gauss's law is always true, but it is *useful* for direct field calculation **only** when there is sufficient symmetry (spherical, cylindrical, or planar).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Derive **Poisson's equation** in electrostatics. Explain how it reduces to Laplace's equation and state its significance in modeling potential distribution near a charged electrode in an electrolyte.

**(b) (7 marks)** A parallel-plate capacitor has plates of area $200\ \text{cm}^2$ separated by a distance of $2\ \text{mm}$. The space between the plates is filled with a dielectric of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r = 6$. Calculate: (i) the capacitance, (ii) the charge on each plate when a potential difference of $200\ \text{V}$ is applied, and (iii) the energy stored in the capacitor.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Poisson's Equation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Start with Gauss's law in integral form. For a closed surface $S$ enclosing volume $V$ with charge density $\rho$:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}\int_V \rho\, dV$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **divergence theorem** to convert the surface integral to a volume integral:

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{E})\, dV$$

**Step 3:** Equating the two volume integrals (valid for arbitrary $V$):

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 4:** Use the relation between field and potential, $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$:

$$\nabla \cdot (-\nabla V) = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}$$

$$-\nabla^2 V = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}$$

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 V = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}}$$

This is **Poisson's equation**.

**Step 5 — Reduction to Laplace's equation:** In regions where there is **no free charge** ($\rho = 0$):

$$\nabla^2 V = 0$$

This is **Laplace's equation**, the homogeneous form of Poisson's equation.

**Step 6 — Significance in electrochemistry:** Near a charged electrode immersed in an electrolyte, the ionic space charge creates a non-zero $\rho(x)$ in the diffuse double layer. Poisson's equation governs the potential profile $\phi(x)$ from the electrode surface into the bulk solution. Combined with the **Boltzmann distribution** for ion concentrations, it gives the **Poisson-Boltzmann equation** — the central equation of the **Gouy-Chapman model** of the electrical double layer. Laplace's equation, in contrast, applies in the bulk electrolyte and inside the metal electrode (where $\phi$ is constant).

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $A = 200\ \text{cm}^2 = 200 \times 10^{-4} = 0.02\ \text{m}^2$, $d = 2\ \text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 6$, $V = 200\ \text{V}$.

**(i) Capacitance:**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(6)(0.02)}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C = \frac{1.06248 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.31 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F} \approx \mathbf{531\ pF}$$

**(ii) Charge on each plate:**

$$Q = CV = (5.31 \times 10^{-10})(200) = 1.062 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{C} \approx \mathbf{0.106\ \mu C}$$

**(iii) Energy stored:**

$$U = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 = \frac{1}{2}(5.31 \times 10^{-10})(200)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(5.31 \times 10^{-10})(40000)$$

$$U = 1.062 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{J} \approx \mathbf{10.6\ \mu J}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) (7 marks)** State Coulomb's law of electrostatics. Using it, derive the expression for the electric field intensity due to a point charge. Explain the superposition principle with an example of two point charges.

**(b) (7 marks)** Using Gauss's law, derive the expression for the electric field due to an **infinitely long straight charged wire** with uniform linear charge density $\lambda$. A wire of length $50\ \text{cm}$ carries a charge of $5\ \mu\text{C}$ uniformly distributed. Find the electric field at a point $20\ \text{cm}$ perpendicular from the wire.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Coulomb's Law and Electric Field (7 marks):**

**Statement:** The electrostatic force between two point charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ separated by distance $r$ in a medium of permittivity $\varepsilon$ is **directly proportional** to the product of the charges and **inversely proportional** to the square of the distance between them. The force acts along the line joining the two charges.

$$\vec{F}_{12} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}\cdot\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}\hat{r}_{12}$$

**Derivation of Electric Field:**

Consider a source charge $Q$ at the origin. Place a test charge $q_0$ at distance $r$. The Coulombic force is:

$$\vec{F} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\cdot\frac{Q q_0}{r^2}\hat{r}$$

Dividing by $q_0$:

$$\vec{E} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\cdot\frac{Q}{r^2}\hat{r}$$

The direction is **radially outward** for positive $Q$ and **radially inward** for negative $Q$.

**Superposition Principle:** The net electric field at a point due to a system of $n$ point charges is the **vector sum** of the individual fields produced by each charge:

$$\vec{E}_{net} = \vec{E}_1 + \vec{E}_2 + \vec{E}_3 + \cdots + \vec{E}_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q_i}{r_i^2}\hat{r}_i$$

**Example:** For two charges $+Q$ at $(a, 0)$ and $-Q$ at $(-a, 0)$, the field at point $P$ on the perpendicular bisector is found by vectorially adding the two fields. The radial components cancel, and only the axial components add. This gives rise to the **electric dipole field** with the characteristic $1/r^3$ dependence (in contrast to the monopole $1/r^2$ field).

---

**(b) Field of an Infinite Line Charge (7 marks):**

**Derivation using Gauss's Law:**

Consider an infinitely long straight wire with uniform linear charge density $\lambda$ (C/m). By symmetry, the electric field must be **radial** (perpendicular to the wire) and have the same magnitude at all points equidistant from the wire.

**Choose a Gaussian surface:** A cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$ coaxial with the wire.

**Apply Gauss's law:**

$$\oint_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{enc}}{\varepsilon_0}$$

The flux through the flat ends is **zero** ($\vec{E} \perp d\vec{A}$). Flux through the curved surface:

$$E(r) \cdot (2\pi r L) = \frac{\lambda L}{\varepsilon_0}$$

$$E(r) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 r}$$

This is the magnitude of the radial field. The vector form is $\vec{E}(r) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 r}\hat{r}$.

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:** $L = 50\ \text{cm} = 0.5\ \text{m}$, $Q = 5\ \mu\text{C} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C}$, $r = 20\ \text{cm} = 0.2\ \text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Linear charge density:**

$$\lambda = \frac{Q}{L} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5} = 10^{-5}\ \text{C/m}$$

**Step 2 — Electric field at $r = 0.2\ \text{m}$:**

$$E = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 r} = \frac{10^{-5}}{2\pi(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(0.2)}$$

$$E = \frac{10^{-5}}{1.1126 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 8.99 \times 10^{5}\ \text{V/m}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 8.99 \times 10^{5}\ \text{V/m} \approx 0.9\ \text{MV/m}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Electrostatics Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign** in $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$. The negative sign carries the physical meaning that the field points from high to low potential. Marks are lost for writing $\vec{E} = +\nabla V$.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** (cm$^2$ to m$^2$, mm to m, $\mu$C to C) cause cascading numerical errors. Always write the unit-conversion step explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing the permittivity** $\varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon_0$. For air/vacuum, $\varepsilon_r = 1$ and $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$. For any other medium, **always multiply by $\varepsilon_r$**.
> 4. **Not stating the direction** of $\vec{E}$. A magnitude-only answer in a 7-mark problem loses at least 1 mark. Always state: *radially outward* / *radially inward* / *along the line joining*.
> 5. **Forgetting the boundary conditions** when applying Poisson/Laplace equations. KTU examiners specifically look for: (i) potential continuity at interfaces, (ii) reference potential at infinity, (iii) field vanishing inside conductors.
> 6. **Plug-and-chug without showing derivation steps.** Even if the numerical answer is correct, marks are reserved for the *derivation* (Coulomb → E, Gauss → E, E → V). A bare numerical answer in a 7-mark sub-part typically scores 2–3 marks maximum.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Electrostatics (KTU Module 1):**

- [x] **Coulomb's Law:** $\vec{F} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon}\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}\hat{r}$ — inverse-square, central, superposition holds.
- [x] **Electric Field:** $\vec{E} = \vec{F}/q_0$ — vector field, units V/m or N/C.
- [x] **Electric Potential:** $V = W/q_0$ — scalar, units Volt, $V(\infty) = 0$ reference.
- [x] **$\vec{E}$–$V$ relation:** $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ (negative gradient).
- [x] **Gauss's Law:** $\oint \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A} = Q_{enc}/\varepsilon$ — useful only with symmetry.
- [x] **Poisson's Equation:** $\nabla^2 V = -\rho/\varepsilon_0$ — non-zero charge regions.
- [x] **Laplace's Equation:** $\nabla^2 V = 0$ — charge-free regions.
- [x] **Capacitance formulas:** Parallel-plate $C = \varepsilon A/d$; spherical $C = 4\pi\varepsilon a$; cylindrical $C = 2\pi\varepsilon L / \ln(b/a)$.
- [x] **Energy stored:** $U = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2 = Q^2/(2C) = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 \cdot \text{Volume}$.
- [x] **Dipole moment:** $\vec{p} = q \cdot 2\vec{a}$, $V_{dip} = \frac{p\cos\theta}{4\pi\varepsilon r^2}$.
- [x] **Permittivity values to memorize:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m; water $\varepsilon_r \approx 78.5$.
- [x] **Electrochemical link:** EDL = nanometer-scale capacitor; Poisson-Boltzmann = governing PDE for double layer.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU Exam:**
> 1. Draw a free-body / charge diagram before any calculation — examiners reward clear diagrams.
> 2. For Gauss's law problems, **always state the symmetry** first, then choose the surface, then write the flux equation.
> 3. For 14-mark questions, aim for: 2 marks for stating the law/definition, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for any diagram/figure, and the rest for numerical/computational part.
> 4. Always end with a **physical interpretation sentence** — e.g., "Thus, the field decreases as $1/r$ from a long wire, which is why high-voltage transmission lines have a minimum safe approach distance."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electrodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrodynamics** is the branch of physics and electrochemical engineering that studies the motion of electric charges and the associated time-varying electric and magnetic fields in conducting media (electrolytes, electrodes, and interfaces). In the context of electrochemical engineering, it encompasses the quantitative description of charge transport, electric field distribution, and the coupled energy-conversion phenomena that occur at electrodes and within electrolyte solutions.

In electrochemical systems, electrodynamics specifically deals with:

- **Charge transport** through electrolytes (ionic conduction)
- **Charge transfer** across electrode–electrolyte interfaces (Faradaic processes)
- **Electric field and potential distribution** in electrochemical cells
- **Coupled electromagnetic and chemical phenomena** that govern cell operation

The foundation rests on four Maxwell's equations, supplemented by constitutive relations and the Nernst-Planck equation for ionic systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Water-Pipe Analogy for Electrodynamics:**
> Imagine electricity flowing through a wire (or ions flowing through an electrolyte) as water flowing through a pipe.
> - **Voltage (V)** is the *pressure difference* pushing the water/charges.
> - **Current (I)** is the *flow rate* of water/charges.
> - **Resistance (R)** is the *pipe narrowness* opposing flow.
> - **Capacitance (C)** is the *water tank* storing charge.
> - **Electrolyte** is like a pipe containing two types of particles (cations moving one way, anions the other) — both carry current, unlike electrons in a metal.

In electrochemistry, the "pipe" is the electrolyte, the "water" is ions, and the "pumps" are the redox reactions at the electrodes that drive the system.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The fundamental constants used throughout electrodynamics are:

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Vacuum permittivity | $\varepsilon_0$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹²** | F/m |
| Elementary charge | $e$ | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹** | C |
| Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | **1.381 × 10⁻²³** | J/K |
| Avogadro number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³** | mol⁻¹ |
| Faraday constant | $F$ | **96,485** | C/mol |
| Gas constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Molar volume (STP) | $V_m$ | **22.414** | L/mol |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Faraday constant** $F = N_A \cdot e$ is the *signature* constant of electrochemical engineering — it bridges atomic-scale charge ($e$) and macroscopic mole-scale charge ($F$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Maxwell's Equations (Electrochemical Form)

Maxwell's equations are the four pillars of electrodynamics. In the context of electrochemical engineering, we work primarily with the **quasi-static limit** (slowly varying fields, no radiation).

### 2.1.1 Gauss's Law (Electric)

$$\oint_S \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{A} = Q_{enclosed}$$

- **Why:** Total electric displacement flux through a closed surface equals enclosed free charge.
- **How:** Used to calculate electric fields in symmetric geometries (spherical, cylindrical, planar) inside electrolytes and at interfaces.
- $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$ where $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ is the permittivity of the medium.

### 2.1.2 Gauss's Law (Magnetic)

$$\oint_S \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{A} = 0$$

- Magnetic monopoles do not exist; field lines are continuous loops.

### 2.1.3 Faraday's Law of Induction

$$\oint_C \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_S \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{A}$$

- Time-varying magnetic fields induce electric fields (curl $\vec{E} \neq 0$).
- **KTU Insight:** Less central to DC electrochemical cells but critical for AC impedance spectroscopy and electrolysis with pulsed current.

### 2.1.4 Ampère-Maxwell Law

$$\oint_C \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enclosed} + \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{d}{dt}\int_S \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A}$$

- The conduction current plus displacement current together produce magnetic fields.

## 2.2 Electrochemical Quantities — Logic Flow

**Step 1 — Define Current Density** $\vec{J}$:
- Current per unit cross-sectional area, vectorial.
- In metals: $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$ (Ohm's law at the local level).
- In electrolytes: ionic contributions from multiple species (cations + anions).

**Step 2 — Define Conductivity** $\sigma$:
- Measure of a medium's ability to conduct current.
- For electrolytes: $\sigma = F \sum_i z_i^2 u_i c_i$ where $u_i$ is ionic mobility, $c_i$ is concentration.
- Units: **S/m** (Siemens per meter) or equivalently $\Omega^{-1} \cdot m^{-1}$.

**Step 3 — Define Molar and Equivalent Conductivity**:
- **Molar conductivity:** $\Lambda_m = \kappa / c$ (S·m²/mol)
- **Equivalent conductivity:** $\Lambda_{eq} = \kappa / N$ where $N$ is normality (S·m²/equiv)
- **Kohlrausch's Law:** $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^0 - K\sqrt{c}$ for strong electrolytes at low concentrations.

**Step 4 — Charge Transfer at Interface**:
- Butler-Volmer equation: relates current density to overpotential.
- Nernst equation: relates equilibrium potential to concentration.

**Step 5 — Transport in Electrolyte (Nernst-Planck)**:

$$\vec{J}_i = -D_i \nabla c_i - \frac{z_i F}{RT} D_i c_i \nabla \phi + c_i \vec{v}$$

- **Diffusion** (first term) + **Migration** (second term) + **Convection** (third term).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\vec{F} = q\vec{E}$ | Force on charge | Electrode–ion interaction |
| 2 | $V = \int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}$ | Electric potential | Cell voltage computation |
| 3 | $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = Q/\varepsilon_0$ | Gauss's law | Field in symmetric geometry |
| 4 | $C = Q/V$ | Capacitance | Double-layer capacitance |
| 5 | $R = \rho L / A$ | Resistance | Electrolyte resistance |
| 6 | $G = 1/R = \kappa A / L$ | Conductance | Cell constant $K = L/A$ |
| 7 | $\Lambda_m = \kappa / c$ | Molar conductivity | Strong/weak electrolyte analysis |
| 8 | $\alpha_{t^+} = \frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^0} = t^+ + t^-$ | Transport number | Hittorf / moving boundary methods |
| 9 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Cell EMF | Galvanic cell design |
| 10 | $E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Nernst equation | Equilibrium potential |
| 11 | $J = nFk_c C$ | Rate expression (mass transfer) | Limiting current |
| 12 | $i = i_0 [\exp(\alpha_a F \eta / RT) - \exp(-\alpha_c F \eta / RT)]$ | Butler-Volmer | Electrode kinetics |
| 13 | $\Lambda_m^0 = \lambda_+^0 + \lambda_-^0$ | Kohlrausch's law of independent migration | $\Lambda^0$ at infinite dilution |
| 14 | $m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF}$ | Faraday's 1st law | Mass deposited/dissolved |
| 15 | $Q = nF \cdot z$ | Faraday's 2nd law | Charge–mole conversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention for transport number:** $\alpha_{t^+} + \alpha_{t^-} = 1$ always. For KCl at 25°C, $t_{K^+} \approx 0.49$ and $t_{Cl^-} \approx 0.51$ (nearly equal because mobilities are similar).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Electrodynamics in electrochemical engineering enables:

- **Battery design:** Predicting cell voltage, internal resistance, and heat generation.
- **Corrosion protection:** Cathodic protection using impressed current systems; field distribution analysis.
- **Electroplating:** Current distribution uniformity — primary, secondary, tertiary.
- **Electrorefining & electrowinning:** Faradaic efficiency calculations.
- **Fuel cells:** Coupled mass transport and charge transfer modelling.
- **Electrochemical sensors:** Impedance spectroscopy interpretation.
- **Industrial electrolyzers (chlor-alkali):** Optimizing cell voltage to minimize energy consumption.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cell Constant from Conductivity Measurement

**Problem:** Derive the relationship between measured resistance $R$, the cell constant $K$, and the electrolyte conductivity $\kappa$.

**Step 1 — Start with resistance of a uniform conductor:**

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Replace resistivity $\rho$ with conductivity $\kappa = 1/\rho$:**

$$R = \frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Define the cell constant $K$:**

$$K = \frac{L}{A} \quad [\text{units: } m^{-1}]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and rearrange:**

$$R = \frac{K}{\kappa} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \kappa = \frac{K}{R} = \frac{L}{A \cdot R}$$

**Step 5 — Practical implication:**

In a conductivity cell, $L$ and $A$ are not easily measured. So $K$ is calibrated using a standard KCl solution of known $\kappa$. This eliminates geometric uncertainty.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A 0.01 M KCl solution at 25 °C has $\kappa = 0.001413$ S/cm. If a cell gives $R = 1500\ \Omega$, then $K = \kappa R = 0.001413 \times 1500 = 2.1195\ cm^{-1}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Nernst Equation from Thermodynamics

**Problem:** Derive the Nernst equation for the general reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$ with $n$ electrons transferred.

**Step 1 — Start from the change in Gibbs free energy for a cell reaction:**

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient.

**Step 2 — Relate Gibbs free energy to electrical work:**

For a reversible electrochemical cell, the maximum non-PV work equals electrical work:

$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the same relation at standard conditions:**

$$\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0_{cell}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute both into the Gibbs equation:**

$$-nFE_{cell} = -nFE^0_{cell} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $-nF$:**

$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 6 — Convert to base-10 logarithm using $\ln Q = 2.303 \log_{10} Q$:**

$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$

**Step 7 — At 25 °C (298 K), the thermal voltage factor is:**

$$\frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.05916\ V$$

So the practical Nernst equation becomes:

$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} Q \quad \text{(at 25°C)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's note:** Many students forget the minus sign, or mix up $\log_{10}$ with $\ln$. Always write the equation and the numerical factor explicitly.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Molar Conductivity from Kohlrausch's Law for Strong Electrolytes

**Problem:** Derive Kohlrausch's square-root law for strong electrolytes.

**Step 1 — Start from the Debye-Hückel-Onsager equation for strong electrolytes:**

$$\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^0 - (A + B\Lambda_m^0)\sqrt{c}$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants depending on solvent, temperature, and ionic charges.

**Step 2 — At low concentrations, $\Lambda_m \approx \Lambda_m^0$, so:**

$$\Lambda_m \approx \Lambda_m^0 - A'\sqrt{c}$$

**Step 3 — Define the empirical constant $K = A'$:**

$$\boxed{\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^0 - K\sqrt{c}}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**
- At infinite dilution, ion–ion interactions vanish → $\Lambda_m$ approaches maximum $\Lambda_m^0$.
- As concentration rises, ionic atmosphere and electrophoretic effects reduce mobility → $\Lambda_m$ decreases linearly with $\sqrt{c}$.

**Step 5 — Graphical determination of $\Lambda_m^0$:**
- Plot $\Lambda_m$ (y-axis) vs $\sqrt{c}$ (x-axis).
- The y-intercept at $c = 0$ gives $\Lambda_m^0$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Transport Number from Hittorf's Method

**Problem:** Derive the transport number expression from Hittorf's experimental data.

**Step 1 — Define what is measured:**
- Before electrolysis: moles of cation $n_+^{before}$ and anion $n_-^{before}$ around each electrode.
- After electrolysis: moles around each electrode have changed due to migration.

**Step 2 — Define changes:**
- Loss of cation near cathode: $\Delta n_+^{cathode} = n_+^{before} - n_+^{after,cathode}$
- Loss of anion near anode: $\Delta n_-^{anode} = n_-^{before} - n_-^{after,anode}$

**Step 3 — By stoichiometry of ion transport (cation moves toward cathode, anion toward anode):**

$$\frac{\Delta n_+^{cathode}}{\Delta n_-^{anode}} = \frac{t_+}{t_-}$$

**Step 4 — Use the constraint $t_+ + t_- = 1$:**

$$t_+ = \frac{\Delta n_+^{cathode}}{\Delta n_+^{cathode} + \Delta n_-^{anode}}$$

$$t_- = \frac{\Delta n_-^{anode}}{\Delta n_+^{cathode} + \Delta n_-^{anode}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall:** A common mistake is taking the wrong side (anode instead of cathode) for cation. **Rule of thumb:** the ion moves *away* from the electrode region it is *named after* losing. The cation moves toward the cathode; the decrease in cation concentration is observed near the cathode (and vice versa).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Equivalent Conductance of Weak Electrolytes (Ostwald's Dilution Law)

**Problem:** For the weak electrolyte $AB \rightleftharpoons A^+ + B^-$ with dissociation constant $K_a$, derive the relationship between $\alpha$ (degree of dissociation) and $\Lambda$.

**Step 1 — Write the equilibrium expression:**

$$K_a = \frac{[A^+][B^-]}{[AB]} = \frac{c\alpha \cdot c\alpha}{c(1-\alpha)} = \frac{c\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$$

**Step 2 — Use the relation $\alpha = \Lambda_c / \Lambda_0$:**

$$K_a = \frac{c(\Lambda_c / \Lambda_0)^2}{1 - \Lambda_c / \Lambda_0}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $\Lambda_0^2$:**

$$K_a = \frac{c \Lambda_c^2}{\Lambda_0^2 - \Lambda_0 \Lambda_c}$$

**Step 4 — Invert both sides (Ostwald's dilution law form):**

$$\frac{1}{\Lambda_c} = \frac{1}{\Lambda_0} + \frac{\Lambda_0 \cdot c}{K_a \cdot \Lambda_c^2} \cdot \Lambda_c$$

A more useful linearized form:

$$\frac{1}{\Lambda_c} = \frac{1}{\Lambda_0} + \frac{\Lambda_0}{K_a} \cdot \frac{c}{\Lambda_c^2}$$

A plot of $\frac{1}{\Lambda_c}$ vs $\frac{c}{\Lambda_c^2}$ is linear with intercept $\frac{1}{\Lambda_0}$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Law

| Situation | Governing Equation | Key Assumption |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| Steady-state, planar diffusion | $\frac{\partial c}{\partial t} = D\frac{\partial^2 c}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's 2nd law |
| Equilibrium cell potential | Nernst equation | Reversible, isothermal |
| Far-from-equilibrium kinetics | Butler-Volmer | Single-step, single-electron transfer |
| Mass-transfer-limited current | $i_L = nFD c_b / \delta$ | Nernst diffusion layer |
| Strong electrolyte, dilute | Kohlrausch's law | $c \to 0$ limit |
| Weak electrolyte | Ostwald dilution | Single equilibrium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Electrochemical Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Power Supply / Load] --> B[Cathode Lead]
    B --> C[Cathode Surface]
    C --> D[Double Layer - Helmholtz Plane]
    D --> E[Diffusion Layer - Bulk Electrolyte]
    E --> F[Double Layer - Anode Side]
    F --> G[Anode Surface]
    G --> H[Anode Lead]
    H --> A
    I[Ionic Conduction Pathway] -.-> E
    J[Electron Conduction Pathway] -.-> A
    K[Mass Transport Diffusion] -.-> E
    L[Faradaic Charge Transfer] -.-> C
    M[Non-faradaic Capacitance] -.-> D
```

**Caption:** Block-level architecture of a generic two-electrode electrochemical cell, showing the coupled electron (external circuit) and ion (electrolyte) conduction paths, with the electrode–electrolyte interface acting as the charge-transfer region.

## 4.2 Maxwell's Equations in Electrochemical Context

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1["Gauss Electric"]
        ME1[Charge distribution] --> ME2[Electric field E]
    end
    subgraph M2["Faraday Induction"]
        MF1[Time varying B] --> MF2[Induced E curl]
    end
    subgraph M3["Ampere Maxwell"]
        MA1[Current density J] --> MA2[Magnetic field B]
        MA3[Displacement current] --> MA2
    end
    subgraph M4["No Magnetic Monopole"]
        MM1[Divergence B equals 0]
    end
    M1 --> N1[Used in cell potential and double layer]
    M2 --> N2[AC electrochemistry and EIS]
    M3 --> N3[Cell design and stray fields]
    M4 --> N4[Always true constraint]
```

**Caption:** Mapping Maxwell's four equations to their electrochemical engineering use cases.

## 4.3 Charge Transport Mechanism Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Charge Transport in Electrolyte] --> A1[Migration - Electric Field Driven]
    Start --> A2[Diffusion - Concentration Gradient]
    Start --> A3[Convection - Bulk Fluid Motion]
    A1 --> B1[Nernst Planck Migration Term - zFu grad phi]
    A2 --> B2[Ficks Law - D grad c]
    A3 --> B3[Hindered by flow profile v]
    B1 --> C1[Total Flux J plus plus plus]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Applied to current density i plus nF J]
```

**Caption:** Decomposition of ionic flux in the Nernst-Planck framework.

## 4.4 Current Distribution in Electrochemical Cells

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[Current Distribution] --> P1[Primary - Geometric]
    Start --> P2[Secondary - Kinetic]
    Start --> P3[Tertiary - Mass Transport]
    P1 --> C1[Simple geometry, no overpotential]
    P2 --> C2[Butler-Volmer kinetics included]
    P3 --> C3[Concentration overpotential included]
    C1 --> D1[Used for uniform current input]
    C2 --> D2[Used in electrode design]
    C3 --> D3[Used in limiting current analysis]
```

**Caption:** Three levels of current distribution analysis in electrochemical cell design.

## 4.5 Electrochemical Measurement Techniques Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[Electrochemical Measurement] --> R1[DC Methods]
    Root --> R2[AC Methods]
    Root --> R3[Transient Methods]
    R1 --> R11[Potentiostatic - constant E]
    R1 --> R12[Galvanostatic - constant I]
    R1 --> R13[Linear Sweep Voltammetry]
    R2 --> R21[EIS - Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy]
    R2 --> R22[AC Voltammetry]
    R3 --> R31[Chronoamperometry]
    R3 --> R32[Chronopotentiometry]
    R3 --> R33[Cyclic Voltammetry]
    R11 --> Uses[Used for kinetics and transport analysis]
    R12 --> Uses
    R13 --> Uses
    R21 --> Uses
    R22 --> Uses
    R31 --> Uses
    R32 --> Uses
    R33 --> Uses
```

**Caption:** Taxonomy of electrochemical measurement techniques — the practical tools used to validate electrodynamic theory.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define transport number. State Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Transport number ($t_i$):** The fraction of the total current carried by a particular ion in an electrolyte. Mathematically, $t_+ + t_- = 1$, where $t_+$ and $t_-$ are transport numbers of cation and anion, respectively.

> **Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration of Ions:** At infinite dilution, each ion contributes a definite molar conductivity ($\lambda_+^0$ or $\lambda_-^0$) to the total molar conductivity of the electrolyte, independent of the nature of the co-ion. Therefore:
> $$\Lambda_m^0 = \nu_+ \lambda_+^0 + \nu_- \lambda_-^0$$
> where $\nu_+$ and $\nu_-$ are the number of cations and anions per formula unit.

**Marks distribution (KTU scheme):** Definition 1 mark + equation 1 mark + brief explanation 1 mark = **3 marks**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain Faraday's laws of electrolysis.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Faraday's First Law:** The mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.
> $$m = Z \cdot Q = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$
> where $Z$ is the electrochemical equivalent (kg/C).

> **Faraday's Second Law:** When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their equivalent weights.
> $$\frac{m_1}{m_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2}$$

**Marks distribution:** First law + equation (1.5 marks) + Second law + equation (1.5 marks) = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — KTU University Exam Model (Dec 2024)**

**[A] (i)** Derive the Nernst equation for the general electrochemical reaction:
$$aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD + ne^-$$
with $n$ electrons. Apply the result to calculate the cell EMF of a Daniel cell at 25 °C, given $[Zn^{2+}] = 0.1\ M$ and $[Cu^{2+}] = 1.0\ M$. Standard potentials: $E^0_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.76\ V$, $E^0_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34\ V$. (7 marks)

**[A] (ii)** Explain the three types of current distribution (primary, secondary, tertiary) in an electrochemical cell with neat diagrams and discuss their significance in electrode design. (7 marks)

### **Model Solution for A(i):**

**Step 1 — Standard cell EMF:**
$$E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode} = 0.34 - (-0.76) = 1.10\ V$$

**Step 2 — Cell reaction:**
$$Zn + Cu^{2+} \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + Cu, \quad n = 2$$

**Step 3 — Reaction quotient:**
$$Q = \frac{[Zn^{2+}]}{[Cu^{2+}]} = \frac{0.1}{1.0} = 0.1$$

**Step 4 — Apply Nernst equation at 25 °C:**
$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} Q$$
$$E_{cell} = 1.10 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log_{10}(0.1)$$
$$E_{cell} = 1.10 - \frac{0.0592}{2}(-1)$$
$$E_{cell} = 1.10 + 0.0296 = 1.1296\ V \approx 1.13\ V$$

### **Model Solution for A(ii):**

**Primary current distribution** — Considers only electrolyte resistance and cell geometry; neglects activation overpotential. The Laplace equation $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ governs potential distribution. **Significance:** Gives the theoretical best-case uniformity.

**Secondary current distribution** — Includes activation overpotential via Butler-Volmer kinetics. **Significance:** Used in electrode shape optimization (e.g., for high-current-density electroplating).

**Tertiary current distribution** — Adds concentration overpotential (mass transport limitation). **Significance:** Critical for industrial cells operating near limiting current; controls deposit quality.

Diagrams (to be drawn by student): equipotential lines around planar, cylindrical, and recessed electrodes.

---

### **Question B — KTU University Exam Model (Dec 2024 — Alternative Choice)**

**[B] (i)** A conductivity cell with cell constant $K = 1.25\ cm^{-1}$ is filled with 0.05 M KCl at 25 °C. The resistance measured is 200 Ω. Calculate:
- (a) The conductivity of the solution.
- (b) The molar conductivity.
- (c) The equivalent conductivity.
- (d) The transport number of $K^+$ if $\Lambda^0_{K^+} = 73.5$ and $\Lambda^0_{Cl^-} = 76.3\ S\cdot cm^2 / mol$. (7 marks)

**[B] (ii)** What are the different mechanisms of charge transport in an electrolyte? Derive the Nernst-Planck equation and discuss its importance. (7 marks)

### **Model Solution for B(i):**

**Given:** $K = 1.25\ cm^{-1}$, $R = 200\ \Omega$, $c = 0.05\ mol/L = 0.05\ mol/dm^3$.

**(a) Conductivity:**
$$\kappa = \frac{K}{R} = \frac{1.25}{200} = 0.00625\ S/cm = 6.25 \times 10^{-3}\ S/cm$$

**(b) Molar conductivity:**
$$\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa \times 1000}{c} = \frac{0.00625 \times 1000}{0.05} = \frac{6.25}{0.05} = 125\ S\cdot cm^2 / mol$$

**(c) Equivalent conductivity:**
For KCl, equivalent weight = molar weight (n = 1), so:
$$\Lambda_{eq} = \Lambda_m = 125\ S\cdot cm^2 / equiv$$

**(d) Transport number of $K^+$:**
$$\Lambda^0_{m} = \lambda^0_{K^+} + \lambda^0_{Cl^-} = 73.5 + 76.3 = 149.8\ S\cdot cm^2/mol$$
$$t_{K^+} = \frac{\lambda^0_{K^+}}{\Lambda^0_m} = \frac{73.5}{149.8} = 0.491$$
$$t_{Cl^-} = 1 - 0.491 = 0.509$$

**Mark split:** (a) 1.5 marks, (b) 2 marks, (c) 1.5 marks, (d) 2 marks.

### **Model Solution for B(ii):**

Three mechanisms of charge transport in an electrolyte:

1. **Migration** — Movement of ions under an applied electric field. Flux: $\vec{J}_{mig} = -\frac{z_i F D_i c_i}{RT} \nabla \phi$.
2. **Diffusion** — Movement of ions down a concentration gradient. Flux: $\vec{J}_{diff} = -D_i \nabla c_i$.
3. **Convection** — Bulk motion of the electrolyte carrying ions. Flux: $\vec{J}_{conv} = c_i \vec{v}$.

**Nernst-Planck Equation Derivation:**

Total flux of species $i$:
$$\vec{J}_i = -D_i \nabla c_i - \frac{z_i F D_i c_i}{RT} \nabla \phi + c_i \vec{v}$$

**Derivation logic (Nernst-Einstein relation):**
- Starting from chemical potential $\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln c_i + z_i F \phi$.
- Force on ion: $-\nabla \mu_i$.
- Drift velocity: $v_{drift} = -u_i \nabla \mu_i$ where mobility $u_i = D_i / RT$.
- Combining diffusion, migration, and convection gives the Nernst-Planck equation.

**Importance:** Forms the foundation of all modern electrochemical transport models; coupled with Butler-Volmer kinetics and continuity equation, it enables simulation of batteries, fuel cells, and electrolyzers.

**Mark split:** Three mechanisms + diagram = 3 marks, derivation = 2.5 marks, importance = 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign error in Nernst equation:** Forgetting the negative sign in $E = E^0 - (0.0592/n) \log Q$. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing molar and equivalent conductivity:** Using $N$ (normality) vs $c$ (molarity) inconsistently. **Loss: 1.5 marks.**
> 3. **Cell constant units:** Mixing cm⁻¹ and m⁻¹. Always state units explicitly. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Transport number sum:** Forgetting $t_+ + t_- = 1$ check. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **In Kohlrausch's law:** Using $\Lambda^0 = \lambda_+^0 + \lambda_-^0$ but forgetting stoichiometric coefficients for compounds like $CaCl_2$ (which has $\nu_- = 2$). **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 6. **Wrong reaction quotient:** Writing $Q$ for the cell reaction incorrectly (e.g., omitting pure solids). **Loss: 1.5 marks.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Coulomb's law** gives the fundamental force; integrated over a charge distribution gives $\vec{E}$.
- [ ] **Gauss's law** is the integral form used for symmetric geometries; differential form is $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho / \varepsilon_0$.
- [ ] **Cell constant** $K = L/A$ relates measured resistance to conductivity: $\kappa = K/R$.
- [ ] **Molar conductivity** $\Lambda_m = \kappa/c$ has units S·m²/mol; **equivalent conductivity** $\Lambda_{eq} = \kappa/N$.
- [ ] **Kohlrausch's law** applies at infinite dilution: $\Lambda_m^0 = \nu_+ \lambda_+^0 + \nu_- \lambda_-^0$.
- [ ] **Transport number** $t_i = I_i / I_{total}$; $t_+ + t_- = 1$ always.
- [ ] **Nernst equation:** $E = E^0 - (0.0592/n) \log_{10} Q$ at 25 °C.
- [ ] **Faraday's laws:** $m = MIt/(nF)$ and ratio law $m_1/m_2 = E_1/E_2$.
- [ ] **Butler-Volmer equation** is the kinetic basis; relates current to overpotential exponentially.
- [ ] **Nernst-Planck** equation combines diffusion, migration, and convection.
- [ ] **Three current distributions:** primary (geometric), secondary (kinetic), tertiary (mass transport).
- [ ] **Hittorf's method** measures transport numbers by analyzing concentration changes near electrodes.
- [ ] **Moving boundary method** is an alternative for transport number measurement; uses a visible boundary of a follow-on electrolyte.
