- [ ] **Ostwald's dilution law** applies to weak electrolytes: $K_a = c\alpha^2 / (1-\alpha)$ with $\alpha = \Lambda/\Lambda_0$.
- [ ] **Standard conditions** for Nernst: 25 °C, 1 M, 1 atm; remember 0.0592 factor is temperature-dependent.

### Mnemonics

> [!TIP]
> **"FC = I t"** — Faraday's Charge: Faraday's constant × moles = Charge.
> **"T and D add up to Migration"** — Total = Diffusion + Migration + Convection.
> **"P-S-T for Current"** — Primary, Secondary, Tertiary distributions.

### Most Frequently Tested Topics in KTU

1. Nernst equation derivation and application (≥ 60% of past papers)
2. Conductivity and cell constant numericals
3. Kohlrausch's law problems
4. Faraday's law numericals
5. Transport number theory and Hittorf's method

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Tip:** Always write the units in every numerical answer, state the relevant governing law, and check limiting cases (e.g., what happens when $c \to 0$, $T \to 0$, $n \to 1$). Examiners award partial credit for correct methodology even if the final numerical answer is slightly off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Circuit Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electrical Circuit Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrical Circuit Theory** is the foundational branch of electrical engineering that deals with the behavior and analysis of electric circuits composed of passive elements (resistors, capacitors, inductors) and active sources (voltage/current sources) interconnected to perform a specific function. In the context of **Electrochemical Engineering (PEPOT637)**, electrical circuit theory forms the analytical backbone for modeling electrochemical cells, electrode–electrolyte interfaces, and faradaic processes using **equivalent circuit models** comprising charge-transfer resistance ($R_{ct}$), double-layer capacitance ($C_{dl}$), solution resistance ($R_s$), and Warburg impedance ($Z_W$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1**
> Every electrochemical interface (electrode ↔ electrolyte) can be represented as a **linear or non-linear circuit element**. Mastery of KVL, KCL, complex impedance, and AC steady-state analysis is therefore **non-negotiable** for solving Module 2 (Electrode Kinetics) and Module 3 (Corrosion) problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Pipe" Model

Imagine an electrical circuit as a **network of water pipes**:
- **Voltage (V)** = water pressure pushing flow through the pipe.
- **Current (I)** = the actual rate of water flow (liters/sec).
- **Resistor (R)** = a narrow section of pipe that resists flow.
- **Capacitor (C)** = a flexible rubber tank that stores water and releases it.
- **Inductor (L)** = a heavy flywheel coupled to the pipe — it resists sudden changes in flow.

Just as a plumber applies **Kirchhoff's laws** (conservation of mass and energy) to predict flow, an electrochemical engineer applies the same laws to predict **electron and ion transport** through an electrochemical cell.

### 1.3 Core Circuit Elements — Quick Reference

| Element | Symbol | Defining Law | Energy Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resistor | $R$ | $V = IR$ | Dissipates energy as heat |
| Capacitor | $C$ | $I = C \dfrac{dV}{dt}$ | Stores energy in electric field |
| Inductor | $L$ | $V = L \dfrac{dI}{dt}$ | Stores energy in magnetic field |
| Voltage Source | $V_s$ | Constant V | Delivers power |
| Current Source | $I_s$ | Constant I | Delivers power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants to Memorize**
> - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{F/m}$
> - Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \; \text{H/m}$
> - Standard reference potential: $E^0(\text{SHE}) = \mathbf{0.000 \; V}$ at **298.15 K**

### 1.4 Relevance to Electrochemical Engineering

In an electrochemical cell, the **electrode–electrolyte interface** behaves like a **parallel RC combination**:
- $R_{ct}$ (charge-transfer resistance) — pathway for faradaic current.
- $C_{dl}$ (double-layer capacitance) — pathway for non-faradaic (charging) current.

This is why understanding **impedance in AC circuits** is critical — it leads directly to **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)**, a flagship Module 2/3 topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ohm's Law — The Cornerstone

Ohm's Law states that the voltage across a conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it, provided physical conditions (temperature, pressure) remain constant.

$$V = I \cdot R$$

Where:
- $V$ = potential difference in **Volts (V)**
- $I$ = current in **Amperes (A)**
- $R$ = resistance in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**

**Why it matters in electrochemistry:** Ohm's law governs the **ohmic (iR) drop** in electrolytic solutions, which directly affects **cell voltage** calculations in the equation:
$$E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode} - IR_s - \text{other overpotentials}$$

### 2.2 Kirchhoff's Laws — The Twin Pillars

#### 2.2.1 Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)
**"The algebraic sum of currents entering a node equals zero."**
$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k = 0$$

**Why?** Charge cannot accumulate at a node (conservation of charge).

#### 2.2.2 Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)
**"The algebraic sum of voltages around any closed loop equals zero."**
$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} V_k = 0$$

**Why?** Energy is conserved — net work done around a closed path must be zero.

### 2.3 Series and Parallel Combinations

#### Series Combination (same current flows through each element):
$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots + R_n$$
$$C_{eq} = \left( \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{C_n} \right)^{-1}$$
$$L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + \cdots + L_n$$

#### Parallel Combination (same voltage across each element):
$$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{R_n}$$
$$C_{eq} = C_1 + C_2 + C_3 + \cdots + C_n$$
$$\frac{1}{L_{eq}} = \frac{1}{L_1} + \frac{1}{L_2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{L_n}$$

### 2.4 AC Circuit Analysis — Phasors and Impedance

In AC circuits, sinusoidal steady-state signals of the form $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ are best represented as **phasors**:
$$\mathbf{V} = V_m \angle \phi = V_m e^{j\phi}$$

The generalized **Ohm's law in phasor form** introduces **complex impedance** $\mathbf{Z}$:
$$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{Z}$$

| Element | Impedance $\mathbf{Z}$ | Admittance $\mathbf{Y}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Resistor $R$ | $R$ | $1/R$ |
| Inductor $L$ | $j\omega L$ | $1/(j\omega L)$ |
| Capacitor $C$ | $1/(j\omega C)$ | $j\omega C$ |

Where $j = \sqrt{-1}$ is the imaginary unit, and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency in **rad/s**.

### 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU 2024 numerical questions on circuit theory. Print it or memorize it.

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ohm's Law | $V = IR$ | V, A, $\Omega$ | Linear region only |
| Power Dissipated | $P = I^2 R = V^2/R = VI$ | Watts (W) | Resistor: always positive |
| Energy Stored (Capacitor) | $E_C = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Joules (J) | Field energy |
| Energy Stored (Inductor) | $E_L = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ | Joules (J) | Magnetic energy |
| Time Constant (RC) | $\tau = RC$ | Seconds (s) | 63.2% charge time |
| Time Constant (RL) | $\tau = L/R$ | Seconds (s) | 63.2% current rise |
| Resonant Frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hertz (Hz) | $X_L = X_C$ |
| Quality Factor | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | dimensionless | Sharpness of resonance |
| Magnitude of $\mathbf{Z}$ | $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$ | $\Omega$ | $X = X_L - X_C$ |
| Phase Angle | $\phi = \arctan(X/R)$ | radians or degrees | Negative for capacitive |
| Capacitive Reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ | $\Omega$ | Decreases with $f$ |
| Inductive Reactance | $X_L = \omega L$ | $\Omega$ | Increases with $f$ |
| RMS Value (sine) | $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ | V | DC equivalent heating |
| Average Power | $P_{avg} = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$ | W | $\cos\phi$ = power factor |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **EIS (Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy):** Measures $\mathbf{Z}(\omega)$ of an electrochemical cell to extract $R_{ct}$, $C_{dl}$, and $Z_W$ — used in **battery health monitoring, corrosion rate measurement, and biosensors**.
2. **Power System Design:** AC circuit analysis underpins **transformer design, three-phase power distribution, and motor control**.
3. **Filter Design:** RC and RL networks form the heart of **low-pass, high-pass, and band-pass filters** in signal processing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalent Capacitance of Capacitors in Series

**Statement:** Show that capacitors in series combine as reciprocals.

**Starting Point:** Consider $n$ capacitors $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ connected in series across a total voltage $V_T$ with each carrying the **same charge $Q$** (series property).

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_T &= V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + \cdots + V_n \\
\frac{Q}{C_{eq}} &= \frac{Q}{C_1} + \frac{Q}{C_2} + \frac{Q}{C_3} + \cdots + \frac{Q}{C_n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Q$ is identical for all series capacitors, divide throughout by $Q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{C_{eq}} &= \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2} + \frac{1}{C_3} + \cdots + \frac{1}{C_n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $C_{eq} = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{C_k} \right)^{-1}$ — confirmed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Check:** Series capacitors reduce total capacitance (the dielectric becomes "thicker"), which is why the equivalent is **less than the smallest** individual capacitance.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Complex Impedance of a Series R-C Circuit

**Statement:** Derive the impedance magnitude and phase of a series R-C circuit.

**Circuit:** Resistor $R$ in series with capacitor $C$, driven by AC source $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Identify individual impedances:**
$$Z_R = R, \quad Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C} = -\frac{j}{\omega C}$$

**Step 2 — Series combination (impedances add like resistors in AC):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{Z}_{total} &= Z_R + Z_C = R - \frac{j}{\omega C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of $\mathbf{Z}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert Z \vert &= \sqrt{R^2 + \left( \frac{1}{\omega C} \right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Phase angle:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \arctan\left( \frac{-1/(\omega C)}{R} \right) = -\arctan\left( \frac{1}{\omega RC} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Phasor current:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{I} &= \frac{\mathbf{V}}{\mathbf{Z}} = \frac{V_m \angle 0^\circ}{\vert Z \vert \angle \phi} = \frac{V_m}{\vert Z \vert} \angle (-\phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The current **leads** the voltage by an angle $\vert\phi\vert$ (capacitive circuit behavior).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Factor of an R-L Circuit

**Statement:** Show that average power in an AC R-L circuit equals $V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous power:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
p(t) &= v(t) \cdot i(t) \\
&= V_m \cos(\omega t) \cdot I_m \cos(\omega t - \phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Use the trigonometric identity:**
$$\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$$

Applying this:
$$
\begin{aligned}
p(t) &= \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \big[ \cos(\phi) + \cos(2\omega t - \phi) \big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Average over one full cycle:** The second term $\cos(2\omega t - \phi)$ averages to **zero**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \cos\phi \\
&= \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \cos\phi \\
&= V_{rms} \, I_{rms} \cos\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result Confirmed.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $\cos\phi$ is the **power factor (PF)**. For purely resistive circuits, $\phi = 0$, $\cos\phi = 1$ (unity PF). For purely inductive/capacitive loads, $\cos\phi = 0$ (zero PF, no real power transfer).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Circuit Element Behaviors

| Property | Resistor $R$ | Capacitor $C$ | Inductor $L$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| V-I relation (time) | $V = IR$ | $I = C \dfrac{dV}{dt}$ | $V = L \dfrac{dI}{dt}$ |
| V-I relation (phasor) | $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I} R$ | $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I}/(j\omega C)$ | $\mathbf{V} = j\omega L \mathbf{I}$ |
| Impedance $\mathbf{Z}$ | $R$ | $1/(j\omega C)$ | $j\omega L$ |
| Phase of $\mathbf{I}$ vs $\mathbf{V}$ | In phase | Leads by $90^\circ$ | Lags by $90^\circ$ |
| Power dissipated | Yes (real) | No (reactive) | No (reactive) |
| Energy stored | No | Electric field | Magnetic field |
| DC steady-state | Conducts | Open circuit | Short circuit |
| High-frequency behavior | Unchanged | Short circuit (low $\mathbf{Z}$) | Open circuit (high $\mathbf{Z}$) |
| Electrochemical analog | $R_s, R_{ct}$ | $C_{dl}$ | Rare (transmission line model) |

### 3.5 Derivation 4: Charging of a Capacitor Through a Resistor (Transient)

**Statement:** Derive $V_C(t)$ for an RC series circuit switched on at $t=0$.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the loop:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= V_R + V_C = iR + \frac{q}{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $i = dq/dt$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= R \frac{dq}{dt} + \frac{q}{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into standard linear ODE form:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R \frac{dq}{dt} + \frac{q}{C} &= V_s \\
\frac{dq}{dt} + \frac{q}{RC} &= \frac{V_s}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve using integrating factor $e^{t/RC}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
q(t) \cdot e^{t/RC} &= \int \frac{V_s}{R} e^{t/RC} \, dt + K \\
q(t) \cdot e^{t/RC} &= V_s C \cdot e^{t/RC} + K
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply initial condition $q(0) = 0$:**

Solving for $K$: $0 = V_s C + K \Rightarrow K = -V_s C$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q(t) &= V_s C \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Voltage across the capacitor:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_C(t) &= \frac{q(t)}{C} = V_s \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The product $\tau = RC$ is the **time constant**. After $t = \tau$, the capacitor is charged to **63.2%** of $V_s$. After $t = 5\tau$, it is charged to **>99%** (practically full). This same exponential decay/growth behavior appears in **double-layer charging** at an electrode surface — a key electrochemical concept.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Building Blocks of Electrical Circuit Theory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Circuit Theory Foundations] --> B[Passive Elements]
    A --> C[Active Sources]
    A --> D[Network Laws]
    A --> E[Analysis Methods]
    B --> B1[Resistor R]
    B --> B2[Capacitor C]
    B --> B3[Inductor L]
    C --> C1[Voltage Source Vs]
    C --> C2[Current Source Is]
    D --> D1[Ohms Law: V=IR]
    D --> D2[KCL: Sum of I at node = 0]
    D --> D3[KVL: Sum of V in loop = 0]
    E --> E1[Series and Parallel Reduction]
    E --> E2[Mesh Analysis]
    E --> E3[Nodal Analysis]
    E --> E4[Phasor Domain AC Analysis]
    E1 --> F[Equivalent Impedance Z_eq]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Electrochemical Equivalent Circuit]
    G --> G1[Solution Resistance Rs]
    G --> G2[Charge Transfer Rct]
    G --> G3[Double Layer Capacitance Cdl]
    G --> G4[Warburg Impedance Zw]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Series vs Parallel Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SERIES["Series Combination"]
        S1[Vs] --> S2[R1] --> S3[R2] --> S4[Rn] --> S5[Ground]
    end
    subgraph PARALLEL["Parallel Combination"]
        P1[Vs] --> P2[Node A]
        P2 --> P3[R1 branch]
        P2 --> P4[R2 branch]
        P2 --> P5[Rn branch]
        P3 --> P6[Node B]
        P4 --> P6
        P5 --> P6
        P6 --> P7[Ground]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — RC Transient Response

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uncharged: t = 0, switch open
    Uncharged --> Charging: switch closed, t > 0
    Charging --> PartiallyCharged: t = tau equals RC, 63.2% charged
    PartiallyCharged --> FullyCharged: t = 5 tau, approx 99% charged
    FullyCharged --> SteadyState: i equals 0, Vc equals Vs
    SteadyState --> Discharging: switch opened or shorted
    Discharging --> FullyDischarged: t = 5 tau, Vc equals 0
    FullyDischarged --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Electrochemical Equivalent Circuit (Randles Cell)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Working Electrode WE] --> B[Solution Resistance Rs]
    B --> C[Double Layer Capacitance Cdl]
    B --> D[Charge Transfer Resistance Rct]
    C --> E[Counter Electrode CE]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Diffusion Layer Warburg Zw]
    F --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Randles Cell:** The double-layer capacitor $C_{dl}$ and charge-transfer resistor $R_{ct}$ are in **parallel** (because current can flow through either pathway at the interface). The solution resistance $R_s$ is in **series** with this parallel combination. The Warburg impedance $Z_W$ appears in series with $R_{ct}$ for diffusion-controlled reactions. This is **directly examinable** in KTU 2024 Module 2/3 questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Ohm's Law. State its two limitations.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Ohm's Law states that the current flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across it, provided physical conditions (temperature, mechanical stress) remain constant.

$$V = I \cdot R$$

**Limitations:**
1. **Not valid for non-linear elements** such as diodes, transistors, and electrolytes at high field strengths where $V$ vs $I$ is non-linear.
2. **Temperature dependence:** The resistance of conductors changes with temperature, so Ohm's law holds only at a constant temperature.
3. **Not applicable to non-ohmic devices** like vacuum tubes, semiconductors, and superconductors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners expect students to mention **at least 2 limitations** for full marks. Memorize the V–I linearity assumption.

---

#### **Question 2: Distinguish between AC and DC. Give one example of each in an electrochemical context.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | DC (Direct Current) | AC (Alternating Current) |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Flows in one direction | Reverses periodically |
| Magnitude | Constant (ideally) | Varies sinusoidally |
| Frequency | Zero | $50/60 \; \text{Hz}$ (mains) |
| Storage | Batteries, capacitors | Cannot be stored directly |

**Electrochemical Examples:**
- **DC:** Electroplating, electrorefining, battery charging — all require unidirectional current to drive non-spontaneous redox reactions.
- **AC:** Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) — applies a small sinusoidal AC perturbation ($\pm 5 \; \text{mV}$) superimposed on a DC bias to measure cell impedance.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the current and voltage across a capacitor in a series RC circuit connected to a DC source $V_s$ at $t = 0$. Plot the variation of $V_C(t)$ and $i(t)$ with time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A series RL circuit with $R = 10 \; \Omega$ and $L = 50 \; \text{mH}$ is connected to a $230 \; \text{V}$, $50 \; \text{Hz}$ AC supply. Calculate (i) inductive reactance, (ii) impedance, (iii) RMS current, and (iv) power factor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** Refer to **Derivation 4 in Section 3.5** of these notes. The complete derivation yields:
$$V_C(t) = V_s \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right) \quad \text{and} \quad i(t) = \frac{V_s}{R} e^{-t/RC}$$

**Plot:** $V_C(t)$ rises exponentially from 0 to $V_s$; $i(t)$ decays exponentially from $V_s/R$ to 0.

**Part (b):**

**(i) Inductive Reactance:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
X_L &= 2\pi f L = 2\pi \times 50 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \\
X_L &= 15.71 \; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Impedance:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert Z \vert &= \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} = \sqrt{10^2 + 15.71^2} \\
\vert Z \vert &= \sqrt{100 + 246.81} = \sqrt{346.81} \\
\vert Z \vert &= 18.62 \; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) RMS Current:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{rms} &= \frac{V_{rms}}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{230}{18.62} \\
I_{rms} &= 12.35 \; \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Power Factor:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\phi &= \frac{R}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{10}{18.62} \\
\cos\phi &= 0.537 \; \text{(lagging, since inductive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the concept of phasor representation of sinusoidal AC quantities. Derive the impedance of a series RLC circuit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a series RLC circuit with $R = 30 \; \Omega$, $L = 0.2 \; \text{H}$, and $C = 50 \; \mu\text{F}$, calculate the (i) resonant frequency, (ii) quality factor, and (iii) bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

A sinusoidal signal $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ can be represented as a rotating vector (phasor) of magnitude $V_m$ rotating counter-clockwise at angular velocity $\omega$. The instantaneous value is the projection on the real axis.

$$\mathbf{V} = V_m e^{j\phi} = V_m \angle \phi$$

**Derivation of Series RLC Impedance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{Z}_{RLC} &= R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C} \\
&= R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right) \\
&= R + j(X_L - X_C)
\end{aligned}
$$

Magnitude: $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$

Phase: $\phi = \arctan\left( \dfrac{X_L - X_C}{R} \right)$

**Part (b):**

**(i) Resonant Frequency:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{10^{-5}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3.162 \times 10^{-3}} \\
f_0 &= 50.33 \; \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Quality Factor:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \\
&= \frac{1}{30}\sqrt{\frac{0.2}{50 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{30}\sqrt{4000} \\
&= \frac{63.25}{30} \\
Q &= 2.108
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Bandwidth:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{50.33}{2.108} \\
BW &= 23.88 \; \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write $\Omega$, H, F, Hz, W alongside numerical answers. Marks deducted if units missing.
> 2. **Sign errors in KVL:** Watch the polarity of voltage sources — flipping the assumed direction of current can change the sign of KVL equations.
> 3. **Mixing peak and RMS:** If a question says "230 V", it is **RMS** unless explicitly stated $V_m$. Forgetting $\sqrt{2}$ conversion loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Inductor vs Capacitor phase:** Inductors cause current to **lag**, capacitors cause current to **lead**. Mixing these up will reverse the sign of the phase angle and the power factor (lagging vs leading).
> 5. **Phasor arithmetic:** When adding impedances, **real parts add with real parts** and **imaginary parts add with imaginary parts**. Students often mistakenly add magnitudes.
> 6. **Resonance condition:** $X_L = X_C$ is the resonance condition. Many students write $L = C$ — this is **wrong**. The correct condition is $\omega L = 1/(\omega C)$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Electrical Circuit Theory**

- **Three fundamental laws to master cold:**
  - Ohm's Law: $V = IR$
  - KCL: $\sum I_{in} = \sum I_{out}$ at any node
  - KVL: $\sum V_{rise} = \sum V_{drop}$ around any loop

- **Series vs Parallel mnemonic:**
  - Resistors — series adds, parallel inverts-and-adds
  - Capacitors — **opposite** of resistors
  - Inductors — same as resistors

- **Impedance Triangle:** $\vert Z \vert$ (hypotenuse), $R$ (adjacent), $X$ (opposite); $\phi = \arctan(X/R)$.

- **AC power hierarchy:**
  - Apparent power: $S = V_{rms} I_{rms}$ (in VA)
  - Real power: $P = S \cos\phi$ (in W)
  - Reactive power: $Q_r = S \sin\phi$ (in VAR)

- **Phasor convention:** All voltages/currents represented as $A \angle \phi = A(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi)$. Multiplication in time domain = complex multiplication; addition in time domain = complex addition.

- **Time constants:**
  - RC charging: $V_C(t) = V_s(1 - e^{-t/RC})$, $\tau = RC$
  - RL current growth: $i(t) = (V_s/R)(1 - e^{-tR/L})$, $\tau = L/R$

- **Resonance condition for series RLC:** $X_L = X_C \Rightarrow \omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$.

- **Electrochemical bridge:** $R \rightarrow R_s$ (electrolyte), $R_{ct}$ (charge transfer), $C \rightarrow C_{dl}$ (double layer), $L$ rarely used. Warburg $Z_W = \sigma(1-j)/\sqrt{\omega}$ arises in diffusion-limited systems.

- **Sanity-check numbers:** Always verify order of magnitude. A "12.35 A current" through a $10 \; \Omega$ resistor is plausible if driven by 230 V, but impossible if driven by 5 V.

- **Units Triangle:** $P = VI = I^2 R = V^2/R$. Knowing this triangle lets you solve for any unknown if you know the other two.

- **Frequency effects:** $X_L \uparrow$ with $f$, $X_C \downarrow$ with $f$. At very high $f$, capacitor = short; at very low $f$, inductor = short.

- **Power factor correction:** Adding a capacitor in parallel to an inductive load improves PF toward unity — directly relevant to **industrial electrolysis plant energy efficiency** (a Module 3/4 application).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Electrochemical Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Electrochemical Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **electrochemical system** is a physicochemical assembly in which the interconversion of **chemical energy** and **electrical energy** takes place through **heterogeneous electron transfer reactions** occurring at the interface between an **electronic conductor (electrode)** and an **ionic conductor (electrolyte)**. The discipline that quantitatively studies these systems is termed **electrochemistry**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (PEPOT637), the study of fundamentals encompasses:
1. The nature of charge carriers in different classes of conductors.
2. The thermodynamic and kinetic basis of **half-cell reactions**.
3. The architecture of **galvanic** and **electrolytic** cells.
4. The quantitative laws governing **faradaic processes** (Faraday's Laws).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamental unit of every electrochemical system is the **electrochemical cell**, which comprises **two half-cells**, each consisting of an electrode immersed in an electrolyte, connected externally by a metallic conductor and internally by a salt bridge or porous barrier.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **two-storey building with a staircase**:
- The **electrolyte** is the staircase itself (where ions — the "people" — physically move up and down).
- The **electrode** is the landing at the top and bottom (where the people change their form — i.e., where electrons are exchanged).
- The **external wire** is the elevator on the side of the building (where electrons take a faster, dedicated route).
- The **salt bridge** is the connecting corridor between the two floors that keeps the ionic traffic circulating.

> **Just as a staircase cannot operate without landings at both ends, an electrochemical system cannot exist without at least one anode-electrolyte interface and one cathode-electrolyte interface.**

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following physical constants are central to the quantitative analysis of electrochemical systems and must be memorized verbatim:

- **Faraday's Constant (F)** = **96,485 C mol⁻¹** (≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹ for engineering problems)
- **Charge of an electron (e)** = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
- **Avogadro's Number (Nₐ)** = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹
- **Universal Gas Constant (R)** = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Tip:** In KTU numerical problems, the value **F = 96,500 C mol⁻¹** is universally accepted unless otherwise stated. Do not use 96,485 in exam answers — you will lose ½ mark for unit inconsistency.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Conductors

A **conductor** is any medium that supports the flow of electric charge. For electrochemical engineering, the classification is critical:

| Class | Charge Carrier | Example | Resistance Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electronic (Metallic)** | Free electrons | Cu, Ag, Pt, Graphite | Increases with temperature |
| **Electrolytic (Ionic)** | Cations (+) and Anions (−) | NaCl(aq), H₂SO₄(aq), KOH(aq) | **Decreases** with temperature |
| **Semiconductor** | Electrons and Holes | Si, Ge | Complex — used in photovoltaics |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Distinction:** In **electronic conductors**, the conductor itself is *not* chemically altered by current flow. In **electrolytic conductors**, current flow is accompanied by **chemical change** at the electrodes — this is the essence of electrolysis.

---

## 1.5 Electrodes — The Anode and the Cathode

The terminology of electrodes depends on the **direction of current flow** and is one of the most commonly confused topics in KTU exams.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Mnemonic — "RED CAT, AN OX":**  
> - **RED**uction always occurs at the **CAT**hode.  
> - **AN**ode is where **OX**idation occurs.

| Electrode | Reaction Type | Electron Behavior | Galvanic Cell | Electrolytic Cell |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Anode** | Oxidation | Loses electrons | **Negative** terminal | **Positive** terminal (connected to +ve of battery) |
| **Cathode** | Reduction | Gains electrons | **Positive** terminal | **Negative** terminal (connected to −ve of battery) |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Student Error:** Students assume the anode is always positive. **THIS IS FALSE.** The sign of the anode depends on whether the cell is **galvanic** (spontaneous, anode is negative) or **electrolytic** (non-spontaneous, anode is positive). KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an Electrochemical Cell

Every practical electrochemical cell consists of **four mandatory components**:

1. **Two Electrodes** — an anode and a cathode, typically made of metals, graphite, or inert materials (e.g., Pt, Ti).
2. **Two Electrolytes** — one in each half-cell. These may be the same (as in a concentration cell) or different.
3. **An External Circuit** — a metallic wire (often Cu) connecting the electrodes to allow electron flow.
4. **An Internal Ionic Conduit** — a **salt bridge** (typically KCl or KNO₃ in agar-agar) or a **porous pot** that completes the circuit by allowing ion migration without bulk mixing of electrolytes.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Electrolytes

Electrolytes are classified by their **degree of dissociation (α)** in solution:

| Type | Dissociation | Examples | Conductivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strong Electrolyte** | α ≈ 1 (complete) | HCl, H₂SO₄, NaOH, KCl, all soluble salts | **High** |
| **Weak Electrolyte** | α << 1 (partial) | CH₃COOH, NH₄OH, H₂CO₃ | **Low** |

> **Engineering Insight:** Industrial electrolytic processes (chlor-alkali, electrowinning of Cu, Zn, Al) preferentially use **strong electrolytes** to maximize current density and minimize ohmic (i²R) losses.

---

## 2.3 Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis

Faraday's laws form the **quantitative backbone** of electrochemical engineering calculations and are universally testable in KTU examinations.

### 2.3.1 Faraday's First Law (1833)

> **Statement:** The mass (m) of a substance liberated or dissolved at an electrode is **directly proportional** to the **quantity of electric charge (Q)** passed through the electrolyte.

$$m \;\propto\; Q$$

$$m \;\propto\; I \cdot t$$

Combining both proportionalities:

$$m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$

where:
- *m* = mass of substance deposited/dissolved (in grams)
- *Z* = **electrochemical equivalent** of the substance (g C⁻¹)
- *I* = current in Amperes (A)
- *t* = time in seconds (s)

### 2.3.2 Faraday's Second Law (1834)

> **Statement:** When the **same quantity of charge** is passed through **different electrolytes in series**, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their **chemical equivalent weights**.

$$\frac{m_1}{m_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2}$$

where *E* is the **equivalent weight** of the substance (atomic mass ÷ valency).

### 2.3.3 Combined Faraday Equation

By combining the two laws, the **master equation** used in 90% of KTU numerical problems is obtained:

$$m = \frac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$$

---

## 2.4 Current Efficiency (η)

In practice, **side reactions** (e.g., evolution of H₂ at the cathode during Zn deposition) consume a portion of the input charge, so the **actual mass deposited** is always less than the theoretical value.

$$\eta_{\text{cathode}} = \frac{\text{Actual mass deposited}}{\text{Theoretical mass deposited}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{\text{anode}} = \frac{\text{Actual mass dissolved}}{\text{Theoretical mass dissolved}} \times 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Convention:** Cathode current efficiency is most commonly evaluated in **electrowinning** and **electrorefining** operations, while anode current efficiency is critical in **electrosynthesis** and **anodizing** processes.

---

## 2.5 Energy and Power Relationships

For an electrochemical cell operating at voltage *V* and current *I*:

$$P = V \cdot I \quad \text{(Electrical power input, in Watts)}$$

$$E_{\text{consumed}} = V \cdot I \cdot t \quad \text{(Energy in Joules or kWh)}$$

**Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is a vital industrial metric, often expressed in **kWh per tonne** of product:

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{m_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{V \cdot n \cdot F}{3600 \cdot E \cdot \eta} \quad \left[\text{kWh per kg}\right]$$

---

## 2.6 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$ | Faraday's First Law | g |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{E}{F}$ | Electrochemical equivalent | g C⁻¹ |
| 3 | $m = \dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$ | Combined mass-deposition equation | g |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{\text{Atomic Mass}}{n\text{-factor (valency)}}$ | Equivalent weight | g eq⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | Current efficiency | % |
| 6 | $Q = I \cdot t = n \cdot z \cdot F$ | Charge = moles × electrons × F | C |
| 7 | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | Faraday's Second Law | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{V \cdot n \cdot F}{3600 \cdot E \cdot \eta}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh kg⁻¹ |
| 9 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Power input | W |
| 10 | $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | Cell EMF (Galvanic) | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Valuation Tip:** Always retain at least **4 significant figures** in intermediate calculations and **3 in the final answer** to fetch full marks in KTU examinations. Marks are deducted for premature rounding.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Faraday's laws underpin several major industrial operations:

- **Electrorefining of Copper** — purification from 99% to 99.99% purity.
- **Electrowinning of Zinc and Aluminum** — extraction of metals from ore leachates.
- **Chlor-Alkali Process** — production of Cl₂, H₂, and NaOH from brine.
- **Electroplating** — decorative and protective coatings (Cr, Ni, Au, Ag).
- **Electrosynthesis** — production of adiponitrile, perchlorates, and ozone.
- **Battery Manufacturing** — electrode mass loading calculations (Li-ion, lead-acid).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Combined Faraday Equation

We will derive the **master equation** $m = \dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$ from first principles. This is a high-frequency KTU question.

### Given
- Atomic mass of substance = *A* (g mol⁻¹)
- Valency (number of electrons transferred per ion) = *z*
- Faraday's Constant = *F* (C mol⁻¹)
- Charge passed = *Q = I × t* (C)

### Step 1 — Express equivalent weight
The equivalent weight is defined as the mass of substance that reacts with 1 mole of electrons:

$$E = \frac{A}{z}$$

### Step 2 — Express moles of electrons passed
Total charge *Q* corresponds to a number of moles of electrons *n_e* given by:

$$n_e = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

### Step 3 — Relate moles of electrons to moles of substance deposited
For a substance with valency *z*, each mole of the substance requires *z* moles of electrons for complete reduction. Therefore:

$$n_{\text{substance}} = \frac{n_e}{z} = \frac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by atomic mass to obtain the deposited mass
The mass deposited equals moles × atomic mass:

$$m = n_{\text{substance}} \times A = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{z \cdot F}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the equivalent weight identity
Since $E = A / z$, substituting into the above:

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot E}{F}$$

$$\boxed{\,m = \frac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}\,}$$

> **This is the combined Faraday equation used in virtually every KTU electrochemistry numerical.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Electrochemical Equivalent (Z)

### Given
- 1 Faraday of charge (96,500 C) liberates *E* grams of a substance (by definition of equivalent weight).

### Step 1
By Faraday's First Law, *m* is proportional to *Q*:

$$m \propto Q \;\Longrightarrow\; m = Z \cdot Q$$

### Step 2
If we pass exactly *F* coulombs (i.e., 1 Faraday), the mass liberated must equal *E* grams (by definition of equivalent weight):

$$E = Z \cdot F$$

### Step 3
Rearranging for *Z*:

$$\boxed{\,Z = \frac{E}{F} = \frac{A}{z \cdot F}\,}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical value (worked example for Cu²⁺)
For Cu²⁺: A = 63.5, z = 2, F = 96,500

$$Z_{\text{Cu}} = \frac{63.5}{2 \times 96{,}500} = 3.288 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g C}^{-1}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (Comprehensive KTU-Style)

### Problem
A current of **12.5 A** is passed through a solution of **CuSO₄** for **1 hour 15 minutes**. Calculate:
1. The mass of Cu deposited.
2. The volume of O₂ liberated at the anode at STP.
3. The cathode current efficiency if the actual mass deposited is found to be **14.20 g**.

### Given Data
- I = 12.5 A
- t = 1 h 15 min = 75 min = 75 × 60 = **4500 s**
- Atomic mass of Cu = 63.5 g mol⁻¹; valency of Cu²⁺ = 2
- Atomic mass of O = 16 g mol⁻¹; valency of O in O₂ (from 4e⁻ transfer per O₂) = **4**
- F = 96,500 C mol⁻¹
- Molar volume at STP = 22,400 mL (≈ 22.4 L)

### Part (i): Mass of Cu deposited
Total charge passed:

$$Q = I \cdot t = 12.5 \times 4500 = 56{,}250 \text{ C}$$

Equivalent weight of Cu:

$$E_{\text{Cu}} = \frac{63.5}{2} = 31.75 \text{ g eq}^{-1}$$

Theoretical mass:

$$m_{\text{Cu}} = \frac{E_{\text{Cu}} \cdot Q}{F} = \frac{31.75 \times 56{,}250}{96{,}500}$$

$$m_{\text{Cu}} = \frac{1{,}785{,}937.5}{96{,}500} = 18.50 \text{ g}$$

### Part (ii): Volume of O₂ at STP
Equivalent weight of O₂ (using n = 4):

$$E_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{32}{4} = 8.00 \text{ g eq}^{-1}$$

Theoretical mass of O₂:

$$m_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{8.00 \times 56{,}250}{96{,}500} = 4.66 \text{ g}$$

Moles of O₂:

$$n_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{4.66}{32} = 0.1457 \text{ mol}$$

Volume at STP:

$$V_{\text{O}_2} = 0.1457 \times 22{,}400 = 3263.5 \text{ mL} \approx 3.26 \text{ L}$$

### Part (iii): Cathode current efficiency
$$\eta_{\text{cathode}} = \frac{14.20}{18.50} \times 100 = 76.76\%$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Verification Trick:** Always perform a sanity check. Since 14.20 < 18.50, efficiency must be < 100%. ✓ Confirmed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Electrochemical Equivalents

| Substance | Reaction | Atomic Mass (A) | Valency (z) | Equivalent Weight (E) | Z = E/F (g C⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cu** (acid) | Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu | 63.5 | 2 | 31.75 | 3.288 × 10⁻⁴ |
| **Cu** (cyanide) | Cu⁺ + e⁻ → Cu | 63.5 | 1 | 63.50 | 6.580 × 10⁻⁴ |
| **Ag** | Ag⁺ + e⁻ → Ag | 107.87 | 1 | 107.87 | 1.118 × 10⁻³ |
| **Zn** | Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Zn | 65.38 | 2 | 32.69 | 3.387 × 10⁻⁴ |
| **Ni** | Ni²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Ni | 58.69 | 2 | 29.34 | 3.040 × 10⁻⁴ |
| **Cr** | Cr³⁺ + 3e⁻ → Cr | 52.00 | 3 | 17.33 | 1.796 × 10⁻⁴ |
| **H₂** | 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂ | 2.016 | 2 | 1.008 | 1.044 × 10⁻⁵ |
| **O₂** | 2O²⁻ → O₂ + 4e⁻ | 32.00 | 4 | 8.00 | 8.290 × 10⁻⁵ |
| **Cl₂** | 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂ + 2e⁻ | 70.90 | 2 | 35.45 | 3.673 × 10⁻⁴ |
| **Al** | Al³⁺ + 3e⁻ → Al | 26.98 | 3 | 8.99 | 9.316 × 10⁻⁵ |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Valency Watch:** The valency of a *diatomic gas* like O₂ or Cl₂ evolved at an electrode is the **number of electrons transferred per molecule**, NOT per atom. For O₂, 4 electrons are transferred per molecule; for Cl₂, 2 electrons per molecule.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Galvanic (Voltaic) Cell — Daniel Cell

The Daniel cell is the classical KTU example of a galvanic cell. The diagram below depicts its **functional architecture**, ion migration pathways, and electron flow direction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[ZINC HALF-CELL ANODE]
        A1[Zn Electrode] -- Oxidation Reaction --> A2[Zn2 plus ions enter solution]
        A2 -- Releases --> A3[2 Electrons travel through external wire]
    end

    subgraph SB[SALT BRIDGE KCl in agar]
        SB1[Cl minus ions migrate toward Zinc half cell]
        SB2[K plus ions migrate toward Copper half cell]
    end

    A3 -- External Circuit Wire --> B1

    subgraph B[COPPER HALF-CELL CATHODE]
        B1[Cu Electrode]
        B1 -- Reduction Reaction --> B2[Cu2 plus ions deposit as Cu metal]
        B2 -- Consumes --> B3[2 Electrons from external wire]
    end

    A2 --> SB1
    SB2 --> B2

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style SB fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
```

### Read-Out of the Diagram

- **Anode (Zinc half-cell):** Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (Oxidation, mass loss at electrode)
- **Cathode (Copper half-cell):** Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (Reduction, mass gain at electrode)
- **External Circuit:** Electrons flow from Zn → Cu (anode to cathode)
- **Salt Bridge:** Maintains electrical neutrality by ionic migration (Cl⁻ to anode, K⁺ to cathode)
- **Net Cell Reaction:** Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu; **E°cell = +1.10 V**

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Faraday's Mass-Deposition Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[DC Power Source] -->|Provides I amperes| P2[Electrolytic Cell with Electrolyte M n plus]
    P2 -->|At Cathode| P3[Reduction Reaction M n plus plus n e minus yields M]
    P2 -->|At Anode| P4[Oxidation Reaction or gas evolution]
    P3 -->|Deposits| P5[Mass m grams on cathode surface]
    P4 -->|Liberates| P6[Anode product gas or anode dissolution]

    P5 --> P7[Calculate m using m equals E I t over F]
    P6 --> P8[Calculate anode loss using Faraday's law]

    style P1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B7500,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style P5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style P6 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Block: Galvanic vs Electrolytic Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G1[Galvanic Cell Voltaic] --> G2[Spontaneous chemical reaction]
    G2 --> G3[Converts chemical energy to electrical energy]
    G3 --> G4[Example: Daniel Cell, Lead Acid Battery]

    E1[Electrolytic Cell] --> E2[Non spontaneous reaction driven by external power]
    E2 --> E3[Converts electrical energy to chemical energy]
    E3 --> E4[Example: Electroplating, Chlor Alkali Process]

    G4 --> CMP{Common Features}
    E4 --> CMP
    CMP --> CMP1[Two electrodes, electrolyte, ion migration, Faraday's laws apply]
    CMP --> CMP2[Faraday's laws are universal to both]

    style G1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B7500,stroke-width:2px
    style E1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style CMP fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
    style CMP1 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px
    style CMP2 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px
```
