<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Differentiate between **electronic conductors** and **electrolytic conductors**. Give two examples of each and state how their resistance varies with temperature.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Electronic Conductor | Electrotic Conductor |
|---|---|---|
| Charge carrier | Free electrons | Cations and anions |
| Material change during conduction | No | Yes (electrolysis occurs) |
| Temperature effect on resistance | **Increases** with T | **Decreases** with T |
| Examples | Cu, Ag, Al | NaCl(aq), H₂SO₄(aq) |
| Conforms to Ohm's law | Yes | Approximately, only at low V |

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for T-dependence)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
State **Faraday's Second Law of Electrolysis**. A current passing through two electrolytic cells in series liberates **1.20 g of Cu**. How many grams of **Ag** will be liberated in the other cell?

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes connected in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their equivalent weights.

$$\frac{m_{\text{Cu}}}{m_{\text{Ag}}} = \frac{E_{\text{Cu}}}{E_{\text{Ag}}}$$

- E(Cu²⁺) = 63.5 / 2 = 31.75
- E(Ag⁺) = 107.87 / 1 = 107.87

$$m_{\text{Ag}} = m_{\text{Cu}} \times \frac{E_{\text{Ag}}}{E_{\text{Cu}}} = 1.20 \times \frac{107.87}{31.75}$$

$$m_{\text{Ag}} = 1.20 \times 3.398 = 4.08 \text{ g}$$

*(3 marks — 1 mark statement, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final answer with units)*

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 1 Choice 1]**

**(a)** Derive the **combined Faraday equation** $m = \dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$ starting from the definition of equivalent weight. State clearly the assumptions and the meaning of each symbol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sheet of steel of surface area **250 cm²** is to be electroplated with a **0.005 cm thick** layer of **nickel** using a solution of **NiSO₄**. Calculate:
1. The mass of Ni deposited.
2. The time required if a current of **8.5 A** is used and the cathode current efficiency is **92%**.
3. The total energy consumed in **kWh** if the cell voltage is **3.2 V**.

Given: Density of Ni = **8.9 g cm⁻³**, Atomic mass of Ni = **58.69**, Valency = **2**, F = 96,500 C mol⁻¹.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Only the desired faradaic reaction occurs at the electrode (100% current efficiency unless stated).
2. The electrolyte is well-stirred; concentration polarization is negligible.
3. Temperature effects on electrode potential are neglected.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The equivalent weight *E* is defined as:

$$E = \frac{\text{Atomic Mass (A)}}{\text{Valency (z)}}$$

**Step 2:** The total charge passed in time *t* at current *I* is:

$$Q = I \cdot t \;\; \text{(Coulombs)}$$

**Step 3:** The number of moles of electrons transferred is:

$$n_e = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

**Step 4:** For a substance with valency *z*, each mole requires *z* moles of electrons. Hence the moles of substance deposited are:

$$n_{\text{substance}} = \frac{n_e}{z} = \frac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$$

**Step 5:** Multiplying by atomic mass *A*:

$$m = n_{\text{substance}} \cdot A = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot A}{z \cdot F}$$

**Step 6:** Since $E = A / z$:

$$\boxed{\,m = \frac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}\,}$$

Symbol meanings:
- *m* = mass of substance (g)
- *E* = equivalent weight (g eq⁻¹)
- *I* = current (A)
- *t* = time (s)
- *F* = Faraday's constant (96,500 C mol⁻¹)

*(7 marks — 2 marks for assumptions, 4 marks for stepwise derivation, 1 mark for boxed result and symbols)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Area A = 250 cm²
- Thickness t_ni = 0.005 cm
- Density ρ = 8.9 g cm⁻³
- Atomic mass of Ni = 58.69, valency z = 2
- I = 8.5 A, η = 92% = 0.92, V = 3.2 V
- F = 96,500 C mol⁻¹

**Step 1: Volume of nickel layer**

$$V_{\text{Ni}} = \text{Area} \times \text{thickness} = 250 \times 0.005 = 1.25 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2: Mass of Ni (actual)**

$$m_{\text{actual}} = V \times \rho = 1.25 \times 8.9 = 11.125 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: Equivalent weight of Ni**

$$E_{\text{Ni}} = \frac{58.69}{2} = 29.345 \text{ g eq}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Theoretical mass required (using η)**

The theoretical mass corresponds to 100% efficiency. Actual mass = η × theoretical mass:

$$m_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{\eta} = \frac{11.125}{0.92} = 12.092 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5: Time required**

Rearranging $m = \dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$:

$$t = \frac{m_{\text{theoretical}} \cdot F}{E \cdot I}$$

$$t = \frac{12.092 \times 96{,}500}{29.345 \times 8.5}$$

$$t = \frac{1{,}166{,}878}{249.4325} = 4677.6 \text{ s} \approx 1.30 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 6: Energy consumed in kWh**

$$E_{\text{energy}} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{3600 \times 1000}$$

$$E_{\text{energy}} = \frac{3.2 \times 8.5 \times 4677.6}{3{,}600{,}000}$$

$$E_{\text{energy}} = 0.0353 \text{ kWh}$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks part 1, 3 marks part 2, 2 marks part 3)*

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 1 Choice 2]**

**(a)** With a neat **sketch/functional block diagram**, describe the construction and working of a **Daniel Cell**. Write the half-cell reactions, the overall cell reaction, and identify the **anode, cathode, and direction of ion flow** in the salt bridge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **lead-acid storage battery** rated at **200 Ah** is discharged at a steady current of **25 A** for **6 hours**. Calculate:
1. The mass of **Pb** oxidized at the anode.
2. The mass of **PbO₂** reduced at the cathode.
3. The mass of **H₂SO₄** consumed.
4. The average cell voltage if the total energy delivered is **2.85 kWh** (assume a 6-cell battery).

Given: Atomic masses — Pb = 207.2, O = 16, S = 32, H = 1; F = 96,500 C mol⁻¹.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Construction (block-wise):**
- **Zinc electrode** immersed in **ZnSO₄ solution** (anode half-cell).
- **Copper electrode** immersed in **CuSO₄ solution** (cathode half-cell).
- The two solutions are separated by a **porous pot** or connected via a **salt bridge** (KCl/agar).
- Electrodes are connected externally by a metallic wire with a voltmeter/load.

**Half-cell reactions:**

$$\text{Anode (Oxidation):} \quad \text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$$

$$\text{Cathode (Reduction):} \quad \text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$$

**Overall cell reaction:**

$$\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$$

**Ion flow in salt bridge:**
- **Cl⁻ ions** migrate toward the **anode** (to balance Zn²⁺ accumulation).
- **K⁺ ions** migrate toward the **cathode** (to balance SO₄²⁻ excess).

**Standard cell EMF:** E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode = +0.34 − (−0.76) = **+1.10 V**

*(7 marks — 2 marks diagram, 2 marks reactions, 2 marks ion flow, 1 mark EMF)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Capacity = 200 Ah → consistent with 25 A × 8 h (full charge/discharge cycle)
- I = 25 A, t = 6 h = 21,600 s
- Q = I × t = 25 × 21,600 = **5,40,000 C**

**Reactions in lead-acid battery:**

$$\text{Anode: Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^-$$

$$\text{Cathode: PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 1: Mass of Pb oxidized**
- Moles of electrons = Q / F = 5,40,000 / 96,500 = 5.596 mol e⁻
- Moles of Pb = moles of e⁻ / 2 = 5.596 / 2 = 2.798 mol
- Mass of Pb = 2.798 × 207.2 = **579.7 g ≈ 0.580 kg**

**Step 2: Mass of PbO₂ reduced**
- Moles of PbO₂ = moles of e⁻ / 2 = 2.798 mol
- Molar mass of PbO₂ = 207.2 + 2(16) = 239.2 g mol⁻¹
- Mass of PbO₂ = 2.798 × 239.2 = **669.2 g ≈ 0.669 kg**

**Step 3: Mass of H₂SO₄ consumed**
- 2 moles of e⁻ correspond to 2 moles of H₂SO₄
- Moles of H₂SO₄ = 2.798 mol
- Molar mass of H₂SO₄ = 2(1) + 32 + 4(16) = 98 g mol⁻¹
- Mass of H₂SO₄ = 2.798 × 98 = **274.2 g**

**Step 4: Average cell voltage**
- Total energy = 2.85 kWh = 2.85 × 3,600,000 = 1.026 × 10⁷ J
- 6-cell battery; energy per cell = 1.026 × 10⁷ / 6 = 1.71 × 10⁶ J
- Energy per cell = V × Q → V = 1.71 × 10⁶ / 5,40,000 = **3.167 V per cell ≈ 3.17 V**

*(7 marks — 4 sub-parts distributed; 2, 2, 2, 1 marks respectively)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Most Common Mark-Deduction Zones in Module 1 (Fundamentals):**
> 
> 1. **Valency Error in Diatomic Gases:** Students often take valency of O₂ as 2 (instead of 4) when calculating equivalent weight of O₂. This causes a 50% mass error. *Always* count the **total electrons transferred per molecule**, not per atom.
> 
> 2. **Time Unit Mismatch:** Numerical problems give time in *minutes* or *hours*. KTU's Faraday equation demands **seconds**. Failing to convert loses 1 mark outright.
> 
> 3. **Anode vs Cathode Sign Confusion:** In galvanic cells, the anode is *negative*; in electrolytic cells, the anode is *positive*. Examiners will award 0 marks for an ambiguous answer.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to Apply Current Efficiency:** In deposition problems, students compute *theoretical* mass using Faraday's law but then forget to *divide by η* when computing time. This is a classic 1-mark error.
> 
> 5. **Current Density vs Current:** When area is given, students sometimes confuse "current" with "current density" (A cm⁻²). Always read carefully whether *A* or *A/cm²* is being asked.
> 
> 6. **Units in Specific Energy Consumption:** SEC must be expressed in **kWh per kg** (or per tonne). Showing it in J/g loses the unit-mark.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Electrochemical systems interconvert chemical and electrical energy via heterogeneous electron transfer at electrode-electrolyte interfaces.
- **Two conductor classes:** **Electronic** (electron flow, no chemical change) and **Electrolytic** (ion flow, chemical change).
- **Anode = Oxidation; Cathode = Reduction** (in BOTH galvanic and electrolytic cells).
- **Anode sign is NOT fixed** — it is *negative* in galvanic cells, *positive* in electrolytic cells.
- **Faraday's First Law:** $m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$
- **Faraday's Second Law:** $m_1 / m_2 = E_1 / E_2$
- **Combined Equation:** $m = E \cdot I \cdot t / F$ — the **single most used** KTU formula.
- **Electrochemical Equivalent:** $Z = E / F = A / (z \cdot F)$, units **g C⁻¹**.
- **Current Efficiency:** $\eta = (m_{\text{actual}} / m_{\text{theoretical}}) \times 100\%$.
- **Always use** $F = 96{,}500 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$ in KTU exam answers.
- **Equivalent Weight:** $E = A / z$, where *z* = valency (electrons per ion/molecule).
- **Time in Faraday's law is in seconds**, not minutes or hours.
- **Valency of O₂ = 4** (per molecule, not per atom).
- **Salt bridge ions:** Cations migrate to cathode side; anions migrate to anode side.
- **Daniel Cell EMF** = 1.10 V (memorize this — it is a frequent 2-mark question).
- **Industrial relevance:** Chlor-alkali, electrorefining, electroplating, electrowinning, batteries, and corrosion protection all depend on the fundamentals above.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy for KTU Module 1:**  
> Memorize the **combined Faraday equation**, the **10-row formula cheat sheet** in Section 2.6, and the **Daniel Cell reactions**. These three together account for **~70%** of marks in the Module 1 section of the End Semester Examination.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of Electrochemical Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Elements of Electrochemical Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition An **Electrochemical System** is an assembly of two electronically conducting electrodes (metals, semiconductors, or inert carriers) immersed in an ionically conducting medium (electrolyte), separated by a phase boundary (the electrode–electrolyte interface) at which **heterogeneous charge-transfer reactions** (oxidation at the anode and reduction at the cathode) occur spontaneously (galvanic mode) or are driven by an external power source (electrolytic mode). The study of these systems constitutes the discipline of **Electrochemical Engineering**, which quantitatively couples ionic transport, reaction kinetics, and electrical circuit behavior. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** *Module 1 – Elements of Electrochemical Systems* introduces the **minimum necessary inventory** of components and terminology required to describe any electrochemical device — galvanic cells, electrolyzers, fuel cells, batteries, and corrosion cells alike. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Think of an electrochemical system as a **hydraulic water circuit** mapped onto an electrical circuit: - **Water flow** ⇔ **Ionic current** (charge carried by ions in the electrolyte). - **Pump / Turbine** ⇔ **Electrode reactions** (the "engine" that converts chemical energy to electrical energy, or vice versa). - **Pipes** ⇔ **Electrolyte and external metallic wires** (the conduction pathways). - **Pressure difference** ⇔ **Cell Potential (E)** (the driving force). - **Valves** ⇔ **Phase boundaries / interfaces** (where ions cross from one phase to another). Just as a pump needs an inlet and outlet, an electrochemical system needs **two distinct half-cells** separated by an interface where electrons are exchanged. ## 1.3 The Five Essential Elements Every electrochemical system — from a $1.5\text{ V}$ AA dry cell to a multi-megawatt chlor-alkali plant — comprises exactly **five indispensable elements**: 1. **Anode** – the electrode where **oxidation** occurs (loss of electrons). 2. **Cathode** – the electrode where **reduction** occurs (gain of electrons). 3. **Electrolyte** – the ion-conducting medium (aqueous, molten salt, polymer, or solid oxide). 4. **External Circuit** – metallic path for electron flow between the electrodes. 5. **Phase Boundary / Interface** – the two-dimensional region where charge transfer takes place. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Symbolism (IUPAC):** > - **Anode** in a **galvanic** cell is the **negative** terminal. > - **Cathode** in a **galvanic** cell is the **positive** terminal. > - Under **electrolytic** conditions, the sign convention **reverses** for the terminals but the *reaction sites* (oxidation at anode, reduction at cathode) remain invariant. ## 1.4 Galvanic vs. Electrolytic Systems | Feature | Galvanic (Voltaic) Cell | Electrolytic Cell | |---|---|---| | **Thermodynamics** | $\Delta G < 0$ (spontaneous) | $\Delta G > 0$ (non-spontaneous) | | **Energy Conversion** | Chemical $\rightarrow$ Electrical | Electrical $\rightarrow$ Chemical | | **Driving Force** | Internal (chemical affinity) | External DC source | | **Anode Polarity** | Negative | Positive | | **Cathode Polarity** | Positive | Negative | | **Examples** | Daniell cell, Zn–MnO₂ dry cell, Fuel cell | Chlor-alkali, electroplating, electrorefining | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of an Electrochemical Process When the circuit is closed, the following sequence of coupled phenomena occurs *simultaneously*: 1. **Faradaic Reaction at Anode:** Neutral or ionic species surrender electrons. The metal atoms of the anode itself may ionize (e.g., $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$) — known as **active dissolution**. 2. **Electron Conduction:** Released electrons travel through the **external metallic circuit** toward the cathode, producing a measurable **current $I$**. 3. **Faradaic Reaction at Cathode:** Electron-accepting species are reduced. Cations in solution may deposit as metal (e.g., $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cu}$). 4. **Ionic Conduction in Electrolyte:** Charge balance is preserved by the migration of ions — **cations move toward the cathode** and **anions move toward the anode** (under the influence of the local electric field and concentration gradients). 5. **Interfacial Charge Layer Formation:** At each electrode surface, an **Electrical Double Layer (EDL)** forms — typically $\sim 1$ to $\sim 10 \text{ nm}$ thick — which governs the local reaction kinetics via the **Butler–Volmer relation**. ## 2.2 The Electrode–Electrolyte Interface (EDL) The interface is not a mathematically sharp boundary. It is a structured region modeled by **Helmholtz**, **Gouy–Chapman**, and **Stern** theories: - **Inner Helmholtz Plane (IHP):** locus of centers of specifically adsorbed ions. - **Outer Helmholtz Plane (OHP):** locus of centers of solvated ions closest to the surface. - **Diffuse Layer:** extends into the bulk electrolyte, characterized by the **Debye length** $\lambda_D$. The **Interfacial Capacitance** $C_{dl}$ (typically $10$–$40 \text{ μF/cm}^2$ for smooth metal surfaces) is a key parameter in dynamic (impedance) analysis. ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Conventions > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic – "AN OX, RED CAT" (always true, irrespective of cell type):** > - **AN**ode = **OX**idation (electrons *produced*). > - **CAT**hode = **RED**uction (electrons *consumed*). > > The signs of the electrodes depend on the mode, but the *reaction* at each electrode never changes. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Name | Variable Definitions | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Gibbs–Electrochemical Energy | $n$ = electrons transferred, $F$ = Faraday constant ($\mathbf{96485\ C/mol}$) | $\text{J/mol}$ | | 2 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Nernst Equation | $E^{\circ}$ = standard potential, $Q$ = reaction quotient | $\text{V}$ | | 3 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q$ at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Nernst (Decadic) | Uses $2.303\,RT/F \approx 0.05916 \text{ V}$ | $\text{V}$ | | 4 | $E_{\text{cell}}^{\circ} = E_{\text{cathode}}^{\circ} - E_{\text{anode}}^{\circ}$ | Standard Cell EMF | Both values from reduction table | $\text{V}$ | | 5 | $m = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's 1st Law (Mass deposited) | $m$ = mass, $M$ = molar mass, $I$ = current, $t$ = time | $\text{kg}$ | | 6 | $Q = n \cdot z \cdot F$ | Faraday's 2nd Law (Charge) | $z$ = charge number per ion | $\text{C}$ | | 7 | $\lambda_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 k_B T}{2 N_A e^2 I}}$ | Debye Length | $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity, $I$ = ionic strength | $\text{m}$ | | 8 | $j = j_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$ | Butler–Volmer Equation | $j_0$ = exchange c.d., $\eta$ = overpotential, $\alpha$ = transfer coeff. | $\text{A/m}^2$ | | 9 | $\eta_{\text{conc}} = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{j}{j_L}\right)$ | Concentration Overpotential | $j_L$ = limiting current density | $\text{V}$ | | 10 | $P = E \cdot I = I^2 R_{\text{cell}}$ | Useful Electrical Power | $R_{\text{cell}}$ = total internal resistance | $\text{W}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Memory Block:** The two equations you **must** memorize cold are **(1) Nernst** and **(5) Faraday's First Law**. Every KTU board paper in this module touches at least one of them. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Energy Storage:** Li-ion, Na–S, redox-flow batteries — all designed by combining thermodynamic ($E^{\circ}$), kinetic (Butler–Volmer), and transport (electrolyte conductivity) considerations. - **Industrial Electrosynthesis:** Chlor-alkali process, aluminum smelting (Hall–Héroult), water electrolysis for green H₂. - **Corrosion Engineering:** Predicting galvanic corrosion by ordering metals in the **galvanic series**. - **Sensors & Biosensors:** pH meters, glucose sensors — all rely on Nernstian response. - **Electrometallurgy:** Electrorefining of copper, electrowinning of zinc. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation A — Nernst Equation from Thermodynamic Equilibrium **Starting Premise:** At equilibrium, the **electrical work** done in transferring $n$ moles of electrons across a potential difference $E$ equals the **change in Gibbs free energy** (with a negative sign because the system does work): $$ \Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{...(i)} $$ For the same cell, the Gibbs free energy at non-standard conditions is given by the **van 't Hoff isotherm**: $$ \Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q \quad \text{...(ii)} $$ **Step 1:** Substitute (i) into the left-hand side of (ii): $$ -nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q $$ **Step 2:** Bring the term $-nFE^{\circ}$ to the left-hand side: $$ -nFE + nFE^{\circ} = RT \ln Q $$ **Step 3:** Factor out $-nF$ on the left-hand side: $$ nF(E^{\circ} - E) = RT \ln Q $$ **Step 4:** Divide both sides by $nF$: $$ E^{\circ} - E = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q $$ **Step 5:** Multiply both sides by $-1$ and rearrange to the standard textbook form: $$ E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q \quad \blacksquare $$ **Step 6 (Conversion to base-10 logarithm):** Using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$ and the numerical value $\frac{2.303\,RT}{F} \approx 0.05916 \text{ V}$ at $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$: $$ E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q \quad \text{(at } 25^{\circ}\text{C)} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation B — Relationship Between Standard Cell EMF and Equilibrium Constant **Starting Premise:** At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ and $Q = K_{eq}$. Substitute $Q = K_{eq}$ and $\Delta G = 0$ into (ii): $$ 0 = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln K_{eq} $$ Therefore: $$ \Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_{eq} $$ **Step 1:** From the electrochemical identity $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$, equate the two expressions: $$ -nFE^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_{eq} $$ **Step 2:** Solve for $E^{\circ}$: $$ E^{\circ} = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln K_{eq} $$ **Step 3 (Base-10 form):** $$ E^{\circ} = \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} K_{eq} \quad \blacksquare $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A positive $E^{\circ}$ implies $K_{eq} > 1$, meaning the **forward (galvanic) reaction is thermodynamically favored**. A negative $E^{\circ}$ implies $K_{eq} < 1$, meaning the reaction must be driven by an external EMF (electrolytic mode). ## 3.3 Derivation C — Faraday's First Law from Charge Conservation **Starting Premise:** A charge $Q$ (in coulombs) is passed through an electrolytic cell. The number of moles of electrons transferred is $Q/F$. Each ion requires $z$ electrons to be deposited/dissolved. Therefore, the moles of substance reacted is: $$ n_{\text{substance}} = \dfrac{n_{e^-}}{z} = \dfrac{Q}{zF} $$ **Step 1:** The mass $m$ of substance is related to its molar mass $M$ (kg/mol) by: $$ m = n_{\text{substance}} \cdot M = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{zF} $$ **Step 2:** Since $Q = I \cdot t$ for a constant current $I$ over time $t$: $$ m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{zF} \quad \blacksquare $$ **Step 3 (Mass per unit charge — Electrochemical Equivalent):** Defining $Z = M/(zF)$ gives the linear proportionality $m = Z \cdot Q$ — this is the **historically original statement** of Faraday's First Law (1834). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Cell Reactions and Their $E^{\circ}$ Values | Cell Reaction (Net) | $n$ | $E^{\circ}$ (V) | $\Delta G^{\circ}$ (kJ/mol) | $\log_{10} K_{eq}$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | $\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$ | 2 | $+1.10$ | $-212$ | $+37.1$ | | $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | 2 | $+2.05$ | $-396$ | $+69.4$ | | $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | 4 | $+1.23$ | $-475$ | $+83.1$ | | $\text{Mg} + \text{Fe}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + \text{Fe}$ | 2 | $+1.93$ | $-372$ | $+65.2$ | | $2\text{NaCl} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2 + 2\text{NaOH}$ | 2 | $-2.19$ | $+422$ | $-74.0$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Generic Electrochemical System The following block diagram captures every constitutive element of an electrochemical system and the energy/charge flows that interconnect them. Mermaid is used here for compile-safety rather than attempting a physical sketch. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[ANODE
Oxidation Site] -->|Releases e-| B1[EXTERNAL CIRCUIT
Electron Conductor] B1 -->|Delivers e-| C1[CATHODE
Reduction Site] C1 -->|Consumes cations| D1[ELECTROLYTE
Ionic Conductor] D1 -->|Migrates anions| A1 A1 -.->|Phase Boundary 1| E1[ELECTRICAL DOUBLE LAYER
Helmholtz and Diffuse Regions] C1 -.->|Phase Boundary 2| E1 E1 --> F1[CELL TERMINAL VOLTAGE Ecell] F1 --> G1[LOAD OR EXTERNAL SOURCE] G1 -.->|Drives or extracts current| B1 H1[THERMODYNAMIC DRIVING FORCE
DeltaG and Eo] --> A1 H1 --> C1 ``` **Reading Guide:** - **Solid arrows** denote **real charge flux** (electron flow externally; ion migration internally). - **Dashed arrows** denote **informational/structural coupling** (e.g., the EDL governs kinetics at *both* interfaces). - The **double-arrow loop** `D1 ↔ A1` and `D1 ↔ C1` represents the bidirectional ionic migration in the electrolyte that completes the circuit. ## 4.2 Block-Level Flow of an Electrochemical Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1 Charge Generation] --> S2[Step 2 Faradaic Charge Transfer at Anode] S2 --> S3[Step 3 Electron Conduction through External Circuit] S3 --> S4[Step 4 Faradaic Charge Transfer at Cathode] S4 --> S5[Step 5 Ionic Migration in Bulk Electrolyte] S5 --> S6[Step 6 Mass Transport by Diffusion and Convection] S6 --> S7[Step 7 Steady State Cell Response] S7 -->|Feedback to Step 1| S1 P1[INPUTS E and T] --> S1 P2[OUTPUTS I and Products] --> S7 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reading:** Each *Step* corresponds to a **governing equation** in Section 2. For example, Step 2 maps to the **Butler–Volmer** equation, Step 5 to **Ohm's law in the electrolyte** ($i = \kappa \nabla \phi$), and Step 6 to the **Nernst–Planck** equation. ## 4.3 Cell Notation Convention (IUPAC) A galvanic cell is written as a single-line schematic: $$ \text{Anode} \,\vert\, \text{Anolyte} \,\vert\!\!\vert\, \text{Catholyte} \,\vert\, \text{Cathode} $$ The **double vertical bar** ($\vert\!\vert$) represents a **salt bridge** or porous barrier that completes the ionic circuit while preventing bulk mixing. Example — the **Daniell cell**: $$ \text{Zn}_{(s)} \,\vert\, \text{Zn}^{2+}(1 \text{ M}) \,\vert\!\!\vert\, \text{Cu}^{2+}(1 \text{ M}) \,\vert\, \text{Cu}_{(s)} $$ # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **Define an electrochemical cell. Distinguish clearly between a galvanic cell and an electrolytic cell.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **electrochemical cell** is a device that converts chemical energy into electrical energy (or vice versa) by means of redox reactions occurring at two electrodes immersed in an electrolyte. The two modes are: - **Galvanic Cell:** Operates *spontaneously* ($\Delta G < 0$, $E_{\text{cell}} > 0$); chemical energy is converted into electrical energy. Example: Daniell cell. - **Electrolytic Cell:** Operates *non-spontaneously* ($\Delta G > 0$, $E_{\text{cell}} < 0$); an external DC source drives the reaction. Example: Electroplating of silver onto a spoon. The fundamental difference is the **direction of energy conversion** and the **source of driving force** (chemical affinity vs. external EMF). ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **State and explain Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Faraday's First Law (1834):** *The mass of a substance liberated or deposited at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.* Mathematically: $$ m \;\propto\; Q \quad \Longrightarrow \quad m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{z \cdot F} $$ where $m$ = mass deposited (g), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = molar mass (g/mol), $z$ = charge number of the ion, and $F$ = **96485 C/mol** (Faraday's constant). The constant of proportionality $Z = M/(zF)$ is called the **electrochemical equivalent** of the substance (g/C). ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Module 1):** > 1. **Sign Errors:** Students frequently confuse the sign of $E_{\text{cell}}$ for spontaneous vs. non-spontaneous reactions. Always write $\Delta G = -nFE$ and check the sign. > 2. **Unit Inconsistencies:** Mixing $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ with $F$ in coulombs *and* $E$ in volts is correct, but $R = 1.987 \text{ cal/(mol·K)}$ is **wrong** for the Nernst equation. > 3. **Forgetting "2.303":** Using $\ln Q$ directly with the constant $0.05916$ is the single most common error in KTU answer sheets. > 4. **Confusing $n$ and $z$:** In Faraday's law, $z$ is the charge per ion; in the Nernst equation, $n$ is the total electrons transferred in the cell reaction. They are *not* always equal. --- ### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 14 Marks] **(a)** Derive the **Nernst equation** for the cell reaction: $$\text{Zn}_{(s)} + \text{Cu}^{2+}_{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}_{(aq)} + \text{Cu}_{(s)}$$ starting from the relationship $\Delta G = -nFE$. Given $E^{\circ}_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.76 \text{ V}$ and $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34 \text{ V}$, calculate $E_{\text{cell}}$ when $[\text{Zn}^{2+}] = 0.01 \text{ M}$ and $[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 1.0 \text{ M}$ at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A current of $5.0 \text{ A}$ is passed through a copper sulfate solution for $40 \text{ minutes**. Calculate (i) the mass of copper deposited, (ii) the volume of gas (at STP) liberated if the same charge is passed through acidified water. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Derivation & Calculation (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Standard Cell Potential:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} = 0.34 - (-0.76) = +1.10 \text{ V}$. **Step 2 — Nernst Equation Form:** For the cell reaction, $Q = \dfrac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]}$ and $n = 2$. $$ E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q = 1.10 - \dfrac{0.05916}{2} \log_{10} \left(\dfrac{0.01}{1.0}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the Logarithm:** $\log_{10}(0.01) = -2$. $$ E_{\text{cell}} = 1.10 - \dfrac{0.05916}{2} \times (-2) = 1.10 + 0.05916 = +1.159 \text{ V} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** The positive value confirms spontaneity. Decreasing the product $[\text{Zn}^{2+}]$ shifts the equilibrium forward, raising the EMF. **Part (b) — Faraday's Law Calculations (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Total Charge Passed:** $Q = I \times t = 5.0 \text{ A} \times (40 \times 60) \text{ s} = 12000 \text{ C}$. **Step 2 — Moles of Electrons:** $n_{e^-} = Q/F = 12000 / 96485 = 0.1244 \text{ mol}$. **Step 3 (i) — Mass of Copper:** Reaction: $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cu}$, so $z = 2$, $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5 \text{ g/mol}$. $$ m_{\text{Cu}} = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{zF} = \dfrac{12000 \times 63.5}{2 \times 96485} = \dfrac{762000}{192970} = 3.949 \text{ g} \approx 3.95 \text{ g} $$ **Step 4 (ii) — Volume of Gas at STP:** Acidified water: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2$. For 4 electrons transferred per "molecule" of water split, the total moles of gas (H₂ + O₂) produced is: $$ n_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{n_{e^-}}{2} = \dfrac{0.1244}{2} = 0.0622 \text{ mol}; \quad n_{\text{O}_2} = \dfrac{0.1244}{4} = 0.0311 \text{ mol} $$ **Step 5 — Total moles of gas:** $n_{\text{total}} = 0.0622 + 0.0311 = 0.0933 \text{ mol}$. **Step 6 — Volume at STP:** $V = n \times 22.4 \text{ L/mol} = 0.0933 \times 22.4 = 2.09 \text{ L}$. --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice for 14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **(a)** What is an **electrical double layer (EDL)**? With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **Helmholtz**, **Gouy–Chapman**, and **Stern** models of the EDL. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A fuel cell operates on the overall reaction $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$. Compute the standard cell EMF and the equilibrium constant at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$, given $E^{\circ}_{\text{O}_2/\text{H}_2\text{O}} = +1.23 \text{ V}$. Comment on the spontaneity. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Electrical Double Layer (7 marks)** The **Electrical Double Layer (EDL)** is the structured arrangement of charges and oriented dipoles that forms at any electrode–electrolyte interface. It comprises a layer of excess charge on the metal side and a compensating layer of counter-ions on the solution side, separated by a molecular-scale gap. - **Helmholtz Model (1853):** Treats the interface as a *parallel-plate capacitor*. The Inner Helmholtz Plane (IHP) is the locus of specifically adsorbed ions (often partially desolvated). The Outer Helmholtz Plane (OHP) is the locus of fully solvated ions. The potential drops **linearly** from the metal to the OHP. - **Gouy–Chapman Model (1910–1913):** Recognizes that thermal agitation *smears* the counter-ion charge into a **diffuse layer** extending into the bulk. The potential decays **exponentially** with the **Debye length** $\lambda_D$. - **Stern Model (1924):** Unifies the two by partitioning the solution-side charge into a **compact (Helmholtz) layer** and a **diffuse (Gouy–Chapman) layer** in series. The differential capacitance is given by $\frac{1}{C_{\text{dl}}} = \frac{1}{C_H} + \frac{1}{C_D}$. **Part (b) — Fuel Cell EMF and $K_{eq}$ (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Number of Electrons:** $n = 4$ (each O₂ accepts 4 e⁻; each H₂ gives 2 e⁻, and 2 H₂ are involved). **Step 2 — Standard EMF:** $E^{\circ} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$. Given $E^{\circ}_{\text{O}_2/\text{H}_2\text{O}} = +1.23 \text{ V}$ and $E^{\circ}_{\text{H}^+/\text{H}_2} = 0.00 \text{ V}$: $$ E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = 1.23 - 0.00 = +1.23 \text{ V} $$ **Step 3 — Equilibrium Constant:** Using $E^{\circ} = \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} K_{eq}$: $$ \log_{10} K_{eq} = \dfrac{n \cdot E^{\circ}}{0.05916} = \dfrac{4 \times 1.23}{0.05916} = \dfrac{4.92}{0.05916} \approx 83.16 $$ **Step 4 — Compute $K_{eq}$:** $K_{eq} = 10^{83.16} \approx 1.45 \times 10^{83}$. **Step 5 — Comment on Spontaneity:** $K_{eq} \gg 1$ (enormous). The reaction is **overwhelmingly spontaneous** under standard conditions. A fuel cell converting H₂ and O₂ to water is therefore a *highly efficient* galvanic device, and the **theoretical thermodynamic efficiency** is $\eta_T = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} \approx 83\%$. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Question B part b):** Students often write $\log K = 83$ and forget the implication. Always close the calculation with a **one-sentence engineering interpretation** — it earns the last 1–2 marks of valuation. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **Five Elements** of an electrochemical system: **A**node, **C**athode, **E**lectrolyte, **E**xternal circuit, **I**nterface. - [ ] **"AN OX, RED CAT"** is *always* valid — reaction sites do not change between galvanic and electrolytic modes. - [ ] **Anode** is **negative** in galvanic mode and **positive** in electrolytic mode. - [ ] **Faraday constant:** $F = \mathbf{96485 \text{ C/mol}}$; **molar gas volume at STP:** $\mathbf{22.4 \text{ L/mol}}$. - [ ] **Nernst Equation** at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$ uses the **decadic form** with **0.05916**, not 0.0257 (which is the natural-log form without the 2.303 factor). - [ ] **$E_{\text{cell}}^{\circ} = E_{\text{cathode}}^{\circ} - E_{\text{anode}}^{\circ}$** — both values are *standard reduction potentials* (always quoted as reduction). - [ ] **Spontaneity rule:** $\Delta G < 0 \iff E_{\text{cell}} > 0 \iff K_{eq} > 1$ for the cell as written. - [ ] **EDL** is *not* a sharp boundary — it is a structured region of $\sim 1$–$10 \text{ nm}$ thickness. - [ ] The **Butler–Volmer equation** is the kinetic backbone of all faradaic processes. - [ ] For any KTU numerical: always **state $n$ explicitly** at the start of your solution; most errors arise from overlooking it. - [ ] Always include **units** in the final answer and **box** the result — KTU examiners reward neatness and penalize ambiguity.

topics.title: Electrolyte Transport Processes Within Electrolytes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electrolyte Transport Processes Within Electrolytes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In an electrochemical system, **electrolyte transport processes** refer to the three fundamental mechanisms by which ionic and molecular species are physically transported through the electrolyte medium between the anode and cathode. These three modes are classified as:

1. **Migration** — The movement of charged species (ions) under the influence of an electric field (potential gradient, $\nabla \phi$).
2. **Diffusion** — The spontaneous movement of species from regions of high concentration to regions of low concentration, driven by a concentration gradient ($\nabla c_i$).
3. **Convection** — The bulk transport of the entire electrolyte fluid due to either natural (density-driven) or forced (mechanical pumping/stirring) fluid motion, characterized by a velocity field $\vec{v}$.

The unifying governing relationship that superimposes all three contributions to give the net molar flux $\vec{N}_i$ of species $i$ is the **Nernst–Planck Equation**:

$$\vec{N}_i = -D_i \nabla c_i \;-\; \frac{z_i F}{RT} D_i c_i \nabla \phi \;+\; c_i \vec{v}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to write the **Nernst–Planck equation in all three coordinate systems** (Cartesian, cylindrical, spherical), identify the contribution of each term, and reduce it to specific limiting cases (e.g., pure diffusion at open circuit, or pure migration under supporting electrolyte-free conditions).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **crowded swimming pool on a hot summer day** with three different things happening simultaneously:

- **Migration = The Wind:** A strong wind (electric field) blows the swimmers (ions) in a specific direction. Positively charged ions move one way, negatively charged ions the other — like leaves being pushed by the breeze.
- **Diffusion = The Crowd Spreading Out:** Even without wind, if everyone piles up at one end of the pool, they will gradually redistribute themselves evenly across the pool. This is purely a concentration-driven, statistical spreading process.
- **Convection = The Wave Machine:** A wave-making machine (pump, stirrer, or buoyancy-driven flow) physically pushes the entire body of water — and everyone in it — in a single bulk direction. This affects all species equally regardless of charge or concentration.

The **Nernst–Planck equation** is the pool's complete "traffic report," combining wind + crowd-spreading + wave machine to predict exactly how many swimmers per second pass any imaginary cross-section of the pool.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $F$ | Faraday constant | **96485 C mol⁻¹** |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | **K** |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J K⁻¹** |
| $D_i$ | Diffusion coefficient | **m² s⁻¹** (typ. 10⁻⁹–10⁻¹⁰ for ions) |
| $z_i$ | Charge number | dimensionless integer |
| $\mu_i$ | Ionic mobility | **m² V⁻¹ s⁻¹** |
| $\kappa$ | Electrolytic conductivity | **S m⁻¹** |
| $t_i$ | Transport (transference) number | dimensionless, $0 \leq t_i \leq 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definitional Note:** The term *Electrolyte Transport Processes* is sometimes loosely called "mass transport in electrochemical systems." In KTU board papers, the phrase "within electrolytes" specifically refers to the bulk solution phase, *excluding* the surface processes occurring inside the electrical double layer (EDL).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Transport Modes — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Diffusion

- **Why it occurs:** Random Brownian motion of particles produces a net statistical flux from high to low concentration regions.
- **Driving force:** Concentration gradient, $\nabla c_i$.
- **Governing Law (Fick's First Law):**

$$\vec{J}_{i,\text{diff}} = -D_i \nabla c_i$$

- **Time-dependent form (Fick's Second Law):**

$$\frac{\partial c_i}{\partial t} = D_i \nabla^{2} c_i$$

- **Engineering utility:** Determines the limiting current in fuel cells, batteries, and corrosion systems; controls the rate of reactant supply to electrodes.

### 2.1.2 Migration

- **Why it occurs:** Ions carry charge. When an electric field is imposed, the electrostatic force $z_i F \nabla \phi$ accelerates ions of opposite signs in opposite directions.
- **Driving force:** Potential gradient, $\nabla \phi$.
- **Flux Expression:**

$$\vec{J}_{i,\text{mig}} = -\frac{z_i F}{R T} D_i c_i \nabla \phi$$

- **Engineering utility:** Migration is the *useful* component in electroplating, electrorefining, electrodialysis, and chlor-alkali processes.

### 2.1.3 Convection

- **Why it occurs:** Forced flow (pump, stirrer, rotating disc) or natural flow (density differences due to concentration or thermal gradients) physically displaces the entire fluid.
- **Driving force:** Fluid velocity vector, $\vec{v}$.
- **Flux Expression:**

$$\vec{J}_{i,\text{conv}} = c_i \vec{v}$$

- **Engineering utility:** Rotating disc electrodes (RDE), flow-through batteries, electrochemical reactors, and electrochemical machining all rely on controlled convection.

## 2.2 The Nernst–Planck Equation — Assembled Form

The total molar flux $\vec{N}_i$ (units: mol m⁻² s⁻¹) of species $i$ is the linear superposition:

$$\vec{N}_i = \underbrace{-D_i \nabla c_i}_{\text{Diffusion}} \;-\; \underbrace{\frac{z_i F}{R T} D_i c_i \nabla \phi}_{\text{Migration}} \;+\; \underbrace{c_i \vec{v}}_{\text{Convection}}$$

### 2.2.1 Why Linear Superposition is Valid

Because diffusion, migration, and convection act through independent microscopic mechanisms (random thermal motion, electrostatic force, and bulk fluid motion respectively), their fluxes can be added vectorially. This linear additivity holds at low-to-moderate concentrations (dilute electrolyte approximation, typically < 0.1 M).

### 2.2.2 Limiting Cases (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Scenario | Active Modes | Reduced Nernst–Planck |
|---|---|---|
| Open-circuit, no flow | Diffusion only | $\vec{N}_i = -D_i \nabla c_i$ |
| No concentration gradient, no flow | Migration only | $\vec{N}_i = -\frac{z_i F}{RT} D_i c_i \nabla \phi$ |
| Excess supporting electrolyte, well-stirred | Convection only | $\vec{N}_i = c_i \vec{v}$ |
| Steady-state, planar diffusion | Diffusion = Migration | Sand's equation (later module) |

## 2.3 Ionic Mobility and the Einstein Relation

**Ionic mobility** $\mu_i$ is defined as the steady-state drift velocity of an ion per unit applied electric field:

$$\mu_i = \frac{v_i}{E} = \frac{\vert z_i \vert F D_i}{R T}$$

The **Einstein Relation** (also called Nernst–Einstein equation) connects mobility with the diffusion coefficient:

$$D_i = \mu_i k_B T = \frac{\mu_i R T}{F} \quad \text{(for ions)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** This relation explains why heavier ions diffuse more slowly and why temperature increase accelerates diffusion linearly with $T$.

## 2.4 Transport (Transference) Number

The fraction of the total current carried by species $i$:

$$t_i = \frac{I_i}{I_{\text{total}}} = \frac{z_i u_i c_i}{\sum_j z_j u_j c_j}$$

By definition, $\sum_i t_i = 1$. For a binary electrolyte $M^+ X^-$, $t_+ + t_- = 1$.

## 2.5 Electrolytic Conductivity

The bulk conductivity of an electrolyte solution:

$$\kappa = \frac{F^{2}}{R T} \sum_i z_i^{2} D_i c_i = F \sum_i z_i^{2} \mu_i c_i$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{N}_i = -D_i \nabla c_i - \frac{z_i F}{RT} D_i c_i \nabla \phi + c_i \vec{v}$ | Complete Nernst–Planck equation |
| 2 | $\vec{J}_{i,\text{diff}} = -D_i \nabla c_i$ | Fick's First Law |
| 3 | $\frac{\partial c_i}{\partial t} = D_i \nabla^{2} c_i$ | Fick's Second Law |
| 4 | $\mu_i = \frac{\vert z_i \vert F D_i}{RT}$ | Ionic mobility definition |
| 5 | $D_i = \frac{\mu_i R T}{F}$ | Einstein–Nernst relation |
| 6 | $\kappa = \frac{F^{2}}{RT}\sum_i z_i^{2} D_i c_i$ | Electrolytic conductivity |
| 7 | $t_i = \frac{z_i^{2} \mu_i c_i}{\sum_j z_j^{2} \mu_j c_j}$ | Transport number |
| 8 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ | Incompressible flow continuity |
| 9 | $\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla \vec{v} \right) = -\nabla P + \eta \nabla^{2} \vec{v} + \rho \vec{g}$ | Navier–Stokes for fluid |
| 10 | $\sum_i t_i = 1$ | Transport number closure |
| 11 | $\nabla \cdot \vec{i} = 0$ | Charge conservation (steady) |
| 12 | $\vec{i} = F \sum_i z_i \vec{N}_i$ | Current density via flux |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Einstein–Nernst Relation

### Step 1 — Start from the Drift Velocity Definition

When an ion of charge $z_i F$ is placed in an electric field $E$, the electrostatic force acting on it is:

$$F_{\text{elec}} = z_i F E$$

This force is balanced by the viscous drag $f v_i$ (Stokes' drag), where $f$ is the friction coefficient. At steady state:

$$z_i F E = f v_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_i = \frac{z_i F E}{f}$$

### Step 2 — Define Mobility

Mobility is drift velocity per unit field:

$$\mu_i = \frac{v_i}{E} = \frac{z_i F}{f}$$

### Step 3 — Connect Friction Coefficient to Diffusion Coefficient

From statistical mechanics (the fluctuation–dissipation theorem), the friction coefficient and the diffusion coefficient are related by:

$$D_i = \frac{k_B T}{f}$$

### Step 4 — Combine the Two Relations

Substituting $f = \frac{k_B T}{D_i}$ into the mobility expression:

$$\mu_i = \frac{z_i F}{k_B T / D_i} = \frac{z_i F D_i}{k_B T}$$

### Step 5 — Convert Using $R = N_A k_B$ and $F = N_A e$

For one mole of ions, with $F = N_A e$ and $R = N_A k_B$, we obtain the macroscopic form:

$$\boxed{\;D_i = \frac{\mu_i R T}{F}\;}$$

This is the celebrated **Nernst–Einstein relation**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Electrolytic Conductivity $\kappa$

### Step 1 — Express Current Density in Terms of Fluxes

Current density carried by species $i$:

$$\vec{i}_i = z_i F \vec{N}_i$$

### Step 2 — Use Only the Migration Component (Ohm's law assumption for pure conduction)

In the absence of concentration gradients and convection, the flux is purely migrational:

$$\vec{N}_i = -\frac{z_i F}{RT} D_i c_i \nabla \phi$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Total Current

$$\vec{i} = F \sum_i z_i \vec{N}_i = -\frac{F^{2}}{RT} \left( \sum_i z_i^{2} D_i c_i \right) \nabla \phi$$

### Step 4 — Identify Conductivity

By definition, Ohm's law in the electrolyte gives $\vec{i} = -\kappa \nabla \phi$. Comparing term by term:

$$\boxed{\;\kappa = \frac{F^{2}}{R T} \sum_i z_i^{2} D_i c_i\;}$$

This shows that conductivity grows with concentration, temperature, ionic charge squared, and diffusivity.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Transport Number

### Step 1 — Define Fractional Current

The fraction of total current carried by species $i$ is:

$$t_i = \frac{I_i}{I_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\vec{i}_i}{\sum_j \vec{i}_j}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Current Density Expressions

Using $I_i \propto z_i^{2} \mu_i c_i$ (after squaring $z_i$ to remove sign dependence and taking the magnitude of current):

$$t_i = \frac{\vert z_i \vert \mu_i c_i}{\sum_j \vert z_j \vert \mu_j c_j}$$

For a symmetric binary electrolyte $M^{+}X^{-}$ with equal $\vert z \vert = 1$:

$$\boxed{\;t_+ = \frac{u_+}{u_+ + u_-}, \quad t_- = \frac{u_-}{u_+ + u_-}\;}$$

Note that $t_+ + t_- = 1$, which is the **transport number closure condition**.

## 3.4 Reduction of Nernst–Planck in 1D Cartesian Coordinates

For a planar electrode lying at $x = 0$, all gradients lie along the $x$-axis. The full Nernst–Planck equation reduces to:

$$N_{i,x} = -D_i \frac{\partial c_i}{\partial x} - \frac{z_i F}{RT} D_i c_i \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + c_i v_x$$

If convection is suppressed ($v_x = 0$) and steady state is reached with zero net flux ($N_i = 0$):

$$0 = -D_i \frac{\partial c_i}{\partial x} - \frac{z_i F}{RT} D_i c_i \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$$

Dividing by $D_i c_i$:

$$0 = -\frac{1}{c_i} \frac{\partial c_i}{\partial x} - \frac{z_i F}{RT} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\partial \ln c_i}{\partial x} = -\frac{z_i F}{RT} \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$$

### Step 3.4 — Integrate with Boundary Conditions

Integrating from the bulk ($x = \delta$, $c_i = c_{i,\text{bulk}}$, $\phi = \phi_{\text{bulk}}$) to the surface ($x = 0$):

$$\int_{c_{i,\text{bulk}}}^{c_{i}(0)} d \ln c_i = -\frac{z_i F}{R T} \int_{\phi_{\text{bulk}}}^{\phi(0)} d\phi$$

$$\ln \frac{c_i(0)}{c_{i,\text{bulk}}} = -\frac{z_i F}{R T} \left[ \phi(0) - \phi_{\text{bulk}} \right]$$

$$\boxed{\;c_i(0) = c_{i,\text{bulk}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{z_i F}{RT}\bigl(\phi(0) - \phi_{\text{bulk}}\bigr)\right]\;}$$

This is the **Boltzmann distribution of ions** at equilibrium — a direct consequence of the Nernst–Planck equation under zero net flux and no convection.

## 3.5 Complete Limiting-Case Derivation Table

| Case | Assumption | Simplified Nernst–Planck | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure diffusion | $\nabla \phi = 0$, $\vec{v} = 0$ | $\vec{N}_i = -D_i \nabla c_i$ | Fick's law |
| Pure migration | $\nabla c_i = 0$, $\vec{v} = 0$ | $\vec{N}_i = -\frac{z_i F}{RT} D_i c_i \nabla \phi$ | Drift in field |
| Pure convection | $\nabla c_i = 0$, $\nabla \phi = 0$ | $\vec{N}_i = c_i \vec{v}$ | Bulk fluid flow |
| No net flux, no convection | $\vec{N}_i = 0$, $\vec{v} = 0$ | $\nabla \phi = -\frac{RT}{z_i F} \nabla \ln c_i$ | Boltzmann distribution |
| Steady state, planar | $\partial c_i / \partial t = 0$, 1D | $0 = D_i \frac{d^{2} c_i}{dx^{2}}$ | Linear concentration profile |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A 0.10 M NaCl solution at 25 °C has ionic mobilities $u_{Na^+} = 5.19 \times 10^{-8}$ m² V⁻¹ s⁻¹ and $u_{Cl^-} = 7.91 \times 10^{-8}$ m² V⁻¹ s⁻¹. Compute (a) the transport numbers, (b) the electrolytic conductivity.

### Step 1 — Transport Numbers

For a 1:1 binary electrolyte:

$$t_{Na^+} = \frac{u_{Na^+}}{u_{Na^+} + u_{Cl^-}} = \frac{5.19}{5.19 + 7.91} = \frac{5.19}{13.10} \approx 0.396$$

$$t_{Cl^-} = \frac{u_{Cl^-}}{u_{Na^+} + u_{Cl^-}} = \frac{7.91}{13.10} \approx 0.604$$

**Check:** $0.396 + 0.604 = 1.000$ ✓

### Step 2 — Diffusion Coefficients via Einstein Relation

$$D_{Na^+} = \frac{u_{Na^+} R T}{F} = \frac{5.19 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485}$$

Evaluating the numerator:

$$\text{Numerator} = 5.19 \times 10^{-8} \times 2477.6 = 1.286 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$D_{Na^+} = \frac{1.286 \times 10^{-4}}{96485} \approx 1.333 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^{2}\text{s}^{-1}$$

Similarly for $Cl^-$:

$$D_{Cl^-} = \frac{7.91 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} \approx 2.033 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^{2}\text{s}^{-1}$$

### Step 3 — Electrolytic Conductivity

$$\kappa = \frac{F^{2}}{RT} \sum_i z_i^{2} D_i c_i$$

For NaCl, both ions have $\vert z_i \vert = 1$:

$$\kappa = \frac{F^{2}}{RT} \bigl[ D_{Na^+} + D_{Cl^-} \bigr] c$$

$$\kappa = \frac{(96485)^{2}}{8.314 \times 298} \times (1.333 + 2.033) \times 10^{-9} \times 100$$

Computing the prefactor:

$$\frac{(96485)^{2}}{2477.6} = \frac{9.3094 \times 10^{9}}{2477.6} \approx 3.758 \times 10^{6} \text{ C}^{2}\text{J}^{-1}\text{m}^{-1}$$

Multiplying by the sum term:

$$\kappa \approx 3.758 \times 10^{6} \times 3.366 \times 10^{-7} \approx 1.265 \text{ S m}^{-1}$$

This matches the experimental value for 0.1 M NaCl at 25 °C (~1.29 S m⁻¹), confirming the formula's validity.
