# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **Define migration, diffusion, and convection as applied to electrolyte transport. Give the mathematical expression for each flux component.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Migration:** Movement of charged ions under an electric field. The migrational flux is $\vec{J}_{i,\text{mig}} = -\frac{z_i F}{RT} D_i c_i \nabla \phi$. **(1 mark)**
- **Diffusion:** Movement of species from high to low concentration regions due to a concentration gradient. The diffusive flux is $\vec{J}_{i,\text{diff}} = -D_i \nabla c_i$. **(1 mark)**
- **Convection:** Bulk fluid motion transporting species. The convective flux is $\vec{J}_{i,\text{conv}} = c_i \vec{v}$. **(1 mark)**

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
> **State and explain the Nernst–Einstein equation relating diffusion coefficient and ionic mobility.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Nernst–Einstein equation is:

$$D_i = \frac{\mu_i R T}{F}$$

where $\mu_i = \vert z_i \vert F D_i / (R T)$ is the ionic mobility, $R$ is the gas constant, $T$ is the absolute temperature, and $F$ is Faraday's constant. **(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for physical significance.)**

**Physical significance:** It links the random thermal motion (diffusion) with the directed drift under an electric field (migration), showing both arise from the same ionic friction coefficient. **(1 mark)**

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Module Choice Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **(a)** Derive the **Nernst–Planck equation** for the molar flux of species $i$ in an electrolyte solution, clearly stating the assumptions used. Identify the contribution of each of the three transport modes. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Starting from the Nernst–Planck equation, show that under conditions of zero net flux, no convection, and thermal equilibrium, the concentration profile of an ion near an electrode follows a Boltzmann distribution. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a) — Derivation of Nernst–Planck Equation

**Step 1 — Identify the driving forces (1 mark):**

For a charged species $i$ in an electrolyte, three independent driving forces can act simultaneously:
- Concentration gradient $\nabla c_i$ → diffusion
- Electric field $\nabla \phi$ → migration
- Bulk fluid velocity $\vec{v}$ → convection

**Step 2 — Write the diffusion flux (2 marks):**

Fick's first law gives the diffusive molar flux:

$$\vec{J}_{i,\text{diff}} = -D_i \nabla c_i$$

**Step 3 — Write the migration flux (2 marks):**

The drift velocity under an electric field is $v_i = \mu_i E = -\mu_i \nabla \phi$ (since $E = -\nabla \phi$). Using $\mu_i = \vert z_i \vert F D_i / (R T)$:

$$\vec{J}_{i,\text{mig}} = c_i v_i = -\frac{z_i F}{R T} D_i c_i \nabla \phi$$

**Step 4 — Write the convective flux (1 mark):**

$$\vec{J}_{i,\text{conv}} = c_i \vec{v}$$

**Step 5 — Apply linear superposition (1 mark):**

The total molar flux is the vector sum (valid for dilute electrolytes):

$$\boxed{\;\vec{N}_i = -D_i \nabla c_i - \frac{z_i F}{RT} D_i c_i \nabla \phi + c_i \vec{v}\;}$$

#### Solution to Part (b) — Boltzmann Distribution Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the conditions (1 mark):**

Set $\vec{N}_i = 0$, $\vec{v} = 0$, and assume one-dimensional geometry (gradient along $x$):

$$0 = -D_i \frac{d c_i}{d x} - \frac{z_i F}{R T} D_i c_i \frac{d \phi}{d x}$$

**Step 2 — Divide through by $D_i c_i$ (2 marks):**

$$0 = -\frac{1}{c_i} \frac{d c_i}{d x} - \frac{z_i F}{R T} \frac{d \phi}{d x}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the logarithmic derivative (1 mark):**

Since $\frac{d \ln c_i}{d x} = \frac{1}{c_i} \frac{d c_i}{d x}$:

$$\frac{d \ln c_i}{d x} = -\frac{z_i F}{R T} \frac{d \phi}{d x}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from bulk to surface (2 marks):**

Integrating from $x = \delta$ (bulk) to $x = 0$ (electrode surface):

$$\int_{\ln c_{i,\text{bulk}}}^{\ln c_i(0)} d \ln c_i = -\frac{z_i F}{R T} \int_{\phi_{\text{bulk}}}^{\phi(0)} d\phi$$

**Step 5 — Final result (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\;c_i(0) = c_{i,\text{bulk}} \exp\!\left[ -\frac{z_i F}{R T} \bigl( \phi(0) - \phi_{\text{bulk}} \bigr) \right]\;}$$

This is the **Boltzmann distribution** of ions in an electrostatic potential field.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Module Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
> **(a)** Define the **transport number** (transference number) of an ion. Derive its expression in terms of ionic mobilities for a binary electrolyte. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a 0.05 M KCl solution at 25 °C, the ionic mobilities are $u_{K^+} = 7.62 \times 10^{-8}$ m² V⁻¹ s⁻¹ and $u_{Cl^-} = 7.91 \times 10^{-8}$ m² V⁻¹ s⁻¹. Compute (i) the transport numbers, (ii) the diffusion coefficients of each ion, and (iii) the electrolytic conductivity. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a) — Transport Number Derivation

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**

The transport number $t_i$ is the fraction of the total current passing through the electrolyte that is carried by ionic species $i$:

$$t_i = \frac{I_i}{I_{\text{total}}} = \frac{i_i}{\sum_j i_j}$$

**Step 2 — Write the current carried by species $i$ (2 marks):**

The current density carried by species $i$ due to its drift velocity is:

$$i_i = z_i F c_i v_i = z_i^{2} F c_i \mu_i E \quad (\text{taking magnitude})$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio (2 marks):**

For a binary electrolyte $M^+ X^-$ with $\vert z \vert = 1$:

$$t_+ = \frac{\mu_+ c_+}{\mu_+ c_+ + \mu_- c_-}, \qquad t_- = \frac{\mu_- c_-}{\mu_+ c_+ + \mu_- c_-}$$

For electroneutral solutions with $c_+ = c_- = c$:

$$\boxed{\;t_+ = \frac{\mu_+}{\mu_+ + \mu_-}, \qquad t_- = \frac{\mu_-}{\mu_+ + \mu_-}\;}$$

with $t_+ + t_- = 1$. **(1 mark for closure condition.)**

#### Solution to Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Step 1 — Transport numbers (2 marks):**

$$t_{K^+} = \frac{7.62}{7.62 + 7.91} = \frac{7.62}{15.53} \approx 0.491$$

$$t_{Cl^-} = \frac{7.91}{15.53} \approx 0.509$$

**Check:** $0.491 + 0.509 = 1.000$ ✓

**Step 2 — Diffusion coefficients using Einstein–Nernst relation (2 marks):**

$$D_{K^+} = \frac{u_{K^+} R T}{F} = \frac{7.62 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485}$$

$$D_{K^+} = \frac{7.62 \times 10^{-8} \times 2477.6}{96485} = \frac{1.888 \times 10^{-4}}{96485} \approx 1.957 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^{2}\text{s}^{-1}$$

$$D_{Cl^-} = \frac{7.91 \times 10^{-8} \times 2477.6}{96485} \approx 2.033 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^{2}\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Electrolytic conductivity (3 marks):**

$$\kappa = \frac{F^{2}}{RT} \sum_i z_i^{2} D_i c_i = \frac{F^{2}}{RT} \bigl( D_{K^+} + D_{Cl^-} \bigr) c$$

$$\kappa = \frac{(96485)^{2}}{2477.6} \times (1.957 + 2.033) \times 10^{-9} \times 50$$

$$\kappa = 3.758 \times 10^{6} \times 3.990 \times 10^{-7} \times 50 \approx 0.750 \text{ S m}^{-1}$$

This agrees well with experimental conductivity of 0.05 M KCl at 25 °C (~0.78 S m⁻¹).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign Confusion in Migration Term:** Students frequently write $\frac{+z_i F}{RT}$ instead of the correct $-\frac{z_i F}{RT}$ in the migration term. Always remember: $\vec{J}_{\text{mig}} = c_i \vec{v}_i$ and $\vec{v}_i = -\mu_i \nabla \phi$, so the negative sign is mandatory. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Wrong Units of $D_i$:** $D_i$ is in m² s⁻¹, NOT cm² s⁻¹. KTU examiners specifically mark for unit consistency. Convert using $1 \text{ cm}^{2}\text{s}^{-1} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}\text{s}^{-1}$. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Forgetting Temperature in $RT$:** Always convert °C to K before substituting. A common mistake is using $T = 25$ instead of $T = 298$. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 4. **Missing Closure Condition:** Always write $t_+ + t_- = 1$ as a verification step; KTU examiners reward the explicit closure check.
> 5. **Not Specifying Coordinate System:** When asked to "write the Nernst–Planck equation," KTU often expects a 1D form. Always reduce to Cartesian 1D if the problem states "planar electrode."

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Three Transport Modes:** Migration (electric field driven), Diffusion (concentration driven), Convection (bulk flow driven) — these are the only three mechanisms of mass transport in electrolytes.
- **Nernst–Planck Equation:** The master equation combining all three modes; written as $\vec{N}_i = -D_i \nabla c_i - \frac{z_i F}{RT} D_i c_i \nabla \phi + c_i \vec{v}$. Every other transport relation is a limiting case.
- **Einstein–Nernst Relation:** $D_i = \mu_i R T / F$ — links diffusion and mobility through thermal energy. Heavier ions, lower temperature → lower $D$.
- **Boltzmann Distribution:** Direct consequence of zero-flux Nernst–Planck at equilibrium; $c_i(0) = c_{i,\text{bulk}} \exp[-z_i F (\phi_0 - \phi_\infty) / RT]$.
- **Transport Number Closure:** $t_+ + t_- = 1$ (or $\sum_i t_i = 1$ generally). Used to determine which ion carries majority of the current.
- **Electrolytic Conductivity:** $\kappa = \frac{F^{2}}{RT} \sum_i z_i^{2} D_i c_i$. Increases with $T$ (mildly), concentration, charge squared, and diffusivity.
- **Continuity Equation (Mass Conservation):** $\frac{\partial c_i}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot \vec{N}_i = 0$. Combines with Nernst–Planck to give the full transport PDE.
- **Limiting Cases Always Asked:** Be ready to derive pure diffusion, pure migration, and pure convection as special cases of Nernst–Planck.
- **Boundary Conditions (Quick Recall):**
  * At electrode surface: $\vec{N}_i \cdot \hat{n} = -\frac{i}{n F}$ (flux = reaction rate / charge)
  * At bulk: $c_i \to c_{i,\text{bulk}}$, $\phi \to \phi_{\text{bulk}}$, $\vec{v} \to 0$ (no slip convention)
  * No-slip: $\vec{v} = 0$ at solid walls.
- **Common Exam Traps:** Sign of migration term; unit conversions for $D_i$; Celsius-vs-Kelvin errors; ignoring electroneutrality $c_+ = c_- = c$ for binary salts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Empirical Laws of Electrolyte Conductivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Empirical Laws of Electrolyte Conductivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ MODULE 1 — EMPIRICAL LAWS OF ELECTROLYTE CONDUCTIVITY

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 1 Core Concept:** Electrolytic conductivity is *not* a single variable — it is governed by **four experimentally discovered (empirical) laws** that describe how ions move, migrate, and respond to dilution in solution. Mastering these laws is foundational for every subsequent module (cells, batteries, corrosion, electroplating).

---

## 1.1 What is Electrolyte Conductivity? — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Electrolyte Conductivity (κ — Kappa):** It is the reciprocal of resistivity of an electrolytic solution, numerically equal to the conductance of a **1 cm cube** of the solution placed between two parallel electrodes of area **1 cm²** separated by **1 cm**.
>
> **SI Units:** **S·m⁻¹** (Siemens per metre) ; **CGS Units:** **S·cm⁻¹** (commonly used in textbooks).

Mathematically:

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{l}{A} \cdot \frac{1}{R}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = resistivity (Ω·m)
- $l$ = distance between electrodes (m)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²)
- $R$ = resistance (Ω)

Two derived quantities of paramount importance for the *empirical laws*:

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|----------|--------|------------|------|
| **Molar Conductivity** | $\Lambda_m$ | $\kappa / c$ (conductivity ÷ molar concentration) | S·m²·mol⁻¹ |
| **Equivalent Conductivity** | $\Lambda_{eq}$ | $\kappa / N$ (conductivity ÷ normality) | S·m²·eq⁻¹ |

where $N = z \cdot c$ and $z$ is the charge number (valence factor).

---

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Crowded Highway"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a multi-lane highway where cars represent **ions** travelling under an applied electric field (think of traffic lights driving them forward).
>
> - At **high concentration (rush hour)** → too many cars, they bump into each other, traffic slows down → **conductivity decreases** with $\sqrt{c}$ (Debye-Hückel-Onsager).
> - At **infinite dilution (empty road)** → each car moves freely at its own "limiting speed" → **conductivity is the SUM of individual ionic speeds** → this is **Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration**.
> - For **weak electrolytes (broken-down cars)** → only a fraction $\alpha$ of vehicles are actually moving → **Ostwald's Dilution Law** captures this.

---

## 1.3 The Four Empirical Laws — Overview

The KTU Module 1 syllabus recognizes **four empirical laws** that form the cornerstone of ionic transport theory:

1. **Kohlrausch's Law (√c dependence)** — $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ - A\sqrt{c}$
2. **Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration of Ions** — $\Lambda_m^\circ = \nu_+ \lambda_+^\circ + \nu_- \lambda_-^\circ$
3. **Ostwald's Dilution Law** — for weak binary electrolytes: $K_a = \dfrac{\Lambda_m^2 \, c}{\Lambda_m^\circ(\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m)}$
4. **Debye-Hückel-Onsager Equation** — quantitative form of Kohlrausch's √c law.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** KTU examiners **love** Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration. Expect a 7-mark derivation or numerical in Part B every cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Kohlrausch's Law — The √c Dependence (Strong Electrolytes)

**Experimental observation (Friedrich Kohlrausch, 1876):** When molar conductivity $\Lambda_m$ of a **strong electrolyte** is plotted against $\sqrt{c}$, a straight line with **negative slope** is obtained.

### Logic Steps:

1. At finite concentration, ions experience **inter-ionic attractions** (the ionic atmosphere effect).
2. These attractions **slow down** ion migration under the applied field.
3. The retarding effect is proportional to the **square root of concentration** (a consequence of electrostatic screening — derived rigorously by Debye-Hückel-Onsager).
4. As $c \to 0$, ions are infinitely far apart → no inter-ionic effect → $\Lambda_m$ approaches a maximum called **limiting molar conductivity $\Lambda_m^\circ$**.

### The Equation:

$$\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ - A\sqrt{c}$$

where $A$ is a constant depending on:
- Solvent (dielectric constant, viscosity)
- Temperature
- Charge type of the electrolyte

> **Real-world utility:** This law is used in industry to **determine the limiting conductivity** of salts that cannot be measured at infinite dilution (e.g., sparingly soluble salts like AgCl, BaSO₄).

---

## 2.2 Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration of Ions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **not** the same as the √c law! It deals with **$\Lambda_m^\circ$ values**, not their concentration dependence.

**Statement:** *At infinite dilution, the molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of the limiting molar conductivities of its constituent ions, each multiplied by the number of ions per formula unit.*

### Logic Steps:

1. At infinite dilution, **ion–ion interactions vanish**.
2. Each cation contributes its individual limiting conductivity $\lambda_+^\circ$.
3. Each anion contributes its individual limiting conductivity $\lambda_-^\circ$.
4. Migration becomes **additive** — independent of the co-ion.

### The Equation:

$$\Lambda_m^\circ = \nu_+ \, \lambda_+^\circ + \nu_- \, \lambda_-^\circ$$

For example, for Al₂(SO₄)₃:

$$\Lambda_m^\circ = 2 \, \lambda_{\text{Al}^{3+}}^\circ + 3 \, \lambda_{\text{SO}_4^{2-}}^\circ$$

> **Real-world utility:** Allows calculation of $\Lambda_m^\circ$ for **weak electrolytes** (CH₃COOH, NH₄OH) which cannot be directly measured — by combining strong-electrolyte data.

---

## 2.3 Ostwald's Dilution Law (Weak Electrolytes)

**Statement:** Applies to **weak (binary) electrolytes**. Relates the dissociation constant $K_a$ to the degree of dissociation $\alpha$ and concentration $c$.

### Logic Steps:

1. A weak electrolyte HA dissociates: $\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$
2. Let $\alpha$ = degree of dissociation; then conductivity ratio gives: $\alpha = \Lambda_m / \Lambda_m^\circ$
3. Apply **law of mass action** to the equilibrium.
4. Rearrange to obtain the dilution law.

### The Equation:

$$K_a = \frac{\Lambda_m^2 \, c}{\Lambda_m^\circ \left(\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m\right)}$$

> **Real-world utility:** Used to determine **dissociation constants** of weak acids/bases from conductivity measurements — a classical physico-chemical technique.

---

## 2.4 Debye-Hückel-Onsager Equation — The Rigorous √c Law

For a **binary strong electrolyte** (e.g., NaCl, KNO₃), the constant $A$ in Kohlrausch's √c law is theoretically given by:

$$\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ - (A + B\Lambda_m^\circ)\sqrt{c}$$

where:

$$A = \frac{82.4}{\eta \, (DT)^{1/2}} \quad \text{and} \quad B = \frac{8.20 \times 10^5}{(DT)^{3/2}}$$

with $\eta$ = viscosity (poise), $D$ = dielectric constant, $T$ = absolute temperature.

> **Note:** For dilute solutions ($\sqrt{c} < 0.1$), the $B\Lambda_m^\circ$ term dominates, recovering the simple linear form $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ - A'\sqrt{c}$.

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Module 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table** — covers ~80% of numerical questions asked from this topic.

| # | Law / Relation | Governing Equation | Applies To | Key Symbol |
|---|----------------|--------------------|------------|------------|
| 1 | Kohlrausch √c Law | $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ - A\sqrt{c}$ | Strong electrolytes | $A$ = constant |
| 2 | Independent Migration | $\Lambda_m^\circ = \nu_+\lambda_+^\circ + \nu_-\lambda_-^\circ$ | Infinite dilution | $\lambda^\circ$ = ionic contribution |
| 3 | Degree of dissociation | $\alpha = \Lambda_m / \Lambda_m^\circ$ | All electrolytes | $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$ |
| 4 | Ostwald's Dilution Law | $K_a = \dfrac{\Lambda_m^2 c}{\Lambda_m^\circ(\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m)}$ | Weak binary electrolytes | $K_a$ = dissociation constant |
| 5 | Debye-Hückel-Onsager | $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ - (A + B\Lambda_m^\circ)\sqrt{c}$ | Dilute strong electrolytes | $A, B$ depend on solvent & T |
| 6 | Conductivity → Molar conductivity | $\Lambda_m = \dfrac{\kappa \times 1000}{c \, (\text{mol/L})}$ | Definition | $\kappa$ in S·cm⁻¹ |
| 7 | Molar → Equivalent conductivity | $\Lambda_{eq} = \Lambda_m / z$ | Conversion | $z$ = charge number |
| 8 | Kohlrausch Application (Weak Acid) | $\Lambda_m^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}) = \lambda_{\text{H}^+}^\circ + \lambda_{\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-}^\circ$ | Worked via salt + HCl + NaCl | Indirect determination |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common unit trap:** When $\kappa$ is in S·cm⁻¹ and $c$ is in mol·L⁻¹, then $\Lambda_m = \dfrac{1000\,\kappa}{c}$ (the factor 1000 converts cm³ → dm³). Examiners deduct full marks if this factor is missed.

---

## 2.6 Comparison: Strong vs Weak Electrolytes

| Feature | Strong Electrolyte | Weak Electrolyte |
|---------|-------------------|------------------|
| Dissociation | Complete ($\alpha \approx 1$) | Partial ($\alpha \ll 1$) |
| $\Lambda_m$ vs $\sqrt{c}$ plot | Linear (straight line) | Curved (asymptotic to $\Lambda_m^\circ$) |
| Governing Law | Kohlrausch √c law | Ostwald's Dilution Law |
| Limiting $\Lambda_m^\circ$ | Extrapolation of straight line | Calculated from Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration |
| Example | NaCl, KCl, HCl | CH₃COOH, NH₄OH |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** KTU valuation keys give **full 7 marks** only when every algebraic step is *visible*. The following derivations are written in examiner-pleasing detail.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Degree of Dissociation α from Conductivity Ratio

### Given:
An electrolyte dissociates as: $\text{AB} \rightarrow \text{A}^+ + \text{B}^-$

### Step 1 — Write conductivity contributions.

Total conductivity at concentration $c$ is the sum of contributions from undissociated molecules + free ions. Since undissociated molecules carry no charge:

$$\kappa_{\text{observed}} = \kappa_{\text{free ions}} = \alpha \, c \, (F \, \mu_+ + F \, \mu_-)$$

where $\mu_+, \mu_-$ are ionic mobilities and $F$ is Faraday's constant.

### Step 2 — Write conductivity at infinite dilution.

At infinite dilution, $\alpha = 1$ completely:

$$\kappa_\infty = c \, (F \, \mu_+ + F \, \mu_-)$$

### Step 3 — Form the ratio.

$$\frac{\kappa}{\kappa_\infty} = \alpha$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $\kappa = \Lambda_m \, c$ and $\kappa_\infty = \Lambda_m^\circ \, c$.

$$\frac{\Lambda_m \, c}{\Lambda_m^\circ \, c} = \alpha$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = \frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^\circ}}$$

**Verification:** For a strong electrolyte, $\Lambda_m \approx \Lambda_m^\circ \Rightarrow \alpha \approx 1$ ✓; for a weak electrolyte, $\Lambda_m \ll \Lambda_m^\circ \Rightarrow \alpha \ll 1$ ✓.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ostwald's Dilution Law (Full Derivation)

### Given:
A weak binary electrolyte HA dissociates:

$$\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$$

Initial concentration: $c$ mol/L. Equilibrium degree of dissociation: $\alpha$.

### Step 1 — Write the equilibrium concentrations.

| Species | HA | H⁺ | A⁻ |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|
| Initial | $c$ | 0 | 0 |
| Equilibrium | $c(1-\alpha)$ | $c\alpha$ | $c\alpha$ |

### Step 2 — Apply the Law of Mass Action.

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} = \frac{(c\alpha)(c\alpha)}{c(1-\alpha)} = \frac{c\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $\alpha = \Lambda_m / \Lambda_m^\circ$.

$$K_a = \frac{c \left(\dfrac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^\circ}\right)^2}{1 - \dfrac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^\circ}}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the denominator.

$$1 - \frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^\circ} = \frac{\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^\circ}$$

### Step 5 — Combine.

$$K_a = \frac{c \, \Lambda_m^2}{(\Lambda_m^\circ)^2} \cdot \frac{\Lambda_m^\circ}{\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m}$$

$$\boxed{K_a = \frac{\Lambda_m^2 \, c}{\Lambda_m^\circ \left(\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m\right)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** This equation is also called the **Ostwald's Dilution Law**. It is exact for **weak binary (1:1) electrolytes** in dilute solution.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration — Proof Sketch

### Given:
At infinite dilution, ion–ion interactions vanish. Each ion moves independently under the electric field.

### Step 1 — Molar conductivity at infinite dilution.

For electrolyte $A_{\nu_+} B_{\nu_-}$ that gives $\nu_+$ cations and $\nu_-$ anions per formula unit:

$$\Lambda_m^\circ = \nu_+ \, F \, \mu_+^\circ + \nu_- \, F \, \mu_-^\circ$$

where $\mu_+^\circ, \mu_-^\circ$ are **limiting ionic mobilities**.

### Step 2 — Define limiting ionic conductivity.

By definition:

$$\lambda_+^\circ = F \, \mu_+^\circ \qquad \text{and} \qquad \lambda_-^\circ = F \, \mu_-^\circ$$

### Step 3 — Substitute back.

$$\Lambda_m^\circ = \nu_+ \, \lambda_+^\circ + \nu_- \, \lambda_-^\circ$$

$$\boxed{\Lambda_m^\circ = \nu_+ \lambda_+^\circ + \nu_- \lambda_-^\circ} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$

### Step 4 — Worked example — Limiting conductivity of acetic acid.

Acetic acid cannot be measured at infinite dilution because it is weak. But:

$$\Lambda_m^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}) = \lambda_{\text{H}^+}^\circ + \lambda_{\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-}^\circ$$

Using strong-electrolyte data:

$$\Lambda_m^\circ(\text{HCl}) = \lambda_{\text{H}^+}^\circ + \lambda_{\text{Cl}^-}^\circ$$

$$\Lambda_m^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}) = \lambda_{\text{Na}^+}^\circ + \lambda_{\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-}^\circ$$

$$\Lambda_m^\circ(\text{NaCl}) = \lambda_{\text{Na}^+}^\circ + \lambda_{\text{Cl}^-}^\circ$$

By algebraic combination:

$$\Lambda_m^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}) = \Lambda_m^\circ(\text{HCl}) + \Lambda_m^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}) - \Lambda_m^\circ(\text{NaCl})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** This "triangulation" technique is a **favourite 7-mark question**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** The molar conductivity of a 0.01 M solution of acetic acid is 16.30 S·cm²·mol⁻¹. The limiting molar conductivity of acetic acid is 390.5 S·cm²·mol⁻¹. Calculate the dissociation constant of acetic acid. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 type]

**Given:**
- $c = 0.01$ mol·L⁻¹
- $\Lambda_m = 16.30$ S·cm²·mol⁻¹
- $\Lambda_m^\circ = 390.5$ S·cm²·mol⁻¹

**Step 1 — Apply Ostwald's dilution law.**

$$K_a = \frac{\Lambda_m^2 \, c}{\Lambda_m^\circ \left(\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m\right)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$K_a = \frac{(16.30)^2 \times 0.01}{390.5 \times (390.5 - 16.30)}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate numerator.**

$$(16.30)^2 \times 0.01 = 265.69 \times 0.01 = 2.6569$$

**Step 4 — Calculate denominator.**

$$390.5 \times 374.2 = 146{,}125.1$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation.**

$$K_a = \frac{2.6569}{146{,}125.1} = 1.818 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{K_a \approx 1.82 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Verification:** Standard value of $K_a$ for acetic acid is $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ at 25 °C — perfect agreement. ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Law

| Situation in Question | Use This Law | Reason |
|----------------------|--------------|--------|
| Strong electrolyte, data at various $c$ | Kohlrausch √c Law | Linear $\Lambda_m$ vs $\sqrt{c}$ |
| $\Lambda_m^\circ$ for weak electrolyte (no direct measurement) | Kohlrausch's Independent Migration Law | Combine strong electrolyte $\Lambda_m^\circ$ values |
| Find $K_a$ of weak acid from conductivity | Ostwald's Dilution Law | Direct formula |
| Plot curvature, dilute regime | Debye-Hückel-Onsager | Theoretically rigorous √c |
| Sparingly soluble salt (e.g., AgCl) | Kohlrausch's Independent Migration | $\Lambda_m \approx \Lambda_m^\circ$ at saturation |
| Find solubility from $\kappa$ | $s = \dfrac{\kappa}{\Lambda_m^\circ}$ | Molar conductivity definition |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Conceptual Map of the Four Empirical Laws

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Electrolyte Conductivity"]:::root --> B["Strong Electrolytes"]
    A --> C["Weak Electrolytes"]
    A --> D["Infinite Dilution Behaviour"]

    B --> B1["Kohlrausch Square Root Law"]
    B1 --> B1a["Lambda m equals Lambda m zero minus A square root c"]
    B1 --> B1b["Linear plot of Lambda m vs square root c"]

    B --> B2["Debye Huckel Onsager Equation"]
    B2 --> B2a["Lambda m zero minus A plus B Lambda m zero times square root c"]

    C --> C1["Ostwald Dilution Law"]
    C1 --> C1a["Ka equals Lambda m squared c over Lambda m zero times Lambda m zero minus Lambda m"]
    C1 --> C1b["Curved plot of Lambda m vs square root c"]

    D --> D1["Kohlrausch Law of Independent Migration of Ions"]
    D1 --> D1a["Lambda m zero equals nu plus lambda plus zero plus nu minus lambda minus zero"]
    D1 --> D1b["Enables Lambda m zero of weak electrolytes via strong electrolyte data"]

    B1a --> E["Application: Predict Lambda m at any concentration"]
    B2a --> E
    C1a --> F["Application: Find dissociation constant Ka"]
    D1a --> G["Application: Determine Lambda m zero for weak electrolytes"]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Strong vs Weak Electrolyte Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Strong["STRONG ELECTROLYTE PATH"]
        S1["Ions in Solution"] --> S2["Ionic Atmosphere Effect"]
        S2 --> S3["Retarding Force on Migration"]
        S3 --> S4["Conductivity decreases with square root c"]
        S4 --> S5["Output: Linear Lambda m vs square root c plot"]
    end

    subgraph Weak["WEAK ELECTROLYTE PATH"]
        W1["Partial Dissociation alpha less than 1"] --> W2["Equilibrium HA to H plus A minus"]
        W2 --> W3["Mass Action gives Ka equals c alpha squared over one minus alpha"]
        W3 --> W4["Curved Lambda m vs square root c plot"]
        W4 --> W5["Output: Lambda m zero found via Independent Migration Law"]
    end

    Strong --> R["Combined Output: Lambda m zero = nu plus lambda plus zero + nu minus lambda minus zero"]
    Weak --> R
    R --> Apply["Engineering Applications: Battery design, Conductivity cells, Corrosion rate, Solubility products"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flowchart for Solving Numerical Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Given Conductivity Data"]:::start --> Q1{"Strong or Weak Electrolyte?"}
    Q1 -->|"Strong"| P1["Use Kohlrausch Square Root Law"]
    P1 --> P1a["Find Lambda m zero by extrapolation of Lambda m vs square root c"]
    P1 --> P1b["Or use Debye Huckel Onsager if dilute"]

    Q1 -->|"Weak"| P2["Use Ostwald Dilution Law"]
    P2 --> P2a["Find Ka from Lambda m at given c"]

    Q1 -->|"Find Lambda m zero of weak electrolyte"| P3["Use Kohlrausch Independent Migration Law"]
    P3 --> P3a["Combine strong electrolyte data"]
    P3 --> P3b["Example: CH3COOH = HCl + CH3COONa minus NaCl"]

    P1a --> Final["Final Answer with Units"]
    P1b --> Final
    P2a --> Final
    P3b --> Final

    classDef start fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Schematic Interpretation — Why √c and not c?

> [!NOTE]
> The **square-root dependence** on concentration arises from the **Debye length** of the ionic atmosphere: $\kappa^{-1} \propto \sqrt{c}$. The retarding force on a central ion is proportional to the **charge density of the surrounding atmosphere**, which scales as $\sqrt{c}$ due to Boltzmann distribution + Poisson electrostatics. Hence Kohlrausch's empirical observation $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ - A\sqrt{c}$ has rigorous theoretical backing in the Debye-Hückel-Onsager equation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Note:** KTU 2024 ESE pattern for PEPOT637 follows the standard 3-mark (Part A) + 14-mark (Part B with module choice) format. The questions below strictly follow this pattern.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**State Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration of Ions. Why is it important for determining the limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

**Statement (1.5 marks):** *At infinite dilution, each ion makes an independent contribution to the total molar conductivity of the electrolyte, regardless of the nature of the co-ion.*

Mathematically: $\Lambda_m^\circ = \nu_+ \lambda_+^\circ + \nu_- \lambda_-^\circ$

**Importance for weak electrolytes (1.5 marks):** Weak electrolytes like CH₃COOH cannot be measured at infinite dilution because dissociation is incomplete. By combining $\Lambda_m^\circ$ values of strong electrolytes (HCl, NaCl, CH₃COONa), the $\Lambda_m^\circ$ of acetic acid can be calculated indirectly as:

$$\Lambda_m^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}) = \Lambda_m^\circ(\text{HCl}) + \Lambda_m^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}) - \Lambda_m^\circ(\text{NaCl})$$

This enables the use of Ostwald's dilution law to find $K_a$.

---

### **Question A2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between the conductometric behaviour of strong and weak electrolytes with respect to concentration.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

| Feature (1.5 marks) | Strong Electrolyte | Weak Electrolyte |
|---------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Variation of $\Lambda_m$ with $\sqrt{c}$ | Linear (straight line, obeys Kohlrausch √c law) | Curved (asymptotic to $\Lambda_m^\circ$) |
| Dissociation | Complete ($\alpha \approx 1$) | Partial ($\alpha \ll 1$) |

**Governing laws (1.5 marks):**
- **Strong electrolytes** → Kohlrausch's law: $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ - A\sqrt{c}$
- **Weak electrolytes** → Ostwald's dilution law: $K_a = \dfrac{\Lambda_m^2 c}{\Lambda_m^\circ(\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m)}$

For strong electrolytes, $\Lambda_m^\circ$ is found by **extrapolation**; for weak electrolytes, it is found via **Kohlrausch's Independent Migration Law**.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question B-A.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the degree of dissociation ($\alpha$) and molar conductivity for a binary electrolyte. Show that $\alpha = \Lambda_m / \Lambda_m^\circ$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The molar conductivities at infinite dilution of HCl, CH₃COONa, and NaCl are 426.2, 91.0, and 126.5 S·cm²·mol⁻¹ respectively at 25 °C. Calculate the limiting molar conductivity of acetic acid and the dissociation constant of a 0.05 M acetic acid solution whose molar conductivity is 14.5 S·cm²·mol⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a binary electrolyte AB dissociating as $\text{AB} \rightarrow \text{A}^+ + \text{B}^-$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Let $\alpha$ be the degree of dissociation. At concentration $c$ mol·L⁻¹, the actual concentrations of free ions are $c\alpha$ each.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The conductivity $\kappa$ at concentration $c$ is proportional to the number of free ions:

$$\kappa = \alpha \, c \, F (\mu_+ + \mu_-)$$

where $\mu_+, \mu_-$ are ionic mobilities.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** At infinite dilution ($\alpha = 1$):

$$\kappa_\infty = c \, F (\mu_+^\circ + \mu_-^\circ)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Form the ratio and substitute $\Lambda_m = \kappa/c$, $\Lambda_m^\circ = \kappa_\infty/c$:

$$\frac{\kappa}{\kappa_\infty} = \alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^\circ} = \alpha$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = \frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^\circ}} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:

**Step 1 — Apply Kohlrausch's Independent Migration Law (3 marks):**

$$\Lambda_m^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}) = \Lambda_m^\circ(\text{HCl}) + \Lambda_m^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}) - \Lambda_m^\circ(\text{NaCl})$$

Substituting values:

$$\Lambda_m^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}) = 426.2 + 91.0 - 126.5$$

$$\boxed{\Lambda_m^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}) = 390.7 \text{ S·cm}^2\text{·mol}^{-1}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ostwald's Dilution Law (2 marks):**

$$K_a = \frac{\Lambda_m^2 \, c}{\Lambda_m^\circ \left(\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values (1 mark):**

$$K_a = \frac{(14.5)^2 \times 0.05}{390.7 \times (390.7 - 14.5)} = \frac{210.25 \times 0.05}{390.7 \times 376.2}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate (1 mark):**

$$K_a = \frac{10.5125}{146{,}981.34} = 7.15 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{K_a(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}) \approx 7.15 \times 10^{-5}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit error (–1 mark):** Many students forget that $\Lambda_m$ values from tables are in **S·cm²·mol⁻¹**, not S·m²·mol⁻¹. Ensure units are consistent.
> 2. **Sign convention (–1 mark):** In Kohlrausch's triangulation, do NOT write $\Lambda_m^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COONa}) - \Lambda_m^\circ(\text{NaCl}) + \Lambda_m^\circ(\text{HCl})$ in random order — the ions must cancel exactly. Write out the ion terms and cancel visibly.
> 3. **Formula misquote (–2 marks):** Writing $K_a = \dfrac{\Lambda_m^2 c}{\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m}$ (forgetting $\Lambda_m^\circ$ in denominator) is a common error. Use the boxed form correctly.

---

### **Question B-B.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** State and explain Ostwald's Dilution Law. Derive the mathematical expression relating molar conductivity to dissociation constant for a weak binary electrolyte. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The specific conductance of a 0.02 M solution of a weak monobasic acid at 25 °C is $5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ S·cm⁻¹. The limiting molar conductivities of H⁺ and A⁻ at infinite dilution are 349.6 and 51.0 S·cm²·mol⁻¹ respectively. Calculate (i) the degree of dissociation $\alpha$, (ii) the dissociation constant $K_a$, and (iii) the pKa of the acid. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:

**Statement (2 marks):** *For a weak binary electrolyte, the dissociation constant $K_a$ is related to the molar conductivity $\Lambda_m$, limiting molar conductivity $\Lambda_m^\circ$, and concentration $c$ by:*

$$K_a = \frac{\Lambda_m^2 \, c}{\Lambda_m^\circ \left(\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m\right)}$$

*As $c \to 0$ (infinite dilution), $\Lambda_m \to \Lambda_m^\circ$ and the dissociation becomes complete.*

**Explanation (1 mark):** The law combines (a) mass-action equilibrium for dissociation and (b) conductivity ratio giving degree of dissociation. It is the "conductivity analogue" of the equilibrium constant expression.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

For $\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$ with initial concentration $c$ and degree of dissociation $\alpha$:

$$K_a = \frac{c\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$$

Substituting $\alpha = \Lambda_m / \Lambda_m^\circ$:

$$K_a = \frac{c \left(\dfrac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^\circ}\right)^2}{1 - \dfrac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^\circ}} = \frac{c \, \Lambda_m^2}{(\Lambda_m^\circ)^2 - \Lambda_m^\circ \Lambda_m}$$

Factoring $\Lambda_m^\circ$:

$$\boxed{K_a = \frac{\Lambda_m^2 \, c}{\Lambda_m^\circ \left(\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m\right)}} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:

**Given:**
- $\kappa = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ S·cm⁻¹
- $c = 0.02$ mol·L⁻¹
- $\Lambda_m^\circ = \lambda_{\text{H}^+}^\circ + \lambda_{\text{A}^-}^\circ = 349.6 + 51.0 = 400.6$ S·cm²·mol⁻¹

**Step 1 — Calculate $\Lambda_m$ (1 mark):**

$$\Lambda_m = \frac{1000 \times \kappa}{c} = \frac{1000 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.02} = \frac{0.5}{0.02} = 25.0 \text{ S·cm}^2\text{·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\alpha$ (2 marks):**

$$\alpha = \frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^\circ} = \frac{25.0}{400.6} = 0.0624$$

$$\boxed{\alpha \approx 0.0624 \text{ (or } 6.24\%)}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $K_a$ (2 marks):**

$$K_a = \frac{\Lambda_m^2 \, c}{\Lambda_m^\circ (\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m)} = \frac{(25.0)^2 \times 0.02}{400.6 \times (400.6 - 25.0)}$$

$$K_a = \frac{625 \times 0.02}{400.6 \times 375.6} = \frac{12.5}{150{,}465.36}$$

$$\boxed{K_a \approx 8.31 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate pKa (2 marks):**

$$\text{p}K_a = -\log_{10}(K_a) = -\log_{10}(8.31 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$\text{p}K_a = 5 - \log_{10}(8.31) = 5 - 0.9196$$

$$\boxed{\text{p}K_a \approx 4.08}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting factor 1000 in $\Lambda_m$ calculation (–2 marks):** When $\kappa$ is in S·cm⁻¹ and $c$ in mol·L⁻¹, $\Lambda_m = \dfrac{1000\,\kappa}{c}$. Omitting 1000 gives wrong units and wrong values.
> 2. **Wrong $\Lambda_m^\circ$ (–2 marks):** $\Lambda_m^\circ$ is the **sum** of cation and anion contributions, not a single ionic value.
> 3. **Sign error in $\log_{10}$ calculation (–1 mark):** For $K_a = a \times 10^{-5}$, $\text{p}K_a = 5 - \log_{10}(a)$. Do NOT write $\text{p}K_a = \log_{10}(1/K_a)$ directly without showing intermediate steps.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Empirical Laws of Electrolyte Conductivity:**

### 📌 Core Definitions
- ☐ **Specific conductivity (κ):** Conductance of 1 cm³ cube of solution; units S·cm⁻¹ or S·m⁻¹.
- ☐ **Molar conductivity (Λₘ):** $\Lambda_m = \dfrac{\kappa}{c}$ = $\dfrac{1000\,\kappa}{c}$ (when κ in S·cm⁻¹, c in mol·L⁻¹); units S·cm²·mol⁻¹.
- ☐ **Equivalent conductivity (Λ_eq):** $\Lambda_{eq} = \Lambda_m / z$.
- ☐ **Degree of dissociation (α):** $\alpha = \Lambda_m / \Lambda_m^\circ$.

### 📌 The Four Laws
- ☐ **Kohlrausch's √c Law:** $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ - A\sqrt{c}$ → strong electrolytes.
- ☐ **Kohlrausch's Independent Migration Law:** $\Lambda_m^\circ = \nu_+ \lambda_+^\circ + \nu_- \lambda_-^\circ$ → at infinite dilution.
- ☐ **Ostwald's Dilution Law:** $K_a = \dfrac{\Lambda_m^2 c}{\Lambda_m^\circ(\Lambda_m^\circ - \Lambda_m)}$ → weak electrolytes.
- ☐ **Debye-Hückel-Onsager Equation:** $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ - (A + B\Lambda_m^\circ)\sqrt{c}$ → rigorous √c dependence.

### 📌 Numerical Problem Workflow
1. ☐ Identify electrolyte type (strong/weak) from context.
2. ☐ Compute $\Lambda_m$ from given $\kappa$ and $c$ using $\Lambda_m = 1000\kappa/c$.
3. ☐ For weak electrolyte $\Lambda_m^\circ$: use **Independent Migration Law** (triangulation).
4. ☐ Apply appropriate law to find requested quantity.

### 📌 Key Numerical Constants (KTU Data Book)
- ☐ $\lambda_{\text{H}^+}^\circ = 349.6$ S·cm²·mol⁻¹ at 25 °C (highest of all ions).
- ☐ $\lambda_{\text{OH}^-}^\circ = 199.1$ S·cm²·mol⁻¹.
- ☐ $\Lambda_m^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}) = 390.7$ S·cm²·mol⁻¹ (from triangulation).
- ☐ $K_a(\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}) = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ → pKa ≈ 4.74.

### 📌 Plot Shapes to Remember
- ☐ **Strong electrolyte:** $\Lambda_m$ vs $\sqrt{c}$ → **straight line with negative slope** intersecting y-axis at $\Lambda_m^\circ$.
- ☐ **Weak electrolyte:** $\Lambda_m$ vs $\sqrt{c}$ → **curved**, asymptoting to $\Lambda_m^\circ$ at very low $c$.

### 📌 Common Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
- ☐ ❌ Mixing S·cm⁻¹ with mol·m⁻³ without unit conversion.
- ☐ ❌ Writing $\Lambda_{eq} = \Lambda_m \cdot z$ (it's $\Lambda_{eq} = \Lambda_m / z$).
- ☐ ❌ Applying Ostwald's law to strong electrolytes.
- ☐ ❌ Forgetting the factor of 1000 in $\Lambda_m = 1000\kappa/c$.
- ☐ ❌ Confusing Kohlrausch's **two** different laws (√c law vs Independent Migration law).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions from this topic, always (1) state the law with the equation, (2) show the derivation step-by-step with clear logical connectives, (3) box the final answer with correct units. Examiner's eye rewards **clarity and completeness** over brevity.

---

✅ **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — EMPIRICAL LAWS OF ELECTROLYTE CONDUCTIVITY**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — PEPOT637 Electrochemical Engineering*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of pH and Buffer Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of pH and Buffer Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of pH

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** pH is defined as the negative logarithm (to the base 10) of the **hydronium ion (H₃O⁺)** activity (or hydrogen ion concentration in dilute solutions). It was introduced by the Danish biochemist **Søren Peder Lauritz Sørensen** in **1909** at the Carlsberg Laboratory, Copenhagen, while working on the quality control of beer brewing.

Mathematically, pH is expressed as:

$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10} \left[ \text{H}_3\text{O}^+ \right] = -\log_{10} \left[ \text{H}^+ \right]$$

where $[\text{H}_3\text{O}^+]$ denotes the molar concentration (in mol/L or M) of hydronium ions in an aqueous solution. The 'p' in pH stands for the German word **"Potenz"** (meaning power or exponent), and H refers to Hydrogen.

### 1.2 The pH Scale — Operational Range

The pH scale is a **logarithmic scale** that typically ranges from **0 to 14** at standard temperature (**25 °C** or **298.15 K**), though negative pH values and pH values above 14 are possible for extremely concentrated strong acids and bases respectively.

| Classification | pH Range | Ion Dominance | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Strongly Acidic | $0 - 3$ | $[\text{H}^+] \gg [\text{OH}^-]$ | HCl (1 M), Battery Acid |
| Weakly Acidic | $4 - 6$ | $[\text{H}^+] > [\text{OH}^-]$ | Rainwater, Coffee, Urine |
| Neutral | $7$ (at 25 °C) | $[\text{H}^+] = [\text{OH}^-] = 10^{-7}$ M | Pure Water |
| Weakly Basic / Alkaline | $8 - 10$ | $[\text{H}^+] < [\text{OH}^-]$ | Seawater, Baking Soda, Blood |
| Strongly Basic | $11 - 14$ | $[\text{H}^+] \ll [\text{OH}^-]$ | Ammonia, NaOH (1 M), Bleach |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** A change of **one pH unit** corresponds to a **10-fold change** in $[\text{H}^+]$. A change of **two pH units** corresponds to a **100-fold change**. This logarithmic nature is what makes pH a brilliant metric for representing the enormous range of $[\text{H}^+]$ in real solutions.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Concentration Thermometer"

Imagine you have a swimming pool and a teaspoon, both filled with water. Adding one teaspoon of strong acid to the pool represents a tiny change in concentration. However, on a linear concentration scale, the difference between acidic vinegar and pure water would look enormous and unmanageable.

**pH acts like a "concentration thermometer" for acidity.** Just as a thermometer compresses a huge range of temperatures into an easy-to-read scale (e.g., −10 °C to 50 °C), pH compresses hydrogen ion concentrations spanning many orders of magnitude into a simple 0–14 scale. This makes it incredibly convenient for chemists, biologists, and electrochemical engineers to quickly communicate the acidic or basic character of a system.

### 1.4 Buffer Solutions — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A **buffer solution** (or simply a *buffer*) is an aqueous solution that resists dramatic changes in pH upon the addition of small amounts of a **strong acid** or a **strong base**, or upon dilution. It typically consists of a mixture of a **weak acid** and its **conjugate base**, or a **weak base** and its **conjugate acid**.

There are two main types:
1. **Acidic Buffer:** A mixture of a **weak acid** (e.g., acetic acid, CH₃COOH) and its **conjugate base** (e.g., sodium acetate, CH₃COONa).
2. **Basic (Alkaline) Buffer:** A mixture of a **weak base** (e.g., ammonia, NH₃) and its **conjugate acid** (e.g., ammonium chloride, NH₄Cl).

### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy for Buffers — The "Sponge Metaphor"

Think of a buffer solution as a **chemical sponge for H⁺ and OH⁻ ions**. 
- If you drip a strong acid (a source of H⁺) into the system, the **conjugate base component** of the buffer "absorbs" the extra H⁺ ions, neutralizing them before they can shift the pH.
- If you drip a strong base (a source of OH⁻) into the system, the **weak acid component** donates H⁺ to neutralize the OH⁻ ions.

Just as a real sponge soaks up water and prevents flooding, the buffer soaks up added H⁺ or OH⁻ and prevents a pH "flood" (i.e., a large pH shift). This is why human blood contains a **carbonate buffer system** (H₂CO₃/HCO₃⁻) — it keeps our blood pH in a tight range of **7.35 to 7.45**, as a deviation outside **6.8 to 7.8** is fatal.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Auto-Ionization of Water and the Ionic Product ($K_w$)

Pure water undergoes a very slight, spontaneous self-ionization (autoprotolysis):

$$\text{H}_2\text{O} \,(l) \,+\, \text{H}_2\text{O} \,(l) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_3\text{O}^+ \,(aq) \,+\, \text{OH}^- \,(aq)$$

The equilibrium constant for this reaction at **25 °C (298.15 K)** is:

$$K_w = [\text{H}_3\text{O}^+] \, [\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$$

where $K_w$ is the **ionic product of water**. The negative logarithm of $K_w$ is:

$$\text{p}K_w = -\log_{10} K_w = 14.00 \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $K_w$ matters for Electrochemical Engineering:** The value of $K_w$ is **temperature-dependent**. At higher temperatures (e.g., in fuel cells, electrolyzers, and high-temperature batteries), $K_w$ increases significantly, which means **neutral pH shifts below 7**. This is a common viva question and is highly relevant when designing electrochemical systems operating at elevated temperatures.

### 2.2 The Fundamental pH–pOH–$K_w$ Relationship

Taking the negative logarithm of $K_w$:

$$-\log([\text{H}^+]) - \log([\text{OH}^-]) = -\log(K_w)$$

$$\boxed{\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = \text{p}K_w = 14 \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}}$$

### 2.3 Dissociation of Weak Acids and Bases (Ka and Kb)

For a generic weak acid **HA** dissociating in water:

$$\text{HA} \,(aq) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ \,(aq) + \text{A}^- \,(aq)$$

The acid dissociation constant is:

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+] [\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

For a generic weak base **BOH** (or B + H₂O ⇌ BH⁺ + OH⁻):

$$K_b = \frac{[\text{BH}^+] [\text{OH}^-]}{[\text{B}]}$$

For a conjugate acid–base pair, the following elegant relationship holds (especially useful in buffer problems):

$$\boxed{K_a \times K_b = K_w = 10^{-14} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{p}K_a + \text{p}K_b = \text{p}K_w}$$

### 2.4 The Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation (Buffer Equation)

This is the **most important equation** in buffer chemistry and the most heavily tested formula in KTU exams for this module.

For an **acidic buffer** consisting of weak acid HA and its salt NaA:

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10} \left( \frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} \right) = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10} \left( \frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Acid}]} \right)}$$

For a **basic buffer** consisting of weak base B and its salt BH⁺Cl⁻:

$$\text{pOH} = \text{p}K_b + \log_{10} \left( \frac{[\text{BH}^+]}{[\text{B}]} \right) = \text{p}K_b + \log_{10} \left( \frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Base}]} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** A buffer works most effectively when the pH is within **±1 unit** of the $\text{p}K_a$ of the weak acid (for acidic buffers) or within **±1 unit** of the $\text{p}K_b$ of the weak base (for basic buffers). Outside this range, the buffer capacity drops drastically.

### 2.5 Buffer Capacity ($\beta$)

Buffer capacity is a quantitative measure of a buffer's resistance to pH change. It is defined as the number of moles of strong acid or strong base required to change the pH of **1 litre** of buffer solution by **one unit**.

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{dn}{d(\text{pH})} = 2.303 \, C_T \frac{K_a [\text{H}^+]}{(K_a + [\text{H}^+])^2}}$$

where:
- $dn$ = moles of strong acid or base added per litre
- $C_T = [\text{HA}] + [\text{A}^-]$ = total buffer concentration
- $K_a$ = acid dissociation constant
- $[\text{H}^+]$ = hydrogen ion concentration

Maximum buffer capacity occurs when $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$ (i.e., when $[\text{HA}] = [\text{A}^-]$), giving:

$$\beta_{\max} = 0.576 \, C_T$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | Definition of pH | Dimensionless; $[\text{H}^+]$ in mol/L |
| 2 | $\text{pOH} = -\log_{10}[\text{OH}^-]$ | Definition of pOH | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = \text{p}K_w$ | pH–pOH relationship | $= 14$ at $T = 25$ °C |
| 4 | $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ | Ionic product of water | At **25 °C** (298.15 K) |
| 5 | $K_a = \dfrac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$ | Weak acid dissociation constant | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $K_b = \dfrac{[\text{OH}^-][\text{BH}^+]}{[\text{B}]}$ | Weak base dissociation constant | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $K_a \cdot K_b = K_w$ | Conjugate acid–base relation | $K_a K_b = 10^{-14}$ at 25 °C |
| 8 | $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Acid}]} \right)$ | **Henderson–Hasselbalch** | Acidic buffer |
| 9 | $\text{pOH} = \text{p}K_b + \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Base}]} \right)$ | Henderson–Hasselbalch | Basic buffer |
| 10 | $\beta = 2.303 \, C_T \dfrac{K_a[\text{H}^+]}{(K_a + [\text{H}^+])^2}$ | Buffer capacity | mol/(L·pH unit) |
| 11 | $\beta_{\max} = 0.576 \, C_T$ | Max buffer capacity | At $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$ |
| 12 | $\text{pH} = \dfrac{1}{2} \text{p}K_a - \dfrac{1}{2} \log C$ | Weak acid (no salt) | Approximation; $C$ = acid concentration |
| 13 | $\text{pH} = 7 + \dfrac{1}{2} \text{p}K_a + \dfrac{1}{2} \log C$ | Salt of weak acid + strong base | Hydrolysis |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concepts of pH and buffer solutions are **fundamental to electrochemical engineering**:

- **Battery Electrolytes:** The pH of electrolytes in **lead–acid, alkaline, and fuel cells** directly affects the cell voltage, internal resistance, and electrode corrosion rates.
- **Corrosion Engineering:** The rate of corrosion of metals (e.g., iron, aluminum, copper) is highly sensitive to pH. Buffered corrosion-testing solutions simulate industrial environments.
- **Electroplating:** pH determines the **hydrogen evolution reaction** rate, current efficiency, and quality of metal deposition.
- **Water Treatment:** pH adjustment and buffering are crucial in **wastewater treatment plants** and **boiler feedwater chemistry** to prevent scaling and corrosion.
- **Biomedical Devices:** **Pacemakers**, **glucose sensors**, and **biofuel cells** operate within narrow pH ranges; buffer chemistry is essential for stability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation (Exhaustive)

Consider a generic weak acid **HA** that partially dissociates in aqueous solution:

$$\text{HA} \,(aq) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ \,(aq) + \text{A}^- \,(aq)$$

The acid dissociation constant is given by:

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 1:** Take the negative logarithm (base 10) of both sides:

$$-\log K_a = -\log \left( \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Apply logarithm rules: $-\log(AB/C) = -\log A - \log B + \log C$. So:

$$-\log K_a = -\log[\text{H}^+] - \log[\text{A}^-] + \log[\text{HA}]$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange the equation to group $[\text{H}^+]$ terms on the left:

$$-\log[\text{H}^+] = -\log K_a + \log[\text{A}^-] - \log[\text{HA}]$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$ and $\text{p}K_a = -\log K_a$:

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log[\text{A}^-] - \log[\text{HA}]$$

**Step 5:** Combine the two logarithms using $\log X - \log Y = \log(X/Y)$:

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10} \left( \frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Commentary:** In a buffer solution containing the salt **NaA** (which fully dissociates into Na⁺ and A⁻), the concentration of the conjugate base $[\text{A}^-]$ is approximately equal to the salt concentration, since the salt is a strong electrolyte that dissociates completely. Similarly, the concentration of undissociated weak acid $[\text{HA}]$ is approximately equal to the initial acid concentration (assuming the degree of dissociation is small, which is valid for weak acids).

### 3.2 Derivation of Buffer Capacity Equation

The buffer capacity $\beta$ is defined as:

$$\beta = \frac{dn_{\text{base}}}{d(\text{pH})}$$

where $dn_{\text{base}}$ is the infinitesimal number of moles of strong base (e.g., NaOH) added per litre.

**Step 1:** Adding a strong base converts some of the weak acid HA into its conjugate base A⁻:

$$\text{HA} + \text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{A}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2:** Let $a$ = moles of strong base added per litre, $C_a$ = initial concentration of HA, $C_b$ = initial concentration of NaA. After adding the base:
- $[\text{HA}] = C_a - a$
- $[\text{A}^-] = C_b + a$
- Total buffer concentration: $C_T = C_a + C_b$

**Step 3:** Apply the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation:

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log \left( \frac{C_b + a}{C_a - a} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate with respect to pH:

$$d(\text{pH}) = d \left[ \log \left( \frac{C_b + a}{C_a - a} \right) \right] = 0.4343 \, d \left[ \ln \left( \frac{C_b + a}{C_a - a} \right) \right]$$

**Step 5:** Use the derivative $\dfrac{d}{da} \ln\left(\dfrac{C_b + a}{C_a - a}\right) = \dfrac{1}{C_b + a} + \dfrac{1}{C_a - a} = \dfrac{C_T}{(C_b + a)(C_a - a)}$:

$$d(\text{pH}) = 0.4343 \, \frac{C_T}{(C_b + a)(C_a - a)} \, da$$

**Step 6:** Invert and use $da = dn$ (moles per litre):

$$\beta = \frac{dn}{d(\text{pH})} = \frac{2.303 \, (C_b + a)(C_a - a)}{C_T}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $C_a - a = [\text{HA}]$ and $C_b + a = [\text{A}^-]$:

$$[\text{HA}] \cdot [\text{A}^-] = \frac{[\text{H}^+] [\text{A}^-]^2}{K_a} = \frac{[\text{H}^+] [\text{HA}]^2}{K_a} \cdot \frac{K_a^2}{K_a^2} \cdots$$

A cleaner derivation uses $[\text{HA}][\text{A}^-] = \dfrac{K_a [\text{A}^-]^2}{[\text{H}^+]} = \dfrac{[\text{H}^+][\text{HA}]}{K_a} \cdot \frac{1}{K_a}$... after algebraic manipulation:

$$\boxed{\beta = 2.303 \, C_T \frac{K_a [\text{H}^+]}{(K_a + [\text{H}^+])^2}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of pH of a Weak Acid (No Salt) — Analytical Approximation

For a weak acid HA of initial concentration $C$ mol/L that dissociates as:

$$\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$$

Let $x$ = amount dissociated. At equilibrium: $[\text{H}^+] = x$, $[\text{A}^-] = x$, $[\text{HA}] = C - x$.

$$K_a = \frac{x^2}{C - x}$$

**Approximation** (valid for weak acids where $\alpha < 5\%$): $C - x \approx C$, so:

$$K_a \approx \frac{x^2}{C} \implies x = \sqrt{K_a C}$$

Therefore:

$$[\text{H}^+] = \sqrt{K_a C}$$

Taking the negative logarithm:

$$-\log[\text{H}^+] = -\frac{1}{2} \log(K_a) - \frac{1}{2} \log(C)$$

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = \frac{1}{2} \text{p}K_a - \frac{1}{2} \log_{10} C}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of pH of a Salt of Weak Acid + Strong Base (Hydrolysis)

Consider a salt **NaA** (e.g., sodium acetate) dissolved in water. The anion A⁻ undergoes hydrolysis:

$$\text{A}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HA} + \text{OH}^-$$

The hydrolysis constant is:

$$K_h = \frac{K_w}{K_a} = \frac{[\text{HA}][\text{OH}^-]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$

For an initial salt concentration $C$ and degree of hydrolysis $h$:

$$K_h = \frac{C h^2}{1 - h} \approx C h^2 \quad (h \ll 1)$$

$$h = \sqrt{\frac{K_h}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{K_w}{K_a C}}$$

Therefore:

$$[\text{OH}^-] = C h = \sqrt{\frac{K_w C}{K_a}}$$

Taking $-\log$ and converting to pH:

$$\text{pOH} = \frac{1}{2}\text{p}K_w - \frac{1}{2}\text{p}K_a - \frac{1}{2}\log C$$

Using $\text{pH} = 14 - \text{pOH}$:

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = 7 + \frac{1}{2}\text{p}K_a + \frac{1}{2}\log C}$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** A buffer solution is prepared by mixing **500 mL** of **0.20 M acetic acid (CH₃COOH)** with **500 mL** of **0.30 M sodium acetate (CH₃COONa)**. Given that $\text{p}K_a$ of acetic acid is **4.76** at 25 °C, calculate:
(i) The pH of the buffer.
(ii) The pH after adding **100 mL of 0.10 M HCl** to **400 mL** of this buffer.

**Solution (i):**

After mixing equal volumes, concentrations are halved:
- $[\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}] = 0.10$ M
- $[\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-] = 0.15$ M

Apply Henderson–Hasselbalch:

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log \left( \frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} \right) = 4.76 + \log \left( \frac{0.15}{0.10} \right)$$

$$\text{pH} = 4.76 + \log(1.5) = 4.76 + 0.176 = 4.936 \approx 4.94$$

**Solution (ii):**

Moles of acid in 400 mL of buffer:
- $n_{\text{HA}} = 0.10 \times 0.400 = 0.040$ mol
- $n_{\text{A}^-} = 0.15 \times 0.400 = 0.060$ mol

Moles of HCl added:
- $n_{\text{HCl}} = 0.10 \times 0.100 = 0.010$ mol

Strong acid converts A⁻ → HA:
- $n_{\text{HA, new}} = 0.040 + 0.010 = 0.050$ mol
- $n_{\text{A}^-, \text{new}} = 0.060 - 0.010 = 0.050$ mol

New total volume = $400 + 100 = 500$ mL = 0.500 L

New concentrations:
- $[\text{HA}]_{\text{new}} = 0.050 / 0.500 = 0.10$ M
- $[\text{A}^-]_{\text{new}} = 0.050 / 0.500 = 0.10$ M

Apply Henderson–Hasselbalch:

$$\text{pH} = 4.76 + \log \left( \frac{0.10}{0.10} \right) = 4.76 + 0 = 4.76$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The pH shifted only from 4.94 → 4.76 (ΔpH = 0.18) despite adding 0.010 mol of strong HCl. If the same amount of HCl were added to pure water, the pH would drop by 4–5 units. This is the **buffering action** in action.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
