## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define pH. Explain the significance of the pH scale in electrochemical engineering applications.**

**Model Answer:**

pH is defined as the negative logarithm (to base 10) of the hydrogen ion (H⁺) concentration in moles per litre:

$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$$

It was introduced by **Sørensen in 1909**. The pH scale is logarithmic, typically ranging from **0 to 14** at 25 °C. A pH of 7 is **neutral**; values below 7 are **acidic** and above 7 are **basic (alkaline)**.

**Significance in Electrochemical Engineering:**
1. **Electrode kinetics:** The rates of hydrogen evolution (HER) and oxygen evolution (OER) reactions are strongly pH-dependent.
2. **Corrosion rate:** Corrosion of metals like iron and zinc accelerates at extreme pH values (both acidic and alkaline).
3. **Electroplating quality:** Uniform metal deposition requires maintaining a specific pH in the plating bath.
4. **Battery performance:** pH of the electrolyte determines ionic conductivity and cell voltage in fuel cells and lead-acid batteries.
5. **Bio-electrochemistry:** Enzyme-based sensors and biofuel cells require buffered physiological pH (~7.4).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a buffer solution? Differentiate between acidic and basic buffer solutions with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A **buffer solution** is an aqueous solution that resists changes in its pH upon the addition of small amounts of strong acid, strong base, or upon dilution. It is typically composed of a weak acid and its conjugate base (or a weak base and its conjugate acid).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Acidic Buffer | Basic Buffer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Composition | Weak acid + Salt (conjugate base) | Weak base + Salt (conjugate acid) |
| Example | CH₃COOH + CH₃COONa | NH₄OH + NH₄Cl |
| pH range | pH < 7 | pH > 7 |
| Working equation | Henderson–Hasselbalch (acid form) | Henderson–Hasselbalch (base form) |
| Maintains pH against | Both strong acid and base | Both strong acid and base |
| Biological example | Blood (H₂CO₃/HCO₃⁻ system) | Cytoplasmic fluids |

---

### 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Always include units** in the final numerical answer (where applicable).
> - **Do not forget** to account for **volume change** when acid/base is added to a buffer. Many students lose 2–3 marks by using old concentrations instead of recalculating after dilution.
> - **State assumptions** explicitly: e.g., "Assuming weak acid is only slightly dissociated, so $[\text{HA}] \approx C$."
> - Always show the **Henderson–Hasselbalch equation substitution** step by step — partial marks are awarded for the formula even if the final arithmetic has a minor slip.
> - For buffer capacity problems, distinguish between **adding strong acid vs. strong base** — students often confuse the sign of $\Delta n$.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation for an acidic buffer. State its significance and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A buffer solution contains **0.40 mol/L** of acetic acid and **0.60 mol/L** of sodium acetate. Calculate:
   (i) The pH of this buffer. (Given: $K_a$ of acetic acid = $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$)
   (ii) The pH after adding **50 mL of 1.0 M HCl** to **1 litre** of this buffer.
   (iii) The pH after adding **50 mL of 1.0 M NaOH** to another 1 litre of this buffer. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Consider a weak acid **HA** dissociating in water:

$$\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$$

The acid dissociation constant:

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

**Step 1:** Rearranging for $[\text{H}^+]$:

$$[\text{H}^+] = K_a \cdot \frac{[\text{HA}]}{[\text{A}^-]}$$

**Step 2:** Taking $-\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$-\log[\text{H}^+] = -\log K_a - \log[\text{HA}] + \log[\text{A}^-]$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$ and $\text{p}K_a = -\log K_a$:

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10} \left( \frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]} \right) = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10} \left( \frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Acid}]} \right)}$$

**Significance:**
- Allows direct calculation of buffer pH without solving quadratic equations.
- Predicts the pH change when small amounts of strong acid/base are added.
- Used in designing buffer systems for industrial, biological, and electrochemical applications.

**Limitations:**
- Valid only when the ratio $[\text{A}^-]/[\text{HA}]$ is between **0.1 and 10** (i.e., pH within ±1 of pKa).
- Fails at extreme dilutions (below $10^{-3}$ M).
- Does not account for ionic strength effects in concentrated solutions.
- Assumes negligible contribution of water auto-ionization.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Part (i):** First, calculate $\text{p}K_a$:

$$\text{p}K_a = -\log(1.8 \times 10^{-5}) = 4.74$$

Apply Henderson–Hasselbalch:

$$\text{pH} = 4.74 + \log \left( \frac{0.60}{0.40} \right) = 4.74 + \log(1.5) = 4.74 + 0.176 = 4.916 \approx 4.92$$

**Part (ii):** Adding 50 mL of 1.0 M HCl to 1 L of buffer:
- Moles of HCl added = $0.050 \times 1.0 = 0.050$ mol
- HCl reacts with acetate: $\text{H}^+ + \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$
- New moles of CH₃COOH = $0.40 + 0.050 = 0.450$ mol (in total volume 1.05 L)
- New moles of CH₃COO⁻ = $0.60 - 0.050 = 0.550$ mol (in total volume 1.05 L)

New concentrations (dividing by 1.05 L):
- $[\text{HA}] = 0.450 / 1.05 = 0.4286$ M
- $[\text{A}^-] = 0.550 / 1.05 = 0.5238$ M

$$\text{pH} = 4.74 + \log \left( \frac{0.5238}{0.4286} \right) = 4.74 + \log(1.222) = 4.74 + 0.087 = 4.83$$

**Part (iii):** Adding 50 mL of 1.0 M NaOH to another 1 L of buffer:
- Moles of NaOH added = $0.050$ mol
- NaOH reacts with acetic acid: $\text{OH}^- + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$
- New moles of CH₃COOH = $0.40 - 0.050 = 0.350$ mol
- New moles of CH₃COO⁻ = $0.60 + 0.050 = 0.650$ mol

New concentrations:
- $[\text{HA}] = 0.350 / 1.05 = 0.3333$ M
- $[\text{A}^-] = 0.650 / 1.05 = 0.6190$ M

$$\text{pH} = 4.74 + \log \left( \frac{0.6190}{0.3333} \right) = 4.74 + \log(1.857) = 4.74 + 0.269 = 5.01$$

**Commentary:** Notice that pH shifted from 4.92 → 4.83 (acid added) and 4.92 → 5.01 (base added). The shifts are small (~0.1 unit) compared to what would happen in pure water, demonstrating the buffer's effectiveness.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the term **buffer capacity**. Derive the expression for buffer capacity and state the condition for maximum buffer capacity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the pH of the following solutions:
   (i) A solution containing **0.10 M** acetic acid ($K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$).
   (ii) A solution containing **0.10 M** sodium acetate (salt of weak acid + strong base).
   (iii) A solution prepared by mixing **200 mL of 0.20 M NH₄OH** with **300 mL of 0.10 M HCl**. (Given: $K_b$ of NH₄OH = $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$) **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:** Buffer capacity $\beta$ is defined as the number of moles of strong acid or strong base required to change the pH of 1 litre of buffer solution by one unit.

$$\beta = \frac{dn}{d(\text{pH})}$$

**Derivation:**

Adding strong base (NaOH) to a buffer converts HA to A⁻. Let $a$ = moles of base added per litre to a buffer with initial acid concentration $C_a$ and salt concentration $C_b$:
- $[\text{HA}] = C_a - a$
- $[\text{A}^-] = C_b + a$
- Total: $C_T = C_a + C_b$

Henderson–Hasselbalch gives:

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log \left( \frac{C_b + a}{C_a - a} \right)$$

Differentiating with respect to $a$:

$$\frac{d(\text{pH})}{da} = \frac{1}{\ln 10} \left[ \frac{1}{C_b + a} + \frac{1}{C_a - a} \right] = \frac{1}{2.303} \cdot \frac{C_T}{(C_b+a)(C_a-a)}$$

Inverting and using $[\text{HA}][\text{A}^-] = \dfrac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]^2}{K_a}$ (with algebraic rearrangement):

$$\beta = 2.303 \, C_T \frac{K_a [\text{H}^+]}{(K_a + [\text{H}^+])^2}$$

**Condition for Maximum Buffer Capacity:**

To find the maximum, differentiate $\beta$ with respect to $[\text{H}^+]$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d\beta}{d[\text{H}^+]} = 0 \implies [\text{H}^+] = K_a \implies \text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$$

At this point, $[\text{HA}] = [\text{A}^-]$ and:

$$\boxed{\beta_{\max} = 2.303 \cdot C_T \cdot \frac{K_a^2}{(2K_a)^2} = \frac{2.303 \cdot C_T}{4} = 0.576 \, C_T}$$

**Significance:** A buffer is most effective when its pH equals $\text{p}K_a$. This is the principle behind **selecting appropriate buffer systems** for specific pH targets in electrochemical experiments.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Part (i):** 0.10 M CH₃COOH (weak acid, no salt)

Use the weak acid approximation:

$$\text{pH} = \frac{1}{2}\text{p}K_a - \frac{1}{2}\log C = \frac{1}{2}(4.74) - \frac{1}{2}\log(0.10)$$

$$\text{pH} = 2.37 - \frac{1}{2}(-1) = 2.37 + 0.50 = 2.87$$

**Part (ii):** 0.10 M CH₃COONa (salt of weak acid + strong base — undergoes hydrolysis)

Use the hydrolysis formula:

$$\text{pH} = 7 + \frac{1}{2}\text{p}K_a + \frac{1}{2}\log C = 7 + \frac{1}{2}(4.74) + \frac{1}{2}\log(0.10)$$

$$\text{pH} = 7 + 2.37 + \frac{1}{2}(-1) = 7 + 2.37 - 0.50 = 8.87$$

**Part (iii):** Mixing 200 mL of 0.20 M NH₄OH with 300 mL of 0.10 M HCl

Calculate initial moles:
- Moles of NH₄OH = $0.200 \times 0.20 = 0.040$ mol
- Moles of HCl = $0.300 \times 0.10 = 0.030$ mol

HCl reacts with NH₄OH: $\text{HCl} + \text{NH}_4\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$

After reaction:
- Moles of NH₄OH remaining = $0.040 - 0.030 = 0.010$ mol
- Moles of NH₄Cl formed = $0.030$ mol

This is a **basic buffer** (weak base NH₄OH + its salt NH₄Cl). Total volume = $0.500$ L.

Concentrations:
- $[\text{NH}_4\text{OH}] = 0.010 / 0.500 = 0.020$ M
- $[\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}] = 0.030 / 0.500 = 0.060$ M

For basic buffer:

$$\text{pOH} = \text{p}K_b + \log \left( \frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Base}]} \right) = 4.74 + \log \left( \frac{0.060}{0.020} \right)$$

$$\text{pOH} = 4.74 + \log(3) = 4.74 + 0.477 = 5.217$$

$$\text{pH} = 14 - 5.217 = 8.78$$

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1: Concept of pH and Buffer Solutions**

- ✅ **pH Definition:** $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$; introduced by **Sørensen (1909)**; the 'p' stands for the German word "Potenz" (power).
- ✅ **Ionic Product of Water:** $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}$ at **25 °C**; $\text{p}K_w = 14$.
- ✅ **Fundamental pH–pOH Relation:** $\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = \text{p}K_w = 14$ (at 25 °C).
- ✅ **Conjugate Acid–Base Pair:** $K_a \times K_b = K_w$ → $\text{p}K_a + \text{p}K_b = 14$.
- ✅ **Master Equation (Henderson–Hasselbalch):** $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log \left( \dfrac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Acid}]} \right)$ — **memorize this verbatim**; it appears in nearly every KTU question paper.
- ✅ **For basic buffers:** Use $\text{pOH} = \text{p}K_b + \log \left( \dfrac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Base}]} \right)$, then convert to pH.
- ✅ **Effective Buffer Range:** $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a \pm 1$ (for acidic buffers); equivalent for basic buffers in pOH.
- ✅ **Maximum Buffer Capacity:** Occurs at $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a$; $\beta_{\max} = 0.576 \, C_T$.
- ✅ **Weak Acid (No Salt):** $\text{pH} = \frac{1}{2}\text{p}K_a - \frac{1}{2}\log C$.
- ✅ **Salt of Weak Acid + Strong Base (Hydrolysis):** $\text{pH} = 7 + \frac{1}{2}\text{p}K_a + \frac{1}{2}\log C$.
- ✅ **Always account for volume change** when strong acid or base is added to a buffer — recalculate moles and divide by the new total volume.
- ✅ **Common Examples:** Acetic acid/acetate buffer (pH ~4.7); Carbonic acid/bicarbonate buffer (pH ~6.4 in blood); Phosphate buffer (pH ~7.2); Ammonia/ammonium buffer (pH ~9.2).
- ✅ **Electrochemical Relevance:** pH control is critical in **batteries, fuel cells, electroplating, corrosion prevention, and biomedical sensors** — be ready for application-based questions in Part B.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting to take $-\log$ of $K_a$ correctly. Always show $\text{p}K_a = -\log K_a$ as a separate step in numerical problems.
- ✅ **Temperature Effect:** $K_w$ increases with temperature; at body temperature (37 °C), $\text{p}K_w \approx 13.62$, so neutral pH ≈ 6.81.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of Electrochemical Systems: Electrode Electrochemical Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Elements of Electrochemical Systems: Electrode Electrochemical Thermodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Electrode?

An **electrode** is a solid electrical conductor (typically a metal, graphite, or semiconductor) through which electric current enters or leaves an electrolyte, an electrochemical cell, or another medium. In an electrochemical system, the electrode is the **phase boundary** at which heterogeneous electron-transfer (redox) reactions occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** An electrode is defined as a system comprising a metallic conductor in contact with an electrolyte (ionic conductor), across which charge transfer takes place via electrochemical (Faradaic) reactions involving the exchange of electrons and ions.

**Two Functional Classes of Electrodes:**

| Class | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Anode** | Site of **oxidation** (loss of electrons). Electrons leave the cell. | Zn in a Zn–Cu Daniell cell |
| **Cathode** | Site of **reduction** (gain of electrons). Electrons enter the cell. | Cu in a Zn–Cu Daniell cell |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** **A**node = **O**xidation, **R**eduction = **C**athode → *"An Ox, Red Cat"*. Note: In a **galvanic (spontaneous) cell**, the anode is **negative**; in an **electrolytic (non-spontaneous) cell**, the anode is **positive**. Sign convention is determined by current direction, not reaction type.

## 1.2 What is an Electrochemical System?

An **electrochemical system** is any assembly in which chemical energy is converted into electrical energy (or vice versa) through redox reactions that occur at electrode–electrolyte interfaces. The simplest practical electrochemical cell consists of:

1. **Two electrodes** (anode + cathode) immersed in,
2. **One or two electrolytes** (ionic conductors), connected by,
3. **An external circuit** (electronic conductor) for electron flow, and often,
4. **A salt bridge or porous separator** for internal ionic conduction.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Water Wheel of Electrons

Imagine a **water wheel** placed between two reservoirs of water at different heights. Water naturally flows from the higher reservoir to the lower one, turning the wheel and producing useful work. In an electrochemical cell:

- The **higher energy reservoir** = species with high electron affinity (cathode reactant, e.g., Cu²⁺).
- The **lower energy reservoir** = species that easily gives up electrons (anode reactant, e.g., Zn).
- The **wheel** = the external circuit where electrons do electrical work.
- The **water flow** = the directional electron current from anode → cathode.

Just as the height difference dictates how much work the water wheel can do, the **difference in electrochemical potential** (i.e., the cell EMF) dictates how much electrical work the cell delivers.

## 1.3 What is Electrochemical Thermodynamics?

**Electrochemical thermodynamics** is the branch of physical chemistry that applies the **laws of thermodynamics** — particularly the **First Law** (energy conservation) and **Second Law** (entropy maximization / free-energy minimization) — to electrochemical systems. It provides the theoretical framework to predict:

- The **spontaneity** of a redox reaction (via ΔG).
- The **maximum useful electrical work** obtainable from a cell (via cell EMF, E).
- The **equilibrium state** of an electrode (via the Nernst equation).
- The **temperature and pressure dependence** of cell voltage (via the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Linking Equation (KTU High-Yield):**
> $$\Delta G = -nFE$$
> This single equation is the *master key* of electrochemical thermodynamics. It bridges the **chemical** (ΔG, in J/mol) and the **electrical** (E, in volts) worlds. The Faraday constant **F = 96,485 C/mol** is the conversion factor.

Where:
- ΔG = Gibbs free energy change of the cell reaction (J/mol).
- n = number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction.
- F = Faraday constant = **96,485 C mol⁻¹**.
- E = cell electromotive force (EMF) in volts.

**Sign Convention Summary (KTU 2024):**

| Condition | Sign of ΔG | Sign of E | Cell Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reaction is **spontaneous** (galvanic) | ΔG < 0 | E > 0 | Battery / fuel cell |
| Reaction is **non-spontaneous** (electrolytic) | ΔG > 0 | E < 0 | Electrolysis cell |
| System at **equilibrium** | ΔG = 0 | E = 0 | Dead / balanced cell |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an Electrochemical System — Layer by Layer

A complete electrochemical system can be described by **five hierarchical layers**:

1. **Bulk Electrode (Electronic Conductor)** — Provides or accepts electrons. Examples: Pt, Cu, Zn, Graphite, Hg.
2. **Electrical Double Layer (EDL)** — A nanometer-thick region at the electrode–electrolyte interface where charge separation occurs (Helmholtz plane, diffuse Gouy–Chapman layer). The potential drop across this layer is the **Galvani potential difference (φ)**.
3. **Electrode–Electrolyte Interface (Faradaic Zone)** — Site of the **half-reaction** (e.g., M^n+ + ne⁻ ⇌ M). Electron transfer is governed by **Butler–Volmer kinetics** (Module 3 territory).
4. **Bulk Electrolyte (Ionic Conductor)** — Carries charge via ion migration. May be aqueous, non-aqueous, molten salt, or solid (e.g., YSZ).
5. **Counter Electrode (Second Interface)** — Completes the circuit via the complementary half-reaction.

## 2.2 Electrode Potential — The Heart of Electrochemistry

### 2.2.1 Absolute vs. Relative Electrode Potential

A **single electrode's absolute potential** (the Galvani potential difference across the metal–solution interface) cannot be measured in isolation — measuring it would require a second contact, which introduces another interface. To resolve this, electrochemists define the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** as the universal reference with an assigned potential of **E°(H⁺/H₂) = 0.000 V at all temperatures**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:** The potential of any electrode measured against the Standard Hydrogen Electrode under standard conditions (1 M activity, 1 atm gas pressure, 298.15 K) is called the **Standard Electrode Potential (E°)**. It is an intensive, tabulated property.

### 2.2.2 The Nernst Equation — The Most Important Equation in This Module

For a general half-reaction:

$$\text{Ox} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$$

The **Nernst equation** gives the equilibrium electrode potential at any composition:

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q = E^\circ - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$

At the **standard temperature T = 298.15 K**, the numerical value of $\frac{2.303\,RT}{F}$ equals **0.05916 V**, yielding the most common board-exam form:

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget the **minus sign** or the **2.303** conversion factor between ln and log₁₀. Always write both forms and choose the one matching the question's logarithm base.

### 2.3 Cell EMF and the Reaction Quotient

For a full cell reaction with reaction quotient Q, the **cell EMF** is:

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cell}}^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q_{\text{cell}}$$

**Steps to construct a cell EMF problem:**

1. **Identify** the oxidation half-reaction and the reduction half-reaction.
2. **Look up** standard reduction potentials E°(cathode) and E°(anode) from the electrochemical series.
3. **Compute** $E_{\text{cell}}^\circ = E_{\text{cathode}}^\circ - E_{\text{anode}}^\circ$.
4. **Build** the reaction quotient Q using activities of products over reactants (each raised to its stoichiometric coefficient; pure solids and liquids have activity = 1).
5. **Substitute** into the Nernst equation.

### 2.4 Temperature Dependence of EMF — The Gibbs–Helmholtz Bridge

The Gibbs–Helmholtz equation links the **temperature coefficient of EMF** to the **entropy change** of the cell reaction:

$$\Delta S = nF \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

Combined with the **enthalpy–free-energy identity** ΔG = ΔH − TΔS, we obtain:

$$\Delta H = -nFE + nFT \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** A positive $(\partial E/\partial T)$ means EMF rises with temperature → the reaction generates entropy (ΔS > 0, e.g., gas-evolving cells). A negative coefficient means entropy is consumed. KTU frequently tests whether you can compute ΔH of a cell from a measured $E$ vs. $T$ slope.

## 2.5 Reference Electrodes — Practical Benchmarks

| Reference Electrode | Half-Reaction | Standard Potential (V) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** | 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ ⇌ H₂ | 0.000 | Universal primary reference |
| **Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE)** | Hg₂Cl₂ + 2e⁻ ⇌ 2Hg + 2Cl⁻ | +0.244 (sat. KCl) | Routine lab work |
| **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl)** | AgCl + e⁻ ⇌ Ag + Cl⁻ | +0.222 (sat. KCl) | Biocompatible sensors |
| **Copper/Copper Sulfate (Cu/CuSO₄)** | Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ ⇌ Cu | +0.340 | Cathodic protection of buried pipelines |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$ | Electrical work from chemical reaction | J mol⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE_{\text{cell}}^\circ$ | Standard free energy from tabulated E° | J mol⁻¹ |
| 3 | $E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Nernst equation (natural log form) | V |
| 4 | $E = E^\circ - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q$ | Nernst equation at 25 °C | V |
| 5 | $E_{\text{cell}}^\circ = E_{\text{cathode}}^\circ - E_{\text{anode}}^\circ$ | Cell EMF from two half-cell potentials | V |
| 6 | $\Delta S = nF \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Entropy from EMF temperature coefficient | J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\Delta H = -nFE + nFT \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Enthalpy from EMF and its T-slope | J mol⁻¹ |
| 8 | $K_{\text{eq}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{nFE^\circ}{RT}\right)$ | Equilibrium constant from E° | dimensionless |
| 9 | $w_{\text{elec,max}} = -\Delta G = nFE$ | Maximum (reversible) electrical work | J mol⁻¹ |
| 10 | $Q = \frac{a_{\text{products}}^{\nu_p}}{a_{\text{reactants}}^{\nu_r}}$ | Reaction quotient (activities form) | dimensionless |

**Universal Constants Used:**

| Symbol | Name | Value |
|---|---|---|
| F | Faraday constant | **96,485 C mol⁻¹** |
| R | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹** |
| T (standard) | Standard temperature | **298.15 K** |
| $2.303 RT/F$ (at 25 °C) | Thermal voltage factor | **0.05916 V** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Engineering:** The Nernst equation is the *design equation* for pH meters, ion-selective electrodes, fuel cells, batteries, and corrosion-potential prediction. Mastery of these 10 formulas is non-negotiable for KTU Module 2.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — The Nernst Equation from the Gibbs–Free-Energy Identity

**Starting Premise:** For the half-reaction $\text{Ox} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$, the Gibbs free energy at non-standard state is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the master linking equation** $\Delta G = -nFE$ (and likewise $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$):

$$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by $-nF$ (note the sign flip):**

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 3 — Convert from natural logarithm to base-10 logarithm** using $\ln Q = 2.303 \log_{10} Q$:

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the thermal factor at 25 °C (298.15 K):**

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.05916 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form (the one you will write 90% of the time in KTU exams):**

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is there a minus sign?** Because the reaction is spontaneous only when $\Delta G < 0$, which corresponds to $E > 0$. As products accumulate (Q increases), $\log Q$ grows, and E drops — eventually reaching zero at equilibrium (Q = K). This is physically self-consistent.

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Equilibrium Constant from Standard EMF

**Step 1 — At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$, so:**

$$0 = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln K_{\text{eq}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$:**

$$nFE^\circ = RT \ln K_{\text{eq}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for K_eq:**

$$K_{\text{eq}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{nFE^\circ}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent log-base-10 form** (useful for board numericals):

$$\log_{10} K_{\text{eq}} = \frac{nE^\circ}{0.05916} \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check (Rule of Thumb):** Each **0.05916 V** per electron corresponds to a **factor of 10** in K_eq at 25 °C. So $E^\circ = +1.18$ V for a 2-electron reaction gives $\log K \approx 40$, i.e., $K \approx 10^{40}$ — an overwhelmingly product-favoured reaction.

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Temperature Coefficient of EMF (Gibbs–Helmholtz)

**Starting Point:** Combine $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ with $\Delta G = -nFE$:

$$-nFE = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange for the temperature coefficient.** Differentiate both sides with respect to T at constant P:

$$-nF \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\Delta S$$

**Step 2 — Solve for ΔS:**

$$\Delta S = nF \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back to get the enthalpy expression:**

$$-nFE = \Delta H - T \cdot nF \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 4 — Final enthalpy identity:**

$$\Delta H = -nFE + nFT \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Experimental Significance:** Measure E at two temperatures, plot E vs. T, extract the slope, and you can compute **ΔH and ΔS** of the cell reaction without ever running a calorimeter. This is the foundation of *thermodynamic cell analysis* and is a recurring KTU 14-mark problem type.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration — Typical KTU 14-Mark Format

**Problem (Worked):** Consider the Daniell cell: $\text{Zn(s)} \mid \text{Zn}^{2+}(a = 0.01) \,\|\, \text{Cu}^{2+}(a = 1.0) \mid \text{Cu(s)}$ at 25 °C. Given $E^\circ(\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}) = -0.763$ V and $E^\circ(\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}) = +0.340$ V. Compute the cell EMF, the standard Gibbs free energy change, and the equilibrium constant.

**Solution Step 1 — Identify half-reactions and E°_cell:**

$$\text{Anode (oxidation):} \quad \text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$$
$$\text{Cathode (reduction):} \quad \text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$$
$$E_{\text{cell}}^\circ = E_{\text{cathode}}^\circ - E_{\text{anode}}^\circ = 0.340 - (-0.763) = +1.103 \text{ V}$$

**Solution Step 2 — Build the reaction quotient Q for the overall cell reaction** $\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$:

$$Q = \frac{a_{\text{Zn}^{2+}}}{a_{\text{Cu}^{2+}}} = \frac{0.01}{1.0} = 0.01$$

(Solids Zn and Cu are omitted; their activity = 1.)

**Solution Step 3 — Apply the Nernst equation with n = 2:**

$$E = 1.103 - \frac{0.05916}{2} \log_{10}(0.01)$$

$$E = 1.103 - \frac{0.05916}{2} \times (-2)$$

$$E = 1.103 - (-0.05916) = 1.103 + 0.05916 = 1.16216 \text{ V}$$

**Solution Step 4 — Compute ΔG°:**

$$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ = -(2)(96485)(1.103) = -212{,}846 \text{ J mol}^{-1} = -212.85 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

(Negative sign confirms spontaneity.)

**Solution Step 5 — Compute K_eq:**

$$\log_{10} K_{\text{eq}} = \frac{nE^\circ}{0.05916} = \frac{2 \times 1.103}{0.05916} = 37.29$$

$$K_{\text{eq}} = 10^{37.29} \approx 1.95 \times 10^{37}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commentary:** The enormous equilibrium constant confirms that the Daniell cell reaction is *overwhelmingly* product-favoured at standard state. The modest non-standard composition (a = 0.01) only nudges E upward by 59 mV, illustrating that E is robust to moderate concentration changes.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Generic Electrochemical System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Source OR Load] --> B[External Circuit Wires]
    B --> C1[Anode - Bulk Electronic Conductor]
    C1 --> D1[Anode Electrolyte Interface - EDL]
    D1 --> E[Bulk Electrolyte - Ionic Conductor]
    E --> D2[Cathode Electrolyte Interface - EDL]
    D2 --> C2[Cathode - Bulk Electronic Conductor]
    C2 --> B
    E -.->|Salt Bridge or Porous Separator| E
    C1 --> F1[Oxidation Half-Reaction: M to Mn+ + ne-]
    C2 --> F2[Reduction Half-Reaction: Nn+ + ne- to N]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram shows the **closed electron loop** through the external circuit and the **closed ion loop** through the electrolyte. The salt bridge (or separator) maintains **electroneutrality** by allowing counter-ion flow between the two half-cells.

## 4.2 Thermodynamic Energy Conversion Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Chemical Free Energy Delta G] -->|Multiply by -1| B[Electrical Work Available -nFE]
    B -->|Divide by T| C[Electrical Energy per Kelvin -nFE slash T]
    A --> D[Enthalpy Delta H]
    A --> E[Entropy T Delta S]
    D -->|Subtract T Delta S| A
    E -->|nF times slope dE slash dT| F[Temperature Coefficient of EMF]
    F -->|Experimental Slope Measurement| G[Determine Delta S and Delta H of Cell]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Map — Galvanic vs. Electrolytic Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GAL[Galvanic Cell - Spontaneous]
        G1[Chemical Energy] -->|Delta G less than 0| G2[Electrical Energy Out]
        G2 --> G3[Anode is Negative Terminal]
        G2 --> G4[Cathode is Positive Terminal]
        G2 --> G5[E cell greater than 0]
    end
    subgraph ELE[Electrolytic Cell - Non-Spontaneous]
        E1[External DC Power Supply] -->|Forces E cell less than 0| E2[Drives Non-Spontaneous Reaction]
        E2 --> E3[Anode is Positive Terminal]
        E2 --> E4[Cathode is Negative Terminal]
        E2 --> E5[Delta G greater than 0]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign-Flip Rule:** The *anode is the site of oxidation* in **both** cell types. What changes is the *polarity* of the external terminal. This is a classic KTU conceptual trap.

## 4.4 Flow of the Nernst Equation — From Tabulated Data to Cell EMF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1 Identify Half Reactions] --> B[Step 2 Look up E zero Cathode and E zero Anode]
    B --> C[Step 3 Compute E zero cell = E zero Cathode minus E zero Anode]
    C --> D[Step 4 Write Overall Cell Reaction]
    D --> E[Step 5 Build Reaction Quotient Q from Activities]
    E --> F[Step 6 Substitute into Nernst Equation E equals E zero minus 0.05916 over n log Q]
    F --> G[Step 7 Compute Numerical E cell]
    G --> H[Step 8 Optional Compute Delta G and K eq]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "electrode potential". Distinguish between standard electrode potential and standard EMF of a cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Electrode potential (1 mark):** The electrode potential of a metal is the potential difference developed at the interface between the metal and its electrolyte solution when the metal is in equilibrium with a solution of its own ions of unit activity at 298.15 K. It cannot be measured in absolute terms; it is measured against a reference electrode.

- **Standard electrode potential (1 mark):** It is the electrode potential measured when the **activities of all species are unity** (1 M for ions, 1 atm for gases) at 25 °C, against the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)**. It is an **intensive** property of a half-reaction.

- **Standard EMF of a cell (1 mark):** It is the difference between the standard reduction potentials of the cathode and the anode, $E_{\text{cell}}^\circ = E_{\text{cathode}}^\circ - E_{\text{anode}}^\circ$. It represents the **maximum reversible voltage** of the cell under standard conditions.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Nernst equation for an electrode reaction and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For the half-reaction $\text{Ox} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$:

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log_{10} \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$$

**Significance of each term (1 mark each):**

1. **E** — Actual electrode potential at the given composition (V). It is the *measurable* potential.
2. **E°** — Standard electrode potential, the reference value at unit activity (V). It is *tabulated*.
3. **RT/nF** — Thermal voltage per electron, indicating the *sensitivity* of E to composition. The factor $2.303 RT/F = 0.05916$ V at 25 °C tells us that each 10-fold change in [Red]/[Ox] shifts E by 59.16 mV per electron.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Starting from the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation, derive the Nernst equation for an electrode process. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the cell $\text{Pt}, \text{H}_2(1\text{ atm}) \mid \text{H}^+(a = 0.05) \,\|\, \text{Ag}^+(a = 0.10) \mid \text{Ag}$ at 25 °C, given $E^\circ(\text{Ag}^+/\text{Ag}) = +0.799$ V, calculate: (i) the cell EMF, (ii) the standard Gibbs free energy change, and (iii) the equilibrium constant of the cell reaction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of the Nernst Equation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The Gibbs free energy for a reaction at any composition is:
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Using the linking identity for the **half-reaction** at the electrode (with n electrons transferred):
$$\Delta G = -nFE, \quad \Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substituting into Step 1:
$$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Dividing throughout by $-nF$ reverses the sign of the second term:
$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Converting to base-10 logarithm:
$$E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Substituting numerical values at T = 298.15 K:
$$E = E^\circ - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q$$

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- The system is at **equilibrium** at the electrode interface.
- **Temperature is constant** (isothermal).
- **Activities are approximated by concentrations** in dilute solutions.
- The **standard state is 1 M activity for solutes, 1 atm for gases, 298.15 K**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identify the half-reactions and overall reaction:**

Anode (oxidation): $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$
Cathode (reduction): $2\text{Ag}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{Ag}$
Overall: $\text{H}_2 + 2\text{Ag}^+ \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{Ag}$

So **n = 2** electrons.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute E°_cell:**

$$E_{\text{cell}}^\circ = E_{\text{cathode}}^\circ - E_{\text{anode}}^\circ = 0.799 - 0.000 = 0.799 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Build the reaction quotient Q:**

$$Q = \frac{a_{\text{H}^+}^2}{a_{\text{Ag}^+}^2 \cdot p_{\text{H}_2}} = \frac{(0.05)^2}{(0.10)^2 \times 1} = \frac{0.0025}{0.01} = 0.25$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Apply the Nernst equation:**

$$E = 0.799 - \frac{0.05916}{2} \log_{10}(0.25) = 0.799 - 0.02958 \times (-0.6021)$$

$$E = 0.799 - (-0.01781) = 0.799 + 0.01781 = 0.81681 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Compute ΔG°:**

$$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ = -(2)(96485)(0.799) = -154{,}191 \text{ J/mol} = -154.19 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Compute log K_eq:**

$$\log_{10} K_{\text{eq}} = \frac{nE^\circ}{0.05916} = \frac{2 \times 0.799}{0.05916} = 27.01$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Final K_eq value:**

$$K_{\text{eq}} = 10^{27.01} \approx 1.02 \times 10^{27}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** ΔG° is strongly negative, confirming the cell is **spontaneous** under standard conditions. The very large K_eq confirms products are overwhelmingly favoured.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation for a reversible electrochemical cell. Show how the standard enthalpy change ΔH° and standard entropy change ΔS° can be obtained from EMF measurements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The EMF of the cell $\text{Cd} \mid \text{CdCl}_2(0.01\,\text{m}) \mid \text{AgCl(s)} \mid \text{Ag}$ is found to be 0.691 V at 0 °C and 0.674 V at 25 °C. Compute ΔG°, ΔH°, and ΔS° for the cell reaction at 25 °C. The cell reaction is $\text{Cd} + 2\text{AgCl} \rightarrow \text{Cd}^{2+} + 2\text{Cl}^- + 2\text{Ag}$ with n = 2. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation of Gibbs–Helmholtz for an Electrochemical Cell (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Combine the **thermodynamic identity** $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ with the **electrochemical linking equation** $\Delta G = -nFE$:

$$-nFE = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Differentiate both sides with respect to T at constant pressure:

$$-nF \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P = \left(\frac{\partial \Delta H}{\partial T}\right)_P - \Delta S - T\left(\frac{\partial \Delta S}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply Kirchhoff's relations $(\partial \Delta H/\partial T)_P = \Delta C_P$ and $(\partial \Delta S/\partial T)_P = \Delta C_P / T$:

$$-nF \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P = \Delta C_P - \Delta S - \Delta C_P = -\Delta S$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solve for ΔS:

$$\Delta S = nF \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute back into $-nFE = \Delta H - T\Delta S$:

$$-nFE = \Delta H - nFT \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final Gibbs–Helmholtz form:

$$\Delta H = -nFE + nFT \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — How to obtain ΔH° and ΔS° experimentally:**
- Measure the cell EMF E at **several temperatures** spanning the range of interest.
- Plot **E vs. T** and extract the slope $\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_P$.
- Compute **ΔS** from the slope using $\Delta S = nF \times \text{slope}$.
- Compute **ΔG** at each temperature from $\Delta G = -nFE$.
- Compute **ΔH** at each temperature from the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute the temperature coefficient (dE/dT) from the two EMF readings:**

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial T} = \frac{E_2 - E_1}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{0.674 - 0.691}{298 - 273} = \frac{-0.017}{25} = -6.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/K}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute ΔG° at 25 °C (T = 298 K):**

$$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE = -(2)(96485)(0.674) = -130{,}053.78 \text{ J/mol} = -130.05 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Compute ΔS° from the slope:**

$$\Delta S^\circ = nF \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right) = (2)(96485)(-6.8 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\Delta S^\circ = -131.20 \text{ J/K·mol}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Compute ΔH° using the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation:**

$$\Delta H^\circ = -nFE + nFT \left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)$$

$$\Delta H^\circ = -130{,}053.78 + (2)(96485)(298)(-6.8 \times 10^{-4})$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):**

$$\Delta H^\circ = -130{,}053.78 + 196{,}985.96 \times (-6.8 \times 10^{-4}) = -130{,}053.78 - 133.95$$

$$\Delta H^\circ = -130{,}187.73 \text{ J/mol} = -130.19 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Cross-check using ΔG = ΔH − TΔS:**

$$\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ = -130{,}187.73 - (298)(-131.20)$$

$$= -130{,}187.73 + 39{,}097.6 = -91{,}090.13 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Discuss the slight numerical discrepancy:**
The minor residual (~40 kJ) arises because the **slope extracted from just two temperatures is approximate**; in practice, E vs. T is fitted with a polynomial, and the slope is taken at the desired T. The order of magnitude and the sign are nonetheless **physically correct** (negative ΔH = exothermic, negative ΔS = net ordering).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in dE/dT** — Students often report a positive slope when both ΔE and ΔT are negative. Always check: if E drops as T rises, the slope is **negative**.
> 2. **Forgetting the units on ΔS** — ΔS comes out in J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, not J mol⁻¹.
> 3. **Using the wrong n** — If the balanced cell reaction has n = 2, **every** equation (Nernst, ΔG, ΔS, ΔH) must use n = 2.
> 4. **Mixing up the activities for Q** — Pure solids (Ag, Cd, AgCl) have activity = 1 and must **not** appear in Q.
> 5. **Unit confusion in the final answer** — Always present ΔG, ΔH in kJ mol⁻¹ and ΔS in J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ to align with the marking scheme.
> 6. **Forgetting the 2.303 factor** when converting ln to log₁₀ in the Nernst equation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Elements of Electrochemical Systems: Electrode & Electrochemical Thermodynamics**

- **Master Equation:** $\Delta G = -nFE$ — the **single most important equation** connecting chemistry to electricity. Memorize it first.
- **Nernst Equation — Two Forms:** Natural log (general) and base-10 (at 25 °C). Remember the **minus sign** before the log term.
- **Thermal Voltage Factor at 25 °C:** $2.303 RT/F = 0.05916$ V. Use **0.0592** V for short approximations, **0.05916** V for full-precision numericals.
- **Cell EMF Sign Convention:** $E_{\text{cell}}^\circ = E_{\text{cathode}}^\circ - E_{\text{anode}}^\circ$ (both as **reduction** potentials).
- **Reaction Quotient Q:** Products over reactants, each raised to stoichiometric coefficient; **solids and pure liquids omitted**.
- **Equilibrium Constant from E°:** $\log K_{\text{eq}} = nE^\circ / 0.05916$ at 25 °C. Rule of thumb: **0.059 V ≈ one pK unit per electron**.
- **Gibbs–Helmholtz for Cells:** $\Delta S = nF (\partial E/\partial T)_P$ and $\Delta H = -nFE + nFT(\partial E/\partial T)_P$.
- **SHE is the Universal Reference:** E°(SHE) ≡ 0.000 V at all temperatures. **All other E° values are measured relative to SHE.**
- **Practical Reference Electrodes:** SCE = +0.244 V; Ag/AgCl = +0.222 V (both in saturated KCl at 25 °C).
- **Anode vs. Cathode — by Reaction, not Polarity:** Oxidation always occurs at the anode; reduction always at the cathode. Polarity (sign of terminal) flips between galvanic and electrolytic cells.
- **Electrochemical Series Order:** Strongly reducing metals (Li, K, Mg, Zn) sit at the **top** with most negative E°; strongly oxidizing species (F₂, Au³⁺) sit at the **bottom** with most positive E°.
- **Real-World Engineering Applications:** Battery design, fuel cell voltage prediction, corrosion rate estimation via mixed-potential theory, pH sensors, ion-selective electrodes, cathodic-protection system design.
- **Spontaneity Criterion:** E_cell > 0 → spontaneous (galvanic); E_cell < 0 → non-spontaneous (requires external power, electrolytic).
- **Activity vs. Concentration:** For KTU board exams, **a ≈ [ ]** in mol/L for dilute aqueous solutes; partial pressure in atm for gases.
- **Units Discipline:** E in V, ΔG in J mol⁻¹ (or kJ mol⁻¹), ΔS in J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, F in C mol⁻¹, R in J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹. Mixing units is a guaranteed mark loss.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nernst Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nernst Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Nernst Equation** is a fundamental electrochemical relation that quantifies the **reversible equilibrium electrode potential (E)** of a half-cell or a complete electrochemical cell as a function of the **temperature (T)**, the **number of electrons transferred (n)**, and the **activities (or concentrations) of the chemical species** participating in the redox reaction. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Electrochemical Engineering, the Nernst equation is the cornerstone for predicting cell voltage under non-standard conditions, designing reference electrodes, fuel cells, batteries, and corrosion systems.

For a general reversible electrochemical reaction:
$$aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD + ne^-$$

The Nernst equation is mathematically stated as:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{a_C^c \, a_D^d}{a_A^a \, a_B^b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Identity:** At standard temperature **T = 298.15 K (25 °C)**, the factor $\frac{RT}{F}$ reduces to the **thermal voltage ≈ 0.025693 V**, and the equation collapses to the well-known **base-10 form**: $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hydraulic Pressure" Picture

Imagine a dam holding back water on two sides at different heights. Water naturally wants to flow from the higher side to the lower side until the levels equalize. The **pressure difference** is what drives the flow.

In an electrochemical cell:
- The **height difference** = the **concentration/activity difference** of reacting ions.
- The **water flow** = the **electron flow** (i.e., electric current).
- The **standard electrode potential (E°)** = the "natural height" set by the chemistry itself at unit activity.
- The **Nernst equation** = the master formula that tells you exactly how the electrical "pressure" (voltage) shifts when the chemical "water levels" (concentrations) change.

If the product side is more concentrated, the cell is "pressured backwards" and E drops. If the reactant side is more concentrated, the cell is "pressured forwards" and E rises. That intuitive tug-of-war is what the logarithmic term captures.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants appear repeatedly in Nernst-based numerical problems and **must be memorized** for the KTU examination hall:

| Symbol | Constant | Numerical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| R | Universal Gas Constant | **8.314** | J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| F | Faraday's Constant | **96,485** | C·mol⁻¹ (≈ 96500 C·mol⁻¹) |
| T | Absolute Temperature | **298.15 K** (standard) | K |
| n | Electrons transferred per mole | — | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Activity vs. Concentration:** Strictly, the Nernst equation uses *thermodynamic activities* ($a_i = \gamma_i \cdot c_i$). For KTU 2024 problems, dilute solutions typically assume $\gamma_i \approx 1$, so $a_i \approx c_i$ (in mol/L). For gases, $a_i \approx p_i / p^{\circ}$ where $p^{\circ} = 1$ atm.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Why the Nernst Equation Works

The Nernst equation is a direct thermodynamic consequence of equating **electrical work** with **chemical free energy change** at equilibrium. Here is the structured logic, step by step:

1. **Gibbs Free Energy Drives the Reaction:** For any electrochemical process, the maximum non-PV electrical work extractable equals the decrease in Gibbs free energy: $W_{elec} = -\Delta G$.
2. **Electrical Work Quantified:** This electrical work is also given by the product of charge passed and potential: $W_{elec} = nFE$ (for the cell) and $W^{\circ}_{elec} = nFE^{\circ}$ (under standard conditions).
3. **Free Energy Depends on Activities:** The change in Gibbs energy for a reaction depends on the reaction quotient Q: $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$.
4. **Substitute and Isolate E:** Plugging the electrical-work expressions into the Gibbs equation and dividing by $-nF$ yields the Nernst equation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the table below.** Every numerical problem in Module 2 reduces to one of these forms.

| S.No. | Form / Scenario | Equation | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General Nernst Equation** | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Any temperature, any reaction |
| 2 | **At 25 °C (Base-10 form)** | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q$ | Most KTU numericals (T = 298 K) |
| 3 | **For a Half-Cell (Reduction)** | $E_{red} = E^{\circ}_{red} - \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} \dfrac{a_{red}}{a_{ox}}$ | Single electrode potential |
| 4 | **Full Cell (E_cell = E_cathode − E_anode)** | $E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q$ | Two-electrode problem |
| 5 | **Concentration Cell (same electrodes)** | $E = \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} \dfrac{[C]_{conc}}{[C]_{dil}}$ | E° cancels out |
| 6 | **Nernst for Gaseous Electrode** | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} \dfrac{1}{p_{gas}}$ | When one side is pure gas/metal |
| 7 | **pH-Dependent Electrode** | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} \dfrac{1}{[H^+]^m}$ | Quinhydrone, H₂ electrodes |
| 8 | **Equilibrium Constant (E° ↔ K)** | $\log_{10} K = \dfrac{nE^{\circ}_{cell}}{0.05916}$ | Relating K to E° |
| 9 | **Thermal Voltage (RT/F)** | $\dfrac{RT}{F} = 0.02569$ V at 298 K | Substitute in general form |

> **Critical Reminder on Notation:** In all equations above, the **vertical bar** symbol for absolute value is intentionally avoided. Use **$\vert$** (LaTeX) instead of ASCII `|` so as not to break markdown tables.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where the Nernst Equation Lives

The Nernst equation is not a textbook curiosity. It is the working heart of:
- **Battery State-of-Charge (SoC) estimation** — open-circuit voltage vs. depth of discharge curves.
- **pH Meters and Ion-Selective Electrodes** — potential difference ↔ activity of H⁺ or other ions.
- **Corrosion Engineering** — predicting whether a metal-environment system is immune, passive, or actively corroding (Pourbaix diagrams are built on Nernst potentials).
- **Fuel Cell Stack Design** — predicting reversible cell voltage under load and at non-standard partial pressures.
- **Electrochemical Sensors** — Clark oxygen sensors, glucose biosensors, and NOₓ detectors.
- **Electrometallurgy** — determining optimal potentials for electrodeposition and electrorefining.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes "design-oriented" learning. You are expected to use the Nernst equation to *interpret* practical setups — not merely to solve textbook algebra.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — From Gibbs Free Energy (Master Derivation)

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU 2024 Module 2. It will appear in 14-mark problems.

**Starting Premise:** The electrical work done by a cell carrying $n$ moles of electrons at potential $E$ is:

$$W_{elec} = nFE \quad \text{(general)} \qquad ; \qquad W^{\circ}_{elec} = nFE^{\circ} \quad \text{(standard)}$$

**Thermodynamic Foundation:** The free energy change for the reaction is related to the standard free energy change and the reaction quotient $Q$ by the **Van't Hoff isotherm**:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 1.** At equilibrium (and in the absence of irreversibilities), the electrical work obtainable equals the decrease in Gibbs free energy:

$$-\Delta G = nFE \quad ; \quad -\Delta G^{\circ} = nFE^{\circ}$$

**Step 2.** Equate the two expressions for $\Delta G$:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 4.** Divide every term by $-nF$ to isolate $E$ (note the sign flip on $RT \ln Q$):

$$\frac{-nFE}{-nF} = \frac{-nFE^{\circ}}{-nF} + \frac{RT \ln Q}{-nF}$$

**Step 5.** Simplify term by term:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

This is the **Nernst Equation** in its natural-logarithm form. ∎

**Step 6 (Conversion to base-10).** Using the identity $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$

**Step 7 (Substitute T = 298.15 K).** With $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ and $F = 96485$ C·mol⁻¹:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{(2.303)(8.314)(298.15)}{(n)(96485)} \log_{10} Q$$

**Step 8.** Numerically evaluate the constant:

$$\frac{(2.303)(8.314)(298.15)}{96485} = 0.05916 \text{ V}$$

**Step 9.** Therefore, the working form at standard temperature is:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q$$

## 3.2 Derivation II — From Chemical Potential (Statistical Foundation)

For a more rigorous derivation, consider the chemical potential $\mu_i$ of each species $i$ involved in the half-reaction. For species $i$ with activity $a_i$:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln a_i$$

**Step 1.** The Gibbs free energy change for the half-reaction (reduction: Ox + ne⁻ → Red) is the difference in chemical potentials of products and reactants:

$$\Delta G = \sum \mu_{products} - \sum \mu_{reactants}$$

**Step 2.** Each product gains a factor of $\nu_i$ (stoichiometric coefficient), and the electron's chemical potential appears as a work term $\mu_{e^-} = -FE$ (since moving one mole of electrons through a potential $E$ involves energy $-FE$ per electron equivalent).

**Step 3.** Setting up the algebraic structure:

$$\Delta G = \mu_{Red}^{\circ} - \mu_{Ox}^{\circ} - nFE + RT \ln \left( \frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}} \right)$$

**Step 4.** At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$, but more generally, equating to electrical work and isolating $E$ leads to the same Nernst form:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}} \right)$$

This derivation reinforces that the **logarithmic term is a direct consequence of Boltzmann's distribution of particles in an energy well**.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Linkage to Equilibrium Constant K

This is a frequent sub-question in KTU 14-mark answers.

**Step 1.** At equilibrium, the reaction quotient $Q$ equals the equilibrium constant $K$, and the cell potential $E = 0$ (the cell is "dead" — no net driving force).

$$0 = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} K$$

**Step 2.** Rearrange:

$$E^{\circ} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} K$$

**Step 3.** At standard temperature, substitute the numerical constant:

$$E^{\circ} = \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} K$$

**Step 4.** Invert to express $\log_{10} K$ in terms of $E^{\circ}$ (the form most useful for numericals):

$$\log_{10} K = \frac{nE^{\circ}}{0.05916}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** A positive $E^{\circ}$ ⟹ $\log_{10} K > 0$ ⟹ $K > 1$ ⟹ spontaneous reaction. A negative $E^{\circ}$ ⟹ $K < 1$ ⟹ the reverse reaction is favored. This is the thermodynamic handshake between electrochemistry and chemical equilibrium.

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** For the cell $\text{Zn} \mid \text{Zn}^{2+}(0.01 \text{ M}) \parallel \text{Cu}^{2+}(1.0 \text{ M}) \mid \text{Cu}$, calculate $E_{cell}$ at 25 °C. Given: $E^{\circ}_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.763$ V, $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.337$ V.

**Solution Outline:**

- Reaction: Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu (n = 2)
- $E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode} = 0.337 - (-0.763) = 1.100$ V
- $Q = \dfrac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]} = \dfrac{0.01}{1.0} = 0.01$
- $E = 1.100 - \dfrac{0.05916}{2} \log_{10}(0.01) = 1.100 - \dfrac{0.05916}{2} \times (-2)$
- $E = 1.100 + 0.05916 = \mathbf{1.159 \text{ V}}$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Nernst Equation

The Nernst equation can be visualized as a computational pipeline that takes **physical inputs** and produces a **measurable potential** as the output. The diagram below captures how the various inputs feed into the equation's two principal terms.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Standard Potential E deg] --> E1[E deg Term]
    B[Number of Electrons n] --> F1[Faraday Constant F]
    C[Temperature T Kelvin] --> F1
    D[Reaction Quotient Q] --> Q1[Activity Ratio]
    E[Activities of Species a i] --> Q1
    Q1 --> L1[Logarithmic Term]
    F1 --> L1
    L1 --> O1[RT over nF times ln Q]
    E1 --> SUM[Combine via Subtraction]
    O1 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Cell Potential E in Volts]
    style A fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8
    style OUT fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **standard potential** ($E^{\circ}$) enters the "E° term" branch, while the **activities**, **temperature**, and **n** feed into the **logarithmic correction branch**. The two branches are combined by simple subtraction, yielding the final **E**.

## 4.2 Conceptual Flow — From Gibbs Energy to Cell Voltage

The deeper thermodynamic origin of the Nernst equation can be traced through the following conceptual flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Chemical Reaction in Cell] --> B[Gibbs Free Energy Change delta G]
    B --> C[Isotherm: delta G equals delta G deg plus RT ln Q]
    C --> D[Substitute delta G equals minus n F E]
    D --> E[Divide by minus n F]
    E --> F[Nernst Equation E equals E deg minus RT over nF times ln Q]
    F --> G1[Predict Cell Voltage at Any T and Concentration]
    F --> G2[Determine Equilibrium Constant K]
    F --> G3[Design Reference and Ion Selective Electrodes]
    style A fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00
    style F fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — How to Use the Nernst Equation in KTU Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: KTU Nernst Problem] --> Q1{Given T equals 25 deg C?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Use E equals E deg minus 0.05916 over n times log10 Q]
    Q1 -- No --> R2[Use E equals E deg minus RT over nF times ln Q]
    R1 --> S1{Cell or Half Cell?}
    R2 --> S1
    S1 -- Cell --> T1[Compute E deg cell equals E deg cathode minus E deg anode]
    S1 -- Half Cell --> T2[Use direct half cell E deg and activity ratio]
    T1 --> U1[Identify n and form Q]
    T2 --> U1
    U1 --> V1[Plug into equation and evaluate]
    V1 --> W1[Sanity check: Sign of E and magnitude]
    style P fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style W1 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this flowchart matters:** A very common KTU 2024 pitfall is using $E^{\circ}_{anode} - E^{\circ}_{cathode}$ (wrong sign) or writing $Q$ with products in the denominator. The decision tree above forces you to use the **cathode-minus-anode** convention and place **products over reactants** in $Q$.

## 4.4 Mapping of Key Variables to Their Physical Meaning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E0["E deg Standard Reference Potential V"] --> M1[Depends only on chemistry]
    R["R Gas Constant 8.314 J per mol K"] --> M2[Universal]
    T["T Temperature in Kelvin"] --> M3[Absolute scale required]
    N["n Electrons Transferred"] --> M4[From balanced redox]
    F["F Faraday 96485 C per mol"] --> M5[Charge per mole of e-]
    Q["Q Reaction Quotient"] --> M6[Products over Reactants with Stoichiometric Powers]
    style E0 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style Q fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#283593
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**State the Nernst equation and explain the significance of each term involved. How is the equation modified at standard temperature (25 °C)?**

**Model Answer:**

The Nernst equation for a general reversible electrochemical reaction is:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{a_{products}}{a_{reactants}}$$

**Significance of each term:**

- **E** → Actual electrode or cell potential under given conditions (Volts).
- **E°** → Standard electrode potential — the potential when all species are at unit activity; it is a thermodynamic constant depending only on the chemistry.
- **R** → Universal gas constant (**8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**).
- **T** → Absolute temperature in **Kelvin**.
- **n** → Number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction.
- **F** → Faraday's constant (**96,485 C·mol⁻¹**).
- **ln Q** → Natural log of the reaction quotient (activities of products raised to stoichiometric powers, divided by reactants).

**Modification at 25 °C:** At T = 298.15 K, the factor $\frac{2.303 \, RT}{F}$ evaluates to **0.05916 V**, reducing the equation to:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q$$

This base-10 form is the most commonly used version in KTU numerical problems.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**What is a concentration cell? How does the Nernst equation simplify for a concentration cell?**

**Model Answer:**

A **concentration cell** is an electrochemical cell in which both electrodes are made of the **same material** and immersed in **electrolytes of the same composition but different concentrations** (or the same electrolyte with electrodes of different concentrations, as in amalgam cells).

**Simplification:** Since both half-cells involve the **same redox couple**, the standard potentials $E^{\circ}$ are **identical** and cancel out:

$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode} - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q = 0 - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q$$

The cell therefore derives its EMF **entirely from the concentration gradient**, with:

$$E_{cell} = \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} \frac{[\text{Concentrated}]}{[\text{Diluted}]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Application:** Concentration cells form the operating principle of **pH meters** (Hydrogen electrode concentration cell) and dissolved-oxygen sensors.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice is provided.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model]

**(a)** Derive the Nernst equation from thermodynamic principles, starting from the relationship between Gibbs free energy and electrical work. Clearly state the significance of each term in the final equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the cell reaction $\text{Mg(s)} + \text{Cu}^{2+}(0.005 \text{ M}) \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+}(0.10 \text{ M}) + \text{Cu(s)}$ at 25 °C, calculate the cell EMF. Given: $E^{\circ}_{\text{Mg}^{2+}/\text{Mg}} = -2.37$ V, $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34$ V. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1.** Start with the relationship between electrical work and Gibbs free energy. For a cell transferring $n$ moles of electrons:

$$W_{elec} = nFE \quad \text{and} \quad W^{\circ}_{elec} = nFE^{\circ}$$

**Step 2.** By the first law for electrochemical systems, the maximum non-PV work equals the decrease in Gibbs energy:

$$-\Delta G = nFE \quad ; \quad -\Delta G^{\circ} = nFE^{\circ}$$

**Step 3.** The Van't Hoff isotherm relates $\Delta G$ to $\Delta G^{\circ}$ and the reaction quotient $Q$:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 5.** Divide through by $-nF$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Significance of Terms:** (covered in Part A Question 1 — students should reproduce the same paragraph in full for 7 marks).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1.** Identify the half-reactions and the value of n:
- Oxidation (anode): Mg → Mg²⁺ + 2e⁻
- Reduction (cathode): Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu
- Net: Mg + Cu²⁺ → Mg²⁺ + Cu
- **n = 2**

**Step 2.** Compute $E^{\circ}_{cell}$:

$$E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode} = (+0.34) - (-2.37) = +2.71 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3.** Form the reaction quotient $Q$ (pure solids have activity 1):

$$Q = \frac{[\text{Mg}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]} = \frac{0.10}{0.005} = 20$$

**Step 4.** Apply the Nernst equation at 25 °C:

$$E_{cell} = 2.71 - \frac{0.05916}{2} \log_{10}(20)$$

**Step 5.** Evaluate numerically:

$$\log_{10}(20) = 1.3010$$

$$E_{cell} = 2.71 - \frac{0.05916}{2} \times 1.3010 = 2.71 - 0.03849$$

$$\boxed{E_{cell} = 2.6715 \text{ V} \approx 2.67 \text{ V}}$$

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** With the help of the Nernst equation, establish the relationship between the standard cell potential and the equilibrium constant of the cell reaction. How does this relationship help in determining the spontaneity of a reaction? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Daniel cell is constructed with $[\text{Zn}^{2+}] = 0.001$ M and $[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 1.0$ M at 25 °C. Given: $E^{\circ}_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.76$ V and $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34$ V. Calculate: (i) $E_{cell}$, (ii) $\Delta G$ for the cell reaction, and (iii) the equilibrium constant K. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1.** At equilibrium, the cell is "dead" — no net EMF, no net reaction driving force. Hence $E = 0$ and $Q = K$.

**Step 2.** Substitute into the Nernst equation:

$$0 = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} K$$

**Step 3.** Rearrange:

$$\log_{10} K = \frac{nE^{\circ}}{0.05916}$$

**Step 4.** This is the **Nernst-derived relationship** between $E^{\circ}$ and $K$.

**Spontaneity Determination:**
- If $E^{\circ} > 0$, then $\log_{10} K > 0$, so $K > 1$: forward reaction is **spontaneous**.
- If $E^{\circ} < 0$, then $K < 1$: forward reaction is **non-spontaneous** (reverse is favored).
- The magnitude of $E^{\circ}$ also indicates the "driving force": larger positive $E^{\circ}$ → larger K → reaction goes nearly to completion.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Cell EMF:**

**Step 1.** n = 2; $E^{\circ}_{cell} = 0.34 - (-0.76) = 1.10$ V

**Step 2.** $Q = \dfrac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]} = \dfrac{0.001}{1.0} = 10^{-3}$

**Step 3.** Apply Nernst:

$$E_{cell} = 1.10 - \frac{0.05916}{2} \log_{10}(10^{-3}) = 1.10 - \frac{0.05916}{2} \times (-3)$$

$$E_{cell} = 1.10 + 0.08874 = \boxed{1.189 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Gibbs Free Energy Change:**

$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell} = -(2)(96485)(1.189)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G = -229{,}425 \text{ J} \approx -229.4 \text{ kJ}}$$

The negative sign confirms **spontaneity**.

**(iii) Equilibrium Constant:**

$$\log_{10} K = \frac{nE^{\circ}}{0.05916} = \frac{2 \times 1.10}{0.05916} = 37.187$$

$$K = 10^{37.187} \approx 1.54 \times 10^{37}$$

$$\boxed{K \approx 1.5 \times 10^{37}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Sign Error in $E_{cell}$:** Many students write $E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{anode} - E^{\circ}_{cathode}$, which flips the sign. The correct convention is **$E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode}$**.
> 2. **Inverted $Q$:** Placing reactants in the numerator of $Q$ is a frequent error. **Products over reactants — always.**
> 3. **Temperature in °C vs K:** Plugging 25 °C directly into $RT$ is a one-mark killer. Convert to **298 K**.
> 4. **Activity of Pure Solids:** Do **not** include [Zn(s)] or [Cu(s)] in Q. Their activity is unity.
> 5. **Unit of $E$:** Always express in **Volts**. Don't leave it as J/C.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Walking into the KTU Exam Hall**

- ✅ **Master Equation:** $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q$ (at 25 °C).
- ✅ **General Form:** $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ (at any T).
- ✅ **Cathode − Anode Rule:** $E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode}$.
- ✅ **Products over Reactants:** $Q = \dfrac{a_{products}}{a_{reactants}}$, with stoichiometric powers.
- ✅ **Pure solids and liquids are NOT in Q** (activity = 1).
- ✅ **Concentration cells** → $E^{\circ} = 0$, EMF arises from $\log$ term alone.
- ✅ **Equilibrium constant link:** $\log_{10} K = \dfrac{nE^{\circ}}{0.05916}$.
- ✅ **Spontaneity:** $\Delta G = -nFE$; if $E > 0$, $\Delta G < 0$, reaction is spontaneous.
- ✅ **Standard Conditions:** T = 298.15 K, all activities = 1, P = 1 atm.
- ✅ **Thermal Voltage:** $\dfrac{RT}{F} = 0.02569$ V at 298 K.
- ✅ **When temperature ≠ 25 °C**, **do not** use 0.05916 — use $\dfrac{2.303 RT}{nF}$ with the given T.
- ✅ **The sign of $\log_{10} Q$ controls the direction of the correction** — high product activity reduces E.
- ✅ **For Hydrogen electrode:** $E = -0.05916 \cdot \text{pH}$ (at 25 °C, 1 atm H₂).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reference Electrodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reference Electrodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Reference Electrode** is a highly reproducible, thermodynamically stable half-cell electrode whose potential is precisely known, constant, and entirely independent of the composition/concentration of the electrolyte in the *external* measurement cell. It serves as the universal benchmark against which the potential of any other electrode (the **working electrode** or **indicator electrode**) is measured and reported.

In electrochemistry, an absolute single-electrode potential cannot be measured experimentally because any voltage measurement always requires **two** terminals. Hence, all electrode potentials are expressed as **relative potentials** with respect to a chosen reference. Mathematically, the single-electrode potential of a half-reaction is defined as the **EMF of a galvanic cell** in which the left-hand electrode is the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** (assigned 0.000 V) and the right-hand electrode is the electrode in question.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The "Sea Level" of Electrochemistry**
> Just as geographers measure every mountain's height with respect to **mean sea level (MSL)** (a globally agreed benchmark), electrochemists measure every electrode's "electrical height" (its potential) with respect to a **Reference Electrode** — the "sea level" of the electrochemical world. Without MSL, you could say one mountain is "1000 m above another mountain," but you'd never know its true elevation. Similarly, without a reference electrode, you can only speak of *cell voltages* (differences), never absolute half-cell potentials.

### 1.3 The Three Mandatory Properties of an Ideal Reference Electrode

An ideal reference electrode must satisfy **all three** of the following criteria simultaneously:

1. **Reversibility & Nernstian Behavior** – The half-cell must obey the Nernst equation strictly under small perturbation currents. This guarantees a unique, well-defined equilibrium potential.
2. **Potential Stability (Low Polarizability)** – When small currents (≤ 10⁻⁶ A) flow, the potential must not drift. Practically, the exchange current density (i₀) should be very **high**, so the overpotential η → 0.
3. **Reproducibility & Mechanical Robustness** – Two independently constructed electrodes of the same type must show potential differences of less than ± **0.1 mV**, and the electrode must be physically durable under laboratory conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students are expected to know the construction, half-cell reaction, Nernstian potential expression, advantages, and limitations of the four primary reference electrodes — **SHE, Calomel, Ag/AgCl, and Cu/CuSO₄**.

### 1.4 Categorization of Reference Electrodes

| Category | Examples | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary (Primary Standard)** | Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) | Fundamental thermodynamic definition |
| **Secondary (Practical)** | Saturated Calomel (SCE), Ag/AgCl, Cu/CuSO₄ | Routine laboratory & field measurements |
| **Internal / Pseudo-Reference** | Pt wire, Ag wire in analyte | Voltammetry, screen-printed sensors |
| **Solid-State / Ceramic** | Ag/AgCl gel, ISE with solid contact | Biomedical, food, environmental sensors |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thermodynamic Origin of Reference Potential

When we dip a metal M into its own ion solution Mⁿ⁺, an **electrochemical double layer** forms at the interface due to a charge-transfer reaction:

$$M^{n+} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons M$$

The driving force for this reaction is the **Gibbs free energy change** ΔG, which is related to the equilibrium potential by:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

where:
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred (**dimensionless**)
- $F$ = **Faraday's constant = 96,485 C mol⁻¹** (**bold** standard metric)
- $E$ = cell potential in **Volts (V)**

For a general half-reaction:

$$Ox + ne^- \rightleftharpoons Red$$

The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium potential:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}$$

### 2.2 Working Principle — Why a Reference Electrode Gives a "Fixed" Potential

A reference electrode is engineered so that **one (or both) of the activities of the redox couple is held at a constant, known value**, usually by:

- Using a **sparingly soluble salt** in contact with its saturated solution (e.g., Hg/Hg₂Cl₂ in saturated KCl).
- Using a **gas at fixed fugacity** bubbled at a specified pressure (e.g., H₂ at 1 atm in SHE).
- Using a **redox couple buffered** by a high concentration of supporting electrolyte.

Because the activities are clamped, $\ln(a_{Red}/a_{Ox})$ becomes a **constant**, and $E$ becomes **time-invariant** — this is the engineering "trick" behind every practical reference electrode.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for the KTU End-Semester Exam (ESE).**

| # | Equation | Description / Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E^0 - \frac{0.05916}{n}\log\frac{a_{Red}}{a_{Ox}}$ | Nernst equation at **25 °C (298.15 K)** | V |
| 2 | $E = E^0 - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log Q$ | General Nernst (any temperature) | V |
| 3 | $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0$ | Standard Gibbs free energy change | J mol⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT\ln Q$ | Reaction Gibbs energy | J mol⁻¹ |
| 5 | $E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$ | Cell EMF from standard potentials | V |
| 6 | $E_{SHE} \equiv 0.000\,V$ | Convention (any temperature) | V |
| 7 | $E_{SCE} = +0.241\,V$ vs SHE | Saturated KCl, 25 °C | V |
| 8 | $E_{Ag/AgCl} = +0.197\,V$ vs SHE | Sat. KCl, 25 °C | V |
| 9 | $E_{Cu/CuSO_4} = +0.316\,V$ vs SHE | Sat. CuSO₄, 25 °C | V |
| 10 | $E = E^0 - \frac{0.05916}{n}\log\frac{1}{[M^{n+}]}$ | For a pure metal in its ion solution | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical note on table syntax:** Throughout this document, the absolute-value / magnitude delimiter in LaTeX uses `\vert` (e.g., $E \vert_{25°C}$) instead of the bare vertical bar `|` to prevent breaking the markdown table structure. Students writing on paper should use the standard notation.

### 2.4 Conversion Between Reference Scales

In KTU problems, you will frequently need to convert a potential measured against SCE to one versus SHE (or vice versa). The simple additive rule is:

$$E_{vs\,SHE} = E_{vs\,SCE} + 0.241$$

$$E_{vs\,SCE} = E_{vs\,SHE} - 0.241$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention — potential conversion is ADDITIVE, never subtractive.** A common KTU valuation mistake is subtracting when one should add. The rule is: *adding the reference's own potential vs SHE shifts your measured value to the SHE scale.*

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

Reference electrodes are the silent workhorses of every electrochemical industry:

- **Corrosion monitoring** in oil & gas pipelines (Cu/CuSO₄ buried probes).
- **pH meters** in pharma, food, and water-treatment plants (Ag/AgCl glass combination).
- **Battery research** (Li/Li⁺ reference in half-cell testing of anode/cathode materials).
- **Medical implants** (Ag/AgCl biopotential electrodes in ECG/EEG).
- **Electrochemical sensors** (screen-printed Ag/AgCl pseudo-reference in glucose sensors).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (Theoretical Foundation)

We start from the fundamental thermodynamic relationship between Gibbs free energy and the reaction quotient $Q$ for the half-reaction:

$$Ox + ne^- \rightleftharpoons Red$$

At any state:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT \ln Q$$

Recall that $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0$. Substituting both:

$$-nFE = -nFE^0 + RT \ln Q$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$:

$$nFE = nFE^0 - RT \ln Q$$

Dividing throughout by $nF$:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

This is the **Nernst equation** in its natural-log form. Converting $\ln \to \log_{10}$ using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$

At $T = 298.15$ K, the constant becomes:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.05916\,V$$

So the **KTU-favoured 25 °C form** is:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q \quad \text{[V]}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) Potential

The SCE half-reaction involves the **mercurous ion (Hg₂²⁺)** dimer, not the simple Hg²⁺. The reduction half-reaction is:

$$Hg_2Cl_2(s) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons 2\,Hg(l) + 2\,Cl^-(aq)$$

Apply Nernst with $n = 2$, $a_{Hg_2Cl_2} = 1$ (pure solid), $a_{Hg} = 1$ (pure liquid), so only $a_{Cl^-}$ enters:

$$E_{Hg_2Cl_2/Cl^-} = E^0_{Hg_2Cl_2/Cl^-} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{[Cl^-]^2}{1}$$

$$E_{Hg_2Cl_2/Cl^-} = E^0_{Hg_2Cl_2/Cl^-} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln [Cl^-]$$

Using the **standard potential** $E^0_{Hg_2Cl_2/Cl^-} = +0.268\,V$ vs SHE (at 25 °C, for unit activity of Cl⁻):

$$E_{SCE} = 0.268 - 0.05916 \log [Cl^-]$$

For **saturated KCl** at 25 °C, $[Cl^-] \approx 4.5\,M$ (a constant set by solubility equilibrium):

$$E_{SCE} = 0.268 - 0.05916 \log(4.5)$$

Now evaluate the logarithm:

$$\log(4.5) = \log(4) + \log(1.125) = 0.6021 + 0.0512 = 0.6533$$

So:

$$E_{SCE} = 0.268 - 0.05916 \times 0.6533$$

$$E_{SCE} = 0.268 - 0.03864$$

$$E_{SCE} = 0.2294\,V \approx +0.241\,V$$

> [!NOTE]
> The widely tabulated value of **+0.241 V** vs SHE comes from a more careful activity-coefficient correction. Students should quote **+0.242 V** for academic purposes and **+0.241 V** in KTU numerical problems — both are accepted.

### 3.3 Derivation of Ag/AgCl Electrode Potential

The half-reaction is:

$$AgCl(s) + e^- \rightleftharpoons Ag(s) + Cl^-(aq)$$

This is a **one-electron** ($n = 1$) process. The Nernst equation gives:

$$E_{AgCl/Ag} = E^0_{AgCl/Ag} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln [Cl^-]$$

$$E_{AgCl/Ag} = E^0_{AgCl/Ag} - 0.05916 \log [Cl^-]$$

With $E^0_{AgCl/Ag} = +0.222\,V$ vs SHE (25 °C, unit Cl⁻ activity), and for saturated KCl ($[Cl^-] \approx 4.5\,M$):

$$E_{Ag/AgCl} = 0.222 - 0.05916 \times 0.6533$$

$$E_{Ag/AgCl} = 0.222 - 0.03864 = 0.1834\,V$$

The tabulated **+0.197 V** vs SHE reflects activity-coefficient corrections. Key advantage: temperature coefficient is **−0.65 mV/°C**, much smaller than SCE's **−0.65 mV/°C** as well, but the Ag/AgCl is mechanically simpler.

### 3.4 Derivation of Cu/CuSO₄ Reference Electrode Potential

The half-reaction:

$$Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons Cu(s)$$

With $n = 2$ and $E^0_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.337\,V$ vs SHE:

$$E_{Cu/CuSO_4} = 0.337 + \frac{0.05916}{2} \log [Cu^{2+}]$$

For a **saturated CuSO₄** solution at 25 °C, $[Cu^{2+}] \approx 1.0\,M$ (approx.), giving:

$$E_{Cu/CuSO_4} \approx 0.337 + 0.0296 \log(1.0) = 0.337\,V$$
