# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Gibbs Free Energy to Cell EMF

**Starting Premise:** The maximum non-PV (pressure–volume) work obtainable from a chemical reaction at constant $T$ and $P$ equals $-\Delta G$.

**Step 1:** For a redox reaction transferring $n$ moles of electrons per mole of reaction:

$$W_{max,elec} = -nFE_{cell}$$

**Step 2:** By the First Law of Thermodynamics, the free energy change of the cell reaction is:

$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting standard-state values ($\Delta G^{\circ}$, $E^{\circ}$):

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

**Step 4:** Using $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ (equilibrium constant), we get:

$$\begin{aligned}
-nFE^{\circ} &= -RT \ln K_{eq} \\
E^{\circ} &= \frac{RT}{nF} \ln K_{eq}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The standard cell EMF is logarithmically related to the equilibrium constant. A positive $E^{\circ}$ implies $K_{eq} > 1$ (product-favored), confirming spontaneous discharge.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Nernst Equation from Chemical Potential

**Starting Premise:** Electrochemical potential of species $i$ is $\tilde{\mu}_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln a_i + z_i F \phi$.

**Step 1:** For the half-reaction $aA + ne^- \rightarrow bB$, at equilibrium:

$$\sum_i \nu_i \tilde{\mu}_i = 0$$

**Step 2:** Explicitly expanding:

$$a(\mu_A^{\circ} + RT\ln a_A) + n(\mu_{e^-}^{\circ} + RT\ln a_{e^-} - F\phi_M) = b(\mu_B^{\circ} + RT\ln a_B) - bF\phi_{sol}$$

*(Convention: solid phases have unit activity, $\phi_M$ = metal, $\phi_{sol}$ = solution potential)*

**Step 3:** Collecting potential terms:

$$(b\phi_{sol} - n\phi_M)F = a\mu_A^{\circ} + n\mu_{e^-}^{\circ} - b\mu_B^{\circ} + RT(a\ln a_A + n\ln a_{e^-} - b\ln a_B)$$

**Step 4:** The electrode potential $E = \phi_M - \phi_{sol}$ combines with the standard electrode potential $E^{\circ}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{a_B^b}{a_A^a}\right) \\
E &= E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** At $T = 298.15$ K, substituting constants and converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$:

$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} \approx 0.02569 \text{ V}$$

Multiplying by $2.303$ for log-base-10 conversion:

$$\frac{2.303 RT}{F} = 0.05916 \text{ V at 25 °C}$$

Therefore:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.05916}{n}\log_{10} Q \quad (\text{V})$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Butler–Volmer Equation

**Starting Premise:** The rate of an electrochemical reaction follows an Arrhenius-type dependence on the activation energy barrier, which is asymmetrically modified by applied potential.

**Step 1:** Anodic (oxidation) current density at electrode surface:

$$j_a = j_0 \exp\left(+\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Cathodic (reduction) current density at electrode surface:

$$j_c = j_0 \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Net current density (taking cathodic as negative):

$$j_{net} = j_a - j_c = j_0\left[\exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4:** At equilibrium ($\eta = 0$), $j_{net} = 0$ and $j_a = j_c = j_0$ — defining the **exchange current density**.

**Step 5:** Low-overpotential linearization using Taylor expansion $\exp(x) \approx 1 + x$ for $|x| \ll 1$:

$$j_{net} \approx j_0 \left[\left(1 + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)\right]$$

$$j_{net} \approx j_0 \frac{(\alpha_a + \alpha_c)F\eta}{RT}$$

With $\alpha_a + \alpha_c = 1$:

$$j_{net} \approx \frac{j_0 F \eta}{RT}$$

**Step 6:** Defining the **charge-transfer resistance** $R_{ct} = \frac{RT}{j_0 F}$:

$$j_{net} = \frac{\eta}{R_{ct}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta = j_{net} \cdot R_{ct}$$

This shows that at low current, the electrode behaves like a linear resistor.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Photovoltaic Cell Efficiency Limit (Shockley–Queisser Concept)

**Step 1:** Photon energy is $E_{ph} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$, where $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s.

**Step 2:** For photon wavelength $\lambda$ in nm:

$$E_{ph} (\text{eV}) = \frac{1240}{\lambda (\text{nm})}$$

**Step 3:** Maximum theoretical efficiency of a single-junction solar cell occurs when semiconductor bandgap $E_g \approx 1.34$ eV (Shockley–Queisser limit, AM1.5 spectrum).

**Step 4:** Fill Factor relates max power rectangle to $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$:

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**Step 5:** Conversion efficiency:

$$\eta_{PV} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A hydrogen fuel cell operates at 80 °C with partial pressures $P_{H_2} = 1.5$ atm and $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm. The product water is liquid. Calculate $E_{cell}$.

**Given:** $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V at 25 °C; the reaction is $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O(l)$.

**Step 1:** Standard EMF at 25 °C: $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V.

**Step 2:** Apply Nernst at temperature $T = 80 + 273 = 353$ K:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

**Step 3:** Reaction quotient (liquid water has activity = 1):

$$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times (0.21)^{0.5}}$$

$$Q = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 0.4583} = \frac{1}{0.6874} = 1.4548$$

**Step 4:** Compute the Nernst term with $n = 2$:

$$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96485} = \frac{2934.84}{192970} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 1.229 - 0.01521 \times \ln(1.4548) \\
\ln(1.4548) &= 0.3747 \\
E &= 1.229 - 0.01521 \times 0.3747 \\
E &= 1.229 - 0.00570 \\
E &= 1.223 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The reversible open-circuit voltage at 80 °C is approximately **1.223 V** — slightly lower than the standard 1.229 V due to higher temperature. The fuel cell will deliver less than this in practice due to activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam – Model PEPOT637 ESE]`

**Q.** Define the term **exchange current density** ($j_0$) and explain its significance in electrochemical kinetics.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The exchange current density $j_0$ is the equal magnitude of the cathodic and anodic current densities flowing across an electrode–electrolyte interface at **dynamic equilibrium** (zero net current). Mathematically, at $\eta = 0$:

$$j_a = j_c = j_0$$

**Significance:**
1. **Magnitude Indicator:** Larger $j_0$ indicates a faster intrinsic electron-transfer kinetics at equilibrium.
2. **Polarization Predictor:** A small $j_0$ causes large activation overpotential for a given net current — electrodes with low $j_0$ are called "sluggish."
3. **Practical Design:** Catalysts (e.g., Pt in fuel cells) are chosen specifically to maximize $j_0$ for the desired redox reaction, reducing activation losses.

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam – PEPOT637 Model Paper]`

**Q.** Distinguish between **specific energy** and **energy density** of a battery. Which is more relevant for EV applications?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Property | Specific Energy | Energy Density |
|----------|----------------|----------------|
| Definition | Energy stored per unit mass (Wh kg$^{-1}$) | Energy stored per unit volume (Wh L$^{-1}$) |
| Measures | Gravimetric performance | Volumetric performance |
| Relevant for | EVs, aerospace (mass-sensitive) | Smartphones, laptops (space-sensitive) |
| Typical Li-ion | 150–250 Wh kg$^{-1}$ | 400–700 Wh L$^{-1}$ |

For **EV applications**, **specific energy** is more relevant because vehicle range is fundamentally limited by mass — every extra kg requires more energy to propel, creating a vicious cycle of weight-driven consumption.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Part B in PEPOT637 ESE provides an internal choice between two questions, each carrying 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q.** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)**. Write the half-reactions at the anode and cathode, and derive the Nernst equation for the cell operating at temperature $T$.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Schematic Description (3 marks):**
A PEMFC consists of:
- **Anode:** Porous carbon paper with Pt catalyst; fed with H$_2$ gas.
- **Cathode:** Porous carbon paper with Pt catalyst; fed with O$_2$ (or air).
- **Membrane:** Nafion™ (perfluorosulfonic acid polymer) — conducts H$^+$ but blocks electrons and gases.
- **Bipolar Plates:** Graphite/metal plates distributing gases and collecting current.

**2. Half-Reactions (2 marks):**

Anode (oxidation):
$$H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- \quad E^{\circ} = 0.00 \text{ V (SHE)}$$

Cathode (reduction):
$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O(l) \quad E^{\circ} = +1.229 \text{ V (SHE)}$$

Overall: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O(l)$, $E^{\circ}_{cell} = 1.229$ V.

**3. Nernst Derivation (2 marks):** As shown in Section 3.2 above, applying electrochemical potentials:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

For $n = 2$, $Q = \dfrac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$, at 25 °C:

$$E = 1.229 - \frac{0.05916}{2}\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q.** A PEMFC operates at 70 °C with $P_{H_2} = 3$ atm and $P_{O_2} = 1$ atm. The cell delivers 0.7 V at a current density of 0.6 A cm$^{-2}$. Compute (i) reversible cell EMF at operating temperature, (ii) total overpotential loss, and (iii) power density.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Reversible EMF (3 marks):**

$T = 343$ K, $n = 2$:

$$E_{rev} = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$

$$Q = \frac{1}{3 \times 1^{0.5}} = 0.3333$$

$$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 343}{2 \times 96485} = 0.01477 \text{ V}$$

$$\ln(1/0.3333) = \ln(3) = 1.0986$$

$$E_{rev} = 1.229 - 0.01477 \times 1.0986 = 1.229 - 0.01623 = 1.2128 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Total Overpotential Loss (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{total} = E_{rev} - V_{cell} = 1.2128 - 0.7 = 0.5128 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Power Density (2 marks):**

$$P = V_{cell} \times j = 0.7 \times 0.6 = 0.42 \text{ W cm}^{-2}$$

Convert: $0.42 \text{ W cm}^{-2} = 4.2 \text{ kW m}^{-2}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q.** Discuss the construction, working, and key advantages of **Lithium-ion batteries**. Compare their specific energy, cycle life, and voltage with **lead-acid** and **Ni-Cd** batteries.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Construction (2 marks):**
A Li-ion cell comprises:
- **Cathode:** LiCoO$_2$ (LCO), LiFePO$_4$ (LFP), or LiNi$_x$Mn$_y$Co$_z$O$_2$ (NMC) on Al current collector.
- **Anode:** Graphite (or Si–C composite) on Cu current collector.
- **Electrolyte:** 1 M LiPF$_6$ in EC:DMC (1:1) organic carbonate solvent.
- **Separator:** Polyethylene/polypropylene microporous film.

**2. Working (2 marks):**
- **Discharge (anode = graphite, cathode = LCO):**
  - Anode: $Li_xC_6 \rightarrow xLi^+ + xe^- + 6C$
  - Cathode: $Li_{1-x}CoO_2 + xLi^+ + xe^- \rightarrow LiCoO_2$
- **Charge:** Reverse reactions; Li$^+$ intercalates back into graphite.

**3. Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Lead-Acid | Ni-Cd | Li-ion (LCO) |
|-----------|-----------|-------|--------------|
| Nominal Voltage | 2.0 V | 1.2 V | 3.6–3.7 V |
| Specific Energy | 30–50 Wh kg$^{-1}$ | 40–60 Wh kg$^{-1}$ | 150–250 Wh kg$^{-1}$ |
| Cycle Life | 200–500 | 500–1000 | 500–2000 |
| Memory Effect | No | Yes | No |
| Self-Discharge/month | 3–5% | 15–20% | 2–3% |
| Toxicity | High (Pb) | High (Cd) | Moderate |

**Advantages of Li-ion:** High specific energy, no memory effect, low self-discharge, long cycle life.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q.** A 3.7 V Li-ion cell has internal resistance 25 m$\Omega$ and delivers 10 A constant current for 30 minutes. Calculate (i) energy delivered, (ii) heat generated due to internal resistance, and (iii) effective capacity in Ah and Wh.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Energy Delivered (3 marks):**

Terminal voltage during discharge: $V_t = 3.7 - I \cdot R = 3.7 - (10)(0.025) = 3.7 - 0.25 = 3.45$ V

$$E = V_t \times I \times t = 3.45 \times 10 \times (30 \times 60) = 3.45 \times 10 \times 1800 = 62100 \text{ J}$$

Convert: $62100 \text{ J} / 3600 = 17.25$ Wh.

**(ii) Heat Generated (2 marks):**

$$Q_{heat} = I^2 R t = (10)^2 \times 0.025 \times 1800 = 100 \times 0.025 \times 1800 = 4500 \text{ J} = 1.25 \text{ Wh}$$

**(iii) Capacity (2 marks):**

Charge delivered: $Q = I \times t = 10 \times 0.5 = 5$ Ah

Nominal energy: $E_{nom} = 3.7 \times 5 = 18.5$ Wh
Actual energy: 17.25 Wh (from part i)

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PEPOT637 Module 3:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Confusing $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ vs. $E_{anode} - E_{cathode}$. The first convention is the IUPAC standard and is what KTU examiners expect.
>
> 2. **Activity vs. Concentration:** For gas-phase reactants/products, use **partial pressures (atm)**; for solutes, use **molar concentrations (M)**. Pure solids and liquids have **activity = 1** — forgetting this is a 1-mark error per occurrence.
>
> 3. **Temperature Substitution:** When using the 0.05916 V form of Nernst, you **must** state "at 25 °C / 298 K." Using it at other temperatures without correction is a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Forgetting $\alpha_a + \alpha_c = 1$:** In Butler–Volmer problems, this symmetry rule is frequently missed, leading to wrong Tafel slopes.
>
> 5. **Confusing Fuel Cell vs. Battery:** Fuel cells are **open systems** (continuous fuel supply, water/CO$_2$ removed) — they don't "discharge to zero." Batteries are **closed systems** with finite capacity.
>
> 6. **Li-ion Naming:** Examiners want **"Li-ion"** not "lithium battery" — primary lithium cells (non-rechargeable, e.g., Li–SOCl$_2$) are different from secondary Li-ion (rechargeable).
>
> 7. **Polarization Curve Plot:** When asked to sketch polarization curves, students often forget to **label the three regions** (activation, ohmic, concentration) and the **limiting current density** — at least 1 mark is reserved for proper labels.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Rapid Revision Checklist — Electrochemical Energy Engineering**

**Thermodynamics Essentials:**
- $\Delta G = -nFE$ (free energy ↔ EMF)
- $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$
- Standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) reference: $E^{\circ}(H^+/H_2) = 0.00$ V
- Electrochemical series: more negative $E^{\circ}$ = stronger reducing agent (anode tendency)

**Kinetics Essentials:**
- Butler–Volmer: $j = j_0[\exp(\alpha_a F\eta/RT) - \exp(-\alpha_c F\eta/RT)]$
- Tafel slope: $2.303 RT/(\alpha F)$ ≈ 60 mV/dec for $\alpha = 1$
- Exchange current density $j_0$: high $j_0$ → low overpotential → good catalyst
- Charge-transfer resistance: $R_{ct} = RT/(j_0 F)$

**Polarization Components (3 Losses):**
- **Activation loss** (low current) — dominated by sluggish electrode kinetics
- **Ohmic loss** (mid current) — $IR_{int}$ from electrolyte/membrane resistance
- **Concentration loss** (high current) — mass-transport limitation near $j_L$

**Fuel Cells — Memorize:**
- **PEMFC:** 60–80 °C, H$^+$ ion, Pt catalyst, automotive applications
- **SOFC:** 600–1000 °C, O$^{2-}$ ion, ceramic electrolyte, stationary power
- **MCFC:** 600–700 °C, CO$_3^{2-}$ ion, molten carbonate, natural gas
- **PAFC:** 150–200 °C, H$^+$ ion, phosphoric acid, CHP systems
- **AFC:** 50–200 °C, OH$^-$ ion, alkaline, space applications (Apollo)

**Batteries — Memorize:**
- **Primary (non-rechargeable):** Zn-C, Alkaline, Li-SOCl$_2$
- **Secondary (rechargeable):** Lead-acid, Ni-Cd, Ni-MH, Li-ion
- **Lithium-ion chemistries:** LCO, LFP, NMC, NCA, LMO — each with different energy/power/safety trade-offs
- **Specific energy order:** Zn-air < Pb-acid < Ni-Cd < Ni-MH < Li-ion < Li-S (theoretical)

**Solar Cells — Memorize:**
- Bandgap $E_g$ ≈ 1.1 eV for Si; optimum $E_g$ ≈ 1.34 eV for max efficiency
- Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc} \propto \ln(I_L/I_0 + 1)$
- Typical Si PV efficiency: 18–22%; Shockley–Queisser limit ≈ 33%

**Supercapacitors — Memorize:**
- **EDLC (Electric Double-Layer Capacitor):** Capacitance from ion adsorption at electrode–electrolyte interface
- $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A/d$, where $d$ is nanoscale (~ nm)
- **Pseudocapacitor:** Faradaic redox at surface (e.g., RuO$_2$, MnO$_2$)
- Ragone plot: supercapacitors deliver high power but low energy vs. batteries
- Cycle life: > 100 000 cycles (vs. ~ 500–2000 for Li-ion)

**Conversion Efficiencies to Remember:**
- Carnot limit: $\eta_C = 1 - T_C/T_H$
- Fuel cell thermodynamic: $\varepsilon = \Delta G/\Delta H$
- PV cell: $\eta_{PV} = V_{oc} I_{sc} FF / P_{in}$
- Round-trip battery: $\eta_{RT} = E_{out}/E_{in}$ over full cycle

**Numerical Problem Formulas (Always Ready):**
- $E = E^{\circ} - 0.05916/n \log Q$ at 25 °C
- $j = j_0 F \eta / RT$ at low $\eta$
- $\eta_{ohmic} = IR_{int}$
- $P_{max} = V_{max} \times I_{max}$ for any electrical device
- Specific energy (Wh/kg) = $V_{nom} \times Q_{Ah}$ / mass

**Final KTU Tip:** Whenever a numerical problem provides partial pressures, **always convert to atm** before substituting into the Nernst equation; ensure standard state (1 M, 1 atm) reference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Batteries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Batteries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Batteries

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **electrochemical battery** is a device that converts the **free energy of a spontaneous redox (oxidation–reduction) chemical reaction** into usable electrical energy through spatially separated half-cell reactions connected by an external circuit, employing an ion-conducting electrolyte as the internal charge carrier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim expected answer):**
> *"A battery is a portable electrochemical energy storage device consisting of one or more galvanic cells that convert stored chemical energy into electrical energy via spontaneous electron-transfer reactions at the electrodes, separated by an electrolyte."*

A **cell** is the fundamental electrochemical unit; a **battery** is an assembly of multiple cells connected in series, parallel, or series–parallel combinations to achieve desired **voltage** and **ampere-hour (Ah) capacity**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tower & Plumbing" Model

Think of a battery like a **water tower connected to a faucet**:

| Water System Component | Battery Equivalent | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Water height in the tower | **Cell Voltage (V)** | The "pressure" pushing electrons |
| Diameter of the water tank | **Capacity (Ah)** | Total quantity of charge available |
| Width of the pipe | **Internal Resistance (Rᵢ)** | Restriction to flow |
| Water itself | **Charge (Q = It)** | What actually flows out |
| Pump refilling the tower | **Charger** | Pushes reaction in reverse (rechargeable) |

> [!NOTE]
> A **higher voltage** = stronger "push" (drives more demanding devices).
> A **higher capacity** = larger reservoir (runs longer).
> A **lower internal resistance** = wider pipe (better high-current delivery).

## 1.3 Master Classification of Batteries (Board-Exam Favorite)

Batteries are broadly classified into **two super-families** based on reversibility of the cell reaction:

### A) **Primary Batteries** (Single-Use / Non-Rechargeable)
The electrochemical reaction is **irreversible**; once discharged, the cell cannot be restored to its original state by applying an external current. Internal conversion of active materials to products is **permanent**.

**Common examples:**
- **Leclanché (Dry) Cell** — Zn / NH₄Cl + MnO₂
- **Alkaline (Zn–MnO₂) Cell**
- **Lithium Primary Cells** (Li–MnO₂, Li–SOCl₂, Li–CFₓ)
- **Mercury / Silver Oxide Button Cells**

### B) **Secondary Batteries** (Rechargeable / Storage Batteries)
The cell reaction is **reversible**; by applying an external DC source, the products can be reconverted back to original reactants. These are also called **accumulators** or **storage cells**.

**Common examples:**
- **Lead-Acid (Pb–PbO₂) Battery**
- **Nickel–Cadmium (Ni–Cd) Battery**
- **Nickel–Metal Hydride (Ni-MH) Battery**
- **Lithium-Ion (Li-ion) Battery**
- **Sodium-Sulfur (Na-S) Battery** (high-temp)

### C) **Reserve & Specialty Batteries**
- **Fuel Cells** (continuous-feed reactants — H₂/O₂, methanol)
- **Flow Batteries** (Vanadium Redox, Zn–Br)
- **Thermal (Molten Salt) Batteries**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's distinction point:** A *fuel cell* is technically **not a battery** in the strict sense because the active materials are supplied externally and the device operates as long as fuel is fed. It is an *electrochemical converter*, not a *storage* device.

## 1.4 The Seven Golden Performance Metrics of a Battery

Every battery in KTU board problems is judged using these **seven fundamental parameters** — memorize them thoroughly:

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electromotive Force (EMF) | E | **V (volt)** | Theoretical open-circuit voltage |
| 2 | Terminal Voltage | V | V | Actual voltage under load |
| 3 | Internal Resistance | Rᵢ | **Ω (ohm)** | Loss element inside cell |
| 4 | Capacity | C | **Ah (ampere-hour)** | Total charge deliverable |
| 5 | Energy Density | ρₑ | **Wh/kg** | Energy per unit mass |
| 6 | Power Density | ρₚ | **W/kg** | Power per unit mass |
| 7 | Cycle Life | N | **cycles** | Recharge–discharge cycles before failure |

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a Galvanic Cell

Every battery consists of **four mandatory physical components**:

1. **Anode (Negative Electrode)** — Site of **oxidation**; loses electrons; typically the higher-position metal in the electrochemical series (e.g., Zn, Li).
2. **Cathode (Positive Electrode)** — Site of **reduction**; gains electrons; typically an oxide or higher-position ion (e.g., MnO₂, PbO₂).
3. **Electrolyte** — Ionic conductor (aqueous, alkaline, organic, or solid-state); must be an **electronic insulator** but **ionic conductor**.
4. **Separator** — Physically separates anode and cathode to prevent direct electron flow internally (which would short-circuit).

## 2.2 Fundamental Theoretical Framework

### A) The Nernst Equation — Governing Cell EMF

The reversible EMF of a cell depends on **concentration of active species**, not just standard conditions:

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

Where:
- $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ = **Standard EMF (V)**
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96485 C/mol**
- $Q$ = Reaction quotient (activities of products / reactants)

At standard temperature (**T = 298 K**), using $\ln = 2.303 \log_{10}$:

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} Q$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **0.0592 V** is the most-tested numerical value in KTU exams. Always derive it: $(RT/F) \cdot 2.303 = 0.05916$ V at 25 °C.

### B) The Gibb's Energy Link

The maximum electrical work obtainable from a cell reaction:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$$

- If $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} > 0$ → $\Delta G^{\circ} < 0$ → **Spontaneous (Galvanic / Battery)**
- If $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} < 0$ → $\Delta G^{\circ} > 0$ → **Non-spontaneous (Electrolytic)**

### C) Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis (Charge ↔ Mass Conversion)

**First Law:** $m \propto Q = It$

**Second Law:** $m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \frac{M \cdot Q}{nF}$

Combined equation:

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{Q \cdot M}{nF}$$

Where:
- $m$ = Mass deposited/dissolved (g)
- $M$ = Molar mass (g/mol)
- $I$ = Current (A)
- $t$ = Time (s)
- $n$ = Valence / electrons per ion
- $F$ = **96485 C/mol**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrochemical Equivalent (Z)** = $M/(nF)$ in **g/C** — a tabulated property of each element. Multiplying Z by current × time gives mass directly.

## 2.3 Battery Efficiency Definitions

### Coulombic (Faradaic / Ampere-Hour) Efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{Ah}} = \frac{Q_{\text{discharge}}}{Q_{\text{charge}}} \times 100\%$$

### Voltage (Watt) Efficiency:

$$\eta_{V} = \frac{V_{\text{discharge,avg}}}{V_{\text{charge,avg}}} \times 100\%$$

### Overall Energy (Watt-Hour) Efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{Wh}} = \eta_{\text{Ah}} \times \eta_{V}$$

$$\eta_{\text{Wh}} = \frac{\text{Energy out during discharge}}{\text{Energy in during charge}} \times 100\%$$

## 2.4 Capacity, Energy & Power Relationships

$$C_{\text{Ah}} = I \cdot t_{\text{discharge}}$$

$$E_{\text{stored}} = V_{\text{nominal}} \times C_{\text{Ah}} \quad [\text{Wh}]$$

$$\text{Specific Energy} = \frac{E_{\text{stored}}}{m_{\text{cell}}} \quad [\text{Wh/kg}]$$

$$\text{Specific Power} = \frac{V \cdot I}{m_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{P}{m_{\text{cell}}} \quad [\text{W/kg}]$$

## 2.5 Peukert's Equation (Lead-Acid Specialty — KTU Favorite)

For lead-acid batteries, **capacity decreases** as discharge current increases:

$$C_{p} = I^{k} \cdot t$$

$$t = \frac{C_{p}}{I^{k}}$$

Where:
- $C_p$ = Peukert capacity (a constant for the cell)
- $k$ = **Peukert exponent** (typically 1.1–1.3 for lead-acid; closer to 1 = ideal)
- $I$ = Discharge current (A)
- $t$ = Discharge time (h)

> [!NOTE]
> A **higher k** = greater capacity loss at high discharge rates. This is why lead-acid batteries "die fast" when you crank a starter motor — the actual deliverable Ah drops.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Board Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n}\log Q$ | Nernst equation | V | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$ | Free energy – EMF link | J/mol | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $m = \frac{MIt}{nF}$ | Faraday's mass law | g | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $V_{\text{terminal}} = E - IR_{i}$ | Loaded terminal voltage | V | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $C = I \cdot t$ | Capacity definition | Ah | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $E_{\text{stored}} = V \cdot C$ | Energy in Wh | Wh | ⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $\text{Wh/kg} = \frac{V \cdot C}{m}$ | Specific energy | Wh/kg | ⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{Ah}} = \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | Coulombic efficiency | % | ⭐⭐ |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{Wh}} = \frac{V_{\text{d}}C_{\text{d}}}{V_{\text{c}}C_{\text{c}}}$ | Energy efficiency | % | ⭐⭐ |
| 10 | $t = C_p / I^{k}$ | Peukert's equation | h | ⭐⭐ |
| 11 | $\text{SOC} = \frac{C_{\text{remaining}}}{C_{\text{rated}}} \times 100$ | State of charge | % | ⭐ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive SLI (Starting, Lighting, Ignition):** Lead-acid dominates because of high cranking current, low cost, recyclable.
- **EV (Electric Vehicle) Powertrains:** Li-ion, LiFePO₄ — chosen for **high specific energy** (Wh/kg).
- **Grid Storage / Solar Off-Grid:** Flow batteries (Vanadium Redox) chosen for **decoupled energy/power scaling**.
- **Aerospace / Satellites:** Ni-Cd, Ni-H₂, and Li-ion (radiation tolerant).
- **Medical Implants:** Primary Li–I₂ batteries (10+ year life, low self-discharge).
- **Smartphones / Laptops:** Li-ion / Li-Polymer — high energy density, no memory effect.

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation from Gibb's Free Energy

### Starting Premise
The change in Gibbs free energy for an electrochemical reaction at constant T and P equals the maximum non-expansion (electrical) work:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

For non-standard conditions:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

### Substituting and Equating:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

### Solving for E:

$$nFE = nFE^{\circ} - RT \ln Q$$

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

### At Standard Temperature (T = 298.15 K):

Substituting $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T = 298.15$ K, $F = 96485$ C/mol:

$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.02569 \text{ V}$$

Converting natural log to base-10: $\ln Q = 2.303 \log_{10} Q$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.02569 \times 2.303}{n} \log_{10} Q$$

$$\boxed{E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Terminal Voltage Under Load (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law)

### Setup
A battery with EMF **E** and internal resistance **Rᵢ** drives a current **I** through external load resistance **R_L**.

```
      ┌──── R_L ─────┐
      │              │
    ──┴──/\/\/────────┴──
    +  E             Rᵢ  −
    ─────────────────────
```

### Applying KVL around the closed loop:

$$E = IR_{i} + IR_{L}$$

### Solving for Terminal Voltage:

$$V_{\text{terminal}} = IR_{L} = E - IR_{i}$$

$$\boxed{V = E - IR_{i}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **the** most-tested 14-mark derivation in KTU Module 3. Memorize the sign convention: EMF **minus** ohmic drop.

### Power Delivered to Load:

$$P_{\text{load}} = I^{2} R_{L} = I(E - IR_{i})$$

### Condition for Maximum Power Transfer:

Set $dP_{\text{load}}/dR_{L} = 0$:

$$\frac{dP}{dR_{L}} = \frac{d}{dR_{L}}\left[\frac{E^{2}R_{L}}{(R_{i}+R_{L})^{2}}\right] = 0$$

$$E^{2}\left[\frac{(R_{i}+R_{L})^{2} - 2R_{L}(R_{i}+R_{L})}{(R_{i}+R_{L})^{4}}\right] = 0$$

$$(R_{i}+R_{L}) - 2R_{L} = 0$$

$$\boxed{R_{L} = R_{i}}$$

**Maximum power delivered:** $P_{\max} = E^{2}/(4R_{i})$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lead-Acid Battery EMF from Nernst (Discharge Reaction)

### Overall Cell Reaction:

$$\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_{2} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4} \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_{4} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$$

**Number of electrons transferred:** $n = 2$ (each Pb → Pb²⁺ gives 2 e⁻)

### Reaction Quotient:

$$Q = \frac{[\text{PbSO}_{4}]^{2}[\text{H}_{2}\text{O}]^{2}}{[\text{Pb}][\text{PbO}_{2}][\text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4}]^{2}}$$

Since Pb and PbO₂ are pure solids (activity = 1):

$$Q = \frac{a_{\text{H}_{2}\text{O}}^{2}}{a_{\text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4}}^{2}} = \frac{[\text{H}_{2}\text{O}]^{2}}{[\text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4}]^{2}}$$

### Nernst Application:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log \frac{[\text{H}_{2}\text{O}]^{2}}{[\text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4}]^{2}}$$

$$E = E^{\circ} + 0.0592 \log [\text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4}] - 0.0592 \log [\text{H}_{2}\text{O}]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As discharge proceeds**, H₂SO₄ is consumed (concentration drops) and H₂O is produced (concentration rises). Therefore, **log ratio decreases → E drops** — explaining the gradual decline in terminal voltage during discharge.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Pattern)

### Example 1 — Nernst Application
A Daniel cell has $\text{Zn} \vert \text{Zn}^{2+}(a=0.01) \| \text{Cu}^{2+}(a=1.0) \vert \text{Cu}$. Given $E^{\circ} = 1.10$ V. Find E at 25 °C.

**Solution:**
Overall reaction: $\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$, so $n = 2$.

$$Q = \frac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]} = \frac{0.01}{1.0} = 0.01$$

$$E = 1.10 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log(0.01) = 1.10 - \frac{0.0592}{2}(-2)$$

$$E = 1.10 + 0.0592 = \boxed{1.1592 \text{ V}}$$

### Example 2 — Faraday's Law / Capacity
A lead-acid battery delivers 5 A for 20 hours. Calculate:
(a) Capacity in Ah
(b) Mass of PbSO₄ formed (theoretical)

**Solution:**
(a) $C = It = 5 \times 20 = 100 \text{ Ah}$

(b) $Q = It = 5 \times 20 \times 3600 = 360{,}000 \text{ C}$

For 2 electrons per Pb (n = 2), M(PbSO₄) = 303.3 g/mol:

$$m = \frac{M \cdot Q}{n \cdot F} = \frac{303.3 \times 360{,}000}{2 \times 96485} = \frac{1.0919 \times 10^{8}}{192970}$$

$$m = \boxed{565.9 \text{ g of PbSO}_{4}}$$

### Example 3 — Energy Efficiency
A Ni-Cd battery is charged at 1.5 A for 10 h at 1.45 V (avg) and discharged at 1.2 A for 11.5 h at 1.20 V (avg). Find all three efficiencies.

**Solution:**
Charge: $Q_c = 1.5 \times 10 = 15$ Ah, $E_c = 1.45 \times 15 = 21.75$ Wh
Discharge: $Q_d = 1.2 \times 11.5 = 13.8$ Ah, $E_d = 1.20 \times 13.8 = 16.56$ Wh

$$\eta_{\text{Ah}} = \frac{13.8}{15.0} \times 100 = 92.0\%$$

$$\eta_{V} = \frac{1.20}{1.45} \times 100 = 82.76\%$$

$$\eta_{\text{Wh}} = \frac{16.56}{21.75} \times 100 = \boxed{76.14\%}$$

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Major Battery Systems

| Property | Lead-Acid | Ni-Cd | Ni-MH | Li-ion (LCO) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal V (per cell)** | 2.0 V | 1.2 V | 1.2 V | 3.6–3.7 V |
| **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** | 30–50 | 45–80 | 60–120 | 150–250 |
| **Cycle Life** | 200–300 | 500–2000 | 500–1000 | 500–2000 |
| **Memory Effect** | No | **Yes** | Mild | No |
| **Self-Discharge / month** | 4–6% | 10–20% | 15–30% | 1–3% |
| **Toxicity / Env. Hazard** | High (Pb) | High (Cd) | Low | Low |
| **Cost (per kWh)** | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Typical Use** | Auto SLI | Power tools | Hybrid EVs | Phones, EVs |

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Battery Classification Hierarchy (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Electrochemical Power Sources"]:::root
    nodeB["Primary Batteries Non-Rechargeable"]:::primary
    nodeC["Secondary Batteries Rechargeable"]:::secondary
    nodeD["Reserve and Specialty Systems"]:::reserve

    nodeE["Leclanche Dry Cell Zn-MnO2"]:::primary
    nodeF["Alkaline Zn-MnO2"]:::primary
    nodeG["Lithium Primary Li-MnO2, Li-SOCl2"]:::primary

    nodeH["Lead-Acid Pb-PbO2"]:::secondary
    nodeI["Nickel-Cadmium Ni-Cd"]:::secondary
    nodeJ["Nickel-Metal Hydride Ni-MH"]:::secondary
    nodeK["Lithium-Ion LiCoO2, LiFePO4"]:::secondary
    nodeL["Sodium-Sulfur Na-S high temp"]:::secondary

    nodeM["Fuel Cells H2-O2, Methanol"]:::reserve
    nodeN["Redox Flow Batteries Vanadium, Zn-Br"]:::reserve

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeC --> nodeK
    nodeC --> nodeL
    nodeD --> nodeM
    nodeD --> nodeN

    classDef root fill:#1f2937,color:#ffffff,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:3px
    classDef primary fill:#fef3c7,color:#92400e,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px
    classDef secondary fill:#dbeafe,color:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px
    classDef reserve fill:#dcfce7,color:#14532d,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Battery Functional Architecture — Discharge Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Anode Active Material Zn or Pb"]:::anode
    nodeB["Anode Reaction Oxidation: M to M to the power n plus plus plus n electrons"]:::reaction
    nodeC["Electrolyte Ionic Conductor H2SO4 or KOH"]:::electrolyte
    nodeD["Cathode Reaction Reduction: M to the power n plus plus plus n electrons to M"]:::reaction
    nodeE["Cathode Active Material PbO2 or MnO2"]:::cathode
    nodeF["External Circuit Electron Flow from Anode to Cathode"]:::circuit
    nodeG["Load Device Bulb, Motor, IC"]:::load

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeB -.Electrons via wire.-> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG -.Returns electrons.-> nodeD

    classDef anode fill:#fee2e2,color:#7f1d1d,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cathode fill:#dbeafe,color:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px
    classDef electrolyte fill:#f3e8ff,color:#581c87,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px
    classDef reaction fill:#fef3c7,color:#92400e,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1px
    classDef circuit fill:#d1fae5,color:#065f46,stroke:#059669,stroke-width:2px
    classDef load fill:#fce7f3,color:#831843,stroke:#be185d,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Complete Battery Pack System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Energy In Charging Mode"]
        nodeA1["AC Mains or DC Source"]
        nodeA2["Rectifier and Charger Controller"]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE["Battery Pack Bank of Cells"]
        nodeB1["Cell 1 series"]
        nodeB2["Cell 2 series"]
        nodeB3["Cell N series"]
        nodeB4["Battery Management System BMS"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Energy Out Discharge Mode"]
        nodeC1["DC Bus Output"]
        nodeC2["Inverter AC Output"]
        nodeC3["Load Devices"]
    end

    subgraph MONITOR["Telemetry and Protection"]
        nodeD1["Voltage Sensor"]
        nodeD2["Current Sensor"]
        nodeD3["Temperature Sensor"]
        nodeD4["State of Charge SOC Estimator"]
    end

    nodeA1 --> nodeA2 --> nodeB1 --> nodeB2 --> nodeB3
    nodeB3 --> nodeC1 --> nodeC2 --> nodeC3
    nodeB3 -.Telemetry.-> nodeD1
    nodeB3 -.Telemetry.-> nodeD2
    nodeB3 -.Telemetry.-> nodeD3
    nodeD1 --> nodeD4
    nodeD2 --> nodeD4
    nodeD3 --> nodeD4
    nodeD4 -.Feedback control.-> nodeB4
    nodeB4 -.Control signals.-> nodeA2
```

## 4.4 Lead-Acid Cell — Internal Schematic

flowchart TB

    end

    nodeA --- nodeD
    nodeD --- nodeB

    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeC -.Gas vents.-> nodeE
