# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish clearly between a primary and a secondary battery. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Primary Battery | Secondary Battery |
|---|---|---|
| **Reversibility** | Irreversible cell reaction | Reversible cell reaction |
| **Recharging** | Cannot be recharged | Can be recharged 100s–1000s of cycles |
| **Internal Design** | Simpler, no current collectors for reverse | Complex, designed for repeated cycling |
| **Self-Discharge** | Generally lower | Higher (especially Ni-Cd, Ni-MH) |
| **Examples** | Leclanché cell, Alkaline Zn–MnO₂, Lithium primary | Lead-acid, Ni-Cd, Li-ion |

Examples:
- **Primary:** (i) Leclanché dry cell (ii) Alkaline manganese cell
- **Secondary:** (i) Lead-acid battery (ii) Lithium-ion battery

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Define (a) Energy density and (b) Power density of a battery. Why is Li-ion preferred over Lead-acid for EV applications despite higher cost?**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Energy Density** = Total energy deliverable per unit mass or volume of the battery.

$$\text{Specific Energy} = \frac{V \cdot C_{\text{Ah}}}{m_{\text{cell}}} \quad [\text{Wh/kg}]$$

It measures **how long** the battery can power a device.

(b) **Power Density** = Maximum power deliverable per unit mass.

$$\text{Specific Power} = \frac{V \cdot I_{\max}}{m_{\text{cell}}} \quad [\text{W/kg}]$$

It measures **how fast** energy can be delivered.

**Why Li-ion over Lead-acid for EVs:**

| Factor | Li-ion | Lead-acid |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy | **150–250 Wh/kg** | 30–50 Wh/kg |
| Cycle Life | 500–2000 | 200–300 |
| Weight | Light (4–5× lighter) | Heavy |
| Maintenance | Maintenance-free | Needs topping up |

For EVs, **range** (governed by specific energy) is critical. Li-ion provides 4–5× the range for the same weight, justifying higher cost.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Important]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Nernst equation from thermodynamic principles. A Daniel cell has Zn²⁺ (a = 0.1) and Cu²⁺ (a = 0.01). Given $E^{\circ} = 1.10$ V, calculate the EMF at 25 °C.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A lead-acid battery has 6 cells in series, each of EMF 2.05 V and internal resistance 0.01 Ω. It delivers 10 A to a load of 1.2 Ω per cell. Calculate (i) Terminal voltage per cell, (ii) Total terminal voltage of battery, (iii) Power delivered to load, (iv) Power lost internally.**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Nernst Derivation

**Step 1: Gibbs Free Energy — Electrical Work Equivalence**

For a constant-T, constant-P electrochemical process, the maximum non-expansion (electrical) work obtainable equals the decrease in Gibbs free energy:

$$\Delta G = W_{\text{elec,max}} = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$$

For non-standard conditions, the free-energy change is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 2: Substituting:**

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 3: Solving for E (divide throughout by –nF):**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 4: At 25 °C (298 K), natural-to-decimal conversion:**

$$\frac{RT}{F} \cdot 2.303 = \frac{8.314 \times 298 \times 2.303}{96485} = 0.0592 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} Q}$$

**Numerical Application:**

For Daniel cell: $\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$, $n = 2$.

$$Q = \frac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]} = \frac{0.1}{0.01} = 10$$

$$E = 1.10 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log(10) = 1.10 - \frac{0.0592}{2}(1)$$

$$\boxed{E = 1.10 - 0.0296 = 1.0704 \text{ V}}$$

#### Part (b) — Lead-Acid Battery Calculations

**Given:**
- 6 cells in series; each cell: $E = 2.05$ V, $R_i = 0.01$ Ω
- Load current $I = 10$ A
- Load resistance per cell = 1.2 Ω

**Step 1: Total EMF and Total Internal Resistance:**

$$E_{\text{total}} = 6 \times 2.05 = 12.30 \text{ V}$$

$$R_{i,\text{total}} = 6 \times 0.01 = 0.06 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 2: Total Load Resistance:**

$$R_{L,\text{total}} = 6 \times 1.2 = 7.2 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3: Terminal Voltage per cell:**

$$V_{\text{cell}} = E - IR_i = 2.05 - (10 \times 0.01) = 2.05 - 0.10 = \boxed{1.95 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4: Total Terminal Voltage:**

$$V_{\text{total}} = 6 \times 1.95 = \boxed{11.70 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 5: Power Delivered to Load:**

$$P_{\text{load}} = I^{2} R_{L,\text{total}} = (10)^{2} \times 7.2 = \boxed{720 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 6: Power Lost Internally:**

$$P_{\text{internal}} = I^{2} R_{i,\text{total}} = (10)^{2} \times 0.06 = \boxed{6 \text{ W}}$$

**Verification (Energy balance):**

$$P_{\text{generated}} = E_{\text{total}} \times I = 12.30 \times 10 = 123 \text{ W}$$

$$\text{But } P_{\text{load}} + P_{\text{internal}} = 720 + 6 = 726 \text{ W}$$

*(Note: The extra power comes from the fact that the load resistor and current here don't match the EMF. In a proper series circuit, current is determined by $I = E_{\text{total}}/(R_{i,\text{tot}}+R_{L,\text{tot}})$.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3 Alternative]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the construction and working of a Lead-acid battery with neat reactions at both electrodes. Comment on the role of H₂SO₄ as electrolyte and indicator of state of charge.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A Ni-Cd battery is charged at 5 A for 8 h. Average charge voltage is 1.45 V. During discharge, it delivers 4 A for 9.5 h at an average of 1.20 V. Calculate (i) Ah efficiency, (ii) Voltage efficiency, (iii) Wh efficiency.**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Lead-Acid Construction & Working

**Construction:**

A lead-acid cell consists of:
- **Positive Plate:** PbO₂ (lead dioxide) paste on a Pb grid
- **Negative Plate:** Sponge Pb (metallic lead) on a Pb grid
- **Electrolyte:** ~37% H₂SO₄ (specific gravity ≈ 1.28)
- **Separator:** Microporous rubber or PVC between plates
- **Container:** Hard rubber or polypropylene
- **Vent Caps:** Allow gas escape; prevent electrolyte loss

**Electrode Reactions During Discharge:**

**At Anode (Negative Plate):**
$$\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_{4}^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_{4} + 2e^{-}$$

**At Cathode (Positive Plate):**
$$\text{PbO}_{2} + 4\text{H}^{+} + \text{SO}_{4}^{2-} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_{4} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**
$$\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_{2} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{SO}_{4} \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_{4} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$$

**Reactions During Charge:** All three reactions reverse (electrolysis).

**Role of H₂SO₄ — Dual Function:**
1. **Ionic Conductor:** Provides SO₄²⁻ and H⁺ ions for charge transfer.
2. **Active Reactant:** H₂SO₄ is consumed during discharge → its concentration drops.

**State-of-Charge Indicator (Hydrometer Reading):**

| State | Specific Gravity | Voltage (per cell) |
|---|---|---|
| Fully Charged | **1.28** | 2.05–2.10 V |
| 50% Discharged | 1.20 | ~1.95 V |
| Fully Discharged | 1.12 | ~1.75 V |

**Conclusion:** As discharge proceeds, H₂SO₄ is replaced by H₂O → SG drops → can be directly measured with a hydrometer to gauge state of charge.

#### Part (b) — Ni-Cd Efficiency Calculations

**Given:**
- Charging: $I_c = 5$ A, $t_c = 8$ h, $V_c = 1.45$ V
- Discharging: $I_d = 4$ A, $t_d = 9.5$ h, $V_d = 1.20$ V

**Step 1: Charge and Discharge Capacities:**

$$C_c = I_c \times t_c = 5 \times 8 = 40 \text{ Ah}$$

$$C_d = I_d \times t_d = 4 \times 9.5 = 38 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 2: Ah Efficiency (Coulombic):**

$$\eta_{\text{Ah}} = \frac{C_d}{C_c} \times 100 = \frac{38}{40} \times 100 = \boxed{95\%}$$

**Step 3: Voltage Efficiency:**

$$\eta_V = \frac{V_d}{V_c} \times 100 = \frac{1.20}{1.45} \times 100 = \boxed{82.76\%}$$

**Step 4: Energy Calculations:**

$$E_c = V_c \times C_c = 1.45 \times 40 = 58 \text{ Wh}$$

$$E_d = V_d \times C_d = 1.20 \times 38 = 45.6 \text{ Wh}$$

**Step 5: Wh Efficiency (Energy):**

$$\eta_{\text{Wh}} = \frac{E_d}{E_c} \times 100 = \frac{45.6}{58} \times 100 = \boxed{78.62\%}$$

**Verification:** $\eta_{\text{Wh}} \approx \eta_{\text{Ah}} \times \eta_V = 0.95 \times 0.8276 = 0.7862$ ✓

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Read Carefully Before Exam:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Nernst Equation:** Forgetting that the sign of the log term is **negative** for oxidation (anode). Always derive from $\Delta G$ to avoid this.
> 2. **Misuse of Faraday Constant:** Using $F = 96500$ (rounded) is acceptable; using $F = 96500$ and $M$ in **kg** instead of g → off by 1000×. Always check mass units.
> 3. **Confusing Series/Parallel Math:** In series, voltages add but current is same; in parallel, currents add but voltage is same. A common 7-mark killer.
> 4. **Forgetting Temperature in Nernst:** The constant 0.0592 is valid ONLY at 25 °C. If T is given, derive $\frac{RT}{F}\cdot 2.303$ from scratch.
> 5. **Wh efficiency mislabeled:** Some students compute Ah efficiency but call it Wh efficiency. Examiner **will** deduct 2 marks.
> 6. **No units in numerical answers:** Always write V, Ah, Wh, W — unitless answer = 0.5 mark penalty per question.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Batteries — Top 15 Things to Remember at a Glance:**

- 🔋 **Battery** = galvanic cell(s) converting chemical → electrical energy via spontaneous redox.
- ⚡ **Anode = oxidation (–ve); Cathode = reduction (+ve).** Memorize once, forever.
- 📐 **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n}\log Q$ (at 298 K).
- 🔢 **Key Constants:** $F = 96485$ C/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $\frac{RT}{F} = 0.0257$ V at 25 °C.
- ⚙️ **Faraday's 2nd Law:** $m = \frac{MIt}{nF}$ — links charge to mass deposited/dissolved.
- 📉 **Terminal Voltage:** $V = E - IR_i$ — drop is linear with current.
- ⚖️ **Three Efficiencies:** $\eta_{\text{Ah}}$ (charge ratio), $\eta_V$ (voltage ratio), $\eta_{\text{Wh}} = \eta_{\text{Ah}} \times \eta_V$.
- 🏭 **Lead-Acid** — 2 V/cell, cheapest, recyclable, heavy; SG of electrolyte is SOC indicator.
- 🔋 **Ni-Cd** — 1.2 V/cell, memory effect, toxic Cd; high cycle life.
- 🚗 **Li-ion** — 3.6–3.7 V/cell, highest specific energy (150–250 Wh/kg), no memory effect.
- 🔋 **Energy stored** = $V \times C_{\text{Ah}}$ (Wh).
- 📊 **Specific energy** = Wh/kg; **Specific power** = W/kg.
- 🌀 **Peukert's Law** — capacity drops at high discharge rates ($k = 1.1$–$1.3$).
- 🧪 **Fuel cells ≠ batteries** — they are open systems (continuous feed).
- 🌡️ **H₂SO₄ in lead-acid** is both electrolyte AND active reactant — concentration changes reflect SOC.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Cell (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):** A fuel cell is a **galvanic (voltaic) electrochemical energy conversion device** that directly converts the **chemical free energy** of a continuously supplied fuel (most commonly hydrogen) and an oxidant (most commonly oxygen from air) into **direct current (DC) electrical energy**, with water and heat as the principal by-products. Unlike a battery, the reactants are not stored internally; they are supplied externally, allowing continuous power generation as long as fuel and oxidant are fed.

The key thermodynamic driving force is the **Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G$)** of the overall cell reaction, which is related to the reversible cell potential $E$ by the fundamental electrochemical relation:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

where $n$ is the number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction, $F$ is **Faraday's constant = 96,485 C mol⁻¹**, and $E$ is the open-circuit cell voltage in volts.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Never-Dying Battery"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a **conventional AA battery** that powers your TV remote. It runs for a few weeks and then *dies* because the chemical reactants inside are completely consumed. Now imagine a battery that **never dies** — instead of running out, you simply keep pouring a liquid fuel (hydrogen) into one side and air into the other. As long as you keep supplying fuel, electricity flows continuously. That is **exactly what a fuel cell does**. It is a *refuellable* galvanic cell where energy conversion is decoupled from the device's internal mass.

A **hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell** is analogous to the **combustion of hydrogen** ($H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$), but the chemical energy is released **electrochemically** (electron-by-electron through an external circuit) rather than as a burst of heat. This is the essence of the **2nd Law of Thermodynamics** advantage: the free-energy-to-work conversion is **not Carnot-limited**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (H₂/O₂, 25°C) |
|---|---|---|
| Theoretical Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) | $E^{0}$ | **1.229 V** |
| Standard Gibbs Free Energy | $\Delta G^{0}$ | **−237.2 kJ mol⁻¹** |
| Standard Enthalpy of Combustion | $\Delta H^{0}$ | **−285.8 kJ mol⁻¹** |
| Maximum Theoretical (Electrical) Efficiency | $\eta_{max}$ | **≈ 83 %** ($\Delta G/\Delta H$) |
| Practical Operating Cell Voltage | $E_{cell}$ | 0.6 – 0.8 V |
| Practical Efficiency | $\eta_{pract}$ | 40 – 60 % |

> [!TIP]
> The **theoretical efficiency of a fuel cell (~83 %)** is dramatically higher than the **Carnot efficiency** of any practical heat engine burning the same fuel (typically 30–40 %), because fuel cells bypass the **heat → work** intermediate step entirely.

## 1.4 Why Fuel Cells Matter in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> - **Zero local emissions** (only water if pure $H_2$ is used).
> - **Modular & scalable** — from 5 W laptop batteries to **multi-MW power plants**.
> - **Silent operation**, fewer moving parts than turbines.
> - **High power density** (esp. PEMFC) — ideal for transport (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO, buses).
> - **Space applications** — Apollo, Space Shuttle Orbiter, and ISS all use alkaline / PEM fuel cells.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Generic H₂–O₂ Fuel Cell

The fuel cell consists of two porous electrodes (the **anode** and **cathode**) separated by an **electrolyte** (acidic, alkaline, or solid). Fuel ($H_2$) is fed to the anode, and oxidant ($O_2$, usually from air) to the cathode. The electrodes are catalysed (typically by **platinum** in low-temperature cells) to accelerate the half-reactions.

### 2.1.1 Half-Reactions and Overall Reaction

**(a) Acidic / Proton-Conducting Electrolyte (e.g., PEMFC, PAFC):**

| Electrode | Half-Reaction |
|---|---|
| Anode (oxidation) | $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$ |
| Cathode (reduction) | $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$ |
| **Overall** | $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad (E^{0} = 1.229\,V)$ |

**(b) Alkaline Electrolyte (e.g., AFC):**

| Electrode | Half-Reaction |
|---|---|
| Anode (oxidation) | $H_2 + 2OH^- \rightarrow 2H_2O + 2e^-$ |
| Cathode (reduction) | $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow 2OH^-$ |
| **Overall** | $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad (E^{0} = 1.229\,V)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **overall reaction is identical** for both electrolytes; only the *pathway* (i.e., the migrating ion, $H^+$ vs. $OH^-$) differs. The reversible cell voltage is therefore the same at standard conditions.

## 2.2 Thermodynamics of Fuel Cells

### 2.2.1 Why $\Delta G$ (Not $\Delta H$) Drives the Cell

In **heat engines**, the entire enthalpy change $\Delta H$ is first released as heat, then partly converted to work (subject to the Carnot limit). In a fuel cell, the **maximum electrical work extractable per mole of reaction** is the change in **Gibbs free energy**:

$$W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G = nFE$$

The remainder of the enthalpy ($T\Delta S$) is released as **waste heat**. Thus, the **theoretical electrical efficiency** is:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = 1 - \frac{T\Delta S}{\Delta H}$$

For the $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$ reaction at 25 °C:
- $\Delta G^{0} = -237.2$ kJ mol⁻¹
- $\Delta H^{0} = -285.8$ kJ mol⁻¹
- $T\Delta S^{0} = -48.6$ kJ mol⁻¹ (heat released)

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.830 = 83.0\,\%$$

### 2.2.2 Effect of Temperature on Reversible Voltage

The reversible cell voltage depends on temperature via:

$$E_T = \frac{-\Delta H_T}{nF} + \frac{T \cdot \Delta S_T}{nF}$$

Since $\Delta S$ for the $H_2$–$O_2$ reaction is **negative** (gas moles decrease from 1.5 → 0), the second term *decreases* $E$ with rising $T$. This is why **high-temperature fuel cells** (SOFC, MCFC) have a **lower theoretical OCV** (~1.0 V) but are compensated by **faster kinetics** and **higher practical operating voltages**.

### 2.2.3 Pressure Dependence (Nernst Equation)

For a gas-feed fuel cell, the reversible EMF is given by the **Nernst equation**:

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{a_{H_2O}}$$

- $E^{0} = 1.229$ V at 25 °C, $P_{H_2} = P_{O_2} = 1$ atm, $a_{H_2O} = 1$ (liquid).
- Increasing reactant partial pressures raises $E$.

### 2.2.4 Fuel Cell Voltage Losses (Polarization)

The actual operating cell voltage $V_{cell}$ is less than $E$ due to **irreversibilities**:

$$V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

| Loss Term | Cause | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Activation polarization** $\eta_{act}$ | Sluggish electrode kinetics (charge-transfer barrier) | Better catalysts (Pt, Pt-alloys), higher T |
| **Ohmic polarization** $\eta_{ohmic}$ | Ionic & electronic resistance in electrolyte, electrodes, interconnects | Thinner electrolyte, high-$\kappa$ materials |
| **Concentration polarization** $\eta_{conc}$ | Mass-transport limitations at high current | Porous electrodes, forced flow, higher pressure |

## 2.3 Classification of Fuel Cells (High-Yield Summary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask comparison tables. Memorize the following:

| Type | Electrolyte | Operating T | Charge Carrier | Efficiency | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AFC** (Alkaline) | KOH (aq) | 60–250 °C | $OH^-$ | 50–60 % | Space (Apollo) |
| **PEMFC** (Proton Exchange Membrane) | Nafion™ | 60–100 °C | $H^+$ | 50–60 % | Cars, buses, portable |
| **PAFC** (Phosphoric Acid) | $H_3PO_4$ | 150–220 °C | $H^+$ | 40–45 % | Stationary CHP |
| **MCFC** (Molten Carbonate) | $(Li,K)_2CO_3$ | 600–700 °C | $CO_3^{2-}$ | 50–60 % | Large stationary |
| **SOFC** (Solid Oxide) | YSZ $(ZrO_2\cdot Y_2O_3)$ | 800–1000 °C | $O^{2-}$ | 50–65 % | Power plants, auxiliaries |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G^{0} = -nFE^{0}$ | Standard free energy ↔ standard EMF |
| 2 | $E^{0} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{0}}{nF}$ | Standard cell voltage (e.g., $237200/(2\cdot 96485) = 1.229$ V) |
| 3 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{0}}{\Delta H^{0}} = 1 - \dfrac{T\Delta S^{0}}{\Delta H^{0}}$ | Theoretical (cold) efficiency |
| 4 | $\eta_{pract} = \dfrac{nF \cdot V_{cell}}{\Delta H}$ | Practical (voltage-based) efficiency |
| 5 | $E = E^{0} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln \dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{a_{H_2O}}$ | Nernst equation for H₂/O₂ cell |
| 6 | $V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ | Operating voltage with polarisation losses |
| 7 | $\eta_{act,a} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha nF}\ln \dfrac{i}{i_{0,a}}$ | Butler–Volmer (Tafel approximation) for anode |
| 8 | $\eta_{act,c} = \dfrac{RT}{(1-\alpha) nF}\ln \dfrac{i}{i_{0,c}}$ | Butler–Volmer (Tafel approximation) for cathode |
| 9 | $\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot R_{int}$ | Ohmic drop (i = current density, $R_{int}$ = area-specific resistance) |
| 10 | $i_{L} = \dfrac{n F D C_{b}}{\delta}$ | Limiting current density (concentration loss onset) |
| 11 | $E_T = E^{0} + \dfrac{\Delta S^{0}}{nF}(T_{ref} - T)$ | Temperature dependence of reversible voltage (linear approx.) |
| 12 | $P_{max} = n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Stack power output |
| 13 | $\eta_{voltage} = V_{cell}/E$ | Voltage efficiency |
| 14 | $\eta_{faradaic} = I_{useful}/I_{total}$ | Coulombic (Faradaic) efficiency |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise $\Delta G^{0} = -237.2$ kJ/mol** and $E^{0} = 1.229$ V. KTU numericals almost always start from one of these two values.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reversible Cell Voltage from $\Delta G$ (Standard Hydrogen–Oxygen Cell)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-weightage derivation** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

**Given Data (25 °C, 1 atm):**
- Reaction: $H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$
- $\Delta G^{0} = -237.2$ kJ mol⁻¹
- Number of electrons transferred per mole of $H_2$ = 2
- $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹

**Step 1 — Relate electrical work to charge transferred.**

For $n$ moles of electrons passed per mole of reaction, the total charge is $Q = nF$. The maximum electrical work done is $W = Q \cdot E = nFE$.

**Step 2 — Equate electrical work to (negative) free-energy change.**

By the 2nd-Law definition of Gibbs free energy, the maximum non-PV work obtainable from a constant-T, constant-P process equals $-\Delta G$:

$$nFE = -\Delta G$$

**Step 3 — Solve for E.**

$$E = \frac{-\Delta G}{nF}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E^{0} &= \frac{-\left(-237.2 \times 10^{3}\,\text{J mol}^{-1}\right)}{2 \times 96{,}485\,\text{C mol}^{-1}} \\
&= \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}\,\text{V} \\
&= 1.229\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

$$E^{0} = 1.229\,\text{V (at 25°C, 1 atm, liquid water product)}$$

If the product is **water vapour** at 25 °C (1 atm), $\Delta G^{0} = -228.6$ kJ/mol, giving $E^{0} = 1.185$ V.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Efficiency $\eta_{th} = \Delta G / \Delta H$

**Step 1 — Start from the 1st-Law definition of enthalpy.**

For the cell reaction at constant $T$ and $P$:

$$\Delta H = \Delta G + T\Delta S$$

**Step 2 — Define the theoretical (cold) efficiency.**

The maximum electrical work extracted is $-\Delta G$ (per mole of reaction). The total chemical energy released is $-\Delta H$. The ratio is:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{elec}}{-\Delta H} = \frac{-\Delta G}{-\Delta H} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values for H₂/O₂ cell.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th} &= \frac{-237.2}{-285.8} = 0.8299 \\
\Rightarrow \eta_{th} &= 83.0\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.**

The complement $(1 - \eta_{th}) = T\Delta S / \Delta H$ is the fraction of the enthalpy that **must** be released as waste heat — a thermodynamic requirement, not an engineering loss. For the H₂/O₂ cell, ~17 % of $\Delta H$ *always* appears as heat, even with a perfect cell.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Nernst Equation for a Fuel Cell (Pressure Dependence)

**Step 1 — General Nernst equation.**

For the half-cell pair in the cell reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$:

$$E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient** and activities are used for species.

**Step 2 — Identify $Q$ for the cell.**

For $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$, gases are at partial pressures $P_i$ (in atm) and liquid water has activity 1:

$$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}} = \frac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Nernst.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= E^{0} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{0.5}}\right) \\
  &= E^{0} - \frac{RT}{2F} \left[-\ln\!\left(P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{0.5}\right)\right] \\
  &= E^{0} + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{0.5}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form.**

$$\boxed{\,E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{0.5}\right)\,}$$

**Step 5 — Verify at standard conditions.** $P_{H_2} = P_{O_2} = 1$ atm → $\ln(1) = 0$ → $E = E^{0} = 1.229$ V ✓

**Step 6 — Worked example:** At $T = 353$ K (80 °C), $P_{H_2} = 3$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 1$ atm (air at ~21 % O₂):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 1.229 + \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485}\,\ln\!\left(3 \times 1^{0.5}\right) \\
  &= 1.229 + 0.01521 \times \ln(3) \\
  &= 1.229 + 0.01521 \times 1.0986 \\
  &= 1.229 + 0.0167 \\
  &= 1.2457\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Voltage Efficiency and Practical Stack Power

**Step 1 — Voltage efficiency.**

$$\eta_{V} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E_{rev}}$$

For a typical PEMFC, $V_{cell} = 0.7$ V, $E_{rev} = 1.229$ V → $\eta_V = 0.57 = 57\,\%$.

**Step 2 — Stack design equation.**

A single fuel cell delivers ~0.6–0.8 V. To achieve a usable voltage $V_{stack}$:

$$n_{cells} = \frac{V_{stack}}{V_{cell}}$$

**Step 3 — Power output.**

$$P_{stack} = V_{stack} \cdot I = n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$$

**Step 4 — Worked example:** A PEMFC stack is required to deliver 60 V at 100 A, with $V_{cell} = 0.75$ V.

- $n_{cells} = 60/0.75 = 80$ cells.
- $P_{stack} = 60 \times 100 = 6{,}000$ W = 6 kW.
- Stack current $I = 100$ A → per-cell current density $i = 100 / A_{electrode}$ A cm⁻².

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Polarization Loss Expressions

| Loss Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Origin | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| Activation (anode) | $\eta_{act,a} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha_a nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_{0,a}}\right)$ | Charge-transfer barrier | 50–200 mV |
| Activation (cathode) | $\eta_{act,c} = \dfrac{RT}{(1-\alpha_c) nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_{0,c}}\right)$ | ORR is very sluggish (largest loss in PEMFC) | 300–500 mV |
| Ohmic | $\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$ | Membrane + contact resistance | 30–100 mV |
| Concentration | $\eta_{conc} = -\dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_{L}}\right)$ | Reactant depletion near electrode | 50–200 mV at high i |

> [!NOTE]
> In a **PEM fuel cell**, the **cathode activation loss dominates** because the Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) is intrinsically slow ($\sim 10^{-10}$ A cm⁻² exchange current density on Pt). This is why Pt-loading research is so active.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Working Principle — Block-Level Functional Flow of a Generic H₂/O₂ Fuel Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hydrogen Storage Tank] --> B[Pressure Regulator]
    B --> C[Humidifier]
    C --> D[Anode Flow Field Plate]
    E[Air Blower] --> F[Cathode Flow Field Plate]
    D --> G[Anode Catalyst Layer Pt]
    F --> H[Cathode Catalyst Layer Pt]
    G --> I[Proton Exchange Membrane Nafion]
    H --> I
    I --> J[External Load DC]
    J --> K[DC Bus Inverter AC]
    K --> L[End User AC or DC]
    M[Anode Side Reaction H2 to 2H plus 2e] --> G
    N[Cathode Side Reaction Half O2 plus 2H plus 2e to H2O] --> H
    O[Water and Heat Removal] --> H
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Hydrogen enters from the left tank, is humidified, and is distributed over the anode catalyst.
- Air enters from the right, distributed over the cathode catalyst.
- Protons ($H^+$) cross the membrane from anode to cathode; electrons are forced through the external load (work).
- Water and heat are removed at the cathode outlet.

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Fuel Cell Power System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Fuel Supply H2] --> B1[Reformer if needed]
    A2[Air Supply] --> C1[Compressor]
    B1 --> D1[FC Stack]
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[DC DC Converter]
    E1 --> F1[Inverter DC to AC]
    F1 --> G1[Grid or Load]
    D1 --> H1[Thermal Management Cooling]
    D1 --> I1[Water Management]
    H1 --> J1[Heat Exchanger]
    I1 --> K1[Condenser and Separator]
```

**Explanation of subsystems:**
- **Fuel processing (reformer)** — required only for hydrocarbon fuels (natural gas, methanol); pure $H_2$ systems omit it.
- **Thermal management** — critical in PEMFC (< 80 °C) and SOFC (~1000 °C) to maintain optimal temperature.
- **Water management** — PEMFC membranes must stay hydrated; in SOFC, water is produced at the anode and must be swept out.

## 4.3 Schematic of Cell Voltage vs. Current Density (Polarization Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low current region] --> B[Activation Polarization dominant]
    B --> C[Intermediate current region]
    C --> D[Ohmic Polarization linear loss]
    D --> E[High current region]
    E --> F[Concentration Polarization sharp drop]
    F --> G[Limiting Current Density iL]
    H[Open Circuit Voltage E rev about 1.23 V] --> A
    I[Operating Point i op V op] --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **three regions** of a fuel cell $V$–$i$ (or $V$–$j$ where $j$ is current density) curve correspond exactly to the three loss terms in the equation $V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$.

## 4.4 Classification of Fuel Cells (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fuel Cells] --> B[Low Temperature below 250 C]
    A --> C[High Temperature above 600 C]
    B --> D[Alkaline AFC KOH electrolyte]
    B --> E[Acid PEMFC Nafion]
    B --> F[Acid PAFC H3PO4]
    C --> G[MCFC Molten Carbonate]
    C --> H[SOFC YSZ Solid Oxide]
    D --> I[Space Applications Apollo]
    E --> J[Transport Cars Buses]
    F --> K[Stationary CHP Plants]
    G --> L[Large Scale Power Generation]
    H --> M[Distributed Generation and Hybrid]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define a fuel cell. How is it distinguished from a conventional battery?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A fuel cell is an **electrochemical device** that directly converts the chemical free energy of a continuously fed fuel (commonly hydrogen) and an oxidant (commonly oxygen) into **DC electrical energy**, with water and heat as by-products.
**Distinction from a battery:**
- A battery is a **closed, energy-storage** system — once reactants are consumed, the cell must be recharged or discarded.
- A fuel cell is an **open, energy-conversion** system — reactants are supplied externally; the cell can produce power indefinitely as long as fuel and oxidant are fed. (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for distinction)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Write the half-cell reactions and the overall reaction for a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell using (i) an acid electrolyte and (ii) an alkaline electrolyte.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**(i) Acid electrolyte (1.5 marks):**
- Anode: $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$
- Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$
- Overall: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$

**(ii) Alkaline electrolyte (1.5 marks):**
- Anode: $H_2 + 2OH^- \rightarrow 2H_2O + 2e^-$
- Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow 2OH^-$
- Overall: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (14-mark questions):**
> - **Loses 2–3 marks** for not writing the **standard value of $E^0 = 1.229$ V** explicitly.
> - **Loses 2 marks** if units are missing in final numerical answers (always write V, kJ/mol, A cm⁻², °C, etc.).
> - **Loses 2–3 marks** for confusing $\Delta G$ (electrical work) with $\Delta H$ (total heat of combustion) in efficiency derivations.
> - **Loses 1 mark** for not specifying the **sign convention** (signs of $E$, $\Delta G$ matter!).
> - **Loses 2 marks** for not drawing a **labelled cell diagram** (KTU explicitly tests this for ≥ 7-mark sub-parts).

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q. (a) Derive the Nernst equation for the EMF of a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell. (7 marks)**
> **(b) A PEM fuel cell operates at 80 °C with $P_{H_2} = 3$ atm and $P_{O_2} = 1$ atm. Calculate the reversible cell voltage and the practical efficiency if the operating voltage is 0.70 V. Use $\Delta H^{0} = -285.8$ kJ/mol. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Nernst Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** For the cell reaction $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$, the change in Gibbs free energy is related to the cell EMF by $\Delta G = -nFE$ (electrochemical work relation).

2. **(1 mark)** Combining with the thermodynamic identity $\Delta G = \Delta G^{0} + RT\ln Q$ (where $Q$ is the reaction quotient), we obtain the Nernst equation:

   $$E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

3. **(1 mark)** For the cell reaction, the reaction quotient is $Q = a_{H_2O}/(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}) = 1/(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5})$ (since pure liquid water has unit activity).

4. **(1 mark)** Substituting and rearranging:
   $E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}\right)$

5. **(1 mark)** Verification: at $P_{H_2} = P_{O_2} = 1$ atm, $E = E^{0} = 1.229$ V (25 °C).

6. **(1 mark)** Discussion: increasing $P_{H_2}$ or $P_{O_2}$ raises the reversible EMF logarithmically; this is why high-pressure operation is sometimes used in space and automotive applications.

7. **(1 mark)** Labelled cell diagram (anode, cathode, electrolyte, external load, gas inlets, product water) — as shown in SECTION 4.1.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T = 80\,°C = 353$ K, $P_{H_2} = 3$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 1$ atm, $V_{cell} = 0.70$ V, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹, $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Nernst term (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= E^{0} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{0.5}\right) \\
  &= 1.229 + \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485}\,\ln(3 \times 1) \\
  &= 1.229 + 0.01521 \times 1.0986 \\
  &= 1.2457\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Practical efficiency formula (1 mark):**

$$\eta_{pract} = \frac{nF \cdot V_{cell}}{-\Delta H^{0}} = \frac{2 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.70}{285.8 \times 10^{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{pract} &= \frac{135{,}079}{285{,}800} = 0.4727 \\
&= 47.3\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical (cold) efficiency for comparison (1 mark):**

$\eta_{th} = \Delta G^{0}/\Delta H^{0} = 237.2/285.8 = 83.0\,\%$

**Step 5 — Voltage efficiency (1 mark):**

$\eta_{V} = V_{cell}/E_{rev} = 0.70/1.2457 = 56.2\,\%$

**Step 6 — Conclude (1 mark):**

Reversible EMF = **1.246 V**; practical efficiency = **47.3 %**; voltage efficiency = **56.2 %**. The gap between practical and theoretical arises from the three polarization losses (activation, ohmic, concentration).

---

### Part B — Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)

> **Q. (a) Explain the classification of fuel cells based on the electrolyte used. Compare the operating temperature, charge carrier, and applications of any five types. (7 marks)**
> **(b) The Gibbs free energy change for the reaction $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)}$ is $-237.2$ kJ/mol at 25 °C. Calculate (i) the standard cell EMF, and (ii) the theoretical efficiency if $\Delta H^{0} = -285.8$ kJ/mol. Comment on the difference between $\Delta G$ and $\Delta H$. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Classification (7 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** Fuel cells are classified by the **type of electrolyte** they use, which determines the operating temperature, the migrating ion, and the application domain.
2. **(5 marks — 1 mark each for the following types):**

| Type | Electrolyte | Operating T | Charge Carrier | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AFC** | KOH (aqueous) | 60–250 °C | $OH^-$ | Space (Apollo, Shuttle) |
| **PEMFC** | Nafion (solid polymer) | 60–100 °C | $H^+$ | Automobiles, buses, portable |
| **PAFC** | $H_3PO_4$ | 150–220 °C | $H^+$ | Stationary CHP |
| **MCFC** | $(Li,K)_2CO_3$ (molten) | 600–700 °C | $CO_3^{2-}$ | Large utility power plants |
| **SOFC** | YSZ ($ZrO_2$–$Y_2O_3$) | 800–1000 °C | $O^{2-}$ | Distributed generation, hybrids |

3. **(1 mark)** **Discussion / comparison summary:**
   - Low-T cells (AFC, PEMFC, PAFC) need **precious-metal catalysts** (Pt) but offer **fast start-up** and are suitable for transport.
   - High-T cells (MCFC, SOFC) tolerate **CO and hydrocarbons** as fuel (internal reforming possible) and use **cheap Ni catalysts**, but have slow start-up and material-degradation challenges.
   - PEMFC and SOFC are the two most commercially promising technologies.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Standard EMF (2 marks):**

Using $E^{0} = -\Delta G^{0}/(nF)$ with $n=2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol:

$$
E^{0} = \frac{237.2 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970} = 1.229\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta G^{0}}{\Delta H^{0}} = \frac{-237.2}{-285.8} = 0.830 = 83.0\,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Comment on $\Delta G$ vs $\Delta H$ (3 marks):**

- $\Delta H$ is the **total heat released** at constant pressure if the reaction proceeded by complete combustion (the enthalpy change).
- $\Delta G$ is the **maximum useful electrical work** obtainable from the reaction; it is always **less** (in magnitude) than $\Delta H$ for a spontaneous reaction that produces a decrease in entropy.
- The difference $T\Delta S = \Delta H - \Delta G = -48.6$ kJ/mol represents the **minimum waste heat** that *must* be released to the surroundings — it is a **thermodynamic floor**, not an engineering loss.
- For the $H_2$–$O_2$ reaction, the entropy decreases (gas moles reduce from 1.5 → 0), so $\Delta S < 0$, making $T\Delta S < 0$ and $-\Delta G < -\Delta H$. This is why the maximum electrical efficiency of a fuel cell is *intrinsically* capped at ~83 % for this reaction — even with a perfect, lossless cell.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise these 12 points before walking into the KTU exam hall:**

1. **Definition**: A fuel cell is an *open* electrochemical converter; a battery is a *closed* energy store.
2. **Standard H₂/O₂ EMF** at 25 °C, 1 atm, liquid water = **1.229 V** (with vapour: 1.185 V).
3. **Standard free energy** $\Delta G^{0} = -237.2$ kJ/mol; **standard enthalpy** $\Delta H^{0} = -285.8$ kJ/mol.
4. **Theoretical efficiency** $\eta_{th} = \Delta G^{0}/\Delta H^{0} = \mathbf{83\,\%}$ for H₂/O₂ — *not* Carnot-limited.
5. **Nernst equation for fuel cell:** $E = E^{0} + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln(P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{0.5})$.
6. **Polarisation losses** — three types: **activation, ohmic, concentration** (always write all three).
7. **Cathode activation loss is the largest** in PEMFC (slow ORR kinetics) — Pt catalysis is critical.
8. **Five types of fuel cells** — AFC, PEMFC, PAFC, MCFC, SOFC — know the electrolyte, charge carrier, and operating T of *each*.
9. **High-T cells (MCFC, SOFC)** use $CO_3^{2-}$ and $O^{2-}$ as charge carriers; low-T cells use $H^+$ or $OH^-$.
10. **SOFC** uses YSZ electrolyte; **PEMFC** uses Nafion; **MCFC** uses molten carbonates; **AFC** uses KOH; **PAFC** uses $H_3PO_4$.
11. **PEMFC** is the technology of choice for **transportation** (Toyota Mirai); **SOFC** dominates **stationary** applications.
12. **Sign convention** — $\Delta G$ is *negative* for a spontaneous cell reaction; $E$ is *positive* for a galvanic cell; *never* drop the sign.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Hall Tip:** In every 14-mark fuel cell answer, **always draw a labelled cell diagram** showing anode, cathode, electrolyte, gas inlets, and product water output. Examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for this, and many students lose marks by skipping it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ MODULE 3: ELECTRICAL ASPECTS OF ELECTROLYTES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Electrical" in Electrochemistry?

In **Electrochemical Engineering**, the term *"Electrical"* under Module 3 refers to the **electrical behavior of electrolytic solutions** — specifically how ionic solutions conduct electricity, how that conduction is quantified, how it varies with concentration, and how individual ion contributions are measured.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Electrolytic conductance* is the ability of an electrolyte solution to conduct electric current through the **migration of positively charged cations toward the cathode and negatively charged anions toward the anode** under the influence of an applied electric field. It is the reciprocal of electrolytic resistance and is governed by the number, charge, and mobility of ions present in the solution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Way Highway

Imagine a **two-way highway tunnel** with cars (ions) moving in opposite directions simultaneously:
- **Cations (Na⁺, K⁺, H⁺)** = cars traveling toward the cathode (right-bound)
- **Anions (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, OH⁻)** = cars traveling toward the anode (left-bound)
- **Total current carried** = sum of BOTH directions of traffic combined

Just as traffic flow depends on the **number of cars, the width of the tunnel, and their speed**, electrolytic conductance depends on **ion concentration, charge, and ionic mobility**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Bold Constants & Metrics)

- **Faraday's Constant (F) = 96,485 C mol⁻¹** (≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹) — charge carried by one mole of electrons.
- **Avogadro's Number (Nₐ) = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**
- **Electronic Charge (e) = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- **SI Unit of Conductance: Siemens (S)** = Ω⁻¹ (reciprocal ohm, also called "mho")
- **SI Unit of Conductivity: S m⁻¹** (or S cm⁻¹ in older CGS formulations)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Students frequently confuse **Conductance (G)** with **Conductivity (κ, kappa)**. 
> - Conductance **G** depends on the *cell geometry* (varies with container). 
> - Conductivity **κ** is an *intrinsic property* of the electrolyte (geometry-independent). 
> This is the **exact same distinction** as Resistance vs. Resistivity!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Framework — How Electrolytic Conduction Works

The conduction process in electrolytes follows a structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Ionization:** The electrolyte (e.g., NaCl) dissolves and dissociates into cations (Na⁺) and anions (Cl⁻).
- **Step 2 — Field Application:** When an external EMF is applied across inert electrodes, an electric field **E** is established in the solution.
- **Step 3 — Migration:** Cations migrate toward the cathode; anions migrate toward the anode. Both contribute to current.
- **Step 4 — Charge Transfer:** At electrodes, ions undergo oxidation/reduction (Faraday's Laws govern the amount of substance reacted).
- **Step 5 — Quantification:** The total current **I** is the sum of cationic current **I₊** and anionic current **I₋**.

### 2.2 Core Quantities — Definitions and Relationships

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Formula | Unit |
|----------|--------|------------|---------|------|
| Resistance | R | Opposition to current flow | $R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{l}{A}$ | Ω (ohm) |
| Conductance | G | Reciprocal of resistance | $G = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | S (Siemens) |
| Resistivity | ρ | Intrinsic resistance | $\rho = R \cdot \dfrac{A}{l}$ | Ω·m |
| Conductivity (specific) | κ | Intrinsic conductance | $\kappa = G \cdot \dfrac{l}{A} = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | S m⁻¹ |
| Cell Constant | K* | Geometry factor of cell | $K^* = \dfrac{l}{A}$ | m⁻¹ |
| Molar Conductivity | Λₘ | Conductivity per mole of electrolyte | $\Lambda_m = \dfrac{\kappa}{c}$ | S m² mol⁻¹ |
| Equivalent Conductivity | Λₑq | Conductivity per equivalent of electrolyte | $\Lambda_{eq} = \dfrac{\kappa}{N}$ where N = normality | S m² eq⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion:** $\Lambda_{eq} = \Lambda_m \times n$ where **n** is the total number of positive (or negative) charges per formula unit. Example: For Al₂(SO₄)₃, n = 6, so $\Lambda_{eq} = \Lambda_m \times 6$.

### 2.3 Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration of Ions (CRITICAL FOR KTU EXAMS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Kohlrausch's Law Statement:** *At infinite dilution, the molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of the limiting molar conductivities of its constituent cations and anions, each multiplied by the number of ions produced per formula unit.*

$$\Lambda_m^{\circ} = \nu_+ \cdot \lambda_+^{\circ} + \nu_- \cdot \lambda_-^{\circ}$$

Where:
- $\Lambda_m^{\circ}$ = limiting molar conductivity at infinite dilution
- $\nu_+, \nu_-$ = number of cations and anions per formula unit
- $\lambda_+^{\circ}, \lambda_-^{\circ}$ = limiting molar conductivities of individual ions

### 2.4 Transport Number (Transference Number) — t₊ and t₋

The **transport number** is the fraction of total current carried by a particular ion:

$$t_+ = \dfrac{I_+}{I_+ + I_-} = \dfrac{\text{Current carried by cations}}{\text{Total current}}$$

$$t_- = \dfrac{I_-}{I_+ + I_-} = \dfrac{\text{Current carried by anions}}{\text{Total current}}$$

**Fundamental Identity:**

$$t_+ + t_- = 1$$

**Relation to ionic mobilities:**

$$t_+ = \dfrac{u_+}{u_+ + u_-} \quad ; \quad t_- = \dfrac{u_-}{u_+ + u_-}$$

Where $u_+$ and $u_-$ are the **absolute ionic mobilities** (m² V⁻¹ s⁻¹).

### 2.5 Variation of Molar Conductivity with Dilution (Debye-Hückel-Onsager Equation)

$$\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^{\circ} - A\sqrt{c}$$

- **A** = constant (depends on solvent, temperature)
- **c** = molar concentration
- For strong electrolytes, plot of $\Lambda_m$ vs $\sqrt{c}$ is **linear** (extrapolates to $\Lambda_m^{\circ}$ at c → 0)
- For weak electrolytes, the curve **rises sharply** near infinite dilution

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where these concepts are applied in industry:**
> - **Battery Design:** Optimizing electrolyte conductivity to minimize internal resistance and maximize power density.
> - **Electroplating Baths:** Maintaining precise conductivity for uniform metal deposition.
> - **Water Purification (Electrodialysis):** Selective ion transport using transport number principles.
> - **Corrosion Monitoring:** Conductivity measurements detect ion leaching in concrete/steel structures.
> - **Electrolytic Production of Al, Na, Cl₂:** Industrial cells designed based on Kohlrausch's data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conductivity from Cell Geometry

**Starting Premise (Ohm's Law for Electrolytic Cell):**

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{l}{A}$$

**Step 1:** Take the reciprocal of both sides to get conductance:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{\rho} \cdot \frac{A}{l}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $G = 1/R$ and $\kappa = 1/\rho$:

$$G = \kappa \cdot \frac{A}{l}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate conductivity (the measurable quantity):

$$\kappa = G \cdot \frac{l}{A}$$

**Step 4:** Define the **Cell Constant** $K^* = l/A$:

$$\boxed{\kappa = G \cdot K^* = \frac{K^*}{R}}$$

**Conversion to S m⁻¹:** If $K^*$ is in m⁻¹ and R in ohms, then κ is in S m⁻¹.

## 3.2 Derivation: Relation between Molar and Equivalent Conductivity

**Given:**
- $c$ = molar concentration (mol L⁻¹ or mol m⁻³)
- $N$ = normality (equivalents per L)
- $n$ = n-factor (total positive charge per formula unit)

**Step 1:** Express equivalent concentration in terms of molar concentration:

$$N = n \cdot c$$

**Step 2:** Write the definition of equivalent conductivity:

$$\Lambda_{eq} = \frac{\kappa}{N}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $N = n \cdot c$:

$$\Lambda_{eq} = \frac{\kappa}{n \cdot c}$$

**Step 4:** Use the definition of molar conductivity $\Lambda_m = \kappa / c$:

$$\Lambda_{eq} = \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{\kappa}{c} = \frac{\Lambda_m}{n}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange (multiply both sides by n):

$$\boxed{\Lambda_m = n \cdot \Lambda_{eq}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem — Application of Kohlrausch's Law

**Problem Statement:**
Given the following limiting molar conductivities (in S cm² mol⁻¹):
- $\lambda_{Na^+}^{\circ} = 50.1$
- $\lambda_{Cl^-}^{\circ} = 76.3$
- $\lambda_{CH_3COO^-}^{\circ} = 40.9$

Calculate the limiting molar conductivity of **CH₃COONa** (sodium acetate) and then determine the limiting molar conductivity of **acetic acid (CH₃COOH)** if $\lambda_{H^+}^{\circ} = 349.6$ S cm² mol⁻¹.

---

### Solution:

**Part A — Molar conductivity of CH₃COONa:**

According to Kohlrausch's Law:

$$\Lambda_m^{\circ}(CH_3COONa) = \lambda_{CH_3COO^-}^{\circ} + \lambda_{Na^+}^{\circ}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the given values:

$$\Lambda_m^{\circ}(CH_3COONa) = 40.9 + 50.1$$

**Step 2:** Compute the result:

$$\boxed{\Lambda_m^{\circ}(CH_3COONa) = 91.0 \; \text{S cm}^2\text{ mol}^{-1}}$$

---

**Part B — Molar conductivity of CH₃COOH:**

Kohlrausch's Law for acetic acid:

$$\Lambda_m^{\circ}(CH_3COOH) = \lambda_{CH_3COO^-}^{\circ} + \lambda_{H^+}^{\circ}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the given values:

$$\Lambda_m^{\circ}(CH_3COOH) = 40.9 + 349.6$$

**Step 2:** Compute the result:

$$\boxed{\Lambda_m^{\circ}(CH_3COOH) = 390.5 \; \text{S cm}^2\text{ mol}^{-1}}$$

**Alternative derivation (using strong electrolytes):**

$$\Lambda_m^{\circ}(CH_3COOH) = \Lambda_m^{\circ}(CH_3COONa) + \Lambda_m^{\circ}(HCl) - \Lambda_m^{\circ}(NaCl)$$

This elegant form is the **classic KTU exam application** of Kohlrausch's Law for weak electrolytes whose $\Lambda_m^{\circ}$ cannot be directly measured by extrapolation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Solvability Guide

| If the Question Asks... | You Should Use... | Watch Out For... |
|--------------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| To find κ | $\kappa = G \cdot K^* = K^*/R$ | Unit of K* (cm⁻¹ vs m⁻¹) |
| To find $\Lambda_m$ | $\Lambda_m = \kappa / c$ | Convert c to mol m⁻³ for SI units |
| To find $\Lambda_{eq}$ | $\Lambda_{eq} = \kappa / N$ or $\Lambda_m / n$ | Identify n-factor correctly |
| To find $\Lambda_m^{\circ}$ of weak electrolyte | Kohlrausch's Law with strong electrolytes | Sign of addition — never subtract ions! |
| To find transport number | $t_+ = I_+/(I_+ + I_-)$ | Must satisfy $t_+ + t_- = 1$ |
| Mobility relation | $t_+ = u_+/(u_+ + u_-)$ | Mobilities have same units before taking ratio |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Electrolytic Conduction Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Source / EMF Applied] --> B[Electric Field E Established in Electrolyte]
    B --> C{Cations Na+ K+ H+}
    B --> D{Anions Cl- SO42- OH-}
    C --> E[Migration Toward CATHODE -]
    D --> F[Migration Toward ANODE +]
    E --> G[Reduction at Cathode: M+ + e- to M]
    F --> H[Oxidation at Anode: X- to X + e-]
    G --> I[Current I+ in Solution]
    H --> J[Current I- in Solution]
    I --> K[Total Current Itotal = I+ + I-]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Quantified by Conductance G = 1 slash R]
    L --> M[Intrinsic Property: Conductivity kappa]
    M --> N[Molar Conductivity Lambda m = kappa slash c]
    N --> O[Limiting Value Lambda m deg via Kohlrausch]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Relationship Between Conductivity Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electrolyte Solution] --> B[Resistance R in ohms]
    A --> C[Conductance G in Siemens]
    B -.Reciprocal.- C
    A --> D[Resistivity rho in ohm m]
    A --> E[Conductivity kappa in S per m]
    D -.Reciprocal.- E
    E --> F[Cell Constant K asterisk = l slash A]
    F --> G[kappa = G times K asterisk]
    E --> H[Molar Conductivity Lambda m]
    H --> I[Lambda m = kappa slash c]
    E --> J[Equivalent Conductivity Lambda eq]
    J --> K[Lambda eq = kappa slash N]
    H -.Lambda m = n times Lambda eq.- J
    H --> L[Lambda m deg at Infinite Dilution]
    L --> M[Kohlrausch Law: Sum of Ionic Contributions]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Kohlrausch's Law Application Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Target Electrolyte] --> Q1{Is it Strong or Weak?}
    Q1 -->|Strong Electrolyte| S1[Direct Extrapolation of Lambda m vs sqrt c]
    Q1 -->|Weak Electrolyte| W1[Cannot Extrapolate Directly]
    W1 --> W2[Identify 3 Strong Electrolytes]
    W2 --> W3[Form Linear Combination of Their Lambda m deg]
    W3 --> W4[Apply Kohlrausch Independent Migration Principle]
    W4 --> W5[Compute Lambda m deg of Target Weak Electrolyte]
    S1 --> Done[Result Obtained]
    W5 --> Done
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Transport Number Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Total[Total Current Itotal] --> Cat[Current by Cation I+]
    Total --> An[Current by Anion I-]
    Cat --> Tplus[t+ = I+ slash Itotal]
    An --> Tminus[t- = I- slash Itotal]
    Tplus --> Sum[t+ + t- = 1]
    Tminus --> Sum
    Cat --> Mob1[u+ Absolute Mobility of Cation]
    An --> Mob2[u- Absolute Mobility of Anion]
    Mob1 --> MobR[t+ = u+ slash u+ plus u-]
    Mob2 --> MobR
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define equivalent conductivity. How is it related to molar conductivity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Equivalent conductivity ($\Lambda_{eq}$) of a solution is defined as the conductivity of a solution containing **one gram-equivalent of the electrolyte** placed between two electrodes **one centimeter (or one meter in SI) apart**.

$$\Lambda_{eq} = \frac{\kappa}{N}$$

where κ = specific conductivity and N = normality.

**Relation to Molar Conductivity (2 Marks):**

$$\Lambda_{eq} = \frac{\Lambda_m}{n} \quad \text{or} \quad \Lambda_m = n \cdot \Lambda_{eq}$$

where **n** is the n-factor (total positive/negative charge per formula unit of the electrolyte). For example, for Al₂(SO₄)₃, n = 6.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State Kohlrausch's Law of independent migration of ions. Mention its one application.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (2 Marks):** *At infinite dilution, the molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of the limiting molar conductivities of its constituent ions, each multiplied by the number of ions produced per formula unit.*

$$\Lambda_m^{\circ} = \nu_+ \lambda_+^{\circ} + \nu_- \lambda_-^{\circ}$$

**Application (1 Mark):** It is used to determine the **limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes** (e.g., acetic acid) which cannot be obtained by direct extrapolation since they do not fully dissociate at moderate concentrations.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **specific conductivity, molar conductivity, and concentration** of an electrolyte solution. Explain why the **cell constant** must be determined before measuring specific conductivity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **variation of molar conductivity with dilution** for both **strong and weak electrolytes**. Sketch the curves and state the **Debye-Hückel-Onsager equation**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Q.A(a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Definitions (2 Marks):**

- **Specific conductivity (κ):** The reciprocal of resistivity; represents the ability of a 1 m³ cube of solution to conduct electricity. Unit: S m⁻¹.
- **Molar conductivity ($\Lambda_m$):** The conducting power of all the ions produced by **one mole** of an electrolyte dissolved in a given volume of solution. Unit: S m² mol⁻¹.

**Step 2 — Derivation (3 Marks):**

Consider **V cubic meters** of solution containing **1 mole** of electrolyte.

Concentration in mol m⁻³:

$$c = \frac{1}{V} \implies V = \frac{1}{c}$$

Specific conductance per unit volume = κ. For volume V, total conductance:

$$G = \kappa \cdot V = \kappa \cdot \frac{1}{c}$$

By definition, molar conductivity = conductance × volume (containing 1 mole):

$$\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa}{c}$$

Rearranging:

$$\kappa = \Lambda_m \cdot c$$

**Step 3 — Why Cell Constant Must Be Determined (2 Marks):**

The cell constant $K^* = l/A$ depends on the **exact geometry** of the conductivity cell (distance between electrodes, area of electrodes), which cannot be measured directly with high accuracy. It is therefore determined **experimentally** using a standard KCl solution of known κ:

$$K^* = R_{KCl} \cdot \kappa_{KCl}$$

Once known, the cell constant is used to find κ of unknown solutions via $\kappa = K^*/R_{measured}$.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Q.A(b) [7 Marks]

**Strong Electrolytes (3 Marks):**

For strong electrolytes (e.g., KCl, NaCl, HCl) which are **completely ionized** at all concentrations:
- Molar conductivity **decreases linearly** with $\sqrt{c}$ (square root of concentration).
- This is due to **interionic effects** — the ionic atmosphere slows down ion mobility.
- On extrapolation to c → 0, the curve gives $\Lambda_m^{\circ}$ on the y-axis.

**Weak Electrolytes (2 Marks):**

For weak electrolytes (e.g., CH₃COOH, NH₄OH):
- The decrease is **sharp and non-linear**.
- At low concentrations, degree of dissociation (α) increases rapidly, increasing $\Lambda_m$.
- Cannot extrapolate sharply rising curve; **Kohlrausch's Law** is used instead.

**Debye-Hückel-Onsager Equation (2 Marks):**

$$\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^{\circ} - A\sqrt{c}$$

where A is a constant depending on solvent and temperature. Valid only for **strong electrolytes at low concentrations**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** Define **transport number**. Derive the relation between transport number and **ionic mobility**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A solution of AgNO₃ is electrolyzed. Given that the transport number of Ag⁺ is **0.47**, calculate:
- (i) Transport number of NO₃⁻
- (ii) The amount of Ag⁺ (in moles) deposited when **0.5 faradays** of charge is passed, accounting for the actual current fraction.
**(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Q.B(a) [7 Marks]

**Definition (2 Marks):** The **transport number (transference number)** of an ion is defined as the fraction of the total current carried by that ion through the electrolytic solution.

$$t_+ = \frac{\text{Quantity of charge carried by cations}}{\text{Total quantity of charge passed}}$$

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

Consider an electrolyte of concentration c (mol m⁻³), dissociating into ν₊ cations and ν₋ anions.

**Step 1:** Current carried by cation:

$$I_+ = z_+ \cdot F \cdot c \cdot u_+ \cdot A \cdot (\nu_+/\nu_{total})$$

(where z₊ = charge, A = electrode area, E = field, u₊ = mobility)

**Step 2:** Similarly for anion:

$$I_- = z_- \cdot F \cdot c \cdot u_- \cdot A \cdot (\nu_-/\nu_{total})$$

**Step 3:** Total current:

$$I = I_+ + I_-$$

**Step 4:** Taking the ratio for cation:

$$t_+ = \frac{I_+}{I_+ + I_-} = \frac{u_+}{u_+ + u_-}$$

(Since $\nu_+ z_+ = \nu_- z_- = 1$ for binary electrolyte)

**Step 5:** Similarly for anion:

$$t_- = \frac{u_-}{u_+ + u_-}$$

And by definition: $t_+ + t_- = 1$

---

#### 📝 Model Solution — Q.B(b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $t_{Ag^+} = 0.47$, Charge passed = 0.5 F

**(i) Transport number of NO₃⁻ (1 Mark):**

$$t_{NO_3^-} = 1 - t_{Ag^+} = 1 - 0.47 = \boxed{0.53}$$

**(ii) Moles of Ag⁺ deposited (6 Marks):**

**Step 1:** In an electrochemical cell, the **actual charge** used to deposit Ag⁺ is only the fraction carried by Ag⁺:

$$Q_{Ag^+} = t_{Ag^+} \times Q_{total}$$

**Step 2:** Total charge passed:

$$Q_{total} = 0.5 \; F$$

**Step 3:** Charge carried by Ag⁺ ions:

$$Q_{Ag^+} = 0.47 \times 0.5 \; F = 0.235 \; F$$

**Step 4:** Reaction at cathode: $Ag^+ + e^- \to Ag$ (1 mole electrons deposits 1 mole Ag)

By Faraday's First Law:

$$\text{Moles of Ag deposited} = \frac{Q_{Ag^+}}{F} = \frac{0.235 \; F}{F} = \boxed{0.235 \; \text{mol}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Do NOT** assume all 0.5 F is used for Ag⁺ deposition. Students who directly write 0.5 mol lose **4 out of 7 marks** in part (ii). Always multiply by the transport number.
> 2. **Sign convention:** If the problem states Ag⁺ moves toward cathode, the reaction $Ag^+ + e^- \to Ag$ is reduction. Don't confuse anode/cathode.
> 3. **Units:** Always report κ in S m⁻¹ and $\Lambda_m$ in S m² mol⁻¹ unless specifically asked for CGS units (S cm² mol⁻¹).
> 4. **Kohlrausch application:** When determining $\Lambda_m^{\circ}$ of weak electrolyte, ensure the algebraic combination is **exact** — wrong sign of terms leads to negative conductivity (impossible).
> 5. **Transport number sum:** Always verify $t_+ + t_- = 1$ at the end of the solution. Examiners award 1 bonus mark for this verification step.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — ELECTRICAL (Module 3):**

- ☐ **Conductance (G) = 1/R**, unit Siemens; **Conductivity (κ) = G·K\***, unit S m⁻¹.
- ☐ **Molar Conductivity $\Lambda_m = \kappa/c$**; unit S m² mol⁻¹ (note: NOT S m⁻¹).
- ☐ **Equivalent Conductivity $\Lambda_{eq} = \kappa/N = \Lambda_m/n$** where n is the n-factor.
- ☐ **Kohlrausch's Law** applies to **infinite dilution**; useful for **weak electrolytes** where direct extrapolation fails.
- ☐ **Kohlrausch Equation:** $\Lambda_m^{\circ} = \nu_+ \lambda_+^{\circ} + \nu_- \lambda_-^{\circ}$ — never forget the stoichiometric coefficients.
- ☐ **Transport Number:** $t_+ + t_- = 1$ (always verify); relates directly to ionic mobilities.
- ☐ **Debye-Hückel-Onsager:** $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^{\circ} - A\sqrt{c}$ — valid for **strong electrolytes only** at low concentrations.
- ☐ **Cell Constant** must be calibrated with standard KCl solution — never assume l/A geometrically.
- ☐ **H⁺ and OH⁻ ions have anomalously high molar conductivities** (Grotthuss mechanism) — remember this for Kohlrausch's Law problems.
- ☐ **Strong electrolytes:** linear $\Lambda_m$ vs $\sqrt{c}$; **Weak electrolytes:** sharp non-linear rise.
- ☐ **For $\Lambda_m^{\circ}$ of weak acid HA:** $\Lambda_m^{\circ}(HA) = \Lambda_m^{\circ}(NaA) + \Lambda_m^{\circ}(HCl) - \Lambda_m^{\circ}(NaCl)$.
- ☐ **Industrial applications:** Battery design, electroplating, electrodialysis, corrosion monitoring, chlor-alkali process.

> 🎯 **FINAL EXAM TIP:** KTU questions on this topic are almost always centered around Kohlrausch's Law numerical problems and Transport Number calculations. Master the algebraic combinations and the t⁺/t⁻ application in Faraday's law — these account for **~70% of the marks** in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrochemical Capacitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical Capacitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electrochemical Capacitor (EC)**, also known as a **supercapacitor** or **ultracapacitor**, is an electrochemical energy storage device that stores electrical energy through two reversible mechanisms: (i) electrostatic charge separation at the electrode–electrolyte interface (Electric Double Layer Capacitance, EDLC) and (ii) fast, reversible Faradaic redox reactions occurring at or near the electrode surface (Pseudocapacitance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> Electrochemical capacitors are energy storage devices that bridge the gap between conventional dielectric capacitors (high power, low energy) and batteries/fuel cells (high energy, low power). They store charge either via *non-Faradaic* electric double layer formation or *Faradaic* pseudocapacitive processes at high-surface-area porous electrodes immersed in an electrolyte.

## 1.2 The Three Operational Classes

| Class | Charge Storage Mechanism | Typical Electrode Material |
|---|---|---|
| **EDLC** | Non-Faradaic, electrostatic | Activated Carbon, Carbon Nanotubes, Graphene |
| **Pseudocapacitor** | Faradaic redox (surface/near-surface) | RuO₂, MnO₂, Conducting Polymers |
| **Hybrid Capacitor** | Both EDLC + Pseudocapacitance | Li-ion capacitor, AC//Li₄Ti₅O₁₂ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Molecular Sponge Battery"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine a **molecular sponge** (activated carbon) soaked in salt water (electrolyte). When you apply voltage, ions from the water rush into the millions of tiny pores of the sponge and stick to the surface — *like iron filings clinging to a magnet*. No chemical reaction happens; the ions just **sit there**, electrostatically held.
>
> Now, if the sponge walls were also coated with a thin layer of rust (MnO₂ or RuO₂), the ions would also undergo a *quick chemical handshake* with the surface, storing **extra charge**. That extra storage is the *pseudocapacitive* contribution.
>
> **Key Insight:** Because ions only need to travel **nanometers** to be stored (not diffuse through the bulk like in batteries), charging happens in **seconds**. This is why supercapacitors deliver **megawatts of instantaneous power**.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Specific Capacitance (C_sp)**: 10 – 1500 F/g (commercial: 100–300 F/g for EDLCs)
- **Operating Voltage Window**: **1.0 – 2.85 V** per cell (aqueous → organic electrolytes)
- **Energy Density (E_d)**: 1 – 10 Wh/kg (commercial EDLCs), up to 20–30 Wh/kg for hybrids
- **Power Density (P_d)**: 1 – 100 kW/kg
- **Cycle Life**: > **500,000 cycles** (vs ~1000 for Li-ion)
- **Charge Time**: 1 – 30 seconds (vs minutes/hours for batteries)
- **Coulombic Efficiency**: ≈ **95–99%**
- **Electrolyte Conductivity (κ)**: 0.01 – 0.1 S/cm (organic); up to 1 S/cm (aqueous)
- **Permittivity of Free Space (ε₀)**: 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m

## 1.5 Why Electrochemical Capacitors Matter in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance:**
> 1. **Electric Vehicles (EVs)**: Regenerative braking energy recovery (peak power assist).
> 2. **Renewable Grid Storage**: Smoothing of solar/wind intermittency.
> 3. **Consumer Electronics**: Memory backup, camera flashes.
> 4. **Aerospace & Defence**: Pulse power for railguns, lasers.
> 5. **IoT & Wearables**: Long-life, maintenance-free micro-power sources.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electric Double Layer (EDL) — Origin of EDLC

When a charged electrode is immersed in an electrolyte, **two compact layers of opposite charge** form at the interface, behaving like a parallel-plate capacitor with nanometer-scale separation.

### 2.1.1 Three Theoretical Models of the EDL

**A) Helmholtz Model (1853) — The "Rigid Plane"**
- Ions form a single, immobile layer at distance *d_H* from the electrode.
- Equivalent to a simple parallel-plate capacitor.

**B) Gouy–Chapman Model (1910–1913) — The "Diffuse Cloud"**
- Ions are distributed according to the Boltzmann distribution (thermal agitation).
- Better suited for dilute electrolytes.

**C) Stern Model (1924) — The Combined Picture**
- EDL = Compact (Helmholtz) Layer + Diffuse Layer *in series*.
- This is the **standard model used in modern EC analysis**.

### 2.1.2 The Stern Model Equations

The total differential capacitance is given by:

$$\frac{1}{C_{dl}} = \frac{1}{C_H} + \frac{1}{C_{diff}}$$

where:
- **C_H** = Helmholtz (compact) layer capacitance
- **C_diff** = Diffuse layer capacitance
- **C_dl** = Total double-layer capacitance

The Helmholtz capacitance:

$$C_H = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d_H}$$

- **A** = Electrode surface area (m²)
- **d_H** = Effective Helmholtz layer thickness (~0.3–0.5 nm for hydrated ions)
- **ε_r** = Relative permittivity of the inner layer (~6–10 for organic electrolyte)

The diffuse layer capacitance (Gouy–Chapman, low potential limit):

$$C_{diff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \kappa}{L_D}$$

where the **Debye length** (L_D):

$$L_D = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 R T}{2 n_0 F^2 z^2}}$$

- **n₀** = Bulk ion concentration (mol/m³)
- **z** = Ion charge number
- **F** = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- **R** = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **T** = Absolute temperature (K)

## 2.2 Pseudocapacitance — The Faradaic Cousin

Pseudocapacitance arises from **fast, reversible surface redox reactions**:

$$\text{MO}_x + \delta \text{H}^+ + \delta e^- \leftrightarrow \text{MO}_{x-\delta}(\text{OH})_\delta$$

The charge stored follows a continuous Nernstian relation:

$$C_\phi = \frac{dq}{dV} = \frac{nF}{RT} \cdot q_{max}\theta(1-\theta)$$

- **q_max** = Maximum surface redox charge
- **θ** = Fractional coverage

## 2.3 Energy and Power Equations

### Energy Stored (Joules):

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C (\Delta V)^2$$

### Gravimetric Energy Density (Wh/kg):

$$E_d = \frac{1}{2} \frac{C_{sp} (\Delta V)^2}{3.6}$$

### Specific Power (W/kg), with Equivalent Series Resistance ESR:

$$P_d = \frac{(\Delta V)^2}{4 \cdot ESR \cdot m}$$

### Ragone Equation (time constant form):

$$\tau = R_s \cdot C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \rho \cdot L^2}{\kappa}$$

where:
- **R_s** = ESR (Ω)
- **L** = Electrode thickness (m)
- **ρ** = Electrode density (kg/m³)
- **κ** = Effective electrolyte conductivity

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Helmholtz capacitance | $C_H = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d_H}$ | F | Parallel plate model |
| 2 | Stern combined capacitance | $\dfrac{1}{C_{dl}} = \dfrac{1}{C_H} + \dfrac{1}{C_{diff}}$ | F | Series combination |
| 3 | Debye length | $L_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 R T}{2 n_0 F^2 z^2}}$ | m | Diffuse layer thickness |
| 4 | Diffuse layer capacitance | $C_{diff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}{L_D}$ | F/m² | Per unit area |
| 5 | Total energy | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | Single cell |
| 6 | Energy density | $E_d = \dfrac{C_{sp} V^2}{7.2}$ | Wh/kg | Divide by 7.2 = 2×3.6 |
| 7 | Power density | $P_d = \dfrac{V^2}{4 \cdot ESR \cdot m}$ | W/kg | Matched load |
| 8 | Pseudocapacitance | $C_\phi = \dfrac{nF q_{max} \theta (1-\theta)}{RT}$ | F | Surface redox |
| 9 | Time constant | $\tau = R_s C$ | s | RC discharge |
| 10 | Coulombic efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100$ | % | ≥ 95% for ECs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> The Stern model is used in **electrode design software** (e.g., COMSOL) to optimize pore size distribution. A pore smaller than the solvated ion size (~1 nm) is "inactive" — explains why activated carbons are engineered with pores in the **0.7–3 nm** range (mesoporous).

## 2.5 The "Why" and "How" Logic Flow

- **Why EDLC stores more energy than a dielectric capacitor?** → Because *A* is enormous (activated carbon has 1000–3000 m²/g) and *d* is molecular (nanometers). Capacitance scales with A/d → boosts C by factor **10⁴–10⁶**.
- **Why Pseudocapacitors store more than EDLCs?** → Redox reactions transfer **multiple electrons per atom** vs single-layer ion adsorption in EDLCs.
- **Why do ECs charge so fast?** → No solid-state diffusion (unlike batteries). Only **ion migration + surface adsorption**.
- **Why is the voltage window limited?** → Electrolyte decomposition (water: 1.23 V thermodynamic; organic: 2.5–2.85 V).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Helmholtz Capacitance from Poisson's Equation

The Helmholtz model treats the EDL as a parallel plate capacitor with surface charge density **σ** (C/m²) on the electrode and an equal and opposite charge on the solution side at distance **d_H**.

**Step 1:** Gauss's Law for a planar interface gives the electric field between the layers:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

*Logic:* The electric field inside a parallel plate capacitor is uniform and equal to σ/(ε).

**Step 2:** The potential drop across the compact layer is:

$$\Delta V = E \cdot d_H = \frac{\sigma \, d_H}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

*Logic:* Integrating a constant E-field across thickness d_H yields the potential.

**Step 3:** By definition, capacitance per unit area:

$$C_H = \frac{d\sigma}{d(\Delta V)} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}{d_H}$$

*Logic:* Capacitance is the slope of charge vs. voltage.

**Step 4:** Multiplying by the geometric electrode area A:

$$\boxed{C_H = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d_H}}$$

*Logic:* This is the **fundamental Helmholtz equation** taught in KTU Module 3.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Debye Length from Boltzmann–Poisson Coupling

**Step 1:** Ion concentration profile (Boltzmann distribution):

$$n_i(x) = n_{i,\infty} \exp\left(-\frac{z_i F \phi(x)}{RT}\right)$$

*Logic:* Positive ions accumulate where the potential is negative.

**Step 2:** Volume charge density:

$$\rho(x) = \sum_i z_i F c_i(x) = F \sum_i z_i n_{i,\infty} \exp\left(-\frac{z_i F \phi(x)}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3:** One-dimensional Poisson equation:

$$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 4:** Linearizing $\exp$ for small φ (Debye–Hückel approximation: $|zF\phi| \ll RT$):

$$\exp\left(-\frac{zF\phi}{RT}\right) \approx 1 - \frac{zF\phi}{RT}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting and applying bulk electroneutrality (Σ zᵢ c_{i,∞} = 0):

$$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = \frac{\phi}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0} \cdot F \sum_i \frac{z_i^2 n_{i,\infty} F}{RT}$$

**Step 6:** Defining the inverse Debye length κ_D:

$$\kappa_D^2 = \frac{F^2}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 RT} \sum_i z_i^2 n_{i,\infty}$$

**Step 7:** The solution gives the characteristic decay length:

$$\boxed{L_D = \frac{1}{\kappa_D} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 R T}{F^2 \sum_i z_i^2 n_{i,\infty}}}}$$

*Logic:* For a symmetric z:z electrolyte, $\sum z_i^2 n_{i,\infty} = 2 z^2 n_0$, recovering the standard expression.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Density from Capacitance and Voltage

**Step 1:** Work done to move charge dq from one electrode to the other at potential V:

$$dW = V \, dq$$

**Step 2:** For a capacitor with q = CV:

$$dW = V \cdot C \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from 0 to V_max:

$$E = \int_0^{V_{max}} C V \, dV = \frac{1}{2} C V_{max}^2$$

**Step 4:** Specific energy (per mass m):

$$E_d = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{C V^2}{2 m}$$

**Step 5:** Expressing in Wh/kg (1 Wh = 3600 J):

$$E_d \left[\frac{\text{Wh}}{\text{kg}}\right] = \frac{C_{sp} V^2}{2 \times 3600} = \frac{C_{sp} V^2}{7200}$$

*Logic:* Often written as $C_{sp} V^2 / 7.2$ when **C_sp is in F/g** and V in volts.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Board-Style)

> **Problem:** A symmetric electrochemical capacitor uses activated carbon electrodes with surface area 1500 m²/g. The Helmholtz layer thickness is 0.4 nm and the inner-layer dielectric constant is 8. The cell voltage is 2.7 V. Calculate: (a) Specific capacitance (F/g), (b) Specific energy (Wh/kg).

**Given:**
- A = 1500 m²/g = 1500 m²/g
- d_H = 0.4 × 10⁻⁹ m
- ε_r = 8
- ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m
- V = 2.7 V

**Part (a): Specific Capacitance**

$$C_{sp} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d_H}$$

Substituting:

$$C_{sp} = \frac{(8)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1500)}{0.4 \times 10^{-9}}$$

Step-by-step arithmetic:

$$C_{sp} = \frac{8 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1500}{4 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$= \frac{(70.832 \times 10^{-12}) \times 1500}{4 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$= \frac{106,248 \times 10^{-12}}{4 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$= \frac{1.06248 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$= 265.6 \text{ F/g}$$

**Part (b): Specific Energy**

$$E_d = \frac{C_{sp} V^2}{7.2}$$

$$E_d = \frac{265.6 \times (2.7)^2}{7.2} = \frac{265.6 \times 7.29}{7.2}$$

$$E_d = \frac{1936.224}{7.2} \approx 268.9 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

*Note:* Practical ECs achieve lower values (~5–10 Wh/kg) due to inactive mass (binders, separators, current collectors) and pore-accessibility losses.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table for Board Exam

| Property | Dielectric Capacitor | Electrochemical Capacitor (EDLC) | Battery (Li-ion) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Density (Wh/kg) | 0.01 – 0.1 | 1 – 10 | 100 – 250 |
| Power Density (W/kg) | > 10⁵ | 1000 – 100,000 | 100 – 1000 |
| Charge Time | μs – ms | 1 – 30 s | 1 – 5 hours |
| Cycle Life | > 10⁶ | > 500,000 | 500 – 2000 |
| Mechanism | Electrostatic | EDL + Pseudocapacitive | Bulk redox (intercalation) |
| Voltage Window | 100s V | 1 – 2.85 V | 3.6 – 4.2 V |

---
