<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Electric Double Layer Capacitor (EDLC) and Pseudocapacitor.**

**Model Answer:**
- **EDLC:** Stores charge **non-Faradaically** via physical adsorption of ions at the electrode–electrolyte interface; uses high-surface-area carbon materials (activated carbon, graphene); follows the Helmholtz/Stern double-layer model.
- **Pseudocapacitor:** Stores charge **Faradaically** via fast, reversible surface redox reactions; uses metal oxides (RuO₂, MnO₂) or conducting polymers (polyaniline, PEDOT); exhibits Nernstian potential dependence on charge coverage.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the significance of the Debye length (L_D) in electrochemical capacitors.**

**Model Answer:**
The Debye length is the characteristic thickness of the diffuse ionic layer in the EDL, given by:

$$L_D = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 RT}{2n_0 F^2 z^2}}$$

**Significance:**
- Defines the **effective charge separation distance** in the diffuse layer.
- Determines the **capacitance contribution** of the diffuse layer: C_diff = ε_r ε₀ / L_D.
- Decreases with increasing electrolyte concentration — concentrated electrolytes yield thinner diffuse layers and higher capacitance.
- Critical in designing **porous electrodes** — pores smaller than 2 × L_D lose double-layer capacity.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Helmholtz capacitance of an electrochemical capacitor, starting from Gauss's Law. Discuss the assumptions of the Helmholtz model. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A symmetric electrochemical capacitor uses activated carbon electrodes (specific surface area = 1800 m²/g, d_H = 0.5 nm, ε_r = 6). Calculate the specific capacitance (F/g) and the specific energy density (Wh/kg) at 2.5 V operating voltage. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

*Step 1: Statement of Gauss's Law:*
The electric displacement field **D** at the electrode surface equals the surface charge density σ:

$$D = \sigma = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 E$$

*Step 2: Electric field in the compact layer:*

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

*Step 3: Potential drop across Helmholtz layer of thickness d_H:*

$$\Delta V = E \cdot d_H = \frac{\sigma \, d_H}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

*Step 4: Define capacitance per unit area:*

$$C_H = \frac{d\sigma}{d(\Delta V)} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}{d_H}$$

*Step 5: For an electrode of area A:*

$$\boxed{C_H = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d_H}}$$

**Assumptions of the Helmholtz Model:**
1. Ions are treated as **point charges** of finite size.
2. The inner layer has a **uniform dielectric constant** ε_r.
3. The solvent (water/organic) is **immobile** — no thermal diffusion.
4. **No specific adsorption** beyond the IHP.
5. The electrode surface is **smooth and planar** — a major limitation in real porous electrodes.
6. **Linear dielectric response** (ε independent of field).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> Students often confuse d_H with the ionic radius. d_H includes the **solvated ion shell** plus the inner-layer solvent (~0.3–0.5 nm). Writing d_H = ionic radius alone will lose 1 mark.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

*Given:*
- SSA = 1800 m²/g
- d_H = 0.5 × 10⁻⁹ m
- ε_r = 6
- ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m
- V = 2.5 V

*Specific Capacitance:*

$$C_{sp} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \cdot SSA}{d_H}$$

$$= \frac{(6)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1800)}{0.5 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$= \frac{6 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1800}{5 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$= \frac{95,623.2 \times 10^{-12}}{5 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$= \frac{9.56232 \times 10^{-8}}{5 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\boxed{C_{sp} = 191.25 \text{ F/g}}$$

*Specific Energy Density:*

$$E_d = \frac{C_{sp} V^2}{7.2} = \frac{191.25 \times (2.5)^2}{7.2}$$

$$= \frac{191.25 \times 6.25}{7.2} = \frac{1195.31}{7.2}$$

$$\boxed{E_d \approx 166.0 \text{ Wh/kg}}$$

---

### Question B: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the Stern model of the electric double layer. Derive the Debye length expression and discuss its physical significance in EC design. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare EDLCs, pseudocapacitors, and hybrid supercapacitors in terms of charge storage mechanism, energy density, power density, and cycle life. Justify why activated carbon is preferred as the standard EDLC electrode. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Stern Model:**

The Stern model **combines the Helmholtz and Gouy–Chapman** pictures by partitioning the EDL into:
- **Inner (Compact) Helmholtz Layer:** from electrode to IHP, with capacitance C_H.
- **Diffuse (Gouy) Layer:** from OHP to bulk, with capacitance C_diff.

These two layers behave as **capacitors in series**:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{C_{dl}} = \frac{1}{C_H} + \frac{1}{C_{diff}}}$$

*Debye Length Derivation (key steps):*

The Poisson equation in 1D:

$$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

Using Boltzmann distribution for ions:

$$n(x) = n_0 \exp\left(-\frac{zF\phi(x)}{RT}\right)$$

Linearizing for small φ and applying electroneutrality, the potential decays as:

$$\phi(x) = \phi_0 \exp(-x/L_D)$$

where:

$$L_D = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 RT}{2 n_0 F^2 z^2}}$$

**Physical Significance in EC Design:**
- **L_D sets the EDL thickness** — defines the *effective plate separation* in the diffuse region.
- Determines **optimal pore size**: pores must be > 2L_D for ion accessibility (typically 0.7–3 nm for organic electrolytes).
- **Smaller L_D → higher capacitance** → favors concentrated electrolytes (e.g., 1 M TEABF₄ in ACN has L_D ≈ 0.3 nm).
- Used in **pore-size engineering** of activated carbons for matching electrolyte ion dimensions.

**Part (b) — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | EDLC | Pseudocapacitor | Hybrid Capacitor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Electrostatic (EDL) | Faradaic (surface redox) | EDL + Faradaic |
| Typical Material | Activated Carbon | RuO₂, MnO₂, Polyaniline | AC + Li₄Ti₅O₁₂ |
| Energy Density | Moderate (5–10 Wh/kg) | Higher (20–50 Wh/kg) | Highest (30–100 Wh/kg) |
| Power Density | Very High | Moderate | High |
| Cycle Life | > 500,000 | 10,000–50,000 | 50,000+ |
| Voltage Window | 2.7–2.85 V | 1.0–1.5 V | 2.0–3.8 V |

**Why Activated Carbon is Preferred for EDLCs:**
1. **Very high specific surface area**: 1500–3000 m²/g.
2. **Tailorable pore structure**: 0.7–3 nm mesopores match electrolyte ions.
3. **Chemical stability**: inert in most electrolytes.
4. **Low cost**: abundant and easy to produce (coconut shell, pitch).
5. **Wide voltage window**: stable up to 2.85 V in organic electrolyte.
6. **Excellent conductivity**: 10–100 S/m (graphitic domains).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> Students often **confuse the equation forms** — 1/C_total = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ for series (EDL), and C_total = C₁ + C₂ for parallel (parallel electrode contributions). Mixing these will cost 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **EDLC = Non-Faradaic (no electron transfer across interface); Pseudocapacitor = Faradaic (electron transfer).**
- ✅ **Three EDL Models:** Helmholtz (compact only), Gouy–Chapman (diffuse only), **Stern (both — most accurate).**
- ✅ **Key Stern Equation:** 1/C_dl = 1/C_H + 1/C_diff — series combination.
- ✅ **Debye length decreases** with increasing electrolyte concentration and ion valency.
- ✅ **Capacitance boosts:** Increase A (porous carbon), Decrease d_H (use small ions), Increase ε_r (high-k electrolytes).
- ✅ **Energy scales as V²:** Doubling voltage gives 4× energy — this is why organic electrolytes (2.7 V) outperform aqueous (1.0 V).
- ✅ **Power limited by ESR:** Lower ESR → higher power; ESR depends on electrolyte conductivity, electrode thickness, contact resistance.
- ✅ **Specific Energy formula (F/g, V in volts):** E_d = C_sp × V² / 7.2 → Wh/kg.
- ✅ **Activated carbon is the industrial standard** EDLC material — high SSA, stable, cheap.
- ✅ **Hybrid capacitors bridge the gap** between ECs (power) and batteries (energy).
- ✅ **Cycle life > 500,000** because no solid-state phase change occurs — unlike batteries.
- ✅ **Capacitance formula memorization:** C_H = ε_r ε₀ A / d_H (single most-tested equation).
- ✅ **Comparison with batteries:** ECs win on power and cycle life; batteries win on energy density.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Electrochemical Processes: Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Industrial Electrochemical Processes: Fundamentals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Electrochemical Processes** are unit operations in chemical engineering where electrical energy is the primary driving force to drive non-spontaneous chemical transformations (electrolytic processes) or where spontaneous chemical reactions are harvested to produce electrical work (galvanic/electrogenerative processes). They form a unique class of heterogeneous reaction systems governed simultaneously by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**, **electrode kinetics (Butler–Volmer equation)**, and **transport phenomena** (mass, charge, momentum).

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, industrial electrochemistry is positioned as the **intersection of reaction engineering, transport phenomena, and electrochemistry** — applied at a manufacturing scale (kg to kilo-tonnes per day).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tower" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of an electrochemical cell as a **water tower connected to two ponds at different elevations**.
> - The two **electrodes** are the ponds.
> - The **electrolyte** is the pipe connecting them.
> - The **DC power supply** is the pump that lifts water from the lower pond (cathode) to the upper pond (anode) — this is the **electrolytic mode**.
> - If the ponds are at different heights naturally, water flows and can turn a turbine — this is the **galvanic mode** (e.g., a battery).
> - The **flow rate** of water corresponds to **current (Amperes)**.
> - The **height difference** corresponds to **cell voltage (Volts)**.
> - Friction in the pipe = **electrolyte resistance (Ohmic drop, IR)**.
> - Any leaks in the tank = **parasitic/secondary reactions (loss of current efficiency)**.

This single mental model unifies electro-winning, electroplating, chlor-alkali, batteries, and fuel cells.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Industrial Electrochemical Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Classification (High-Yield KTU Framework):**

| Class | Sub-Class | Driving Force | Net ΔG | Industrial Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrolytic** | Electrowinning | External DC | > 0 (Non-spontaneous) | Al, Cu, Zn extraction |
| | Electrorefining | External DC | > 0 (Non-spontaneous) | Cu refining (99.99% purity) |
| | Electroplating | External DC | > 0 (Non-spontaneous) | Cr, Ni, Au plating |
| | Electrosynthesis | External DC | > 0 (Non-spontaneous) | Chlor-alkali, MnO₂, adiponitrile |
| | Electroforming | External DC | > 0 (Non-spontaneous) | Ni stampers, foils |
| **Galvanic** | Primary Battery | Spontaneous redox | < 0 | Zn–MnO₂ (Leclanché) |
| | Secondary Battery | Spontaneous + Reversible | < 0 (discharge) | Lead–acid, Li-ion |
| | Fuel Cell | Continuous feed | < 0 | H₂–O₂, PEMFC |
| **Hybrid** | Electrolytic cleaning | External DC | > 0 | Electrocoagulation, effluent treatment |

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants You Must Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C mol}^{-1} \approx 26.8\ \text{Ah mol}^{-1}$
> - **Elementary charge:** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
> - **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314\ \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
> - **Standard Reference Conditions:** $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$, $P = 1\ \text{atm}$, $a_{\text{ions}} = 1$

---

## 1.5 Why Industrial Electrochemistry Matters (Engineering Relevance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Purity advantage:** Electrorefining produces metals of **> 99.999% purity** — unattainable by pyrometallurgy.
> - **Selectivity:** Electro-synthesis routes (e.g., Monsanto's adiponitrile process) avoid toxic catalysts.
> - **Ambient temperature operation:** Many electrolytic processes run at **60–95 °C** vs. > 1000 °C for pyrometallurgy → lower carbon footprint.
> - **Modularity & scalability:** Adding parallel monopolar cells linearly scales production.
> - **Renewable integration:** Electrowinning powered by solar/wind enables "green metals" (green Al, green H₂).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Governing Pillars

Industrial electrochemical processes are simultaneously governed by **two superposed laws**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 1 — Faraday's Laws (Charge ↔ Mass):** Governs the *stoichiometric* link between current passed and product formed.
> **Pillar 2 — Electrode Kinetics & Ohm's Law (Voltage ↔ Current):** Governs the *energy* required per unit mass produced.

You cannot design an industrial cell using only one — both are coupled.

---

## 2.2 Faraday's Laws — Logic Steps

### First Law (1833)
**Statement:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed.

$$m \;\propto\; Q = I \cdot t$$

### Second Law (1833)
**Statement:** The mass liberated by the same quantity of charge is proportional to the **equivalent weight** of the substance.

$$m \;=\; \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \;=\; \frac{Q}{F}\cdot\frac{M}{n}$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of substance liberated (kg)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $M$ = molar mass (kg mol⁻¹)
- $n$ = electrons transferred per mole of product
- $F$ = Faraday constant (96,485 C mol⁻¹)
- $M/n$ = **equivalent weight (E)**

### Why "Equivalent Weight"?
The **why** is subtle: $n$ electrons must traverse the external circuit *per atom deposited*. So one mole of trivalent Al³⁺ demands **3 moles of electrons**, while monovalent Ag⁺ needs only 1. The factor $n$ normalizes this stoichiometry.

---

## 2.3 Current Efficiency (η) — The Real-World Modifier

In practice, **not every electron** does useful work. Side reactions (H₂ evolution at cathode, O₂ at anode) consume charge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
>
> $$\boxed{\eta_{\text{current}} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \;\times\; 100\% \;=\; \frac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{total}}} \;\times\; 100\%}$$

Typical industrial values:
- **Cu electrorefining:** 95–97%
- **Zn electrowinning:** 88–92%
- **Al extraction (Hall–Héroult):** 88–95%
- **Chlor-alkali membrane:** 95–98%

---

## 2.4 Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

The single most important **economic metric** in industrial electrochemistry.

$$\boxed{\text{SEC} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I \cdot t}{m_{\text{actual}}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{q \cdot \eta}$$

Where $q$ is the **electrochemical equivalent** (kg C⁻¹) and $V_{\text{cell}}$ is the average cell voltage (V).

Units: **kWh per tonne** (kWh/t) — the universal "currency" of the industry.

---

## 2.5 Cell Voltage Decomposition — The KTU 5-Component Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU High-Yield Decomposition:**
>
> $$\boxed{V_{\text{cell}} \;=\; E_{\text{eq}} \;+\; \eta_{a,\text{act}} \;+\; \eta_{c,\text{act}} \;+\; \eta_{\text{conc}} \;+\; \sum IR_{\text{ohm}}}$$

| Component | Physical Origin | KTU Symbol | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equilibrium (Reversible) EMF** | Thermodynamic driving force | $E_{\text{eq}}$ | 1.0 – 2.5 V |
| **Anodic Activation Overpotential** | Kinetic barrier at anode | $\eta_{a,\text{act}}$ | 0.1 – 0.5 V |
| **Cathodic Activation Overpotential** | Kinetic barrier at cathode | $\eta_{c,\text{act}}$ | 0.1 – 0.5 V |
| **Concentration Overpotential** | Mass-transport limitation | $\eta_{\text{conc}}$ | 0.05 – 0.4 V |
| **Ohmic Drop** | Electrolyte + diaphragm + contact resistance | $\sum IR$ | 0.2 – 1.0 V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** For chlor-alkali, the equilibrium EMF is ~2.19 V, but actual cell voltage is **3.0–4.5 V** — the *extra* 1–2 V is overpotential and is the lever engineers tune to reduce SEC.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it covers ~80% of numerical Part B questions in Module 4.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical mass | $m = \dfrac{ItM}{nF}$ | kg | Faraday's 2nd Law |
| 2 | Equivalent weight | $E = M/n$ | kg/eq | Cu²⁺ → 31.77 g/eq |
| 3 | Electrochemical equivalent | $q = E/F$ | kg/C | Cu²⁺ → 3.295×10⁻⁷ kg/C |
| 4 | Current efficiency | $\eta = m_{\text{act}}/m_{\text{th}}$ | % | Often 85–97% |
| 5 | SEC | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}}}{q\eta}$ | kWh/t | Industry benchmark |
| 6 | Cell voltage | $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{eq}} + \sum\eta + \sum IR$ | V | 5-component form |
| 7 | Nernst EMF | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | V | For non-standard $T$, $a_i$ |
| 8 | Tafel equation | $\eta = a + b\log(i)$ | V | $b \approx 0.12$ V/decade |
| 9 | Limiting current | $i_L = \dfrac{nFD_{i} C_{b}}{\delta}$ | A/m² | Mass-transport limit |
| 10 | Production rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{I\eta}{nF} \cdot M$ | kg/s | Plant capacity |
| 11 | Number of cells | $N = \dfrac{\text{Total current}}{I_{\text{per cell}}}$ | — | Monopolar vs bipolar |
| 12 | Energy per kg | $W = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot n \cdot F}{\eta \cdot M}$ | J/kg | Inverse of Faraday mass |
| 13 | Space–time yield | $\text{STY} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{V_{\text{reactor}}}$ | kg m⁻³ s⁻¹ | Reactor design metric |
| 14 | Fractional conversion | $X = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{k_a A}{Q}\right)$ | — | Plug-flow electrolyzer |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> - **Aluminum (Hall–Héroult):** SEC ~ **13–15 kWh/kg** — a single 1 Mt/yr smelter consumes more electricity than a mid-sized country.
> - **Copper electrorefining:** SEC ~ **0.25–0.35 kWh/kg** — orders of magnitude lower because Cu is monovalent-equivalent-rich and the cell is well-optimized.
> - **Hydrogen electrolysis (alkaline):** SEC ~ **4.5–5.5 kWh/Nm³ H₂** (LHV basis). Target for "green H₂" is **< 4.0 kWh/Nm³** by 2030.
- **Engineer's lever:** To cut SEC, attack the *biggest* overpotential first. For Al smelting, ohmic drop in the cryolite bath dominates → improve anode–cathode distance (ACD) and use inert anodes.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Combined Faraday–Voltage Energy Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Derive a single closed-form expression for the **energy consumed per unit mass** in terms of measurable cell variables.

### Starting Premises
- Mass deposited by Faraday: $m = \dfrac{I t M}{n F}$
- Energy supplied: $E_{\text{energy}} = V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I \cdot t$
- Actual mass = $\eta \cdot m_{\text{theoretical}}$ (current efficiency factor)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define specific energy (per unit mass):**

$$w = \frac{E_{\text{energy}}}{m_{\text{actual}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute energy and actual mass expressions:**

$$w = \frac{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I \cdot t}{\eta \cdot \dfrac{I t M}{n F}}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the common term $I \cdot t$ (top and bottom):**

$$w = \frac{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot n \cdot F}{\eta \cdot M}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kWh per tonne (industry units):**

$$\text{SEC (kWh/t)} = \frac{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot n \cdot F}{\eta \cdot M} \cdot \frac{1000\ \text{kg}}{3600\ \text{s}} \cdot \frac{1000\ \text{kg}}{1\ \text{t}} \cdot \frac{1\ \text{kWh}}{3.6 \times 10^6\ \text{J}}$$

After unit conversion (one factor $\frac{1}{3.6}$):

$$\boxed{\text{SEC (kWh/t)} = \frac{2.722 \times 10^{-4} \cdot V_{\text{cell}} \cdot n}{\eta \cdot M_{\text{kg/mol}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For Cu electrorefining: $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.30$ V, $n = 2$, $\eta = 0.95$, $M = 0.0635$ kg/mol.
> $$\text{SEC} = \frac{2.722\times10^{-4} \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2}{0.95 \cdot 0.0635} \approx 0.27\ \text{kWh/kg} = 270\ \text{kWh/t}$$
> Industry reports 250–350 kWh/t — **match achieved.** ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Cells in a Monopolar vs Bipolar Bank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monopolar cells:** each electrode is directly wired to the busbar. All cells carry the **same voltage** but currents add in parallel.
> **Bipolar cells:** only end electrodes are wired; intermediate electrodes act as anode on one side, cathode on the other. Cells carry the **same current** but voltages add in series.

### Step 1 — Given data:
- Plant current demand: $I_{\text{total}}$
- Per-cell current rating: $I_{\text{cell}}$
- Per-cell voltage: $V_{\text{cell}}$
- Plant DC bus voltage: $V_{\text{bus}}$

### Step 2 — Monopolar case (parallel-only logic):

$$N_{\text{parallel}} = \left\lceil \frac{I_{\text{total}}}{I_{\text{cell}}} \right\rceil$$

**Total bus voltage** is just $V_{\text{cell}}$ (one cell-equivalent drop). To get a higher bus voltage, monopolar banks are often grouped in *series stacks*:

$$N_{\text{series,mono}} = \left\lceil \frac{V_{\text{bus}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} \right\rceil \quad\Rightarrow\quad N_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{series}} \times N_{\text{parallel}}$$

### Step 3 — Bipolar case (series-only logic):

$$N_{\text{bipolar}} = \left\lceil \frac{I_{\text{total}}}{I_{\text{cell}}} \right\rceil$$

(Here, $I_{\text{cell}} = I_{\text{total}}/N$ if $I_{\text{cell}}$ is fixed.) Bus voltage adds naturally:

$$V_{\text{bus}} = N_{\text{bipolar}} \cdot V_{\text{cell}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial implication:** Bipolar saves **busbar copper** (no inter-cell wiring), reduces footprint, and is standard for chlor-alkali and Al smelters. Monopolar is preferred when **individual cell maintenance** is frequent (e.g., electroplating shops).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Limiting Current Density for a Planar Electrode

The **limiting current** sets the *ceiling* on production rate — exceeding it does not produce more product, only side reactions.

### Step 1 — Mass flux to a planar electrode (Nernst diffusion layer):

$$J_i = -D_i \left(\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial x}\right)_{x=0} = \frac{D_i (C_b - C_s)}{\delta}$$

Where $C_b$ is bulk concentration, $C_s$ is surface concentration, $\delta$ is the diffusion layer thickness.

### Step 2 — At the limiting current, surface concentration → 0:

$$J_{i,L} = \frac{D_i \cdot C_b}{\delta}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to current density using Faraday:

$$i_L = n F J_{i,L} = \frac{n F D_i C_b}{\delta}$$

### Step 4 — Engineering form (with mass-transfer coefficient $k_m = D_i/\delta$):

$$\boxed{i_L = n F k_m C_b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Doubling $C_b$ doubles $i_L$ → doubles production per cell. This is why industrial electrolytes are run at *saturation* (e.g., CuSO₄ saturated, H₂SO₄ ~150–200 g/L for Zn electrowinning).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Full KTU-Style Numerical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **[Problem]** A copper electrorefining plant operates at $I = 25{,}000\ \text{A}$, $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.32$ V, with 240 cells in series. Copper is deposited as Cu²⁺ ($n = 2$, $M = 63.55$ g/mol). Current efficiency = 96%. Calculate:
> (i) Daily Cu production in kg
> (ii) Daily DC energy consumption in kWh
> (iii) Specific energy consumption in kWh/tonne
> (iv) Number of cells if the same plant is reconfigured as a bipolar bank with $I_{\text{cell,max}} = 30{,}000$ A.

### Solution

**Step (i) — Daily production per cell (Faraday):**

$$m_{\text{day}} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot \eta \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{25{,}000 \cdot (24 \cdot 3600) \cdot 0.96 \cdot 0.06355}{2 \cdot 96{,}485}$$

Computing numerator: $25{,}000 \cdot 86{,}400 = 2.16 \times 10^9$ C/day

$$m_{\text{day,per cell}} = \frac{2.16 \times 10^9 \cdot 0.96 \cdot 0.06355}{192{,}970} = \frac{1.318 \times 10^8}{192{,}970} \approx 683\ \text{kg/day per cell}$$

**Total plant production (240 cells in series — current is same, not additive):**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{plant}} = 240 \cdot 683 = 1.64 \times 10^5\ \text{kg/day} = 164\ \text{tonnes/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtle KTU trap:** In *series*, current is identical through every cell. So production **multiplies by cell count**, while total voltage adds. This is the *opposite* of what students often guess.

**Step (ii) — Daily DC energy:**

Total plant voltage: $V_{\text{plant}} = 240 \cdot 0.32 = 76.8$ V

$$E_{\text{daily}} = V_{\text{plant}} \cdot I \cdot t = 76.8 \cdot 25{,}000 \cdot 86{,}400 = 1.659 \times 10^{11}\ \text{J}$$

Convert to kWh: $\dfrac{1.659 \times 10^{11}}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 46{,}080\ \text{kWh/day} \approx 46.1\ \text{MWh/day}$

**Step (iii) — SEC:**

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{E_{\text{daily}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{plant}}} = \frac{46{,}080}{164} \approx 281\ \text{kWh/tonne}$$

This matches the published industrial benchmark of **250–350 kWh/t** for Cu electrorefining. ✓

**Step (iv) — Bipolar reconfiguration:**

In bipolar, the per-cell current equals the line current (series), so:

$$N_{\text{bipolar}} = \left\lceil \frac{25{,}000}{30{,}000} \right\rceil = 1$$

But one cell at 30,000 A cannot produce 164 t/day at our production target. So we must **parallel-stack** the bipolar cells (modules of series-bipolar in parallel):

$$N_{\text{parallel}} = \left\lceil \frac{25{,}000}{30{,}000} \right\rceil = 1\ \text{(bipolar module)}$$

For 164 t/day target at 30,000 A:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{per cell}} = \frac{30{,}000 \cdot 86{,}400 \cdot 0.96 \cdot 0.06355}{192{,}970} \approx 819\ \text{kg/day}$$

$$N_{\text{modules}} = \left\lceil \frac{164{,}000}{819} \right\rceil = 201\ \text{bipolar modules}$$

So: **201 cells in bipolar configuration** vs 240 in original monopolar. A **~16% footprint reduction**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A comprises 4–5 short questions of 3 marks each, no choice. Answers should be 5–8 lines.

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State Faraday's First and Second Laws of Electrolysis. Define the term "electrochemical equivalent" with its SI unit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **First Law (1 m):** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte: $m \propto Q = I t$.
- **Second Law (1 m):** When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their chemical equivalent weights: $m \propto E = M/n$.
- **Electrochemical Equivalent (1 m):** It is the mass of substance liberated by 1 coulomb of charge. Symbol: $z$ or $q$. SI unit: **kg C⁻¹**. Numerically $q = M/(nF)$. For Cu²⁺: $q = 3.295 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{kg/C}$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is meant by current efficiency in an electrolytic process? List two factors that reduce current efficiency in zinc electrowinning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 m):** Current efficiency is the ratio of actual mass deposited to the theoretical mass calculated from Faraday's law, expressed as a percentage:
$$\eta = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\% = \frac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$
- **Two factors (1.5 m):**
  1. **Hydrogen evolution at the cathode** (parasitic side reaction consuming electrons meant for Zn²⁺ reduction).
  2. **Dissolution/short-circuiting of the deposited zinc** back into the acidic electrolyte.
  (Other valid: oxygen evolution at Pb–Ag anode, impurity redox cycling such as Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺, current leakage through busbar insulation failures.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each choice has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Show all derivations; box the final answers; include units.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for **specific energy consumption (SEC)** in an electrolytic process in terms of cell voltage, current efficiency, and electrochemical equivalent. A chlor-alkali membrane cell produces Cl₂ at $V_{\text{cell}} = 3.2$ V with 96% current efficiency. Calculate the SEC in kWh/tonne of Cl₂. ($M_{\text{Cl}_2} = 70.9$ g/mol, $n = 2$).

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **five components of cell voltage** in an industrial electrolyzer. For an aluminum smelter, identify the component that contributes the maximum voltage and suggest two engineering strategies to reduce it.

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Derivation + Numerical

**Step 1 — Theoretical mass (Faraday):** $m_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{I t M}{n F}$ (1 m)

**Step 2 — Actual mass:** $m_{\text{act}} = \eta \cdot m_{\text{th}}$ (1 m)

**Step 3 — Energy consumed:** $E = V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I \cdot t$ (1 m)

**Step 4 — Specific energy:** $w = \dfrac{E}{m_{\text{act}}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I \cdot t}{\eta \cdot I t M / n F} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot n F}{\eta \cdot M}$ (2 m)

**Step 5 — Numerical substitution:**

Given: $V = 3.2$ V, $\eta = 0.96$, $n = 2$, $M = 0.0709$ kg/mol, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

$$w = \frac{3.2 \cdot 2 \cdot 96{,}485}{0.96 \cdot 0.0709} = \frac{617{,}504}{0.0681} = 9.07 \times 10^6\ \text{J/kg}$$

Convert to kWh/tonne: $\dfrac{9.07 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 2.52$ kWh/kg $= 2{,}520$ kWh/tonne. (2 m)

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\text{SEC} = 2{,}520\ \text{kWh/t Cl}_2}$ (1 m for unit conversion clarity)

### Part (b) — Five Components + Al Smelter

The five components of $V_{\text{cell}}$ (from Section 2.5): $E_{\text{eq}} + \eta_{a,\text{act}} + \eta_{c,\text{act}} + \eta_{\text{conc}} + \sum IR$. (3 m for the block diagram and listing, 2 m for Al-specific discussion)

**Al Smelter (Hall–Héroult):** The dominant term is the **ohmic drop in the cryolite–alumina bath** ($\sum IR$), typically contributing **1.5–2.5 V** out of a total 4.0–4.5 V cell voltage. This is because the bath has relatively low conductivity (~2.2 S/cm) and the anode–cathode distance (ACD) must be physically maintained at 4–5 cm to prevent re-oxidation. (2 m)

**Two engineering strategies:** (1 m each)
1. **Reduce ACD** to 3–3.5 cm using computer-controlled anode adjustment — saves ~0.3–0.5 V.
2. **Inert (non-consumable) anodes** — would eliminate the C-anode overpotential (~0.4–0.5 V) and CO₂ emissions; an active area of R&D by Elysis (Alcoa + Rio Tinto) and RUSAL.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** A copper electrorefining plant has **480 cells in series**, each carrying 22,000 A at 0.30 V with 95% current efficiency. Calculate (i) the daily production of refined copper in tonnes, (ii) the total DC power consumption in MW, and (iii) the specific energy consumption in kWh/tonne. ($M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.55$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol).

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **limiting current density** concept for an industrial electrode. A zinc electrowinning cell uses a planar cathode with diffusion layer thickness $\delta = 0.05$ cm, bulk Zn²⁺ concentration $C_b = 1500$ mol/m³, and $D_{\text{Zn}^{2+}} = 7.0 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s. Calculate the limiting current density. ($n = 2$)

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Copper Plant

**Step (i) — Daily production per cell:**

$$m_{\text{day,per cell}} = \frac{I t \eta M}{n F} = \frac{22{,}000 \cdot (24 \cdot 3600) \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.06355}{2 \cdot 96{,}485}$$

Compute numerator: $22{,}000 \cdot 86{,}400 = 1.901 \times 10^9$ C
$m_{\text{per cell}} = \dfrac{1.901 \times 10^9 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.06355}{192{,}970} = \dfrac{1.148 \times 10^8}{192{,}970} = 595$ kg/day (2 m)

**For 480 cells in series — current is identical, so:**
$$\dot{m}_{\text{plant}} = 480 \cdot 595 = 285{,}600\ \text{kg/day} = 285.6\ \text{tonnes/day}$$ (1 m)

**Step (ii) — Total DC power:**

Plant voltage: $V_{\text{plant}} = 480 \cdot 0.30 = 144$ V
Current: $I = 22{,}000$ A

$$P = V_{\text{plant}} \cdot I = 144 \cdot 22{,}000 = 3.168 \times 10^6\ \text{W} = 3.17\ \text{MW}$$ (2 m)

**Step (iii) — SEC:**

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{P \cdot 24\ \text{h}}{\dot{m}_{\text{plant}}} = \frac{3.168 \times 10^3\ \text{kW} \cdot 24}{285.6\ \text{t}} = \frac{76{,}032}{285.6} = 266\ \text{kWh/t}$$ (2 m)

**Final boxed answers:**
- $\dot{m}_{\text{plant}} = 285.6$ t/day
- $P = 3.17$ MW
- $\boxed{\text{SEC} = 266\ \text{kWh/t}}$

### Part (b) — Limiting Current Density

**Conceptual Explanation (3 m):**
The limiting current density $i_L$ is the maximum rate at which an electrode reaction can proceed under given hydrodynamic and concentration conditions, set by the rate at which reactant can be supplied to the electrode surface by mass transport. Beyond $i_L$, the surface concentration drops to zero, the electrode potential rises sharply, and secondary reactions (e.g., H₂ evolution) commence. The plateau on a current–voltage curve marks $i_L$.

**Formula (1 m):** $i_L = \dfrac{n F D C_b}{\delta}$

**Numerical calculation (3 m):**
Given: $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $D = 7.0 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s, $C_b = 1500$ mol/m³, $\delta = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m.

$$i_L = \frac{2 \cdot 96{,}485 \cdot 7.0 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 1500}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

Compute numerator: $2 \cdot 96{,}485 \cdot 7.0 \times 10^{-10} = 1.351 \times 10^{-4}$
Numerator total: $1.351 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1500 = 0.2026$

$$i_L = \frac{0.2026}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 405\ \text{A/m}^2$$

Industrial Zn electrowinning operates at **400–600 A/m²** — so this calculation matches plant reality. ✓

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{i_L = 405\ \text{A/m}^2}$ (0.5 m)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing SEC in kWh/kg instead of kWh/tonne. Always **double-check the unit request** in the question stem.
> 2. **Monopolar vs bipolar confusion:** In *series*, current is the *same* through each cell; in *parallel*, voltage is the *same*. Many students multiply production by current count — this is **wrong** for series.
> 3. **Forgetting $n$:** Cu²⁺ requires 2 electrons, Al³⁺ requires 3. Mixing up valence is a **fatal 1–2 mark loss**.
> 4. **Faraday constant in correct units:** Use $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol when current is in A and time in seconds. The $26.8$ Ah/mol version is only valid when $I$ is in A and $t$ in hours.
> 5. **Concentration units for $i_L$:** $C_b$ must be in **mol/m³** (SI), not g/L or mol/L. Convert if needed: 1 mol/L = 1000 mol/m³.
> 6. **Equilibrium EMF ≠ Cell voltage:** Examiners explicitly test whether you understand that $E_{\text{eq}}$ is only the *thermodynamic* part. Forgetting the overpotential terms costs full marks.
> 7. **Nernst equation sign errors:** When $\log Q > 0$ (product-favored), $E < E^\circ$. Students often flip this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Fundamentals:**
>
> - [ ] **Faraday's 2nd Law** is the cornerstone: $m = \dfrac{ItM}{nF}$.
> - [ ] **Current efficiency $\eta$** must always be included in industrial calculations; the theoretical mass is *never* the actual mass.
> - [ ] **SEC formula** in kWh/tonne: $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{2.722 \times 10^{-4} \cdot V_{\text{cell}} \cdot n}{\eta \cdot M_{\text{kg/mol}}}$. Memorize the constant.
> - [ ] **Cell voltage = 5 components:** $E_{\text{eq}} + \eta_{a} + \eta_{c} + \eta_{\text{conc}} + \sum IR$. The largest contributor depends on the process.
> - [ ] **Nernst equation:** $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$. At 298 K, the factor $\dfrac{RT}{F} = 0.0257$ V; $\log_{10}$ form: $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10} Q$.
> - [ ] **Limiting current density** $i_L = nFk_mC_b$ — sets the upper production bound per unit area.
> - [ ] **Monopolar** → parallel current, series stacks for high voltage. **Bipolar** → series current, natural voltage addition.
> - [ ] **Industrial benchmark SEC values:** Al ~13,000 kWh/t; Cl₂ ~2,500 kWh/t; Zn ~3,000 kWh/t; Cu ~270 kWh/t.
> - [ ] **Hall–Héroult reduction** of Al₂O₃ needs **carbon anode** to capture the oxygen as CO₂ — direct carbon footprint of Al industry.
> - [ ] **Al vs Cu SEC differ by ~50×** because of $n$ (3 vs 2), $E_{\text{eq}}$ (complex vs simple), and bath resistance (cryolite vs CuSO₄/H₂SO₄).
> - [ ] **Bipolar cells save busbar copper** → preferred for large plants (chlor-alkali, Al).
> - [ ] **Tafel equation** $\eta = a + b\log i$ — remember $b \approx 0.12$ V/decade at 298 K for $\alpha = 0.5$, $n = 1$.
> - [ ] **Electrochemical equivalent** $q = M/(nF)$ — engineering shortcut to skip full Faraday calculation.
> - [ ] **Space–time yield (STY)** is the reactor design analog of productivity — kg product per m³ reactor per second.
> - [ ] **For any KTU Part B numerical:** Identify (i) $n$, (ii) $M$, (iii) $\eta$, (iv) $V_{\text{cell}}$, (v) the *target* quantity, (vi) the unit requested — **then** write the formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactor Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Reactor Design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electrochemical Reactor Design** is the engineering discipline concerned with the systematic sizing, configuration, and operational specification of devices in which heterogeneous charge-transfer reactions are driven by an applied potential or current, with the goal of achieving a target production rate, conversion, selectivity, and energy efficiency at minimum capital and operating cost. An **electrochemical reactor** (also called an *electrolytic cell* or *electrochemical cell*) differs fundamentally from a conventional chemical reactor in three respects: 1. The reaction is initiated and sustained by **electron transfer at electrode–electrolyte interfaces** (heterogeneous). 2. **At least two simultaneous half-cell reactions** (anodic oxidation and cathodic reduction) occur at spatially separated electrodes, making current flow the central design parameter. 3. The **applied voltage** (or current) is an additional design variable — the "electrical degree of freedom" not present in purely thermal/catalytic reactors. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Two-Pump Water Treatment Plant** > Imagine a water-purification plant where dirty water enters, passes between two large porous cleaning membranes (the electrodes), and exits cleaner on the other side. The "pumps" are not mechanical — they are the electrodes pumping **electrons** into and out of the solution. The flow rate of electrons (current) controls *how fast* impurities are removed, while the spacing between the membranes and the turbulence of flow control *how uniformly* each water molecule gets cleaned. Reactor design is essentially deciding: *How big should the membranes be? How far apart? How fast should the water flow? And how much electrical "pumping power" do we need?* ## 1.3 Classification of Electrochemical Reactors Electrochemical reactors are classified along **three independent axes**: | Axis | Categories | Significance | |------|------------|--------------| | **Electrode Geometry** | Parallel-plate, Cylinder (concentric), Fluidized bed, Packed bed, Rotating cylinder | Determines current distribution & specific area | | **Flow Pattern** | Batch, Plug Flow (PFR), Continuous Stirred Tank (CSTR), Cascade of CSTRs | Determines conversion profile | | **Cell Configuration** | Divided (membrane-separated) vs. Undivided (single compartment) | Determines product separation & selectivity | ## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Definition) The following **primary design metrics** govern every electrochemical reactor: - **Specific Electrode Area, $a$** $[m^2/m^3]$ — the active electrode area per unit electrolyte volume. - **Space Velocity, $SV$** $[s^{-1}]$ — volumetric flow rate divided by reactor volume; a measure of throughput. - **Current Efficiency (Faradaic Efficiency), $\Phi$** $[-]$ — fraction of total charge that contributes to the *desired* reaction. - **Cell Voltage, $U_{cell}$** $[V]$ — total driving voltage; must be minimized for energy economy. - **Specific Energy Consumption, $E_s$** $[kWh/kg]$ — electrical energy required per kilogram of product. - **Conversion, $X$** $[-]$ — fraction of limiting reactant consumed. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Reactor Design Philosophy — The Sequential Design Algorithm The canonical KTU-aligned approach to electrochemical reactor design follows this **five-step procedure**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 1: Reaction Stoichiometry & Electron Balance** > Write balanced half-reactions. Determine **$n$** = number of electrons transferred per mole of desired product. > > **Step 2: Kinetic Model** > Obtain rate expression $r$ $[mol/(m^2 \cdot s)]$ as a function of concentration, temperature, and current density. > > **Step 3: Mass-Transport Model** > Determine whether the reactor is operating in *charge-transfer control*, *mixed control*, or *mass-transfer control* via the dimensionless **Damköhler number** and the **mass-transfer limiting current density** $i_L$. > > **Step 4: Material & Charge Balance (Reactor Sizing)** > Couple Faraday's law with the species mole balance to compute required electrode area $A$ and electrolyte volume $V$. > > **Step 5: Voltage & Energy Balance** > Sum all overpotentials: $U_{cell} = E_{eq} + \eta_a + \eta_c + IR_{solution} + IR_{electrodes}$. ## 2.2 The Three Limiting Current Regimes | Regime | Condition | Rate-Limiting Step | Design Implication | |--------|-----------|--------------------|--------------------| | **Charge-transfer (Activation) Control** | $i \ll i_L$ | Electron transfer kinetics | Increase catalyst area, raise T | | **Mixed Control** | $i \approx 0.5\,i_L$ | Both kinetics & mass transfer | Optimize both | | **Mass-transfer Control** | $i \to i_L$ | Diffusion/convection of species | Increase flow turbulence, use turbulence promoters | > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in design:** Industrial electrochemical reactors (chlor-alkali, electro-winning, electroplating) almost always operate at the **mass-transfer limit** because maximum productivity is desired. Therefore, **mass-transfer correlations** dominate the design equations. ## 2.3 Mass-Transfer Limiting Current Density The **maximum rate** at which a species can reach the electrode is governed by: $$i_L = n \, F \, k_m \, C_b$$ where: - $n$ = electrons per mole of reaction, - $F$ = **Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol**, - $k_m$ = mass-transfer coefficient $[m/s]$, - $C_b$ = bulk concentration $[mol/m^3]$. For common geometries, $k_m$ is obtained from dimensionless correlations: | Geometry | Correlation | Variables | |----------|-------------|-----------| | Parallel plate, laminar | $Sh = 1.85\,(Re \cdot Sc \cdot d_h/L)^{1/3}$ | $Re<2000$ | | Parallel plate, turbulent | $Sh = 0.023\,Re^{0.8}\,Sc^{0.33}$ | $Re>2000$ | | Rotating cylinder | $Sh = 0.079\,Re^{0.7}\,Sc^{0.356}$ | $Re = \omega r^2/\nu$ | | Packed bed | $Sh = 1.26\,Re^{0.5}\,Sc^{1/3}$ | $Re = d_p u/\nu$ | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** Knowing $i_L$ tells the designer the *maximum allowable* current. Pushing $i > i_L$ wastes energy as water splitting or side reactions. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Design Equations | # | Equation | Variables | Units | |---|----------|-----------|-------| | 1 | $r = \dfrac{i}{nF}$ | Rate per unit area | $mol/(m^2 \cdot s)$ | | 2 | $i_L = nFk_mC_b$ | Limiting current density | $A/m^2$ | | 3 | $X = 1 - \exp(-kA/Q)$ | Conversion (PFR, 1st order) | dimensionless | | 4 | $X = \dfrac{kA/Q}{1 + kA/Q}$ | Conversion (CSTR, 1st order) | dimensionless | | 5 | $\Phi = \dfrac{nF \cdot N_{product}}{I \cdot t}$ | Current efficiency | dimensionless | | 6 | $E_s = \dfrac{n F U_{cell}}{\Phi \cdot M}$ | Specific energy | $kWh/kg$ | | 7 | $SV = Q/V$ | Space velocity | $s^{-1}$ | | 8 | $a = A/V$ | Specific electrode area | $m^2/m^3$ | | 9 | $A = \dfrac{nF \cdot \dot{n}_p \cdot N_{cells}}{\Phi \cdot i_{op}}$ | Required electrode area | $m^2$ | | 10 | $U_{cell} = E_{eq} + \Sigma\eta + IR$ | Cell voltage balance | $V$ | | 11 | $Da = \dfrac{k}{k_m \cdot a}$ | Damköhler number | dimensionless | | 12 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho u d_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | | 13 | $Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D}$ | Schmidt number | dimensionless | | 14 | $Sh = \dfrac{k_m d_h}{D}$ | Sherwood number | dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > In **KTU board examinations**, the single most-failed question type is asking students to *combine* Equation 9 with the voltage balance (Eq. 10) and the energy equation (Eq. 6) to compute the **specific energy consumption** for a stated production rate. Memorize the interconnections. ## 2.5 Reactor-Type Selection Logic > [!IMPORTANT] > **Selection Rule of Thumb (KTU High-Yield):** > - **Batch / Recycle PFR** → low-concentration feeds, expensive product (e.g., organic electrosynthesis, electrowinning of noble metals). > - **CSTR / Cascade** → when *continuous*, *uniform* quality is needed and reaction is **kinetically controlled** (e.g., wastewater treatment, effluent detoxification). > - **Single-pass PFR (parallel plate with high flow)** → when **mass-transfer limited**, requires high $i_L$ from turbulent flow (e.g., chlor-alkali membrane cells). > - **Fluidized bed** → for dissolved metal ion removal from dilute streams (e.g., Cu recovery from mine water). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — The Fundamental Coupling Between Faraday's Law and Species Mole Balance We begin by recognizing that **every electron that crosses the electrode–electrolyte interface corresponds, via Faraday's law, to a fixed number of moles of product**. This is the *anchor* of all electrochemical reactor design. **Step 1: Faraday's law for production rate** For a single cell operating at current $I$ for time $t$: $$m = \frac{\Phi \cdot I \cdot t \cdot M}{n F}$$ **Step 2: Expressing production rate in molar flow units** Let $\dot{n}_p$ be the desired molar production rate of the target product $[mol/s]$. Rearranging: $$\dot{n}_p = \frac{\Phi \cdot I}{n F}$$ **Step 3: Introducing current density** The total current is distributed over the electrode area: $I = i_{op} \cdot A$. Therefore: $$\dot{n}_p = \frac{\Phi \cdot i_{op} \cdot A}{n F}$$ **Step 4: Solving for required electrode area** $$A = \frac{n F \cdot \dot{n}_p}{\Phi \cdot i_{op}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is **Equation 9** of the KTU formula sheet, derived from first principles. Note the inverse relationship with $\Phi$ and $i_{op}$ — to *shrink* the reactor, we must operate at **high current density with high current efficiency**. ## 3.2 Derivation II — Conversion in a Plug-Flow Electrochemical Reactor (PFR) Consider a differential slice of a PFR of length $dx$, cross-section $S_c$, with volumetric flow $Q$ $[m^3/s]$, bulk concentration $C$, and reaction rate $r$ $[mol/(m^3 \cdot s)]$ (volumetric). **Step 1: Mole balance on the slice** $$Q \cdot C \big\vert_x - Q \cdot C \big\vert_{x+dx} - r \, S_c \, dx = 0$$ **Step 2: Taylor expansion** $$-Q \, dC - r \, S_c \, dx = 0$$ $$\Rightarrow -Q \, dC = r \, S_c \, dx$$ **Step 3: Define residence time** $\tau = S_c L / Q$. The rate is often expressed per unit electrode area: $r_{vol} = a \cdot r_{area}$ where $a = A/V$. **Step 4: Convert to conversion variable** $X$ using $C = C_0(1 - X)$, so $dC = -C_0 \, dX$: $$Q \, C_0 \, dX = r \, S_c \, dx$$ **Step 5: Relate volumetric rate to current density** From $r_{area} = i/(nF)$ and $r_{vol} = a \cdot i/(nF)$: $$Q \, C_0 \, dX = a \cdot \frac{i(x)}{n F} \, S_c \, dx$$ **Step 6: Integrate from $x = 0$ to $x = L$** (assuming constant current density, uniform concentration approximation): $$\int_0^X \frac{dX'}{r(X')} = \int_0^L \frac{a \, S_c}{Q \, C_0} \, dx = \frac{a \, V}{Q \, C_0} = \frac{A}{Q \, C_0}$$ **Step 7: For a first-order electrochemical rate** $r = k C = k C_0(1 - X)$: $$\int_0^X \frac{dX'}{k(1 - X')} = \frac{A}{Q C_0}$$ $$-\frac{1}{k}\ln(1 - X) = \frac{A}{Q C_0}$$ $$\boxed{X_{PFR} = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{kA}{Q}\right)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Note:** This exponential form is **distinctive** to PFR. A common student error is writing the CSTR formula here. Memorize: **PFR → exponential, CSTR → rational**. ## 3.3 Derivation III — Conversion in a Continuous Stirred Tank Electrochemical Reactor (CSTR) **Step 1: Steady-state mole balance on the entire well-mixed tank** $$Q C_0 - Q C - r V = 0$$ **Step 2: Express exit concentration in terms of conversion** $C = C_0(1 - X)$: $$Q C_0 X = r V$$ **Step 3: First-order kinetics**, $r = k C_0(1 - X)$: $$Q C_0 X = k C_0 (1 - X) V$$ **Step 4: Define** $\tau = V/Q$ and rearrange: $$X = k \tau (1 - X)$$ $$X + k \tau X = k \tau$$ $$\boxed{X_{CSTR} = \frac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau} = \frac{k V/Q}{1 + k V/Q}}$$ ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **For the same $k$ and $V/Q$, the PFR always yields higher conversion than the CSTR**, because the average reaction rate in a PFR is taken over concentrations that *decrease* along the length, whereas in a CSTR the entire reactor operates at the *lowest* exit concentration. | $k\tau$ | $X_{PFR}$ | $X_{CSTR}$ | $X_{PFR}/X_{CSTR}$ | |---------|-----------|------------|---------------------| | 0.1 | 0.0952 | 0.0909 | 1.05 | | 0.5 | 0.3935 | 0.3333 | 1.18 | | 1.0 | 0.6321 | 0.5000 | 1.26 | | 2.0 | 0.8647 | 0.6667 | 1.30 | | 5.0 | 0.9933 | 0.8333 | 1.19 | ## 3.5 Derivation IV — Specific Energy Consumption **Step 1:** Energy consumed by a cell over time $t$: $$W_{elec} = U_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t \quad [J]$$ **Step 2:** Mass of desired product (from Faraday): $$m = \frac{\Phi I t M}{n F}$$ **Step 3:** Specific energy (energy per unit mass of product): $$E_s = \frac{W_{elec}}{m} = \frac{U_{cell} I t}{\Phi I t M / (n F)} = \frac{n F \, U_{cell}}{\Phi \, M}$$ **Step 4:** Convert to $kWh/kg$ using $1 \, J = 2.778 \times 10^{-7} \, kWh$: $$\boxed{E_s = \frac{n F U_{cell}}{3.6 \times 10^6 \cdot \Phi \cdot M} \quad [kWh/kg]}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **KPI** for any electrochemical process. A chlor-alkali cell achieves $E_s \approx 2.0$–$2.3 \, kWh/kg$ Cl$_2$ — one of the lowest in the industry. ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** An undivided parallel-plate cell is used to reduce Cu$^{2+}$ to Cu metal from a 0.1 kmol/m$^3$ feed at a flow rate of $Q = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m$^3$/s. The mass-transfer coefficient is $k_m = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s, electrode area per cell = 0.05 m$^2$, $n = 2$, $\Phi = 0.9$, $U_{cell} = 2.5$ V, $M_{Cu} = 63.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol. Calculate the **limiting current**, the **operating current** at $i = 0.8\,i_L$, the **Cu production rate**, and the **specific energy consumption**. **Solution:** **Step 1: Limiting current density** $$i_L = n F k_m C_b = (2)(96485)(4 \times 10^{-5})(100)$$ $$i_L = 771.88 \, A/m^2$$ **Step 2: Operating current per cell** $$i_{op} = 0.8 \times 771.88 = 617.5 \, A/m^2$$ $$I_{cell} = i_{op} \cdot A = 617.5 \times 0.05 = 30.88 \, A$$ **Step 3: Cu production rate** $$\dot{n}_{Cu} = \frac{\Phi I_{cell}}{n F} = \frac{0.9 \times 30.88}{2 \times 96485} = 1.44 \times 10^{-4} \, mol/s$$ $$\dot{m}_{Cu} = \dot{n}_{Cu} \cdot M = 1.44 \times 10^{-4} \times 63.5 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\dot{m}_{Cu} = 9.14 \times 10^{-6} \, kg/s = 32.9 \, g/h$$ **Step 4: Specific energy consumption** $$E_s = \frac{n F U_{cell}}{3.6 \times 10^6 \cdot \Phi \cdot M}$$ $$E_s = \frac{2 \times 96485 \times 2.5}{3.6 \times 10^6 \times 0.9 \times 63.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$E_s = \frac{482425}{206.01} = 2.342 \, kWh/kg \, Cu$$ > [!TIP] > This $E_s$ value (~2.34 kWh/kg) is consistent with industrial copper electrowinning data (2.0–2.5 kWh/kg), confirming the calculation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reactor Configuration Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[DC Power Supply] -->|U_cell, I| B[Bus Bars] B --> C1[Cell 1: Anode] B --> C2[Cell 2: Cathode] C1 -->|Anolyte flow| M1[Inlet manifold Q_in] C2 -->|Catholyte flow| M2[Inlet manifold Q_in] M1 --> R1[Reactor Vessel V] M2 --> R1 R1 --> P1[Electrode Stack
Area A, Gap l] P1 --> P2[Membrane or Baffle
Optional] P2 --> R1 R1 --> O1[Outlet: Product + Unreacted] O1 --> SEP[Separator / Recycle] SEP -->|Recycle stream| M1 SEP --> PROD[Pure Product Stream] style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333 style R1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style P1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 style PROD fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00 ``` ## 4.2 Reactor-Type Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S([Start: Define Feed and Target]) --> Q1{Is feed
concentration
> 1 kmol/m3?} Q1 -- Yes --> P1[Use PFR
Parallel Plate] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is reaction
mass-transfer
limited?} Q2 -- Yes --> P2[Use Turbulent
PFR or
Rotating Cylinder] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Continuous
uniform product
required?} Q3 -- Yes --> P3[Use CSTR
or Cascade] Q3 -- No --> P4[Use Batch
or Recycle PFR] P1 --> END([Compute A, V, E_s]) P2 --> END P3 --> END P4 --> END style S fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6 style END fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 ``` ## 4.3 Current-Voltage-Power Operational Envelope ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Limits LIM1[i_L: Mass Transfer Ceiling] LIM2[i_min: Below Activation Threshold] end subgraph Operating Window OP[Feasible Region:
i_min < i_op < 0.8 i_L] end subgraph Outputs OUT1[Target Production
dot_n_p] OUT2[Specific Energy
E_s in kWh per kg] OUT3[Reactor Area A
and Volume V] end LIM1 --> OP LIM2 --> OP OP --> OUT1 OP --> OUT2 OP --> OUT3 style OP fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656 style LIM1 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 style LIM2 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 ``` ## 4.4 Mass-Balance Architecture in a Generic Electrochemical Reactor ```mermaid flowchart TB F_IN[F_in: Molar flow of reactant A in] --> NODE{Reactor
Volume V} IAPPLIED[Applied Current I drives:
r = i over nF] --> NODE NODE --> F_OUT[F_out = F_in 1 minus X] NODE --> F_PROD[F_product = Phi times I over nF] NODE --> HEAT[Heat generation Q_joule = I squared R] F_OUT --> RECYCLE{Recycle?} RECYCLE -- Yes --> REC[Recycle Stream R times F_in] REC --> F_IN RECYCLE -- No --> DISCHARGE[Effluent] style NODE fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf style IAPPLIED fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00 style HEAT fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define current efficiency (Faradaic efficiency) for an electrochemical reactor. Why is it always less than 100% in practice?** **Model Answer:** Current efficiency $\Phi$ is the ratio of the actual amount of product obtained to the theoretical amount predicted by Faraday's law: $$\Phi = \frac{\text{Actual moles of product} \times nF}{I \times t}$$ It is less than 100% in practice because of: 1. **Side reactions** (e.g., oxygen evolution at the anode, hydrogen evolution at the cathode consuming charge). 2. **Secondary reactions** of the desired product (e.g., further oxidation/reduction). 3. **Shunt currents** through cell hardware and bipolar effects. 4. **Non-uniform current distribution** causing regions of high local $i$ that exceed $i_L$. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between a divided and an undivided electrochemical cell. Give one industrial example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Divided Cell | Undivided Cell | |---------|--------------|----------------| | **Separator** | Ion-exchange membrane or diaphragm | No separator | | **Anolyte/Catholyte** | Two distinct streams | Single common stream | | **Product purity** | High (anode and cathode products separated) | Limited (mixing occurs) | | **Industrial example** | Chlor-alkali (NaCl → Cl₂ + H₂) | Electrowinning of Cu, electroplating | | **Voltage** | Higher (membrane resistance) | Lower (no membrane) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the **limiting current density** in a parallel-plate electrochemical reactor operating under laminar flow conditions. State the relevant dimensionless groups. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A divided parallel-plate cell is used to produce chlorine from a 4.5 kmol/m³ NaCl feed at 60 °C. The cell has 25 anode–cathode pairs, each plate is 0.8 m × 0.5 m, $k_m = 3.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s, $n = 1$, $\Phi = 0.95$, $U_{cell} = 3.1$ V, and $M_{Cl_2} = 71 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol. Compute the **Cl₂ production rate in kg/h** and the **specific energy consumption in kWh/kg Cl₂**. Take $i_{op} = 0.7\,i_L$. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part A(a) **Step 1:** Starting from the convective-diffusion equation for a species in fully developed laminar flow between parallel plates (Leveque solution): $$i_L = 0.925 \, n F \, C_b \, D^{2/3} \left(\frac{u_0}{d_h \, x}\right)^{1/3}$$ **Step 2:** Introducing the dimensionless groups: - **Sherwood number**: $Sh = i_L d_h / (n F C_b D)$ - **Schmidt number**: $Sc = \nu / D$ - **Reynolds number**: $Re = u_0 d_h / \nu$ **Step 3:** The compact correlation form (valid for $Re \cdot Sc \cdot d_h/L > 20$): $$Sh = 1.85 \, (Re \cdot Sc \cdot d_h/L)^{1/3}$$ This is the **KTU high-yield correlation** for laminar PFR mass transfer. #### Model Solution — Part A(b) **Step 1: Compute limiting current density** $$i_L = n F k_m C_b = (1)(96485)(3.5 \times 10^{-5})(4500)$$ $$i_L = 1.519 \times 10^{4} \, A/m^2$$ **Step 2: Operating current density and total current** $$i_{op} = 0.7 \times 15190 = 10633 \, A/m^2$$ **Step 3: Area per plate** $$A_{plate} = 0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.4 \, m^2$$ **Step 4: Total current across 25 pairs** $$I_{total} = 10633 \times 0.4 \times 25 = 1.0633 \times 10^{5} \, A$$ **Step 5: Cl₂ production rate (Faraday)** $$\dot{n}_{Cl_2} = \frac{\Phi \, I_{total}}{n F} = \frac{0.95 \times 1.0633 \times 10^{5}}{1 \times 96485} = 1.047 \, mol/s$$ $$\dot{m}_{Cl_2} = 1.047 \times 71 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0743 \, kg/s = 267.6 \, kg/h$$ **Step 6: Specific energy consumption** $$E_s = \frac{n F U_{cell}}{3.6 \times 10^6 \cdot \Phi \cdot M}$$ $$E_s = \frac{1 \times 96485 \times 3.1}{3.6 \times 10^6 \times 0.95 \times 71 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$E_s = \frac{299104}{242.82} = 1.232 \, kWh/kg \, Cl_2$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students confuse the **total current** in a multi-cell stack. A 25-pair stack means **25 anodes and 25 cathodes in series electrically** but **flow in parallel hydraulically**. Use the *parallel hydraulic* flow but the *series electrical* connection — current is **the same through every cell**. Do not multiply $I$ by 25 *and* by 25 again. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, Alternative Choice]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **differences between CSTR and PFR electrochemical reactor configurations**. For a first-order electrochemical reaction, derive the conversion expressions for both and comment on which gives higher conversion for the same electrode area. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A wastewater stream containing 0.05 kmol/m³ of toxic Cr(VI) is treated in a CSTR equipped with iron cathodes. The mass-transfer coefficient is $k_m = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s, electrode area $A = 1.2$ m², volumetric flow $Q = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s, $n = 3$, and the rate constant for the surface reaction is $k = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. Calculate: (i) the **conversion**, (ii) the **limiting current density**, and (iii) the **required current** to operate at $0.6\,i_L$. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part B(a) | Feature | CSTR | PFR | |---------|------|-----| | Concentration profile | Uniform (exit = inside) | Decreases along length | | Conversion for 1st order | $X = \dfrac{k\tau}{1+k\tau}$ | $X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ | | Best for | Kinetically controlled, continuous quality | Mass-transfer limited, plug flow | | Backmixing | Maximum | None (ideal) | **Derivation of CSTR conversion** (from §3.3 above): $$X_{CSTR} = \frac{k V/Q}{1 + k V/Q}$$ **Derivation of PFR conversion** (from §3.2 above): $$X_{PFR} = 1 - e^{-kV/Q}$$ **Comparison:** For identical $V/Q$ and $k$, $X_{PFR} > X_{CSTR}$ always (except at trivial limits). The ratio $X_{PFR}/X_{CSTR} \to 1$ as $k\tau \to 0$ and diverges as $k\tau \to \infty$. For $k\tau = 1$: PFR gives 0.632 vs CSTR's 0.500 (≈26% advantage). #### Model Solution — Part B(b) **Step 1: Conversion (CSTR formula)** $$X = \frac{kA/Q}{1 + kA/Q} = \frac{(1.8 \times 10^{-5})(1.2)/(1.5 \times 10^{-4})}{1 + (1.8 \times 10^{-5})(1.2)/(1.5 \times 10^{-4})}$$ $$X = \frac{0.144}{1.144} = 0.1259 \approx 12.6\%$$ **Step 2: Limiting current density** $$i_L = n F k_m C_b = (3)(96485)(2 \times 10^{-5})(50)$$ $$i_L = 289.5 \, A/m^2$$ **Step 3: Operating current at $0.6\,i_L$** $$i_{op} = 0.6 \times 289.5 = 173.7 \, A/m^2$$ $$I = i_{op} \cdot A = 173.7 \times 1.2 = 208.4 \, A$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):** A common mistake is **unit confusion** in Step 1: students write $k_m C_b$ without ensuring $C_b$ is in $mol/m^3$ (not $kmol/m^3$) and $k_m$ in $m/s$ (not $cm/s$). Watch the units! --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU Module 4 — Reactor Design)** - [ ] **Three design metrics to compute for every problem**: electrode area $A$, electrolyte volume $V$, and specific energy $E_s$. - [ ] **Faraday's law is the anchor** — it links *current* to *moles of product*. Memorize: $\dot{n}_p = \Phi I / (nF)$. - [ ] **Limiting current** $i_L = nFk_mC_b$ is the *ceiling* on productivity. Design $i_{op} = 0.5$–$0.8\,i_L$. - [ ] **PFR vs CSTR conversion**: exponential vs rational form. PFR always wins for first-order kinetics. - [ ] **Voltage balance** must include *all five* terms: $E_{eq}$, $\eta_a$, $\eta_c$, $IR_{solution}$, $IR_{electrode}$. Forgetting any one term loses 2–3 marks. - [ ] **Current efficiency** $\Phi$ is *always* < 1; always specify the side reaction responsible. - [ ] **Specific area** $a$ distinguishes reactor types: $a \sim 100\,m^2/m^3$ (parallel plate) up to $a \sim 5000\,m^2/m^3$ (3D electrodes, packed/fluidized beds). - [ ] **Three dimensionless groups** form the design triangle: $Re$, $Sc$, $Sh$. Always cite the range of validity. - [ ] **Specific energy consumption** in $kWh/kg$ is the *bottom-line* economic metric. Typical industrial values: chlor-alkali ≈ 2.0; Cu electrowinning ≈ 2.0–2.5; Al smelting ≈ 13–15. - [ ] **Membrane vs undivided**: divided cells give pure products but add resistance; undivided cells are simpler but allow cross-contamination. - [ ] **KTU hot tip**: When asked to "design a reactor", the expected answer structure is (1) kinetics, (2) mass transfer, (3) area from Faraday, (4) volume from conversion target, (5) energy from voltage balance. - [ ] **Memorize the constants**: $F = 96485 \, C/mol$; $1 \, F = 26.8 \, Ah/mol$ (useful for time-based current integration).

topics.title: Chlor-Alkali Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Chlor-Alkali Process # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) The **Chlor-Alkali Process** is the large-scale industrial **electrolytic decomposition of aqueous sodium chloride (brine) solution** to simultaneously co-produce three commercially critical chemicals: **chlorine gas (Cl₂)**, **hydrogen gas (H₂)**, and **sodium hydroxide (NaOH / caustic soda)**. It is, by tonnage, the **second-largest electrochemical industry in the world** (after aluminium smelting) and is classified as a **brine electrolysis** process operating on the principle of **aqueous electrolysis with ionic separation**. The KTU 2024 Scheme officially classifies it under the umbrella of **Industrial Electrochemical Processes** and demands that students master both the **electrochemical reaction engineering** (anode/cathode half-reactions, thermodynamic decomposition voltage) and the **process engineering** (cell design choices — mercury, diaphragm, or membrane). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):** > This topic directly maps to **CO4** of PEPOT637 — *"Apply electrochemical principles to the design and analysis of industrial electrolytic processes."* Expect at least one **14-mark question** on this topic in the End Semester Examination (ESE). ## 1.2 The Master (Overall) Reaction $$2\text{NaCl}_{(aq)} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \xrightarrow{\text{DC Electricity}} 2\text{NaOH}_{(aq)} + \text{Cl}_{2(g)} + \text{H}_{2(g)}$$ The standard Gibbs free energy change is **highly positive (≈ +422 kJ per mole of Cl₂)**, meaning the reaction is **non-spontaneous**. Therefore, **electrical energy must be continuously supplied** to drive it forward. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Story Building" of Saltwater > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Apartment Building Electrolyser:** > Imagine a tall apartment building where tenants (ions) must be *sorted* into different floors using a strict "elevator policy": > > 1. **The Brine Lobby (Anode Compartment):** Salt water enters. The "doorman" (anode) only allows **Chloride ions (Cl⁻)** to "leave the building" as **Cl₂ gas** through the chimney. > 2. **The Central Wall (Separator/Membrane/Diaphragm):** A security barrier that **only allows Na⁺ ions to pass through** to the other side, but **blocks Cl⁻ and OH⁻ from migrating back**. > 3. **The Cathode Penthouse (Cathode Compartment):** Pure water arrives. The "maid" (cathode) only takes **H⁺ (from water)** and turns it into **H₂ gas**. The **Na⁺ ions** that crossed over now pair with **OH⁻ (left behind by water splitting)** to form **NaOH (caustic soda)**. > > The **electric current** is the electricity bill that powers the elevators. **No electricity = no sorting = no products.** ## 1.4 Industrial Significance & Production Metrics | Product | World Annual Production (approx.) | Primary Use | |---|---|---| | **Chlorine (Cl₂)** | **~70 Million Tonnes** | PVC plastics, solvents, water disinfection | | **Caustic Soda (NaOH)** | **~80 Million Tonnes** | Pulp & paper, soap, aluminium extraction (Bayer process) | | **Hydrogen (H₂)** | By-product | HCl synthesis, hydrogenation, fuel | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Thumb:** Approximately **1.8 tonnes of NaOH** are produced per **1 tonne of Cl₂**. This ratio is called the **equi-production ratio** and is the key driver of chlorine economics — NaOH is often called **"co-product caustic."** ## 1.5 Historical Evolution of Cell Technology 1. **1892 — Castner-Kellner Mercury Cell** (first industrial process) 2. **1908 — Diaphragm Cell** (Griesheim, Germany) 3. **1970s — Ion-Exchange Membrane Cell** (modern, dominant technology) This evolution is driven by the relentless pursuit of: - **Lower energy consumption** (kWh per tonne of Cl₂) - **Higher product purity** - **Environmental compliance** (eliminating mercury & asbestos) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Electrode Half-Reactions (The Core of the Process) Although the *overall* reaction looks simple, the actual cell contains **two competing electrochemical reactions at each electrode** because water itself can be electrolysed. Selective electrocatalysis is the key engineering challenge. ### 2.1.1 At the **Anode** (Positive Electrode — Oxidation) Two possible reactions: $$\text{Chlorine Evolution: } 2\text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{Cl}_{2(g)} + 2e^- \quad E^\circ = +1.36 \text{ V vs SHE}$$ $$\text{Oxygen Evolution (undesired): } 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_{2(g)} + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^- \quad E^\circ = +1.23 \text{ V vs SHE}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is Cl₂ produced and not O₂?** Although oxygen has a *lower* standard potential (thermodynamically favoured), chlorine evolution has **much faster kinetics** on a suitable **Dimensionally Stable Anode (DSA)** made of **Ti coated with RuO₂/TiO₂**. This is a classic example of **kinetics overriding thermodynamics** — a frequently tested KTU concept. ### 2.1.2 At the **Cathode** (Negative Electrode — Reduction) $$\text{Hydrogen Evolution: } 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_{2(g)} + 2\text{OH}^- \quad E^\circ = -0.83 \text{ V vs SHE}$$ $$\text{Sodium Deposition (NOT desired): } \text{Na}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{Na}_{(s)} \quad E^\circ = -2.71 \text{ V vs SHE}$$ The huge potential gap (−0.83 V vs −2.71 V) guarantees that **water is reduced, not sodium**. This is why aqueous electrolysis of brine works at all. ## 2.2 Theoretical Decomposition Voltage (E°cell) $$E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode} = (-0.83) - (+1.36) = -2.19 \text{ V}$$ The negative sign confirms non-spontaneity. The **absolute theoretical decomposition voltage is 2.19 V**. > [!IMPORTANT] > In practice, due to overpotentials (η) and ohmic drops (IR), the **actual cell voltage** is **3.0 – 4.5 V**, meaning roughly **30–50 % of the input energy is "wasted" as heat**. ## 2.3 Faraday's Laws Applied to Chlor-Alkali ### 2.3.1 First Law — Mass Produced $$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ ### 2.3.2 Second Law — Current Efficiency (Faradaic Efficiency, ηF) $$\eta_F = \frac{\text{Actual mass of product}}{\text{Theoretical mass from Faraday's Law}} \times 100\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **Current Efficiency Loss Pathways in Chlor-Alaki:** > - **Back-migration:** OH⁻ ions crossing to anode, reacting with Cl₂ → hypochlorite (wastes both Cl₂ and NaOH) > - **Oxygen evolution** at anode (competes with Cl₂) > - **Side reactions** forming chlorate (ClO₃⁻) in the membrane cell > Typical ηF for Cl₂ is **95–98 %**; for NaOH it is slightly lower due to back-migration. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Typical Value / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Theoretical Decomposition Voltage | $E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cath} - E^\circ_{anode}$ | **2.19 V** | | 2 | Actual Cell Voltage | $V_{cell} = E_{eq} + \eta_a + \eta_c + IR_{electrolyte}$ | **3.0 – 4.5 V** | | 3 | Mass of Product (Faraday's 1st Law) | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | kg | | 4 | Faradaic / Current Efficiency | $\eta_F = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | **%** (95–98 %) | | 5 | Specific Energy Consumption | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{V_{cell} \cdot 26.8}{\eta_F \cdot M}$ | **kWh / tonne Cl₂** | | 6 | Theoretical Energy (Cl₂) | $E_{th} = 2.19 \times 26.8 / 71 \times 1000$ | **~ 826 kWh/tonne Cl₂** | | 7 | Energy Efficiency (Overall) | $\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{E_{th}}{E_{actual}} \times 100$ | **30 – 50 %** | | 8 | Brine Utilization | $U_{brine} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | **~ 50 %** (single pass) | | 9 | Ohmic Drop in Membrane | $V_{ohm} = J \cdot (R_m + R_{brine} + R_{catholyte})$ | Volts | | 10 | Charge on 1 mole e⁻ | $F = N_A \cdot e$ | **96,485 C/mol** | > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Constant for KTU Numerical Problems:** > **26.8 A·h** = charge required to liberate **1 gram-equivalent** of any substance. Use this for fast tonne-scale calculations. > i.e., to produce 1 tonne of Cl₂ (eq. wt. 35.45 g/eq): $I \cdot t = \dfrac{10^6 \times 10^3}{35.45} \times 26.8 \approx 7.56 \times 10^5$ A·h/tonne. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The Chlor-Alkali process is the **backbone of the inorganic chemical industry**. Without it: - **PVC pipes** (from Cl₂) would be unavailable. - **Bayer process alumina refining** (using NaOH) for aluminium would be impossible. - **Bleach** (NaOCl, made on-site from Cl₂ + NaOH) for sanitation and drinking water treatment would not exist. It also serves as the **prototype for understanding all brine electrolysis processes** — chlorate, perchlorate, and water electrolysis all share the same half-reaction logic. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Decomposition Voltage (E°cell) This derivation is **mandatory** for KTU 14-mark questions on thermodynamics of chlor-alkali. **Step 1 — Identify the spontaneous cell direction.** The overall desired reaction requires energy input, so it is **electrolytic (non-spontaneous)**. We must apply an external voltage at least equal to $-E^\circ_{cell}$ to drive it. **Step 2 — Write the half-reactions with their standard potentials.** Anode (oxidation, so reverse the reduction value): $$2\text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2 + 2e^- \quad E^\circ_{ox} = -1.36 \text{ V}$$ Cathode (reduction, as written): $$2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \quad E^\circ_{red} = -0.83 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the cell potential formula.** $$\begin{aligned} E^\circ_{cell} &= E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode (as reduction)} \\ &= (-0.83) - (+1.36) \\ &= -2.19 \text{ V} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Interpret the sign.** The negative sign means the reaction as written (Cl₂ evolution + H₂ evolution + NaOH formation) is **non-spontaneous**. An external EMF of **at least 2.19 V** must be applied. **Step 5 — Account for Nernstian (concentration) corrections at actual operating conditions.** $$E_{cell} = E^\circ_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ For typical operating concentrations (4 M NaCl, 30 % NaOH, 1 atm gas pressure), the Nernst correction shifts the equilibrium voltage slightly — typically to about **2.25 V** at 80 °C. ## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Energy Consumption per Tonne of Cl₂ This is the **most-asked KTU numerical** on this topic. **Step 1 — Start with Faraday's First Law for 1 tonne of Cl₂.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Mass} &= 1 \text{ tonne} = 10^6 \text{ g} \\ \text{Equivalent weight of Cl}_2 &= \frac{M}{n} = \frac{71}{2} = 35.5 \text{ g/eq} \\ \text{Number of equivalents} &= \frac{10^6}{35.5} = 28{,}169 \text{ eq} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Charge required.** $$\begin{aligned} Q &= \text{equivalents} \times 26.8 \text{ A·h/eq} \\ &= 28{,}169 \times 26.8 \\ &= 7.55 \times 10^5 \text{ A·h per tonne Cl}_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Energy required (theoretical minimum).** $$\begin{aligned} E_{th} &= Q \times V_{cell(theoretical)} \\ &= 7.55 \times 10^5 \text{ A·h} \times 2.19 \text{ V} \\ &= 1.654 \times 10^6 \text{ W·h} \\ &= 1654 \text{ kWh per tonne Cl}_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Actual energy with overpotentials and ohmic losses.** For a typical membrane cell operating at $V_{cell}$ = 3.0 V and $\eta_F$ = 97 %: $$\begin{aligned} E_{actual} &= \frac{V_{cell}}{\eta_F} \times \frac{26.8 \times 10^6}{35.5 \times 1000} \\ &= \frac{3.0}{0.97} \times 755 \\ &\approx 2335 \text{ kWh per tonne Cl}_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Energy efficiency.** $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{energy} &= \frac{E_{theoretical}}{E_{actual}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{1654}{2335} \times 100 \\ &\approx 70.8\% \text{ (membrane cell is highly efficient)} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > [!NOTE] > **Numerical (3 marks):** A chlor-alkali membrane cell operates at 3.2 V and 95 % current efficiency. The cell delivers 50,000 A of current. Calculate: > (a) Tonnes of Cl₂ produced per day. > (b) Actual specific energy consumption in kWh/tonne. > (c) Energy efficiency vs the theoretical 1654 kWh/tonne. ### Part (a) — Mass of Cl₂ per Day $$\begin{aligned} Q_{day} &= I \times t = 50{,}000 \text{ A} \times 24 \text{ h} = 1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ A·h} \\ Q_{effective} &= 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 0.95 = 1.14 \times 10^6 \text{ A·h} \\ m_{Cl_2} &= \frac{Q_{effective} \times 35.5}{26.8 \times 1000} \\ &= \frac{1.14 \times 10^6 \times 35.5}{26.8 \times 1000} \\ &= 1509.9 \text{ kg/day} \\ &\approx 1.51 \text{ tonnes/day of Cl}_2 \end{aligned}$$ ### Part (b) — Actual Specific Energy Consumption $$\begin{aligned} E_{actual} &= \frac{3.2 \times 1.2 \times 10^6}{1.51 \times 1000} \\ &= \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{1510} \\ &\approx 2543 \text{ kWh/tonne Cl}_2 \end{aligned}$$ ### Part (c) — Energy Efficiency $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{energy} &= \frac{1654}{2543} \times 100 \\ &\approx 65.1\% \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix of the Three Cell Technologies | Parameter | Mercury Cell | Diaphragm Cell | Membrane Cell (Modern) | |---|---|---|---| | **Anode** | Graphite / DSA (Ti-RuO₂) | DSA | DSA (Ti-RuO₂) | | **Cathode** | Liquid mercury | Steel mesh | Steel / Nickel with catalytic coating | | **Separator** | None (Hg acts as cathode) | Asbestos / polymer diaphragm | **Perfluorinated ion-exchange membrane (Nafion™)** | | **NaOH Strength Produced** | **50 %** (directly) | **10 – 12 %** (needs evaporation) | **30 – 35 %** (directly) | | **NaOH Purity** | **Very high** (Hg cell grade) | **Low** (contains NaCl) | **Very high** (electronic grade possible) | | **Cl₂ Purity** | **High** | Moderate (contains O₂) | High | | **Cell Voltage** | 4.0 – 4.5 V | 3.0 – 3.5 V | **2.8 – 3.2 V** (lowest) | | **Specific Energy (kWh/tonne Cl₂)** | **3300 – 3600** | **2700 – 3000** | **2200 – 2400** | | **Environmental Hazard** | **Severe (Mercury pollution)** | Asbestos disposal issue | **Lowest** (green process) | | **Capital Cost** | High | Moderate | High (but dropping) | | **Current Status (Global 2024)** | **Phasing out / banned in EU** | Declining | **Dominant > 80 %** | | **Key Weakness** | Mercury toxicity | Dilute NaOH needs evaporation | Membrane fouling / cost | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Overall Process Block Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[BRINE STORAGE
Saturated NaCl Solution] --> B[BRINE PURIFICATION
Removal of Ca, Mg, SO4] B --> C[ANOLYTE TANK
Dechlorinated brine feed] C --> D[ANODE COMPARTMENT
Cl- ions oxidized to Cl2 gas] D --> E[ION EXCHANGE MEMBRANE
Allows Na+ only] E --> F[CATHODE COMPARTMENT
Water reduced to H2 gas] F --> G[CAUSTIC TANK
30 percent NaOH collection] D --> H[CHLORINE COOLING and DRYING] H --> I[LICHLOR LIQUEFACTION] I --> J[LIQUID Cl2 STORAGE] F --> K[H2 COOLING and PURIFICATION] K --> L[H2 STORAGE or COMPRESSOR] G --> M[NaOH STORAGE TANK] M --> N[FINAL PRODUCT DISPATCH] B --> O[DEPLETED BRINE RETURN] O --> P[BRINE RESATURATION UNIT] P --> A ``` ## 4.2 Membrane Cell Cross-Sectional Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ANOLYTE_LOOP [ANOLYTE SIDE - pH 2 to 4] AA[Brine In - 300 g per L NaCl] --> AB[Anode: Ti Substrate with RuO2/TiO2 Coating] AB --> AC[Cl2 Gas Evolution at Anode] AB --> AD[Depleted Brine - 200 g per L NaCl] end subgraph MEMBRANE_ZONE [PERFLUORINATED ION EXCHANGE MEMBRANE - Nafion Type] MA1[Fixed Negative Sulfonate SO3- Sites] MA2[Hydronium and Water Channels] MA3[Selective Na+ Permeation] end subgraph CATHOLYTE_LOOP [CATHOLYTE SIDE - pH greater than 14] CA[Pure Dilute NaOH Feed - 30 percent] --> CB[Cathode: Nickel with Activated Coating] CB --> CC[H2 Gas Evolution at Cathode] CB --> CD[Concentrated NaOH Product - 30 to 35 percent] end AD --> ANOLYTE_LOOP AC --> ANOLYTE_LOOP MA3 --> CATHOLYTE_LOOP CC --> CATHOLYTE_LOOP CD --> CATHOLYTE_LOOP ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Cell Architecture Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MERCURY_CELL [MERCURY CELL - Obsolete] M1[Brine] --> M2[Anode DSA above] M2 --> M3[Liquid Hg flowing cathode] M3 --> M4[Amalgam decomposer with water] M4 --> M5[50 percent pure NaOH] M4 --> M6[H2 gas] M2 --> M7[Cl2 gas] end subgraph DIAPHRAGM_CELL [DIAPHRAGM CELL -
