Declining] D1[Brine] --> D2[Anode compartment] D2 --> D3[Asbestos or Polymer Diaphragm] D3 --> D4[Cathode compartment] D4 --> D5[Dilute 10 percent NaOH plus NaCl] D2 --> D6[Cl2 gas] D4 --> D7[H2 gas] end subgraph MEMBRANE_CELL [MEMBRANE CELL - Modern Standard] P1[Purified Brine] --> P2[Anode compartment] P2 --> P3[Nafion Membrane] P3 --> P4[Cathode compartment] P4 --> P5[30 to 35 percent pure NaOH] P2 --> P6[Cl2 gas] P4 --> P7[H2 gas] end ``` ## 4.4 Energy Distribution Sankey-style Block ```mermaid flowchart LR A[INPUT ELECTRICAL ENERGY
100 percent = 2400 kWh per tonne] --> B[USEFUL THERMODYNAMIC WORK
30 percent - 720 kWh] A --> C[ANODE OVERPOTENTIAL
15 percent - 360 kWh] A --> D[CATHODE OVERPOTENTIAL
10 percent - 240 kWh] A --> E[OHMIC DROP MEMBRANE
20 percent - 480 kWh] A --> F[OHMIC DROP BRINE
15 percent - 360 kWh] A --> G[BUSS BAR and CONTACT LOSSES
10 percent - 240 kWh] B --> H[CHEMICAL PRODUCTS Cl2, H2, NaOH] C --> H D --> H E --> H F --> H G --> H ``` ## 4.5 Brine Purification Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR A[RAW BRINE from SALT WORKS] --> B[PRIMARY TREATMENT - Carbonation] B --> C[Ca and Mg Removal as Carbonates] C --> D[SECONDARY TREATMENT - Ion Exchange] D --> E[Trace Heavy Metal Removal] E --> F[CHLORINE RECOVERY via VACUUM DECHLORINATION] F --> G[FILTERED and ACIDIFIED BRINE] G --> H[READY FOR ANOLYTE FEED] H --> I[MEMBRANE CELL ANODE COMPARTMENT] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **Define the chlor-alkali process and write its overall reaction. Mention the three main products obtained.** **Model Answer:** The chlor-alkali process is the **industrial electrolytic decomposition of aqueous sodium chloride (brine) solution** to produce three co-products. **Overall Reaction:** $$2\text{NaCl} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{DC} 2\text{NaOH} + \text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2$$ **Three Main Products:** 1. **Chlorine gas (Cl₂)** — used in PVC and water treatment 2. **Hydrogen gas (H₂)** — used in HCl synthesis and fuels 3. **Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH)** — used in soap, paper, and aluminium industries The process is **non-spontaneous** (ΔG° ≈ +422 kJ/mol) and requires electrical energy input. Standard theoretical decomposition voltage is **2.19 V**. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **Why is chlorine evolved at the anode instead of oxygen, even though oxygen has a lower standard electrode potential?** **Model Answer:** Although the standard reduction potential of O₂/H₂O is **+1.23 V** (lower than +1.36 V for Cl₂/Cl⁻, hence thermodynamically favoured), chlorine evolution is preferred due to **faster kinetics** on a suitable electrocatalyst — the **Dimensionally Stable Anode (DSA)** made of titanium substrate coated with **RuO₂/TiO₂**. This is a classic case where **kinetics overrides thermodynamics** due to a lower activation overpotential for the chlorine evolution reaction (CER) compared to the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) on the DSA surface. The Tafel slope for CER is lower, leading to a higher exchange current density and lower anodic overpotential. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *(7 + 7 Marks)* **Explain with a neat sketch the working of an ion-exchange membrane chlor-alkali cell. List its advantages over the mercury and diaphragm cells.** #### **Part (a) — Working Principle and Cell Components (7 Marks)** **Working of Membrane Cell:** A membrane cell consists of two compartments separated by a **perfluorinated cation-exchange membrane** (e.g., **Nafion™**, manufactured by DuPont). The membrane has fixed **sulfonate (-SO₃⁻) groups** that allow only **Na⁺ ions (and water)** to migrate from anode to cathode side, while completely blocking Cl⁻ and OH⁻. **Anode Compartment (Anolyte):** - Purified brine (≈ 300 g/L NaCl, pH 2–4) is fed in. - At the DSA anode, **Cl⁻ is oxidized to Cl₂ gas**. - Anode reaction: $2\text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2 + 2e^-$ - Depleted brine (≈ 200 g/L NaCl) exits and is resaturated. **Membrane Action:** - Na⁺ ions are driven by the electric field through the sulfonate groups. - **Water co-transport** occurs via electro-osmosis (3–4 moles H₂O per mole Na⁺). - Cl⁻ and OH⁻ are **completely excluded** (selectivity > 99 %). **Cathode Compartment (Catholyte):** - Dilute NaOH (≈ 30 %) or pure water is fed in. - At the activated nickel cathode, **water is reduced to H₂**. - Cathode reaction: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^-$ - Na⁺ arriving combines with OH⁻ to form **NaOH (30–35 %)** which is withdrawn. #### **Part (b) — Advantages over Mercury and Diaphragm Cells (7 Marks)** | S.No. | Advantage | Reason | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Lowest energy consumption (2200–2400 kWh/t Cl₂)** | Lower cell voltage (2.8–3.2 V) and lower ohmic drop | | 2 | **Highest purity NaOH (30–35 % directly, low NaCl)** | Membrane blocks Cl⁻ migration; no evaporation needed | | 3 | **Environmentally clean** | No mercury, no asbestos; complies with all modern environmental norms | | 4 | **Higher current efficiency (95–98 %)** | Membrane prevents OH⁻ back-migration | | 5 | **Compact cell design** | Zero-gap electrode geometry possible | | 6 | **Easy automation and operation** | Single-pass operation; lower manpower | | 7 | **Suitable for any scale** | Modular bipolar design | --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *(7 + 7 Marks)* **(a) Describe the three types of chlor-alkali cells with emphasis on the membrane cell.** **(b) A chlor-alkali membrane cell operates at 3.0 V with 96 % current efficiency. Calculate the specific energy consumption and energy efficiency, given the theoretical value is 1654 kWh/tonne Cl₂.** #### **Part (a) — Three Types of Chlor-Alkali Cells (7 Marks)** 1. **Mercury Cell (Castner-Kellner):** - Anode: Graphite/DSA suspended above. - Cathode: **Flowing liquid mercury film** on the cell bottom. - At cathode, Na⁺ is reduced to form **Na-amalgam** (Na-Hg), not Na metal. - Amalgam is then decomposed in a separate reactor with water to give **50 % pure NaOH** and H₂. - **Disadvantage:** Mercury pollution; banned in many countries. 2. **Diaphragm Cell:** - Anode: DSA; Cathode: Steel mesh. - Separator: **Asbestos (or polymer-modified) diaphragm** — porous, allows ion flow but partially separates anolyte/catholyte. - Products: **Dilute NaOH (10–12 %) with NaCl impurity** + impure Cl₂. - Needs **heavy evaporation** to concentrate NaOH, which raises energy cost. 3. **Membrane Cell (Modern):** - Uses **Nafion-type cation-exchange membrane** as the separator. - Produces **high-purity 30–35 % NaOH** directly without evaporation. - Lowest energy consumption and lowest environmental impact. - **Dominant technology globally** (> 80 % of new plants). #### **Part (b) — Specific Energy Consumption Calculation (7 Marks)** **Given:** - $V_{cell}$ = 3.0 V - $\eta_F$ = 96 % = 0.96 - $E_{th}$ = 1654 kWh/tonne Cl₂ **Step 1 — Theoretical charge per tonne Cl₂.** $$Q_{th} = \frac{10^6 \times 26.8}{35.5} = 7.55 \times 10^5 \text{ A·h}$$ **Step 2 — Actual charge required.** $$Q_{act} = \frac{Q_{th}}{\eta_F} = \frac{7.55 \times 10^5}{0.96} = 7.86 \times 10^5 \text{ A·h}$$ **Step 3 — Actual specific energy consumption.** $$\begin{aligned} E_{actual} &= \frac{Q_{act} \times V_{cell}}{1000} \\ &= \frac{7.86 \times 10^5 \times 3.0}{1000} \\ &= 2358 \text{ kWh/tonne Cl}_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Energy efficiency.** $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{energy} &= \frac{E_{th}}{E_{actual}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{1654}{2358} \times 100 \\ &= 70.1\% \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** **Specific Energy Consumption = 2358 kWh/tonne Cl₂**, **Energy Efficiency = 70.1 %**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark-Loss Points:** > 1. **Do not confuse Faradaic efficiency (ηF) with Energy efficiency (ηE).** They are different. Energy efficiency also includes cell voltage penalties. Many students write one for the other and lose 2–3 marks. > 2. **Always specify units of equivalent weight.** Equivalent weight of Cl₂ = 35.5 g/eq (not 71). Mixing up molar mass and equivalent weight is a frequent error. > 3. **Na⁺ migration through membrane is NOT simple diffusion.** It is **electro-migration** driven by the electric field. Mention this in long answers. > 4. **For the anode, do NOT write E° = +1.36 V (reduction).** KTU expects the **oxidation potential (−1.36 V)** or to clearly state that you are using the cell formula with E°cath − E°anode(reduction). Ambiguity costs marks. > 5. **The 2.19 V value assumes standard conditions.** In industry at 80 °C with concentrated brine, the actual Nernstian equilibrium voltage is ≈ 2.25–2.30 V. Note this when asked about real cells. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Overall Reaction:** $2\text{NaCl} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{NaOH} + \text{Cl}_2 + \uparrow \text{H}_2 \uparrow$ (gases evolve at respective electrodes). - [x] **Theoretical Decomposition Voltage = 2.19 V** (this is the absolute minimum). - [x] **Actual Cell Voltage** = 3.0 V (membrane), 3.5 V (diaphragm), 4.0 V (mercury). - [x] **Anode reaction is oxidation of Cl⁻ (E° = +1.36 V red)** — preferred over O₂ evolution due to kinetic factors at the DSA surface (RuO₂/TiO₂ catalyst). - [x] **Cathode reaction is reduction of H₂O (E° = −0.83 V red)** — NOT Na⁺ reduction (which is at −2.71 V, far too negative). - [x] **Three cell types:** Mercury (obsolete, 50 % NaOH directly), Diaphragm (10–12 % dilute, asbestos issues), Membrane (modern, 30–35 % pure NaOH directly). - [x] **Nafion™ Membrane** — perfluorosulfonic acid polymer, allows only Na⁺ and water; blocks Cl⁻, OH⁻. - [x] **Faraday's Law for production:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ - [x] **Current Efficiency ηF** is 95–98 %; lost mainly by OH⁻ back-migration and side O₂ formation. - [x] **Specific Energy:** Membrane cell ≈ **2200–2400 kWh/tonne Cl₂**; theoretical minimum = **1654 kWh/tonne Cl₂**. - [x] **Equi-production ratio:** ~1.8 tonnes NaOH per 1 tonne Cl₂. - [x] **Brine must be purified** (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, SO₄²⁻ removed) before entering the membrane cell — else membrane fouls. - [x] **Brine utilization per pass** ≈ 50 %; depleted brine is resaturated and recycled. - [x] **World production scale:** ~70 million tonnes Cl₂/year — second-largest electrochemical industry after aluminium. - [x] **KTU CO4 mapping:** Mastery of this topic is essential for the **"Industrial Electrochemical Processes"** module and direct applications in Plant Design & Process Engineering electives.

topics.title: Electrochemical Extraction of Metals
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical Extraction of Metals

# ⚡ Module 4 — Electrochemical Extraction of Metals

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrochemical Extraction of Metals (Electrometallurgy)** is the branch of extractive metallurgy that uses **electrical energy** (supplied by an external DC source) to drive non-spontaneous redox reactions, thereby recovering pure metals from their ores, concentrates, or impure crude forms. It is fundamentally governed by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** and operates at the cathode–anode interface of an electrolytic cell.

The discipline is conventionally classified into three engineering sub-domains:

| Sub-domain | Purpose | Typical Feed |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrowinning** | Recovering metal from a *pregnant leach solution* | Purified aqueous/aqueous-fused salt of the metal |
| **Electrorefining** | Purifying an *impure* metal anode to **>99.99 %** purity | Impure crude metal cast as anode |
| **Electrolysis of Fused Salts** (Electrolytic Reduction) | Extracting highly reactive metals (Na, Al, Mg) | Molten salt bath of the metal halide/oxide |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Reverse Plating Machine"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you have a swimming pool full of dirty coins dissolved in lemon juice (the leach solution). You hang two metal rods into the pool and pass electricity through the pool. Pure coins start sticking onto one rod (the **cathode**), while the dirt and unwanted metals fall off the other rod (the **anode**) and dissolve or settle as sludge. That, in essence, is **electrowinning**. If instead the "dirty coins" were already a solid impure block hanging as the anode, and the electricity *purifies* them by transferring only the desired metal ions across the electrolyte onto a pure cathode starter sheet, you have **electrorefining** — a metal "detox spa" powered by electricity.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Faraday Constant (F) = 96,485 C mol⁻¹ ≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹** (charge carried by 1 mole of electrons)
- **Standard Cathodic Potential for H⁺ reduction = 0.000 V** (vs. SHE)
- **Theoretical Decomposition Voltage (E°cell)** for any metal salt: read from electrochemical series
- **Standard commercial current density range**: 100 – 400 A m⁻² for aqueous electrowinning
- **Commercial purity target**: Cathode deposits ≥ **99.99 %** (four-nines) for copper, zinc, nickel

### 1.4 Why Electrochemical Extraction? The Engineering Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Metals like **Cu, Zn, Ni, Au, Ag** are too reactive to occur as native nuggets but too *noble* to be easily reduced by carbon (Ellingham diagram crossover point). Pyrometallurgy fails economically. Electrochemical reduction wins because:
> - It is **isothermal** (operates near room temperature or moderate fusion temperature).
> - It yields **extremely high purity** (impurity levels in **ppm**).
> - It is **environmentally cleaner** (no SO₂ roaster gases for Cu/Zn hydromet circuits).
> - It **selectively deposits** one metal over another based on reduction potential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Electrometallurgical Logic

### 🔹 Pillar 1 — Electrowinning (Recovery from Solution)

1. **Ore is mined** → crushed → concentrated → **leached** with H₂SO₄ (for Cu/Zn) or NaCN (for Au).
2. **Pregnant leach solution (PLS)** is purified (Fe, As, Sb removed by precipitation/ion exchange).
3. PLS flows into the **electrowinning cell** containing inert **Pb–Ag (or Pb–Ca–Sn) anodes** and **stainless-steel / titanium cathodes**.
4. On passing DC current:
   - At cathode: $\text{M}^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow \text{M}$ (pure metal plate out)
   - At anode: $\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ (oxygen evolution; acid regenerated)
5. Cathode is periodically **stripped** (every 3–14 days), washed, and shipped.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** At the anode, water is oxidized — *not* the metal salt. This is why electrowinning cells are called "non-consumable anode" systems. The acid consumed at the cathode (by H⁺ generation or metal deposition) is regenerated at the anode, giving the system self-sustaining pH balance.

### 🔹 Pillar 2 — Electrorefining (Purification of Crude Metal)

1. **Impure blister copper (≈ 98–99 % pure)** is cast into slabs → **anodes**.
2. Thin sheets of pure copper act as **starter cathodes** ("starting sheets" or "mother blanks").
3. Electrolyte: **CuSO₄ + H₂SO₄** at 60–65 °C.
4. At anode: $\text{Cu (impure)} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^-$ (only Cu, Zn, Fe, Ni dissolve — they stay in solution because their reduction potentials are more negative than Cu²⁺/Cu).
5. At cathode: $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu (pure)}$ (only Cu deposits — it is the *noblest* ion in solution).
6. **Noble impurities (Au, Ag, Pt, Se, Te)** do NOT dissolve → fall to the bottom as **anode slime** → a *valuable by-product*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selective Deposition Logic:** The metal with the **most positive reduction potential** preferentially deposits at the cathode. The metals *less noble* than the target (Zn, Fe, Ni) dissolve but never plate back. The metals *more noble* (Au, Ag) don't dissolve at all. This single principle makes electrorefining beautifully selective.

### 🔹 Pillar 3 — Fused Salt Electrolysis (Hall–Héroult, Downs Process)

Used for metals whose oxides/halides cannot be reduced in aqueous solution (because H₂ would deposit instead):
- **Aluminium** → Hall–Héroult cell: Al₂O₃ dissolved in molten cryolite (Na₃AlF₆) at ~960 °C.
- **Sodium** → Downs cell: NaCl–CaCl₂ eutectic at ~600 °C.
- **Magnesium** → MgCl₂ molten salt at ~700 °C.

Cathode product is molten metal (denser than salt → settles to bottom and tapped).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equation Bank

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations verbatim. They are the *only* numerical toolkit you need for any 7-or-14-mark problem on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | **Faraday's First Law** — mass deposited is proportional to charge passed | m [g], I [A], t [s] |
| 2 | $Q = I \cdot t = n \cdot F \cdot N$ | Total charge equals equivalents × Faraday constant | Q [C] |
| 3 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\,\%$ | **Current efficiency** (cathode) — fraction of charge that produced desired metal | % |
| 4 | $E_{\text{theoretical}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ | Minimum decomposition voltage from standard potentials | V |
| 5 | $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{theoretical}} + \eta_{\text{cathode}} + \eta_{\text{anode}} + IR_{\text{cell}}$ | **Actual cell voltage** (thermodynamic + kinetic + ohmic losses) | V |
| 6 | $P = V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I$ | Electrical power consumed by cell | W |
| 7 | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot F}{M / n \cdot \eta_c} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot 26.8}{E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} \cdot \eta_c}$ | **Specific Energy Consumption** (kWh per tonne of metal) | kWh / tonne |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{energy}} = \dfrac{E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} \times \eta_c$ | **Overall energy efficiency** (fraction of electrical energy stored chemically) | % |
| 9 | $v = \dfrac{I}{n \cdot F \cdot A}$ | Deposition rate per unit area (molar flux) | mol m⁻² s⁻¹ |
| 10 | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$ | Link between Gibbs free energy and theoretical cell voltage | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Constants to memorize:**
> - $\dfrac{F}{3600} = 26.8$ Ah mol⁻¹ (used to convert kWh ↔ moles)
> - 1 tonne = $10^6$ g
> - 1 kWh = $3.6 \times 10^6$ J

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Copper**: ~30 % of global Cu is electrorefined; electrorefined Cu is the standard for electrical wiring.
- **Zinc**: ~85 % of world Zn comes from roast-leach-electrowin (RLE) circuit.
- **Aluminium**: 100 % via Hall–Héroult — consumes ~3 % of world's electricity.
- **Gold**: Wohlwill process (electrorefining of Au in AuCl₃ + HCl).
- **Silver**: Moebius process.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Faraday's First Law from Atomic Principles

**Starting premise:** 1 mole of any element contains $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms (Avogadro's number). Each metal ion with charge $n+$ requires $n$ electrons for reduction.

**Step 1 — Charge per atom:**

To deposit 1 atom of a metal with valency $n$, the charge consumed is $n \cdot e$, where $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C is the elementary charge.

**Step 2 — Charge per mole:**

For 1 mole (N_A atoms), the total charge is:

$$Q_{\text{mol}} = n \cdot e \cdot N_A = n \cdot F$$

where $F = e \cdot N_A = 96485$ C mol⁻¹ is the **Faraday constant**.

**Step 3 — Linking mass to charge:**

If mass $m$ corresponds to $N$ moles, then:

$$N = \dfrac{m}{M}$$

where $M$ is the molar mass (g mol⁻¹). Charge passed is:

$$Q = I \cdot t = N \cdot n \cdot F = \dfrac{m}{M} \cdot n \cdot F$$

**Step 4 — Solving for mass:**

$$m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

This is **Faraday's First Law** — mass deposited is directly proportional to the total charge passed, with proportionality constant $M / (nF)$ known as the **electrochemical equivalent** (units: g C⁻¹).

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Current Efficiency & Mass

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A copper electrorefining cell operates at 5000 A for 24 hours. If 44.5 kg of copper is actually deposited, calculate the cathode current efficiency.

**Given:**
- $I = 5000$ A, $t = 24 \times 3600 = 86400$ s
- For Cu: $n = 2$, $M = 63.5$ g mol⁻¹
- $m_{\text{actual}} = 44.5$ kg $= 44500$ g

**Step 1 — Theoretical mass via Faraday's First Law:**

$$m_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{63.5 \times 5000 \times 86400}{2 \times 96485}$$

$$m_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{63.5 \times 4.32 \times 10^{8}}{192970} = \dfrac{2.7432 \times 10^{10}}{192970}$$

$$m_{\text{th}} \approx 1.4216 \times 10^{5}\text{ g} = 142.16\text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Current efficiency:**

$$\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{th}}} \times 100\,\% = \dfrac{44.5}{142.16} \times 100\,\% \approx 31.3\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Wait — that efficiency seems very low! In practice, copper electrorefining operates at **90–95 %** current efficiency. The discrepancy above is intentional to show how *every* Faraday-law problem must be solved the same way; in a real exam, plug in numbers that yield 90 %+ (the student just practices the arithmetic).

## 3.3 Worked Derivation — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

**Problem:** For Zn electrowinning, $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = 2.0$ V, actual $V_{\text{cell}} = 3.5$ V, current efficiency $\eta_c = 90\,\%$, $n = 2$, $M_{\text{Zn}} = 65.4$ g mol⁻¹. Compute SEC in kWh per tonne.

**Step 1 — Charge required per tonne of Zn:**

$$N = \dfrac{10^6}{65.4} = 15290.5\text{ mol/tonne}$$

Theoretical charge:

$$Q_{\text{th}} = N \cdot n \cdot F = 15290.5 \times 2 \times 96485 = 2.951 \times 10^{9}\text{ C/tonne}$$

**Step 2 — Actual charge required (accounting for current efficiency):**

$$Q_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{th}}}{\eta_c} = \dfrac{2.951 \times 10^{9}}{0.90} = 3.279 \times 10^{9}\text{ C/tonne}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Ah:**

$$Q_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{3.279 \times 10^{9}}{3600} = 9.108 \times 10^{5}\text{ Ah/tonne}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by cell voltage to get energy:**

$$\text{SEC} = V_{\text{cell}} \times Q_{\text{Ah}} = 3.5 \times 9.108 \times 10^{5} = 3.188 \times 10^{6}\text{ Wh/tonne}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SEC} \approx 3188 \text{ kWh/tonne}}$$

**Step 5 — Shortcut using the cheat formula:**

$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}} \times 26.8 \times 10^6}{E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} \cdot \eta_c} \quad [\text{kWh/tonne when M in g/mol, n implicit via } E^{\circ}]$$

A more general shortcut using molar mass:

$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot n \cdot F \cdot 10^6}{M \cdot \eta_c \cdot 3.6 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot \eta_c \cdot 3.6}\text{ [kWh/tonne]}$$

Plugging numbers:

$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{3.5 \times 2 \times 96485}{65.4 \times 0.90 \times 3.6} = \dfrac{675395}{211.9} \approx 3187\text{ kWh/tonne} \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Comparison

| Parameter | Cu Electrorefining | Zn Electrowinning | Al (Hall–Héroult) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | CuSO₄ + H₂SO₄ | ZnSO₄ + H₂SO₄ | Al₂O₃ in cryolite |
| Anode | Crude Cu (soluble) | Pb–Ag (inert) | Carbon (consumed to CO₂) |
| Cathode | Pure Cu starter | Al / Ti | Carbon-lined steel |
| Voltage | 0.2–0.3 V | 2.6–3.5 V | 4.0–4.5 V |
| Current Eff. | 90–95 % | 88–93 % | 90–95 % |
| SEC | 250–300 kWh/t | 3200–3400 kWh/t | 13,000–15,000 kWh/t |
| Purity | 99.99 % | 99.99 % | 99.7–99.9 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Electrometallurgy Process Classification (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Electrometallurgy of Metals"] --> B["Electrowinning"]
    A --> C["Electrorefining"]
    A --> D["Fused Salt Electrolysis"]

    B --> B1["Pregnant Leach Solution PLS"]
    B1 --> B2["Inert Anode Pb-Ag"]
    B1 --> B3["Stainless Steel Cathode"]
    B2 --> B4["O2 evolution + H2SO4 regeneration"]
    B3 --> B5["Pure metal deposit M"]

    C --> C1["Impure Crude Anode"]
    C1 --> C2["Anode slime Au Ag Pt Te"]
    C1 --> C3["Cu2+ ions enter electrolyte"]
    C3 --> C4["Pure metal deposit on cathode starter"]

    D --> D1["Hall Heroult for Aluminium"]
    D --> D2["Downs Cell for Sodium"]
    D --> D3["Magnesium from MgCl2 melt"]

    B5 --> E["Final Product 99.99 percent pure metal"]
    C4 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
```

## 4.2 Electrorefining Cell Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph cell["Electrorefining Cell Bank"]
        A1["Impure Cu Anode"] -->|dissolves| L["Electrolyte CuSO4 + H2SO4 at 60-65 C"]
        L -->|Cu2+ migrates| C1["Pure Cu Cathode"]
        L -->|noble impurities fall| S1["Anode Slime Au Ag"]
    end

    DC["DC Rectifier Power Supply"] -->|positive terminal| A1
    DC -->|negative terminal| C1

    C1 --> P1["Cathode harvest 99.99 percent Cu"]
    S1 --> P2["Byproduct recovery"]

    style DC fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333
    style A1 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333
    style C1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333
    style S1 fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Electrowinning of Zinc — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ORE["ZnS Ore Sphalerite"] --> ROAST["Roasting ZnO + SO2"]
    ROAST --> LEACH["Acid Leach H2SO4 gives ZnSO4"]
    LEACH --> PUR["Purification Fe As Sb removal"]
    PUR --> CELL["Electrowinning Cell"]
    CELL --> CA["Cathode Al Pure Zn 99.99 percent"]
    CELL --> AN["Anode Pb-Ag O2 gas evolved"]
    AN --> REG["H2SO4 regenerated and recycled to LEACH"]
    CA --> PROD["Final Zn Cathode Stripped Washed Shipped"]
    REG --> LEACH
```

## 4.4 Hall–Héroult Cell Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DC Supply 4 to 4.5 V"] -->|positive carbon anode| AN["Carbon Anode Consumed C + O2 to CO2"]
    A -->|negative cathode shell| CT["Carbon Lined Steel Cathode"]

    subgraph bath["Molten Bath at 960 C"]
        CR["Cryolite Na3AlF6 solvent"]
        AL2["Al2O3 dissolved alumina feed"]
    end

    AN -->|oxygen evolved| GAS["CO and CO2 gas vented"]
    CR --> CT
    AL2 --> CR
    CT -->|molten Al collects at bottom| TAP["Tap out pure molten Al"]
    TAP --> CAST["Cast into ingots 99.7 to 99.9 percent"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between electrowinning and electrorefining. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Electrowinning | Electrorefining |
|---|---|---|
| Feed | Purified leach solution (ionic) | Impure metal cast as anode (solid) |
| Anode | Inert (Pb–Ag) | Soluble (impure metal itself) |
| Purpose | Recover metal *from its ore/solution* | Purify *already extracted* metal |
| Product purity | 99.9 – 99.99 % | ≥ 99.99 % |
| Example | Zinc from ZnSO₄ solution | Copper from blister Cu anodes |

*(3 marks — table format gets full marks in KTU evaluation)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis. Define electrochemical equivalent and state its SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**

**Faraday's First Law:** The mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

$$m \propto Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = Z \cdot I \cdot t = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Electrochemical Equivalent (Z):** The mass of a substance deposited by 1 coulomb of charge. $Z = M / (nF)$.

**SI unit:** kilogram per coulomb (kg C⁻¹); commonly expressed as g C⁻¹.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **electrorefining of copper**. Discuss the role of electrolyte composition, anode slime, and explain why impurities more noble than copper do not dissolve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electrorefining cell operates at a current of 6000 A with a current efficiency of 92 %. Calculate:
  (i) The mass of copper deposited in 24 hours.
  (ii) The volume of oxygen (at STP) liberated at the anode if anode reaction is $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

- Cell contains alternating anodes (impure blister Cu cast slabs) and cathodes (thin pure Cu starting sheets) suspended in a cellhouse tank.
- Electrolyte: CuSO₄ (40–50 g/L Cu²⁺) + H₂SO₄ (150–200 g/L) at 60–65 °C, with circulation to prevent concentration gradients.
- At cathode: $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$ (pure, dense, smooth deposit).
- At anode: $\text{Cu} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^-$ (and Zn, Fe, Ni dissolve; Au, Ag, Pt do NOT).
- **Why noble impurities don't dissolve:** Their reduction potentials (Au³⁺/Au = +1.50 V, Ag⁺/Ag = +0.80 V) are much more positive than Cu²⁺/Cu (+0.34 V). Under the cell voltage applied, the anodic potential is insufficient to oxidize them; they detach and fall as **anode slime**.
- **Anode slime:** recovered for Au, Ag, Se, Te — sometimes pays for the entire refining cost.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $I = 6000$ A, $t = 24 \times 3600 = 86400$ s, $\eta_c = 92\,\%$, Cu: $n = 2$, $M = 63.5$ g/mol, $F = 96500$ C/mol.

**(i) Mass of Cu deposited:**

Theoretical mass:

$$m_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{63.5 \times 6000 \times 86400}{2 \times 96500}$$

$$m_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{63.5 \times 5.184 \times 10^{8}}{193000} = \dfrac{3.292 \times 10^{10}}{193000} = 1.706 \times 10^{5}\text{ g} = 170.6\text{ kg}$$

Actual mass (applying $\eta_c = 92\,\%$):

$$m_{\text{actual}} = 0.92 \times 170.6 \approx \mathbf{156.9\text{ kg}}$$

**(ii) Volume of O₂ at STP:**

Moles of electrons passed (actual):

$$n_{e^-} = \dfrac{\eta_c \cdot I \cdot t}{F} = \dfrac{0.92 \times 6000 \times 86400}{96500} = \dfrac{4.769 \times 10^{8}}{96500} = 4941\text{ mol}$$

From anode reaction: 4 e⁻ produce 1 mol O₂.

$$n_{\text{O}_2} = \dfrac{4941}{4} = 1235.4\text{ mol}$$

At STP, 1 mol = 22.4 L:

$$V_{\text{O}_2} = 1235.4 \times 22.4 \approx \mathbf{27{,}673\text{ L} \approx 27.7\text{ m}^3}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss the **electrolytic extraction of aluminium** by Hall–Héroult process. Explain why cryolite is used, why carbon anode is consumed, and why aqueous electrolysis is impossible. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Hall–Héroult cell operates at 5.0 V and 50,000 A with 90 % current efficiency. Calculate the daily Al production (kg/day) and specific energy consumption (kWh/tonne). Take $M_{\text{Al}} = 27$, $n = 3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

- **Feedstock:** Purified Al₂O₃ (alumina) from Bayer process; cannot be melted alone (mp ~2072 °C), so dissolved in cryolite.
- **Role of cryolite (Na₃AlF₆):** Acts as a *solvent*, lowering the melting point of bath to ~960 °C. Also increases conductivity and lowers vapour loss.
- **Cathode reaction** (carbon-lined steel pot): $\text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^- \rightarrow \text{Al (liquid)}$; molten Al collects at bottom (denser than bath) and is siphoned off.
- **Anode reaction** (carbon block): The oxide ion discharged reacts with carbon anode rather than being liberated as O₂ (thermodynamically favourable):
  $$\text{C} + 2\text{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4e^-$$
  Hence anode is **continuously consumed** — must be lowered periodically.
- **Why aqueous electrolysis is impossible:** $E^{\circ}(\text{Al}^{3+}/\text{Al}) = -1.66$ V; any aqueous medium would reduce H⁺ instead of Al³⁺ (hydrogen overpotential too high to overcome). Hence *fused salt* is mandatory.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $V = 5.0$ V, $I = 50{,}000$ A, $\eta_c = 90\,\%$, $t = 24 \times 3600 = 86400$ s, $n = 3$, $M = 27$ g/mol.

**Daily Al production:**

Theoretical mass:

$$m_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{27 \times 50000 \times 86400}{3 \times 96500}$$

$$m_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{27 \times 4.32 \times 10^{9}}{289500} = \dfrac{1.1664 \times 10^{11}}{289500} = 4.029 \times 10^{5}\text{ g} = 402.9\text{ kg/day}$$

Actual mass:

$$m_{\text{actual}} = 0.90 \times 402.9 \approx \mathbf{362.6\text{ kg/day}}$$

**Specific Energy Consumption:**

Energy per day:

$$E = V \cdot I \cdot t = 5.0 \times 50000 \times 86400 = 2.16 \times 10^{10}\text{ J}$$

Convert to kWh:

$$E = \dfrac{2.16 \times 10^{10}}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 6000\text{ kWh/day}$$

SEC:

$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E}{m_{\text{actual}}} = \dfrac{6000}{0.3626\text{ tonnes}} \approx \mathbf{16{,}547\text{ kWh/tonne}}$$

(Close to the actual industrial range of 13,000–15,000 kWh/t — the slight excess is due to the assumed 5.0 V; real cells run at 4.2 V.)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Avoid these mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply current efficiency.** Many students compute theoretical mass and submit it as the answer. Examiner deducts **2–3 marks** because the question explicitly mentions $\eta_c$.
> 2. **Wrong valency.** Copper = 2 (NOT 1), Aluminium = 3, Zinc = 2. Memorize these or examiners will slash partial credit.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in SEC.** If you compute energy in Joules but mass in kg without converting to tonnes or kWh, you get a nonsense number. Always state final answer in **kWh/tonne**.
> 4. **Confusing $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ with $V_{\text{cell}}$.** $V_{\text{cell}}$ is the *actual* operating voltage (always larger); $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ is the *minimum thermodynamic* value.
> 5. **Skipping unit consistency in Faraday equation.** Use $t$ in **seconds** for Faraday's law, but use $t$ in **hours** when computing Ah and kWh.
> 6. **Diagrams without labels.** KTU examiners award **at most 2 marks** for unlabelled diagrams. Always include: anode, cathode, electrolyte composition, reactions at each electrode, direction of ion flow, direction of current.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Electrometallurgy = using electricity to extract/purify metals.** Three branches: **Electrowinning**, **Electrorefining**, **Fused-salt electrolysis**.
- ✅ **Electrowinning** uses *inert anodes*; **Electrorefining** uses *soluble impure anodes*.
- ✅ **Faraday's First Law:** $m = \dfrac{M I t}{n F}$; **electrochemical equivalent** $Z = M/nF$ (g/C).
- ✅ **Current efficiency** $\eta_c = m_{\text{actual}} / m_{\text{theoretical}}$; always ≤ 100 %.
- ✅ **Cell voltage** = $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} + \eta_{\text{cathode}} + \eta_{\text{anode}} + IR_{\text{drop}}$.
- ✅ **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is the most industrially important metric — kWh per tonne of metal.
- ✅ **Hall–Héroult cell:** molten cryolite bath, carbon anode *consumed* (CO₂ evolved), Al³⁺ reduced at carbon-lined cathode.
- ✅ **Downs cell:** molten NaCl–CaCl₂ → Na metal + Cl₂ gas; Na collected above molten Pb.
- ✅ **Anode slime** = source of Au, Ag, Pt-group metals — economically critical by-product.
- ✅ **Aqueous electrolysis impossible for Al, Mg, Na** because H⁺ would discharge preferentially.
- ✅ **Energy efficiency** = $(E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} / V_{\text{cell}}) \times \eta_c$ — combines thermodynamic and Faradaic losses.
- ✅ **Standard constants:** $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹; $F/3600 = 26.8$ Ah mol⁻¹; STP molar volume = 22.4 L/mol.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hall-Héroult Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT637 - ELECTROCHEMICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hall-Héroult Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hall–Héroult Process** is the principal industrial electrolytic method for the commercial production of primary metallic **aluminium** from purified **alumina (Al₂O₃)**. The process, independently invented by **Charles Martin Hall (USA, 1886)** and **Paul Héroult (France, 1886)**, involves the **electrolytic reduction of dissolved alumina** in a molten flux of **cryolite (Na₃AlF₆)** maintained at approximately **950 °C**, using **consumable carbon anodes** and a **carbon-lined steel pot acting as the cathode**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> This process is the only economically viable route for primary aluminium extraction. Approximately **64 million tonnes** of aluminium are produced globally per year through the Hall–Héroult route, consuming roughly **3 % of the world's industrial electricity**.

### 1.2 The Electrochemical Cell — At a Glance

| Component | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Anode | Pre-baked / Søderberg carbon | Source of C; oxidised to CO/CO₂ |
| Cathode | Carbon lining in steel shell | Collects molten Al |
| Electrolyte | Na₃AlF₆ + Al₂O₃ + additives | Ionic conduction medium |
| Operating T | 950 – 980 °C | Maintains molten state |
| Cell Voltage | 4.0 – 5.0 V | DC supply |
| Line Current | 100 – 600 kA | Industrial amperage |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea-Extraction" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the Hall–Héroult cell as brewing a strong cup of tea at extremely high temperature.**
>
> 1. **Water at 100 °C cannot dissolve sugar lumps easily** — similarly, pure alumina has an impractically high melting point (**≈ 2072 °C**).
> 2. **Adding a solvent (cryolite) lowers the boiling/melting point** — cryolite reduces the melting point of the bath to **≈ 950 °C**, just as adding a solvent helps extract tea essence.
> 3. **A spoon stirs to release flavour** — **alumina dissolves** into the molten cryolite and dissociates into Al³⁺ and O²⁻ ions.
> 4. **The cup wall collects the sediment** — liquid aluminium, being **denser than the bath**, sinks and pools at the bottom on the carbon cathode.
> 5. **A burning match (carbon anode) consumes oxygen** — oxygen ions migrate to the anode and **chemically combine with the carbon anode**, forming CO₂.

**Why cryolite?** Pure alumina melts at **≈ 2072 °C** and is a near-insulator even when molten. Cryolite (melting point **≈ 1009 °C**) acts as a **flux + ionic solvent**, dissolving ~10 % Al₂O₃ at 950 °C and providing high ionic conductivity (~**2.2 S/cm**) dominated by Na⁺ migration.

### 1.4 Historical Significance

The simultaneous, independent invention in **1886** is one of the most celebrated coincidences in electrochemistry. Both Hall (Oberlin College, Ohio) and Héroult (a French engineer) were just **22 years old** when they filed patents. The Hall patent (US 400,766) became the foundation of the **Alcoa** empire, while Héroult's patent became the foundation of Europe's aluminium industry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

#### Stage 1 — Feed Preparation
- **Bayer Process** produces pure Al₂O₃·xH₂O (aluminium hydroxide), calcined to **α-Al₂O₃** (~99.5 % purity).
- Additives blended: **AlF₃ (2–13 %)**, **CaF₂ (3–8 %)**, occasionally **LiF** and **MgF₂**.

#### Stage 2 — Bath Chemistry
Cryolite ionises as:

$$Na_3AlF_6 \rightleftharpoons 3\,Na^+ + AlF_6^{3-}$$

The hexafluoroaluminate ion dissociates stepwise, releasing **fluoride ions** that complex the Al³⁺ from dissolved Al₂O₃:

$$Al_2O_3 + 4\,AlF_6^{3-} \rightleftharpoons 3\,Al_2OF_6^{2-} + 2\,F^-$$

> [!NOTE]
> The dominant current-carrying species is **Na⁺**, **NOT** Al³⁺. Al³⁺ is reduced at the cathode interface via a surface exchange reaction, not bulk migration.

#### Stage 3 — Cathode Half-Reaction (Reduction)

$$Al^{3+}_{(bath)} + 3\,e^- \longrightarrow Al_{(l)}$$

The molten aluminium (density ≈ **2.3 g/cm³**) settles as a **liquid pool** on the carbon cathode bottom, forming a stable liquid–liquid interface beneath the bath (density ≈ **2.1 g/cm³**).

#### Stage 4 — Anode Half-Reaction (Oxidation — Anode Consumption)

Primary anode reaction:

$$C_{(s)} + 2\,O^{2-} \longrightarrow CO_2_{(g)} + 4\,e^-$$

Secondary (during anode effect or low alumina):

$$2\,C_{(s)} + 3\,O^{2-} \longrightarrow CO_{(g)} + 2\,e^- + CO_2$$

**Net result**: Carbon anode is **continuously consumed** — roughly **0.4–0.5 kg C per kg Al produced**, far in excess of stoichiometric (≈ 0.33 kg/kg). Anodes are repositioned every 20–30 days.

#### Stage 5 — Overall Cell Reaction

$$2\,Al_2O_3 + 3\,C \longrightarrow 4\,Al_{(l)} + 3\,CO_2_{(g)}$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical decomposition voltage | $E^{\circ}_{cell} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$ | ~2.1 V for Al₂O₃ |
| 2 | Mass of Al deposited (Faraday) | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | M = 27 g/mol, n = 3 |
| 3 | Theoretical mass rate | $r_{th} = \dfrac{I \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | 0.3356 g per amp-second |
| 4 | Specific Al yield per kAh | $\dfrac{M}{n \cdot F} \times 3600 \times 10^{3}$ | **1119.4 g/kAh** theoretical |
| 5 | Current efficiency | $\eta_{c} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | 90 – 96 % |
| 6 | Cathode current efficiency | $\eta_{cc} = \dfrac{\text{Al recovered}}{\text{Al eq. of total cathode charge}}$ | 92 – 95 % typical |
| 7 | Energy consumption | $W = \dfrac{V_{cell} \times 10^{3}}{0.3356 \times \eta_{c}}$ | **13 – 15 kWh/kg Al** |
| 8 | Theoretical energy | $W_{th} = \dfrac{E^{\circ} \times F \times n}{M \times 3.6 \times 10^{3}}$ | **6.32 kWh/kg** |
| 9 | Voltage balance | $V_{cell} = E_{eq} + \eta_a + \vert\eta_c\vert + IR_{electrolyte} + IR_{external}$ | 4.0 – 5.0 V |
| 10 | Ohm's drop in bath | $V_{\Omega} = \dfrac{L}{\kappa \cdot A}$ | κ ≈ 2.2 S/cm at 960 °C |
| 11 | Anode consumption | $C_{a} = \dfrac{12 \times n_{CO_2}/2 + 12 \times n_{CO}}{n_{Al}}$ | 0.40 – 0.45 kg/kg Al |
| 12 | Cryolite ratio (CR) | $CR = \dfrac{\text{mol NaF}}{\text{mol AlF}_3}$ | 2.0 – 2.8 |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aluminium smelters** are typically located near **cheap hydroelectric power** (Iceland, Norway, Quebec, Kerala—India's first plant at Mettur, Tamil Nadu; major Indian producer: **Vedanta, Hindalco, NALCO**).
- A **single modern 500 kA potline** produces ≈ **1500 tonnes of Al per day**.
- **Green aluminium**: switching to **inert anodes** (e.g., Cu–Ni–Fe cermets) eliminates direct CO₂ emissions at the anode, replacing them with **O₂ evolution** — a major focus of **ELYSIS** (Alcoa + Rio Tinto) research.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Decomposition Voltage of Al₂O₃

The standard free energy of formation of Al₂O₃ at 1000 K:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{f,Al_2O_3}(1000\,K) \approx -1320\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

Half-reaction:
$$Al_2O_3 \longrightarrow 2\,Al + \tfrac{3}{2}\,O_2,\quad n = 6\ e^-$$

Therefore:

$$E^{\circ} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$$

$$E^{\circ} = -\dfrac{(-1320 \times 10^{3})}{6 \times 96485}$$

$$E^{\circ} = \dfrac{1320000}{578910}$$

$$\boxed{E^{\circ} \approx 2.28\ \text{V at 1000 K}}$$

This is the **thermodynamic minimum**. Real cells operate at 4.0–5.0 V due to overpotentials and ohmic drops.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Mass of Aluminium per Coulomb

By **Faraday's First Law**:

$$m = \dfrac{M \cdot Q}{n \cdot F} = Z \cdot Q$$

Where **Electrochemical Equivalent (Z)** for Al:

$$Z_{Al} = \dfrac{M}{nF} = \dfrac{27\ \text{g/mol}}{3 \times 96485\ \text{C/mol}}$$

$$Z_{Al} = \dfrac{27}{289455} = 9.328 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{g/C}$$

Converted to **grams per Ampere-hour** (1 Ah = 3600 C):

$$m_{Ah} = Z \times 3600 = 9.328 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 = 0.3358\ \text{g/Ah}$$

$$\boxed{1\ \text{Ah} \longrightarrow 0.3358\ \text{g Al (theoretical)}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical Energy Consumption

Minimum energy to produce 1 kg Al from Al₂O₃:

$$W_{th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}}{M_{Al} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6}}$$

Plug values (per mole of Al, n = 3):

$$W_{th} = \dfrac{3 \times 96485 \times 2.28}{0.027 \times 3.6 \times 10^{6}}$$

Numerator:

$$3 \times 96485 \times 2.28 = 6.60 \times 10^{5}\ \text{J}$$

Denominator:

$$0.027 \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} = 9.72 \times 10^{4}\ \text{kg·(kJ unit conversion factor)} $$

Convert J → kWh dividing by $3.6 \times 10^{6}$:

$$W_{th} = \dfrac{6.60 \times 10^{5}}{9.72 \times 10^{4}} = 6.79\ \text{kWh/kg}$$

$$\boxed{W_{th} \approx 6.3 - 6.8\ \text{kWh/kg Al}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Current & Energy Efficiency Comparison

Actual industrial cell operates at:
- $V_{cell} = 4.5\ \text{V}$
- $\eta_c = 0.94$ (94 %)

Actual energy:

$$W_{act} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{\eta_c \times 0.3358} = \dfrac{4.5}{0.94 \times 0.3358}$$

$$W_{act} = \dfrac{4.5}{0.3156} = 14.26\ \text{kWh/kg Al}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Overall energy efficiency} = \dfrac{W_{th}}{W_{act}} = \dfrac{6.32}{14.26} \approx 44.3\ \%}$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Cell Voltage Decomposition

A real cell voltage partition:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{cell} &= E_{eq} + \eta_{anode} + \vert\eta_{cathode}\vert + IR_{bath} + IR_{external} \\
V_{cell} &= 2.10 + 0.45 + 0.05 + 1.60 + 0.30 \\
V_{cell} &\approx 4.50\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

Each term in volts:

| Term | Magnitude (V) | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| $E_{eq}$ (decomposition) | 2.10 | Thermodynamic minimum |
| $\eta_{anode}$ | 0.45 | CO₂ bubble overpotential |
| $\vert\eta_{cathode}\vert$ | 0.05 | Negligible (liquid Al) |
| $IR_{bath}$ | 1.60 | Ohmic drop in cryolite (largest loss) |
| $IR_{external}$ | 0.30 | Bus bars, contacts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The bath ohmic drop is the SINGLE largest energy loss (~35–40 % of total voltage).** Research focuses on increasing bath conductivity (LiF additives) and reducing ACD (anode–cathode distance).

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Process Variables vs. Outcomes

| Variable ↑ | Bath Conductivity | Liquidus T | Al₂O₃ Solubility | Current Eff. |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cryolite Ratio (CR)** | ↑ (max at CR=3) | ↓ | ↑ slightly | ↑ |
| **AlF₃ content** | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ (then ↑) |
| **CaF₂** | ↓ slightly | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ (less Na loss) |
| **Temperature** | ↑ | — | ↑ | ↑ |
| **Al₂O₃ content** | ↑ slightly | — | — | ↓ above 6 % |
| **Current density** | — | — | — | ↓ (more back-reaction) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hall–Héroult Cell — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DC["DC Rectifier 100 to 600 kA, 4 to 5 V"] --> BUS["Aluminium Bus Bar Anode Side"]
    BUS --> ANODE["Carbon Anode Pre baked or Soderberg, Consumable"]
    ANODE --> BATH["Molten Electrolyte Na3AlF6 + Al2O3 at 950 C"]
    BATH -->|Al3+ reduced| CATH["Carbon Cathode Lining"]
    BATH -->|O2- oxidised to CO2| ANODE
    BATH --> ALPOOL["Liquid Aluminium Pool Density 2.3 g per cm3, Cathode"]
    ALPOOL --> CATH
    CATH --> CBUS["Cathode Collector Bars Steel embedded in carbon"]
    CBUS --> DC
    ALPOOL -. Sucked by crucible .-> TAP["Aluminium Tapping 950 C liquid metal"]
    ANODE -. Gas vent .-> GAS["Off Gas CO2 plus HF plus SO2 Treatment via Dry Scrubber"]
    FEED["Alumina Feed from Hoppers point feeders"] --> BATH

    classDef power fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef electrode fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000

    class DC,BUS power
    class ANODE,CATH electrode
    class BATH,ALPOOL process
    class TAP,GAS,FEED,CBUS output
```

### 4.2 Process Logic Flow — Inside One Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1 Alumina powder fed through point feeder, crust broken by anode"] --> B["Step 2 Alumina dissolves into bath, releases Al3+ and O2-"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Al3+ migrates to cathode, gains 3 electrons"]
    C --> D["Step 4 Liquid Al droplets coalesce and pool at bottom"]
    D --> E["Step 5 O2- migrates to carbon anode, oxidises C to CO2"]
    E --> F["Step 6 CO2 escapes, anode recedes downward, is repositioned"]
    F --> G["Step 7 Liquid Al siphoned out every 24 to 48 hours"]

    classDef step fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G step
```

### 4.3 Voltage Partition Sankey-style Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V["Total Cell Voltage 4.50 V"] --> V1["Equilibrium 2.10 V"]
    V --> V2["Anode Overpotential 0.45 V"]
    V --> V3["Cathode Overpotential 0.05 V"]
    V --> V4["Bath Ohmic Drop 1.60 V"]
    V --> V5["External Ohmic Drop 0.30 V"]

    classDef total fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef therm fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000

    class V total
    class V2,V3,V4,V5 loss
    class V1 therm
```

### 4.4 Potroom Layout — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUB["Substation 400 kV AC"] --> REC["Silicon Controlled Rectifier SCR"]
    REC --> LINE1["Potline 1, Series Connected 200 to 300 cells"]
    REC --> LINE2["Potline 2, Series Connected 200 to 300 cells"]
    LINE1 --> POT["Individual Cell 500 kA, 4.5 V"]
    LINE2 --> POT2["Individual Cell 500 kA, 4.5 V"]
    POT --> CAST["Casthouse Crucible furnace then direct chill casting"]
    POT2 --> CAST
    CAST --> PROD["Aluminium Ingots, Billets, Wire Rod"]
```

### 4.5 Conceptual Map of Losses & Targets

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR["Input Electrical Energy 14.26 kWh per kg"] --> USEF["Useful Thermodynamic 6.32 kWh per kg, 44.3 percent"]
    PWR --> LOSS1["Ohmic Bath Loss, 1.60 V, 35.6 percent"]
    PWR --> LOSS2["Anode Reaction Loss, 0.45 V, 10 percent"]
    PWR --> LOSS3["External Resistance Loss, 0.30 V, 6.7 percent"]
    PWR --> LOSS4["Faradaic Loss, 6 percent current inefficiency, 3.4 percent"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Note for KTU Students:** The above diagrams replace a traditional physical cross-section drawing. They map every functional interaction (mass, charge, heat, gas) inside the cell and the entire potline.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

#### Q1. Why is cryolite used in the Hall–Héroult process instead of electrolyzing pure alumina?
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pure alumina has a very high melting point (~2072 °C) and poor ionic conductivity even when molten. Cryolite (Na₃AlF₆) serves three critical functions:

1. **Flux action**: Forms a low-melting eutectic with Al₂O₃ (~10 %), reducing the operating temperature from ~2072 °C to ~950 °C. **(1 mark)**
2. **Ionic solvent**: Dissolves Al₂O₃ and provides high ionic conductivity (~2.2 S/cm at 960 °C) via mobile Na⁺ ions. **(1 mark)**
3. **Density & separation**: Bath density (~2.1 g/cm³) is less than molten Al (~2.3 g/cm³), allowing clean phase separation. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Q2. What is the "Anode Effect" in the Hall–Héroult cell and how is it rectified?
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Anode Effect** is a sudden voltage rise (from ~4.5 V to **30–50 V**) accompanied by **arc-like sparking** and **CF₄ / C₂F₆ gas evolution** at the carbon anode, occurring when **Al₂O₃ concentration falls below ~1–2 %**. **(1.5 marks)**

**Cause**: Fluoride ion discharge ($6F^- + C \rightarrow CF_4 + 4e^-$) forms an insulating gas film at the anode–bath interface, blocking current flow. **(0.5 mark)**

**Rectification**: Automatic alumina feeding resumes; the cell is "flashed" with fresh Al₂O₃ which absorbs the fluoride film and restores normal electrolysis. **(1 mark)**

### 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of the Hall–Héroult cell for the production of aluminium. State the role of cryolite. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Hall–Héroult cell operates at **V = 4.5 V**, current **I = 200 kA**, with **current efficiency η_c = 92 %**, and operates continuously for **24 hours**. Calculate the **mass of aluminium produced** and the **specific energy consumption in kWh/kg**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

1. **Steel shell** lined with **carbon cathode** (anthracite/graphite blocks) — **(0.5)**
2. **Carbon anodes** suspended from above, dipped ~3–5 cm into bath (**Anode-Cathode Distance, ACD**) — **(0.5)**
3. **Insulating refractory** between steel and carbon to minimise heat loss — **(0.5)**
4. **Bus bars** (Al/Cu) carry 100–600 kA DC — **(0.5)**
5. **Gas collection hood** above cell — **(0.5)**
6. **Point feeders** for alumina — **(0.5)**

**Working (3 marks):**

1. Al₂O₃ dissolved in molten cryolite at ~950 °C — **(0.5)**
2. At cathode: $Al^{3+} + 3e^- \rightarrow Al_{(l)}$ — **(0.5)**
3. At anode: $C + 2O^{2-} \rightarrow CO_2 + 4e^-$ — **(0.5)**
4. Net: $2Al_2O_3 + 3C \rightarrow 4Al + 3CO_2$ — **(0.5)**
5. Liquid Al tapped periodically; anode lowered as consumed — **(0.5)**
6. CO₂ + HF treated in dry scrubber — **(0.5)**

**Role of cryolite (1 mark):** Lowers mp, dissolves Al₂O₃, increases conductivity, lowers density.

*(Block diagram drawn from Section 4.1 schematic — students may reproduce in simplified form.)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $I = 200{,}000\ \text{A}$, $t = 24 \times 3600 = 86400\ \text{s}$, $\eta_c = 0.92$
- $M_{Al} = 27\ \text{g/mol}$, $n = 3$, $F = 96485\ \text{C/mol}$

**Step 1 — Theoretical mass (2 marks):**

$$m_{th} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

$$m_{th} = \dfrac{200000 \times 86400 \times 27}{3 \times 96485}$$

$$m_{th} = \dfrac{4.666 \times 10^{11}}{289455} = 1.6122 \times 10^{6}\ \text{g} = 1612.2\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Actual mass (2 marks):**

$$m_{act} = \eta_c \times m_{th} = 0.92 \times 1612.2$$

$$\boxed{m_{act} = 1483.2\ \text{kg Al}}$$

**Step 3 — Energy consumed (1.5 marks):**

$$E = V \cdot I \cdot t = 4.5 \times 200000 \times 86400$$

$$E = 7.776 \times 10^{10}\ \text{J}$$

Convert to kWh: divide by $3.6 \times 10^{6}$:

$$E = 21600\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Specific energy (1.5 marks):**

$$W = \dfrac{21600}{1483.2} = 14.56\ \text{kWh/kg}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 14.56\ \text{kWh/kg Al}}$$

This compares with theoretical minimum 6.32 kWh/kg → overall efficiency ≈ **43.4 %**.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the anode and cathode reactions in the Hall–Héroult process. Why are the carbon anodes consumed and what are the consequences of this? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **theoretical energy consumption (kWh/kg Al)** in the Hall–Héroult process given $\Delta G^{\circ}_{Al_2O_3} = -1582\ \text{kJ/mol}$ at 298 K. Compare with the practical value and discuss the reasons for the difference. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Cathode reaction (2 marks):**

At the liquid Al / bath interface:

$$Al^{3+}_{(bath)} + 3e^- \longrightarrow Al_{(l)}$$

Reduction potential (in cryolite) ~ −1.7 V vs. carbon reference. Liquid Al pools on the carbon cathode bottom.

**Anode reaction (3 marks):**

Primary: $C + 2O^{2-} \rightarrow CO_2 + 4e^-$ (E° ≈ +1.0 V)
Secondary (anode effect): $2C + 3O^{2-} \rightarrow CO + CO_2 + 4e^-$

**Why carbon anodes are consumed (1.5 marks):**

The anode product (CO₂/CO) is formed by **chemical combination with carbon**, not by simple oxygen evolution. Thus each mole of Al produced consumes 0.75 mol of carbon (theoretically), and **0.40–0.45 kg C per kg Al** in practice (excess due to air burn, dusting, CO formation).

**Consequences (0.5 marks):**

- Continuous anode lowering required; complete anode replacement every 20–30 days.
- CO₂ emission (~1.2 tonnes per tonne Al from anode).
- Need for an anode-baking plant — adds 0.5 kg C-equivalent per kg Al as PFCs (CF₄, C₂F₆) during anode effect.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Free energy per mole Al (1.5 marks):**

$$2Al + \tfrac{3}{2}O_2 \rightarrow Al_2O_3,\quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -1582\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

Per mole of Al (divide by 2):

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{per\ Al} = \dfrac{-1582}{2} = -791\ \text{kJ/mol Al}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to electrical energy (2 marks):**

Per mole Al, n = 3 e⁻:

$$W_{th} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} = \dfrac{791000}{3 \times 96485} = 2.733\ \text{V}$$

This is the **theoretical decomposition potential per mole Al** = 2.73 V.

**Step 3 — kWh per kg (2 marks):**

Energy per mole Al = $2.733 \times 3 \times 96485 = 7.91 \times 10^{5}\ \text{J}$
Mass per mole Al = 0.027 kg

$$W_{th} = \dfrac{7.91 \times 10^{5}}{0.027 \times 3.6 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{7.91 \times 10^{5}}{97200} = 8.14\ \text{kWh/kg}$$

$$\boxed{W_{th} \approx 8.14\ \text{kWh/kg Al}}$$

(Using 298 K ΔG; at 1000 K this drops to ≈ 6.32 kWh/kg.)

**Step 4 — Comparison and reasons for difference (1.5 marks):**

| Quantity | Theoretical | Practical |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage | ~2.1 – 2.7 V | 4.0 – 5.0 V |
| Energy | ~6 – 8 kWh/kg | 13 – 15 kWh/kg |
| Current eff. | 100 % | 92 – 96 % |

**Reasons for gap:**
- **Ohmic drops** in bath, anode, cathode, external bus bars (~1.9 V).
- **Anode overpotential** for CO₂ evolution (~0.45 V).
- **Faradaic losses**: back-reaction of dissolved Al with CO₂; electronic short circuits; current leakage to cathode collector bars (~5 %).
- **Heat losses** requiring continuous energy input.

The overall **energy efficiency is only 40 – 50 %**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Writing "Al³⁺ migrates to cathode and is reduced by accepting electrons from the battery"** — accept only if you clearly identify that Na⁺ is the *bulk* current carrier and Al³⁺ is reduced *at* the cathode interface. Examiners deduct 1 mark if ionic migration pathway is not clarified.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — typical error: writing energy in J instead of kWh, or using $F = 96500$ instead of $96485$ (in long derivations). Always show unit conversion explicitly.
>
> 3. **Confusing "anode effect" with "anode consumption"** — they are different phenomena. Anode effect is a **gas film** phenomenon; anode consumption is a **chemical reaction** phenomenon.
>
> 4. **Omitting the temperature** — ΔG° depends strongly on T; using 298 K ΔG in a 950 °C cell loses 1 mark unless explicitly justified.
>
> 5. **Treating Hall and Héroult as the same person** — examiners expect you to note they were *different persons* who independently invented the process in the same year (1886).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ Hall–Héroult = **electrolysis of Al₂O₃ dissolved in molten cryolite** at ~950 °C.
- ✅ **Independent inventors in 1886**: Charles Martin Hall (USA) and Paul Héroult (France).
- ✅ **Cathode**: carbon-lined steel pot → collects liquid Al.
- ✅ **Anode**: consumable carbon → forms CO₂ (and CO during anode effect).
- ✅ **Overall reaction**: $2Al_2O_3 + 3C \rightarrow 4Al + 3CO_2$.
- ✅ **Role of cryolite**: lowers mp, dissolves Al₂O₃, increases conductivity, lowers density.
- ✅ **Anode effect**: voltage spike from 4.5 V to 30–50 V due to CF₄ gas film at low Al₂O₃.
- ✅ **Theoretical Z(Al)** = 0.3358 g/Ah; **practical specific energy** = 13–15 kWh/kg.
- ✅ **Theoretical energy** = 6.32 kWh/kg → **overall efficiency ~44 %**.
- ✅ **Voltage decomposition**: $V = E_{eq}(2.1) + \eta_a(0.45) + \vert\eta_c\vert(0.05) + IR_{bath}(1.6) + IR_{ext}(0.3)$.
- ✅ **Current efficiency** 92 – 96 %; losses from back-reaction and electronic shorting.
- ✅ **Additives**: AlF₃ (lowers mp), CaF₂ (reduces Na loss), LiF (raises conductivity).
- ✅ **Environmental concerns**: CO₂ (~1.2 t/t Al), PFCs (CF₄, C₂F₆), HF gas — all addressed by **dry scrubbing** and **inert anode R&D (ELYSIS)**.
- ✅ **Global production**: ~64 Mt/year; largest producers China, India, Russia, Canada, UAE.
- ✅ **Key Indian plants**: Vedanta (Jharsuguda, Korba), Hindalco (Renukoot, Hirakud), NALCO (Angul).
